column8: Support end-to-end sales operations by ensuring accurate processing, strong controls, and compliance across all enterprise sales activities.
column15: This task mixes structured controls (best handled by Traditional AI), multi-system workflow execution (Agentic AI), and heavy documentation/communication (Generative AI), but still requires accountable human judgment for exceptions, negotiations, and policy ownership. Traditional AI can reliably enforce deterministic validations and flag anomalies at scale. Agentic AI can reduce cycle time by orchestrating approvals, collecting evidence, and managing exceptions, but must operate within guardrails. Generative AI accelerates drafting SOPs, summaries, and guided checklists, improving consistency and reducing rep errors, yet cannot be trusted as a sole source of truth. Assumptions: enterprise sales stack includes CRM/CPQ/CLM/ERP with APIs; policies and templates are available for retrieval; organization permits automation for low/medium-risk deals with audit logging. Constraints: regulated customers/regions and non-standard terms increase required human involvement.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving deviations from pricing/contract/compliance policies (e.g., non-standard indemnities, data processing addenda, unusual payment terms) because accountability and risk trade-offs are non-delegable; (2) interpreting ambiguous policies and resolving conflicts between revenue goals and control requirements; (3) maintaining and updating approval matrices, control objectives, and training data/knowledge bases; (4) reviewing AI-flagged anomalies and deciding whether they indicate fraud, error, or legitimate business rationale; (5) incident response and customer escalations where reputational/legal impact is high. Mandatory human checkpoints should exist for high discount bands, regulated industries, public sector, cross-border data transfers, and any deal with manual revenue recognition implications.
column17: Autonomous orchestration could take unintended actions (wrong approver routing, premature submission, overwriting CRM fields) or create compliance gaps if guardrails are incomplete; emergent behavior in complex workflows can be hard to audit. Mitigations: least-privilege access, action allowlists, simulation/sandbox testing, step-level approvals for high-risk actions, comprehensive audit logs, and kill switches/rollback mechanisms.
column18: May hallucinate policy interpretations or misstate deal terms when summarizing; risk of leaking sensitive customer/pricing data if prompts are not governed; tone or wording could create contractual ambiguity if used in customer-facing contexts. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with approved sources, strict redaction/DLP, template locking, human review for external communications, and citation requirements for policy answers.
column19: Rule engines can become brittle/outdated as products and policies change; anomaly models can generate false positives/negatives and may encode historical bias (e.g., discount norms by region). Mitigations: governance for rule updates, monitoring model drift, periodic recalibration, explainability dashboards, and feedback loops from deal desk outcomes.
column20: Data inconsistency across CRM/CPQ/CLM/ERP can cause conflicting decisions; unclear source of truth can lead to duplicated or missing approvals. Orchestrating GenAI outputs into agentic actions can amplify errors (e.g., a hallucinated summary triggering wrong routing). Mitigations: master data management, schema validation, idempotent workflows, human-in-the-loop gates between GenAI text and system actions, and end-to-end observability with reconciliation reports.

column8: Manage day-to-day operational workflows by coordinating handoffs, tracking dependencies, and resolving process gaps with internal partners.
column15: The task mixes structured workflow control (best served by Traditional AI + workflow automation) with dynamic coordination across internal partners (where Agentic AI can orchestrate and follow up within guardrails) and heavy communication/summarization (where Generative AI excels). However, an Enterprise AE at Grade 7/8 must retain ownership of prioritization, relationship management, and approvals for pricing/contract/compliance-sensitive decisions. Assumptions: core systems (CRM/CPQ/ticketing/collab tools) have APIs and clean enough data for automation; governance allows AI-drafted internal comms; agent actions are constrained by policies and require approvals for high-impact steps.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) prioritizing conflicting dependencies across deals and internal teams based on revenue, strategic value, and relationship context; (2) approving escalations that could affect customer commitments, pricing, legal terms, or compliance (e.g., security, privacy, export controls); (3) resolving ambiguous process gaps where policies conflict or data is incomplete; (4) coaching internal partners and driving adoption of new SOPs. Edge cases: novel deal structures, M&A/partner channels, regulated customers, or urgent executive escalations where automated routing could be inappropriate.
column17: Autonomous follow-ups/escalations could spam stakeholders, escalate prematurely, or route tasks to wrong owners; emergent behavior if goals are mis-specified (optimize speed over compliance). Mitigations: strict action permissions, approval gates for escalations/commitments, rate limits, audit logs, simulation/testing in sandbox, and clear stop conditions.

column8: Coordinate with external vendors and internal stakeholders to maintain service levels, align deliverables, and address operational issues promptly.
column15: This Enterprise Account Executive coordination task mixes structured operational monitoring with high-context relationship management. Traditional AI is best for deterministic SLA measurement, routing, and validation on structured data. Generative AI accelerates communication, summarization, and executive narratives but must be grounded in source systems to avoid inaccuracies. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step follow-ups and escalations across tools, reducing latency in coordination; however, external commitments and exception handling remain high-risk. Humans remain essential for negotiation, prioritization under ambiguity, and accountability for contractual/compliance outcomes. Assumptions: access to CRM (e.g., Salesforce), ticketing (e.g., ServiceNow/Jira), vendor portals, and meeting transcripts; clear escalation playbooks and severity matrices exist; organization permits AI-assisted drafting and internal automation with audit logs.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) any binding external commitment (ETAs, scope changes, credits/penalties), (2) interpretation of contract/SOW terms and customer impact trade-offs, (3) high-severity incidents where actions can cause outages or reputational harm, (4) compliance/privacy/security-sensitive communications and data sharing with vendors, and (5) edge cases where data is incomplete/conflicting (multiple sources disagree on SLA clock, vendor disputes ownership). Humans should approve agentic actions that contact external parties, change priorities for premium accounts, or trigger financial/legal consequences; humans also validate GenAI-generated summaries before distribution to executives/customers.

column8: Maintain strong documentation by updating playbooks, process maps, and audit-ready records to support consistent execution and governance.
column15: Documentation maintenance for an Enterprise AE requires both content creation and governance. Generative AI is well-suited to drafting/updating playbooks, SOP text, process-map specifications, and change summaries from heterogeneous inputs, but it cannot be trusted to assert policy truth without verification. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the end-to-end update lifecycle (detect triggers, open tasks, route approvals, assemble audit packages) across tools with minimal manual coordination, yet must operate within strict guardrails. Traditional AI provides reliable, explainable validation (template completeness, required approvals, metadata alignment) and stale-content detection using rules and structured signals. Humans remain essential for setting standards, resolving ambiguities, and approving high-risk changes that affect customer commitments and compliance. Assumptions: documentation lives in common enterprise systems (Confluence/SharePoint, CRM, ticketing), templates/checklists exist or can be defined, and access controls allow AI to read/write within least-privilege boundaries.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) final approval of changes impacting pricing/discounting, legal terms, regulated-industry guidance, or customer-facing commitments; (2) resolving ambiguous or conflicting inputs (e.g., RevOps vs Legal guidance); (3) validating that process maps reflect actual enterprise deal execution and exception handling; (4) periodic audits of AI-driven workflows, permissions, and retention policies; and (5) sign-off on audit packages before external/internal audit submission. Humans are irreplaceable where accountability, policy interpretation, and contextual judgment about business risk are needed.
column17: Autonomous actions could update or publish incorrect documentation, route approvals to wrong stakeholders, or over-automate governance leading to gaps in accountability; emergent behavior from tool integrations can cause unintended changes. Mitigations: least-privilege access, sandbox/draft-only modes, explicit approval gates, action logging, rate limits, and continuous monitoring with rollback/versioning.
column18: May hallucinate policies, invent steps, or misstate approval requirements; can drift in tone/terminology across versions; may leak sensitive customer or employee data if prompts/logs are mishandled. column8: Analyze sales and operational data for trends and exceptions, escalating risks and recommending corrective actions to improve accuracy.
column15: The core detection/measurement work is best handled by Traditional AI (repeatable statistical checks, anomaly detection, reconciliation) and supported by Agentic AI to reliably pull data, run monitors, and route escalations across enterprise tools. Generative AI adds value by translating quantitative findings into decision-ready narratives and proposing plausible corrective actions, but it should not be the source of truth for numbers. Human involvement remains essential to set materiality thresholds, interpret exceptions in account context (e.g., one-off enterprise procurement delays), and approve high-impact escalations/actions that affect customers, forecasts, or compliance. Assumptions: access to clean-ish structured data from CRM/CPQ/billing; governance allows automated ticket creation; models are calibrated per segment/region; sensitive actions require approval gates.

column8: Improve processes through standardization and automation by identifying repetitive work, defining requirements, and partnering on implementation.
column15: This task mixes discovery, judgment-heavy prioritization, and cross-functional change management with data-driven analysis and repeatable documentation. Traditional AI is well-suited to structured telemetry analysis (frequency, cycle time, compliance checks) to objectively surface repetitive work and bottlenecks. Generative AI accelerates the heavy writing/synthesis load: turning messy qualitative inputs into SOPs, process maps, and well-formed requirements/user stories, plus enablement materials. Agentic AI adds value once guardrails are defined by autonomously orchestrating multi-step discovery and running/monitoring pilots across tools, reducing coordination overhead. Humans remain essential for prioritization against revenue strategy, approving controls and exception policies, and securing stakeholder alignment. Assumptions: the org has accessible system logs/APIs, a defined governance model for workflow changes, and a sandbox/pilot environment; without these, agentic automation share drops and human effort rises.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) prioritization decisions that trade off seller time, forecast integrity, and customer experience; (2) approvals for any workflow that changes pricing/discounting, customer communications, data retention, or access controls; (3) defining exception handling (strategic accounts, non-standard deal terms) and escalation paths; (4) validating that proposed standardization does not conflict with compensation plans, regional policies, or regulatory requirements; (5) final sign-off on rollout timing and change-management approach. Edge cases: high-stakes enterprise deals with bespoke legal terms, regulated-industry customers, and quarter-end processes where automation errors can materially impact bookings/forecast.

column8: Support period-close activities by validating inputs, reconciling key metrics, and ensuring timely completion of close checklists.
column15: Period-close support is largely structured and repeatable (data pulls, validations, reconciliations), making Traditional AI strong for deterministic checks and variance computation. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the end-to-end close runbook across tools, tracking dependencies, and driving exception resolution-reducing cycle time. Generative AI is best for narrative outputs (status updates, variance explanations, audit commentary) and interactive guidance, but must be grounded in provided figures to avoid hallucinations. Humans remain essential for governance, approvals, and resolving ambiguous, high-stakes discrepancies that affect revenue reporting and compensation. Assumptions: access to CRM/CPQ/billing data via APIs or exports; a defined close checklist/runbook exists; internal controls require human sign-off for adjustments.

Edge cases: regulated industries, public-sector procurement, data residency, and bespoke SLAs where AI suggestions must be reviewed line-by-line.

column8: Develop and deliver tailored presentations, demos, and business cases, translating customer requirements into clear value narratives and ROI justification for buying committees.
column15: This task mixes high-stakes persuasion and accountability (human-led) with repeatable content production and analysis (AI-assisted). Traditional AI is best for auditable ROI/TCO calculations and structured retrieval/recommendation of approved collateral, where determinism and governance matter. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the end-to-end workflow (collect inputs, assemble packages, manage reviews, schedule rehearsals, log outcomes) and adapting to changes, but autonomy must be bounded due to customer-facing risk. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/discovery notes, governed content library, approved ROI calculator templates, and clear policy on what can be auto-shared externally. Uncertainty: maturity of the org's content governance and data quality will shift work split toward humans if weak.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) strategic positioning choices (what to emphasize/de-emphasize given politics, competition, and procurement), (2) approval of any externally shared claims (security, compliance, performance, roadmap), (3) validating ROI assumptions and ensuring they are defensible to CFO/procurement, (4) live delivery-handling objections, reading the room, and negotiating trade-offs, and (5) edge cases such as regulated customers, public-sector rules, competitive takeouts, or when discovery data is incomplete/contradictory. Mandatory approvals should gate: final deck, demo environment configuration, pricing/ROI outputs, and any non-standard terms or references.
column17: Over-automation could send or schedule customer-facing materials without proper approval; tool misconfiguration could pull wrong account data or outdated collateral; emergent behavior in multi-step planning may bypass compliance steps. Agentic AI can coordinate the recurring workflow (reminders, pulling artifacts, proposing CRM edits) and reduce operational overhead, yet requires guardrails and approval gates to avoid unintended CRM changes. Mandatory approvals should be required for top deals, end-of-quarter changes, and any AI-suggested stage movement or close-date pull-in.
column17: Autonomous actions could overwrite CRM data, spam stakeholders with reminders, or create inconsistent records across systems; emergent behavior in multi-step workflows can be hard to audit. column15: This task spans governance, controls, and execution across quote/contract/order workflows. Traditional AI is best for deterministic validations, approval thresholds, and structured anomaly/KPI monitoring (high maturity, low ambiguity). Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-system workflows and managing exceptions end-to-end, but must be constrained by playbooks and approval gates due to compliance risk. Generative AI is useful for summarizing contracts, drafting SOPs, and producing narrative reporting, yet requires verification because hallucinations or missed nuances can create compliance exposure. Humans remain essential for setting policy, approving high-stakes exceptions, and owning audit/regulatory accountability. Assumptions: organization uses integrated CRM/CPQ/CLM/ERP with event logs; policies are codified enough to translate into rules; sensitive actions (order release, non-standard terms) require human approval. Edge cases include novel deal structures, new products, and jurisdiction-specific requirements where automation confidence is low.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) defining/approving policy changes, approval matrices, and control design; (2) approving non-standard pricing/terms, large discounts, unusual revenue recognition impacts, and any exceptions that could create legal/financial exposure; (3) final accountability for audit readiness (evidence, segregation of duties, access controls) and regulatory compliance; (4) adjudicating conflicts between functions and making strategic trade-offs (speed vs. risk) on priority deals; (5) handling low-confidence AI outputs-e.g., GenAI contract summaries with ambiguous clauses, agentic remediation steps that would pause orders, or anomaly flags that could be legitimate business shifts. Mandatory human checkpoints should exist before: releasing orders above thresholds, accepting non-standard clauses, overriding pricing floors, changing customer master data, and modifying automation rules/agents.
column17: Risks: unintended actions across systems (e.g., auto-submitting orders prematurely), emergent behavior from poorly specified goals, insufficient audit trails, privilege misuse, and over-automation reducing segregation of duties. Mitigations: least-privilege access, action whitelists, approval gates for high-risk steps, simulation/sandbox testing, continuous monitoring with rollback, immutable logs, and clear escalation policies; periodic control testing and agent behavior reviews.
column18: Risks: hallucinated clause interpretations, missed jurisdictional nuances, leakage of confidential customer/contract data, inconsistent tone or unauthorized commitments in drafted communications. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved policy/contract templates; strict grounding/citation requirements; redaction and data-loss prevention; human review for legal/customer-facing outputs; maintain prompt/version control and evaluation on representative contracts.
column19: Risks: brittle rules that lag policy changes, false positives/negatives in anomaly models, bias in historical data (e.g., discount norms), and integration failures causing silent control gaps. Mitigations: governance for rule updates, automated regression tests, model drift monitoring, threshold tuning, and reconciliation controls between systems; clear ownership for rule/model maintenance.
column20: Risks: inconsistent source-of-truth across CRM/CPQ/CLM/ERP, cascading errors when GenAI summaries feed agentic actions, identity/access mismatches, and audit evidence fragmentation. Mitigations: master data management, event-driven architecture with idempotent actions, confidence scoring and human-in-the-loop routing, standardized schemas, end-to-end observability, and periodic audits of automation pathways; ensure GenAI outputs are advisory unless validated by rules/humans.

Agentic AI adds value by executing multi-step workflows across tools (CRM, email, calendar, ticketing) and adapting to changing queues, but must be constrained by playbooks and approvals. Generative AI is strongest for synthesizing unstructured updates into briefs, drafting communications, and producing narrative reporting; it should not be the system of record. Humans remain essential for strategic prioritization, exception handling, and relationship-sensitive issue resolution. Assumptions: enterprise sales stack includes CRM (e.g., Salesforce), ticketing/escalation tracking, and BI dashboards; data access is governed; automation is allowed for internal tasking and notifications but customer-facing actions require approval in many cases.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving priority changes that impact revenue/strategic accounts, (2) handling executive/customer escalations and negotiations, (3) approving customer-facing communications and any commitments (credits, timelines), (4) defining/adjusting SLAs, escalation policies, and automation guardrails, and (5) resolving edge cases like conflicting data sources, ambiguous ownership, or situations where automation could violate contract terms, privacy rules, or internal controls. Humans should review agentic actions via audit logs, sampled QA, and mandatory approvals for high-risk triggers (e.g., reassigning key accounts, escalating to execs, changing forecast categories).
column17: Unintended actions (mass reassignment, spammy notifications), runaway escalation loops, or optimizing for SLA metrics at the expense of relationship outcomes; limited transparency in decision paths. column8: Coordinate with internal partners and external vendors to align on deal execution requirements, handoffs, and timelines for complex enterprise opportunities.
column15: This task mixes relationship-heavy coordination and high-stakes commitments with repeatable operational mechanics. Agentic AI can run the workflow layer (scheduling, task routing, reminders, status monitoring) and keep momentum across many stakeholders, but should operate within guardrails and escalate exceptions. Generative AI is best for synthesizing unstructured inputs into clear briefs and drafting alignment communications/handoff artifacts, reducing time spent on documentation and updates; it should not be the source of truth for facts or commitments. Traditional AI adds value where structured, explainable automation is needed: enforcing CRM completeness, estimating lead times from historical data, and scoring execution risk. Humans remain essential for strategy, negotiation, and final approvals because enterprise deals involve ambiguity, politics, and legal/compliance accountability. Assumptions: test_dhina has modern CRM (e.g., Salesforce), collaboration tools (email/calendar/Slack/Teams), and permissioned access to deal artifacts; vendors/customers allow limited automation and recording where legal.
column16: Human oversight is essential at (1) any binding commitment to customers/vendors (dates, scope, SLAs, pricing/terms), (2) prioritization trade-offs when internal teams disagree or capacity is constrained, (3) compliance/security/privacy decisions (data residency, DPAs, SOC2 evidence sharing, regulated-industry requirements), (4) exception handling when AI detects slippage or conflicting requirements, and (5) relationship management and executive escalations where tone, trust, and negotiation tactics matter. Edge cases requiring humans: novel deal structures, new vendors without historical data, multi-country regulatory constraints, and situations where recorded/transcribed meetings are not permitted. Humans should also periodically review AI rules/models for drift when pricing strategy, org structure, or regulations change.

Constraints: data quality, access controls, and revenue recognition policies limit automation of approvals.

column8: Ensure accurate processing, strong controls, and compliance across all Enterprise Sales operational activities by defining governance checkpoints, reviewing exceptions, and aligning execution to internal policies and audit requirements.
column15: This VP-level governance task mixes (a) deterministic control enforcement and monitoring (well-suited to Traditional AI), (b) cross-system workflow execution and continuous triage (best handled by Agentic AI with guardrails), and (c) heavy documentation/reporting and synthesis of exceptions (where Generative AI accelerates drafting and summarization). However, accountability for control design, risk appetite, and high-stakes exception approvals cannot be delegated: humans must make final decisions, manage stakeholder trade-offs, and provide audit attestations. Assumptions: Enterprise Sales Ops uses integrated systems (CRM/CPQ/CLM/ERP), has defined policies/thresholds, and can log approvals/events for audit trails. Constraints: regulatory/audit requirements demand explainability, immutable logs, and clear human sign-off for material items; GenAI outputs must be grounded in approved sources.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) setting/adjusting control objectives, materiality, and approval thresholds; (2) approving high-risk exceptions (large discounts, non-standard terms, revenue recognition implications, data privacy/security deviations); (3) interpreting ambiguous policies and resolving conflicts between Legal/Finance/Sales priorities; (4) final review of audit evidence and signing representations/attestations; (5) handling edge cases such as novel deal structures, new product/region launches, M&A-driven process changes, or system outages where automated controls may misfire. Humans must also periodically validate model/rule performance, approve playbooks, and ensure governance changes do not create undue sales friction or discriminatory impacts.
column17: Risks: over-automation leading to improper routing/approvals, unintended workflow loops, privilege escalation via tool access, and opaque decision paths that auditors reject. Mitigations: least-privilege tool permissions, hard guardrails (cannot approve-only route), deterministic checkpoints for material actions, comprehensive logging, simulation/testing in sandbox, and kill-switch/manual override.
column18: Risks: hallucinated policy statements, incorrect summaries that omit material exceptions, leakage of sensitive customer/contract data, and inconsistent tone/claims in audit narratives. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with approved policy corpus, mandatory citations, redaction/DLP, human review for audit-facing outputs, and confidence scoring with abstain behavior.
column19: Risks: brittle rules that lag policy changes, false positives that create sales friction, false negatives that miss control failures, and biased anomaly thresholds across segments/regions. Mitigations: change management with versioned rules, periodic threshold recalibration, monitoring precision/recall, segmented baselines, and documented exceptions handling.
column20: Risks: inconsistent data definitions across CRM/CPQ/ERP causing incorrect control outcomes; broken audit trails when systems don't share immutable logs; conflicting recommendations between models; and security/privacy exposure through connectors. Mitigations: master data governance, unified event logging, reconciliation controls, clear source-of-truth hierarchy, staged rollouts, and security reviews for all integrations.
column22: Orchestrate governance workflow across systems (CRM/CPQ/ERP/CLM). Continuous control monitoring and exception triage. Audit evidence package assembly and readiness checks|Draft and maintain policy/control documentation and enablement content. Summarize exceptions and produce executive compliance reporting. Interpret unstructured deal artifacts for compliance signals (assistive)|Define risk appetite, control objectives, and approve governance design. Approve/deny high-risk exceptions and negotiate trade-offs. Resolve ambiguous policy interpretations and manage stakeholder alignment. Own external/internal audit interactions and final attestations|Deterministic control checks and validation rules. Anomaly detection on operational metrics. Access control and SoD analytics
column23: Coordinates multi-step control workflows (e.g., deal desk approvals, discount thresholds, SOX checkpoints) by triggering tasks, routing approvals, collecting evidence artifacts, and enforcing sequencing/SLAs; adapts routing based on deal attributes and prior exceptions while keeping humans in the loop for approvals. Runs scheduled/near-real-time checks, opens cases for anomalies (e.g., non-standard terms, missing approvals, revenue recognition flags), gathers context from linked records, proposes severity/owner, and escalates per playbooks. Automatically compiles control evidence (approval logs, policy attestations, change histories), validates completeness against audit request lists, and prompts owners for missing items; maintains an audit trail of actions taken.|Generates first drafts of SOPs, control narratives, RACI, training snippets, and change communications tailored to Enterprise Sales Ops; summarizes policy changes and produces role-specific guidance, with citations to source policies. Creates readable summaries of exception trends, root-cause themes, and remediation status from structured logs and case notes; drafts QBR/board-ready narratives and risk statements while flagging low-confidence areas. Extracts key clauses/terms from contracts, emails, and notes (e.g., side letters, unusual payment terms) to suggest potential policy conflicts; outputs are advisory and require validation.|Requires executive judgment to balance growth vs. control friction, set materiality thresholds, and ensure alignment with corporate risk, legal, finance, and audit expectations. Exception decisions (non-standard terms, large discounts, revenue recognition impacts) carry business/legal consequences and require accountable sign-off and contextual negotiation. Humans arbitrate conflicts between policy intent and field realities, handle edge cases not covered by rules, and drive adoption across Sales, Deal Desk, Legal, Finance, and RevOps. Auditors require accountable owners for representations; humans validate evidence, respond to inquiries, and sign certifications.|Implements rule-based validations (required fields, approval thresholds, segregation-of-duties, pricing/discount guardrails) and produces explainable pass/fail results suitable for auditability. Uses classical ML/statistical methods to detect outliers in discounts, booking patterns, credit memos, churn/renewal processing times, and manual overrides; provides stable scoring and thresholds. Analyzes role/permission matrices and activity logs to identify toxic combinations and unauthorized actions using graph/rule logic; generates remediation tickets.
column41: Sales Ops Governance, Documentation, and Systems

column8: Manage day-to-day operational workflows for Enterprise Sales, including deal desk coordination, order processing readiness, and issue resolution to keep revenue operations moving on schedule and within control standards.
column15: The task mixes structured operational control (well-suited to Traditional AI rules/analytics), dynamic multi-step coordination (best handled by Agentic AI orchestration with guardrails), and high-volume communication/summarization (GenAI). However, enterprise sales operations include high-stakes exceptions, compliance/accountability, and cross-functional negotiation that require human judgment and authority. Assumptions: mature CRM/CPQ/ticketing integrations exist; policies/approval matrices are documented; AI actions are constrained by role-based access and approval gates. Under these constraints, ~65% of effort can be assisted/automated (routing, checks, summaries, dashboards), while ~35% remains human for approvals, escalations, and novel cases.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) approving pricing/terms exceptions, credit risk, and non-standard contracts; (2) interpreting ambiguous policy conflicts and making risk trade-offs; (3) customer-facing escalations where tone, commitments, and reputational impact matter; (4) governance-defining control standards, audit evidence requirements, and approving workflow changes; (5) supervising agentic actions (what can be auto-executed vs. recommend-only), especially near quarter-end when incentives increase risk. Humans must also review AI outputs when source data is incomplete, contradictory, or sensitive (PII/contract terms).
column17: Over-automation may trigger unauthorized actions (e.g., escalating incorrectly, changing records) or create cascading workflow errors; opaque decision paths complicate audits; agents can be manipulated via prompt injection from emails/tickets. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, approval gates for write actions, deterministic policy constraints, sandbox testing, audit logs, and robust input sanitization.
column18: Hallucinated deal facts or incorrect policy guidance could cause compliance breaches or customer miscommunication; context drift across long deal histories; leakage of sensitive pricing/PII if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigations: retrieval with citations, strict data access controls, redaction, human review for external comms and exceptions, and evaluation against golden sets.
column19: Brittle rules can become outdated as products/pricing change, causing false blocks or missed violations; ML anomaly models may encode historical bias (e.g., flagging certain regions unfairly) and can be gamed. Mitigations: rule governance, periodic recalibration, explainability, monitoring false positive/negative rates, and change-management tied to product/pricing updates.
column20: Data inconsistency across CRM/CPQ/ERP/ticketing can lead to conflicting recommendations; unclear system-of-record causes duplicate actions; latency can break SLA logic; compounded errors when GenAI summaries feed agent decisions. Mitigations: master data management, event-driven architecture with idempotency, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, end-to-end testing, and provenance tracking so every recommendation links to source records.
column22: Workflow orchestration across deal desk, legal, finance, and RevOps queues. Automated issue triage and resolution playbooks. Order readiness checklist execution and dependency tracking. Proactive risk/exception escalation management|Summarize deal status, blockers, and next steps for stakeholders. Draft and standardize communications and documentation. Policy/Q&A assistant for reps and deal desk|Final decision-making on exceptions, pricing strategy, and risk trade-offs. Cross-functional negotiation and conflict resolution. Governance, audit readiness, and control-standard ownership. Handling novel edge cases and customer escalations|Rules-based validation of CPQ/CRM data and approval routing. Anomaly detection on order/deal metrics. SLA tracking, forecasting of cycle times, and dashboarding
column23: Monitors CRM/CPQ/ticketing queues, prioritizes items by SLA and deal stage, routes tasks to the right owners, and triggers reminders/escalations based on policy; adapts plans when dependencies (legal redlines, credit checks) change. Detects blockers (missing approvals, pricing exceptions, product availability), selects an appropriate playbook, gathers required artifacts, opens/updates tickets, and proposes next-best actions; executes low-risk fixes (e.g., request missing fields) with human approval gates. Runs multi-step readiness checks (contract status, PO requirements, billing setup, provisioning prerequisites), tracks dependencies, and continuously updates a readiness score; re-plans when new information arrives. Continuously scans for SLA breaches, quarter-end critical deals, and control-standard violations; escalates to VP/leadership with context and recommended actions, coordinating war-room workflows.|Generates concise, role-tailored summaries from CRM notes, emails, call transcripts, and ticket histories; drafts daily standups, exec updates, and deal desk briefs with citations/links to sources. Creates templates and drafts for approval requests, exception justifications, customer-facing order status updates, and internal SOPs; adapts tone for enterprise customers and internal control language. Answers questions about pricing guardrails, approval matrices, and order requirements using retrieval over approved policy docs; suggests required fields and steps to avoid rework.|Requires accountable judgment for high-stakes approvals (non-standard terms, large discounts, credit risk) and balancing revenue vs. compliance; humans own the decision and rationale. Resolving disputes between sales, legal, finance, and product often involves persuasion, relationship management, and nuanced context not reliably automated. Humans define/approve policies, ensure regulatory and contractual compliance, and sign off on process changes; accountability cannot be delegated to AI. Unprecedented deal structures, sensitive customer situations, or reputational risks need human-led response and communications oversight.|Applies deterministic checks (required fields, discount thresholds, product compatibility, approval matrix) and routes approvals; produces explainable pass/fail outputs aligned to control standards. Uses classical ML/statistical methods to flag unusual discounts, margin erosion, atypical payment terms, or sudden stage changes that may indicate risk or process breakdown. Computes queue times, bottleneck analytics, and predicted close/order processing durations using structured data; feeds operational dashboards for the VP and RevOps.

column8: Coordinate with internal partners and external vendors to align systems, data flows, and service levels, ensuring timely support for sales execution and consistent adherence to documented procedures.
column15: This VP Enterprise Sales task mixes executive governance (prioritization, negotiation, accountability) with operational coordination and technical assurance. Humans remain essential for revenue-impact trade-offs, vendor governance, and compliance sign-offs (40%). Agentic AI can reliably run the coordination machinery-creating tasks, tracking dependencies, monitoring SLAs, and escalating within guardrails-reducing cycle time (25%). Generative AI accelerates the heavy communication/documentation load (briefs, SOPs, summaries) but must be grounded in source systems to avoid drift (20%). Traditional AI is best for deterministic controls and structured analytics (anomaly detection, SLA dashboards, forecasting) that feed both agentic workflows and human decisions (15%). Assumptions: the org uses integrated tooling (CRM, ticketing, iPaaS, data warehouse) with APIs; clear RACI and change-management gates exist; sensitive actions require approvals.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) prioritization decisions that affect revenue, customer commitments, and internal capacity; (2) contractual/vendor governance, including escalations and commercial concessions; (3) approvals for production changes, data-sharing, security/privacy/SOX controls, and exceptions to SOPs; (4) resolving ambiguous ownership or conflicting stakeholder incentives; (5) edge cases where data signals are incomplete (e.g., silent failures, political constraints) or where automated escalation could damage relationships. Humans should also audit AI-generated SOP updates and any agentic actions that modify workflows, permissions, or integrations.
column17: Over-automation causing premature escalations, noisy alerts, or unintended workflow changes; acting on incorrect system states; opaque decision paths; privilege misuse if integrated with admin APIs. Mitigations: strict guardrails (allowed actions), approval gates for high-impact steps, audit logs, simulation/testing in sandbox, rate limits, and fallback to human incident commander.
column18: Hallucinated or outdated procedure text; misinterpretation of technical constraints; leakage of sensitive customer/vendor data in prompts; tone or commitments that create contractual risk. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved docs, redaction/DLP, approval workflows for external comms, and version control with human review.
column19: Brittle rules leading to false positives/negatives; model drift as processes change; limited ability to capture nuanced business context; potential bias in prioritization heuristics. Mitigations: periodic rule/model review, monitoring precision/recall, human-in-the-loop tuning, and clear ownership for KPI definitions.
column20: Conflicting outputs between systems (CRM vs. warehouse), identity/permission mismatches, and cascading failures when agentic workflows depend on unreliable telemetry; governance gaps over which system is source of truth; compliance exposure when combining logs, customer data, and vendor data. Mitigations: master data management, unified observability, consistent RBAC, data lineage, change-management discipline, and incident runbooks that define escalation paths and decision rights.
column22: Orchestrate cross-functional workflow for system/data alignment (tickets, tasks, reminders, dependencies). Continuously monitor integration health and SLA compliance across systems. Automate vendor coordination logistics (meeting scheduling, agenda distribution, action-item tracking)|Synthesize requirements and translate between business and technical stakeholders. Draft and maintain SOPs/runbooks and communications. Prepare QBR/MBR materials and post-incident summaries|Set priorities and make trade-offs aligned to revenue impact and customer commitments. Negotiate and govern vendor relationships (commercial terms, escalations, accountability). Approve high-risk changes and ensure compliance (security, privacy, SOX, customer commitments). Resolve ambiguous cross-functional conflicts and drive alignment|Detect anomalies and data-quality issues in key sales data flows. SLA reporting and forecasting of support capacity/incident volume. Policy/rule enforcement checks for procedural adherence
column23: Uses predefined playbooks to create/assign tasks across RevOps, IT, Security, Finance, and vendors; monitors SLAs, escalates blockers, and adapts plans based on status signals from Jira/ServiceNow/CRM. Operates within guardrails (approval gates, allowed actions). Polls/receives events from integration platforms (iPaaS), CRM, data warehouse, and vendor status pages; correlates incidents to sales-impacting processes (lead routing, CPQ, provisioning) and triggers incident workflows and stakeholder notifications. Coordinates calendars, sends structured agendas, captures decisions/action items, and follows up automatically; updates shared runbooks and project plans after approvals.|Summarizes sales execution needs (e.g., lead-to-cash, renewals) into clear integration requirements, data definitions, and acceptance criteria; drafts user stories and vendor briefs from notes and existing documentation. Generates and updates documented procedures, change notices, and stakeholder updates; produces tailored comms for execs vs. ops teams while referencing source-of-truth docs to reduce drift. Creates structured narratives from metrics, timelines, and logs; highlights root causes, remediation, and preventive actions for internal partners and vendors.|Requires executive judgment to balance speed vs. risk, allocate scarce resources, and decide what to defer; involves accountability for revenue outcomes. High-stakes relationship management, contractual interpretation, and escalation diplomacy require human leadership and authority. Humans must review/approve changes with regulatory, security, or customer-impact implications; accountable sign-off cannot be delegated to AI. When stakeholders disagree on definitions, ownership, or process, human facilitation and decision-making are needed to reach durable agreements.|Applies rules/thresholds and classical ML to flag missing fields, routing failures, duplicate accounts, unusual conversion drops, or latency spikes in ETL/ELT pipelines. Uses time-series forecasting and KPI dashboards to predict ticket volume, vendor response performance, and potential SLA breaches; supports capacity planning for sales support teams. Deterministic checks ensure required approvals, mandatory fields, and step completion in workflows (e.g., change management gates, security reviews) before deployments or vendor changes proceed.

column8: Maintain strong documentation for processes, controls, and approvals, keeping playbooks current and accessible so teams can execute consistently and leadership can validate operational readiness.
column15: This task mixes content creation, governance, and operational enforcement. Generative AI is strong for drafting/updating playbooks, summarizing changes, and producing templates, but requires grounding and review to avoid inaccuracies. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating end-to-end workflows: monitoring upstream system changes, scheduling audits, routing approvals, and publishing updates-reducing coordination overhead typical in Enterprise Sales operations. Traditional AI is best for deterministic quality gates (completeness, broken links, versioning), taxonomy tagging, and anomaly detection in approval logs. Humans remain essential for setting standards, making risk/revenue trade-offs, approving high-impact changes, and ensuring adoption across leaders and regions. Assumptions: documentation lives in a central KB; workflow tooling exists (Jira/ServiceNow); integrations to CRM/CPQ/CLM are feasible; sensitive content is access-controlled. Constraints: cross-functional approvals (Legal/Finance) and regional policy differences limit full automation.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) defining/adjusting governance: what controls are mandatory, approval thresholds, and acceptable exception paths; (2) approving changes that affect pricing authority, revenue recognition, legal terms, data privacy, or regulated industries; (3) resolving conflicts when systems/process owners disagree (e.g., Sales Ops vs Finance); (4) validating that documentation reflects reality in edge cases (multi-entity deals, channel/partner deals, public sector procurement, non-standard payment terms); (5) ensuring tone and intent align with leadership direction and culture; (6) final sign-off on 'operational readiness' assertions presented to executives/board/auditors. Humans should also intervene when AI flags anomalies, when source data is incomplete, or when GenAI outputs cannot be fully grounded to authoritative references.
column17: Risks: unintended workflow actions (publishing unapproved content, notifying wrong audiences), permission misconfiguration, automation that bypasses nuanced exception handling, and opaque decision logic when prioritizing updates/escalations. Mitigations: least-privilege access; approval gates and two-person rule for publishing; sandbox/testing; audit logs; deterministic policies for routing; kill switch and manual override; monitoring for drift and integration failures.
column18: Risks: hallucinated steps/controls, outdated guidance if not grounded to current policies, overconfident language that implies approvals not actually granted, leakage of sensitive deal/customer data in prompts, and inconsistent terminology across regions. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources only; strict citation requirements; redaction/DLP; human review for policy sections; style guides and controlled vocabularies; maintain versioned prompts/templates.
column19: Risks: brittle rules that miss novel deal constructs, false positives that create alert fatigue, and biased/incorrect classifiers that mis-route documents (e.g., wrong region/segment). Mitigations: periodic rule review; threshold tuning; human-in-the-loop for classifier retraining; clear taxonomies; measure precision/recall; allow easy correction feedback loops.
column20: Risks: inconsistent sources of truth across CRM/CPQ/CLM/KB leading to conflicting guidance; identity/role mapping errors causing access leaks; cascading failures where one system change triggers incorrect doc updates; and compliance gaps if audit evidence isn't retained end-to-end. Mitigations: establish a master data/taxonomy layer; strong IAM and role-based access control; end-to-end versioning and immutable audit trails; staged rollout with canary publishing; reconciliation checks between systems; clear ownership (RACI) for each integration.
column22: Continuously monitor source-of-truth systems for process changes and trigger documentation update workflows. Orchestrate periodic documentation audits and readiness checks across regions/segments. Automate publishing, access control, and distribution of updated playbooks|Draft and refresh playbook content from structured inputs and meeting artifacts. Summarize changes and generate role-specific 'what changed' briefs. Create and maintain templates for controls, approvals, and evidence capture|Define governance standards, risk appetite, and the authoritative 'source of truth' for processes. Approve high-impact process/control changes and adjudicate exceptions. Validate real-world usability and adoption; coach leaders to enforce execution|Validate documentation completeness and consistency against required fields and rules. Classify, tag, and route documents based on metadata and taxonomy. Detect anomalies in approval/control workflows using logs
column23: Connects to CRM (e.g., Salesforce), CPQ, contract lifecycle management, enablement LMS, and policy repositories; detects signals like new approval steps, changed discount thresholds, updated legal clauses, or new product SKUs; opens tickets, assigns owners, and orchestrates review cycles with due dates and escalation. Runs scheduled audit playbooks (quarterly/after major launches), pings process owners, collects attestations, routes exceptions to the right approvers, and compiles an operational readiness status for leadership review. After approvals, pushes content to the correct knowledge base locations (Confluence/SharePoint/Guru), updates navigation, tags, and permissions by role/region, and notifies impacted teams with targeted change summaries.|Transforms process maps, SOP bullet points, call transcripts, and policy notes into clear, standardized playbook sections (purpose, scope, steps, RACI, SLAs, controls, FAQs) tailored to Enterprise Sales execution. Produces concise diffs and impact statements for AEs, Sales Ops, Deal Desk, Legal, Finance, and leadership; creates enablement snippets and quick-reference checklists to drive consistent execution. Generates reusable artifacts (approval matrices, exception request forms, control descriptions, audit evidence checklists) aligned to internal governance and common enterprise deal scenarios.|VP Enterprise Sales (with Sales Ops/Legal/Finance) must set what 'good' looks like, decide which controls are mandatory, and resolve conflicts between speed-to-revenue and compliance. Human judgment is required for policy trade-offs, customer-specific concessions, and changes that affect revenue recognition, legal exposure, or regulatory compliance. Humans must test playbooks in live deal cycles, gather qualitative feedback, ensure frontline buy-in, and hold teams accountable-areas where AI cannot fully assess organizational dynamics.|Uses deterministic checks (required sections present, correct naming conventions, versioning, links not broken, approval matrix populated, mandatory controls included) and flags deviations for remediation. Applies rules/ML classifiers to label by product, region, segment, deal type, and policy domain; ensures documents appear in the right collections and search facets. Analyzes workflow/event logs to identify missing approvals, unusually fast/slow cycles, repeated exceptions, or bypass patterns that indicate documentation gaps or control weaknesses.
column41: Sales Ops Governance, Documentation, and Systems

column8: Analyze operational and sales data for trends and exceptions, investigate root causes, and provide actionable insights to leadership to improve forecast reliability and reduce processing errors.
column15: This VP Enterprise Sales task mixes high-volume structured analytics (best handled by Traditional AI) with cross-system workflow execution (Agentic AI) and executive communication (Generative AI), but it remains high-stakes: insights influence forecast calls, revenue reporting, and process controls. Traditional AI can reliably compute trends, reconcile systems, and detect anomalies with auditability, forming the quantitative backbone (~0.35). Agentic AI adds leverage by automating recurring data pulls, monitoring, and exception triage across CRM/CPQ/order-to-cash, reducing manual coordination (~0.20). Generative AI accelerates interpretation and packaging of findings into leadership-ready narratives and helps iterate on hypotheses/queries (~0.15), but must be grounded to avoid fabrication. Humans remain essential (~0.30) for metric governance, causal validation, and decisions that affect commitments, compensation, and cross-functional change management. Assumptions: access to clean event logs and a governed semantic layer; ability to integrate with enterprise systems via APIs; and a controlled environment where agent actions require approvals for policy/system changes.
column16: Human oversight is essential at (1) metric/definition governance (forecast categories, pipeline stages, booking/billing rules) to prevent 'analysis drift' and ensure finance alignment; (2) causal validation-humans must confirm whether anomalies are true operational issues vs seasonality, one-off deals, or data backfills; (3) approvals for corrective actions that change workflows (validation rules, stage gates), impact rep behavior/comp plans, or alter customer commitments; (4) handling sensitive personnel/performance implications and ensuring fairness; (5) compliance/privacy review when combining datasets (customer data, rep activity logs). Edge cases requiring humans: major product launches, M&A territory changes, CRM migrations, quarter-end booking surges, and when data sources disagree or are partially unavailable.
column17: Risks: unintended actions (e.g., creating tickets/alerts storms, modifying dashboards/thresholds without approval), brittle integrations, emergent behavior in multi-step workflows, and opaque decision trails. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for write actions, rate limits, sandbox testing, immutable audit logs, and clear runbooks/fallback to manual processes.
column18: Risks: hallucinated explanations or invented numbers; overconfident narratives that mask uncertainty; leakage of sensitive customer/rep data in prompts; bias in language framing (blaming teams). Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations, strict no-number fabrication policies, redaction/PII controls, calibrated confidence statements, and human review for exec outputs.
column19: Risks: model drift as sales motions change; brittle rules causing false positives/negatives; spurious correlations in driver models; fairness concerns if used to evaluate reps. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, monitoring precision/recall, interpretable models with documented assumptions, holdout testing by segment, and governance separating analytics from performance management decisions.
column20: Risks: inconsistent metric definitions across tools; feedback loops where agent actions change the data generating process; compounding errors when GenAI summarizes flawed Traditional AI outputs; security gaps across connectors. Mitigations: single semantic layer/metric store, end-to-end lineage, automated reconciliation checks before narrative generation, staged deployments, and centralized IAM/secrets management.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end data pipeline and monitoring across sales systems. Automated exception triage and root-cause workflow execution. Continuous improvement loop for forecast/process controls|Narrative synthesis of insights for executive consumption. Hypothesis generation and scenario framing. Natural-language querying and semantic layer assistance|Define business context, success criteria, and metric governance. Validate root-cause conclusions and approve corrective actions. Executive decision-making and stakeholder management|Data validation, reconciliation, and rule-based exception detection. Statistical trend analysis and anomaly detection on structured metrics. Forecast accuracy diagnostics and driver attribution modeling
column23: Autonomously schedules and runs recurring pulls from CRM (e.g., Salesforce), CPQ, billing, support, and data warehouse; monitors freshness/quality SLAs; opens tickets or pings owners when feeds break; adapts runbooks based on failure patterns while keeping humans in approval loops for changes. When anomalies are detected, agentic workflows gather supporting evidence (drill into opportunity history, stage changes, rep activity, approval logs, order-to-cash steps), propose likely causes, and route to the right functional owner (RevOps, Deal Desk, Finance) with recommended next actions. Tracks which interventions reduced error rates/forecast variance; proposes new controls (e.g., required fields, validation gates) and A/B tests alert thresholds, subject to human approval and governance.|Transforms quantitative findings into concise leadership-ready narratives (what changed, why it matters, confidence level), tailored to VP Enterprise Sales and exec stakeholders; drafts QBR/MBR slides, emails, and talking points with citations to source metrics. Suggests plausible drivers of trend shifts (pipeline hygiene, seasonality, product mix, approval bottlenecks) and proposes what additional cuts to analyze; generates 'what-if' scenarios and questions to validate with data owners. Helps translate business questions into SQL/BI queries, metric definitions, and dashboard annotations; summarizes dashboard changes and explains metric movements in plain language, reducing analyst time.|VP/RevOps leadership sets definitions (what counts as forecast error, processing error), acceptable risk thresholds, and ensures alignment with finance/legal; resolves metric disputes and prioritizes which insights matter. Humans confirm causality vs correlation, incorporate qualitative field intel, and approve changes that affect compensation, customer commitments, or system controls; ensures accountability and change management. Leadership decides trade-offs (tightening controls vs rep friction), communicates decisions, and drives adoption across Sales, Finance, and Operations; handles sensitive performance implications.|Applies deterministic checks (required fields, stage progression rules, duplicate detection, booking vs. billing reconciliation, timestamp/order constraints) to identify processing errors and hygiene issues with high precision and auditability. Uses time-series decomposition, control charts, and classical ML anomaly models (e.g., isolation forest) on forecast accuracy, pipeline coverage, conversion rates, cycle time, and error rates to flag significant deviations. Runs interpretable models (regression/GBMs with SHAP) to quantify drivers of forecast misses (stage slippage, close-date push, deal size volatility, rep-level bias) and segment results by region/vertical/rep tenure.

column8: Improve processes through standardization and automation by prioritizing high-impact opportunities, partnering with systems teams, and validating that changes strengthen controls and reduce cycle time.
column15: This VP-level task mixes strategy, governance, and cross-functional execution. Traditional AI is strong for structured measurement, prioritization scoring, and deterministic automation/control checks (25%). Generative AI accelerates documentation, playbook drafting, business-case narratives, and test-script creation but must be grounded in internal sources and reviewed (20%). Agentic AI can orchestrate ongoing monitoring and multi-step rollout workflows across tools, reducing coordination overhead, but should operate within guardrails and approval gates (20%). Humans remain essential for setting priorities/risk appetite, negotiating stakeholder trade-offs, approving control/policy changes, and adjudicating high-stakes edge cases (35%). Assumptions: access to CRM/CPQ/CLM data, event logs, and a systems team able to implement workflows; mature governance for model/tool access; clear KPI definitions for cycle time and control effectiveness.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) prioritization decisions that affect revenue strategy, customer experience, and regional nuances; (2) approvals for changes to pricing/discount authority, contracting policies, data retention/access, and any SOX/ISO-relevant controls; (3) validation of automation that could create customer harm (wrong approvals, delayed deals) or compliance breaches; (4) resolving conflicts between stakeholders (Sales vs Legal vs Finance) and managing change adoption; (5) handling non-standard enterprise deal exceptions where context is incomplete or incentives are misaligned. Humans should also review GenAI outputs for factual accuracy and ensure agentic workflows cannot deploy changes without gated approvals and audit logging.
column17: Autonomous orchestration can take unintended actions (e.g., creating tickets, changing configs) or escalate incorrectly; emergent behavior from tool chaining; difficult root-cause analysis if decisions aren't logged. Mitigations: least-privilege tool access, approval gates for production changes, simulation/sandbox runs, rate limits, comprehensive audit trails, and kill switches.
column18: May hallucinate process details, controls, or ROI assumptions; can leak sensitive deal/customer data if prompts/logs are not governed; may produce plausible but non-compliant control narratives. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved internal docs, redaction/DLP, prompt templates, mandatory citations, human review/sign-off, and restricted data scopes.
column19: Rules/ML can be brittle when policies change; biased or incomplete data can mis-prioritize opportunities or flag false exceptions; over-reliance on proxy metrics may optimize the wrong outcomes. Mitigations: periodic rule/model reviews, drift monitoring, explainability, balanced scorecards (cycle time + control quality), and feedback loops from users.
column20: Data quality and identity mismatches across CRM/CPQ/CLM create inconsistent metrics and automation errors; conflicting recommendations between AI components; security/compliance gaps when moving data between systems; change-management failures if automation outpaces training. Mitigations: master data management, unified event taxonomy, API governance, end-to-end testing, clear ownership/RACI, and phased rollouts with adoption metrics.
column22: Continuously monitor process telemetry and trigger improvement workflows. Orchestrate cross-system automation rollout tasks|Elicit and document current-state processes and pain points. Design standardized 'to-be' playbooks and enablement content. Generate hypotheses and business cases for automation candidates. Draft control narratives and test scripts for validation|Set strategic priorities and risk appetite for standardization. Stakeholder alignment and change leadership. Approve control design, policy changes, and high-risk automation. Validate edge cases and customer-impacting exceptions|Quantify and prioritize opportunities using structured analytics. Automate well-defined steps with rules and RPA. Detect anomalies and control exceptions
column23: Connects to CRM/CPQ/CLM/ticketing/event logs to watch cycle-time, rework, SLA breaches, and control exceptions; when thresholds are crossed it opens an improvement 'case', gathers context, proposes next steps, and routes tasks to owners with reminders/escalations. Plans and executes multi-step rollout checklists (sandbox config, UAT coordination, enablement scheduling, phased deployment), adapts to blockers (missing approvals, failed tests), and maintains an auditable trail of actions and decisions.|Runs structured interviews/surveys with sales ops, reps, deal desk, legal, finance; summarizes narratives into process maps, SIPOC, and problem statements; drafts 'as-is' documentation and highlights ambiguity/hand-offs. Drafts standardized SOPs, RACI, templates (MEDDICC fields, approval matrices), training decks, and comms; tailors by segment/region while keeping core controls consistent. Synthesizes benchmarks and internal data to propose automation ideas (e.g., auto-approval for low-risk discounts), drafts ROI models, and enumerates control impacts and required policy changes for review. Produces draft control descriptions, evidence requirements, and UAT/test scripts (positive/negative cases) for SOX/ISO-aligned controls; flags where human sign-off is required.|VP must align improvements to revenue strategy, customer experience, and compliance posture; decides trade-offs (speed vs. control) and resolves conflicts across regions/segments. Requires executive influence to secure buy-in from Sales Ops, IT, Legal, Finance, RevOps; manages incentives, adoption, and organizational resistance. Humans must approve changes affecting pricing authority, contracting, data access, and compliance; accountable for audit readiness and regulatory obligations. Complex enterprise deals, non-standard terms, and strategic accounts require judgment beyond automated rules; ensures exceptions are handled appropriately.|Uses deterministic KPI dashboards and classical ML (e.g., regression/GBM) to estimate drivers of cycle time, predict bottlenecks, and score opportunities by impact, effort, and risk using explainable features. Implements rule engines/workflows (e.g., routing, mandatory fields, approval thresholds) and RPA for repetitive tasks (data entry, status updates) where logic is stable and auditable. Applies rules/anomaly detection to identify out-of-policy discounts, missing approvals, unusual contract terms, or data quality issues; generates exception queues for review.

column8: Support period-close activities and reporting requirements by validating inputs, reconciling key metrics, and ensuring submissions are accurate, timely, and aligned to finance and compliance expectations.
column15: This period-close support task mixes deterministic data processing (best handled by Traditional AI), multi-step workflow coordination across systems and stakeholders (Agentic AI), and narrative/reporting support (Generative AI). However, as a VP Enterprise Sales at executive level, the role retains accountability for certifications, materiality judgments, and cross-functional decisions under finance/compliance constraints, keeping the task in Human-AI Collaboration. Assumptions: core sales systems (CRM/CPQ/billing/BI) have accessible APIs/exports; finance provides definitions (bookings, ARR, churn) and close calendar; SOX/ASC606 controls require human attestation. Constraints: data quality and system-of-record conflicts limit full automation; sensitive submissions require approvals.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) setting/approving materiality thresholds and close assumptions (e.g., treatment of multi-year deals, FX, cutoff timing), (2) final certification/sign-off to Finance and Compliance (SOX control ownership and accountability), (3) adjudicating ambiguous or novel exceptions where policy interpretation is needed (side letters, non-standard terms, channel arrangements), and (4) stakeholder management-negotiating priorities with Finance, RevOps, Deal Desk, Legal, and regional sales leaders when timelines slip. Humans must also review any AI-generated narratives to ensure they match the true business context and do not disclose restricted information.
column17: Risks: over-automation causing premature submissions, incorrect ticket routing/escalations, unintended actions in connected systems (e.g., changing records), and opaque decision logic during exceptions. Mitigations: least-privilege access, action approval steps for high-impact operations, sandbox/testing for close cycles, deterministic guardrails (no-write modes by default), and comprehensive audit trails with rollback procedures.
column18: Risks: hallucinated variance explanations, incorrect policy interpretation, leakage of confidential deal/customer data in prompts/outputs, and inconsistent tone that could mislead executives/auditors. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved finance policy sources, strict grounding to provided numbers, redaction/DLP controls, human review gates for all external/audit-facing text, and versioning/audit logs of prompts and outputs.
column19: Risks: brittle rules that drift as finance definitions change, false positives/negatives in anomaly detection, and reconciliation errors due to schema changes or mismatched master data. Mitigations: governance for rule updates tied to policy changes, monitoring/alerting on data pipeline health, unit tests for reconciliation logic, and periodic backtesting of anomaly models with human calibration.
column20: Risks: inconsistent metric definitions across tools, cascading errors when agentic workflows consume flawed traditional AI outputs, and GenAI narratives amplifying incorrect reconciliations. Security/compliance risks arise from cross-system data movement and logging. Mitigations: single metric dictionary/semantic layer, contract tests between systems, staged pipelines (validate->reconcile->narrate), human approval checkpoints before submission, centralized identity/access management, and end-to-end lineage/auditability.
column22: Orchestrate period-close checklist across sales ops systems. Automated reconciliation workflow execution and exception routing|Draft close commentary and variance narratives for executive/finance reporting. Summarize exceptions and propose likely root causes/remediation steps. Translate finance/compliance requirements into sales-friendly guidance|Define/approve close assumptions, materiality thresholds, and final sign-off. Resolve ambiguous exceptions and negotiate cross-functional decisions. Ensure compliance governance and respond to audit inquiries|Validate structured inputs against rules and master data. Detect anomalies in key close metrics and data feeds. Compute reconciliations and tie-outs across systems
column23: Coordinates a close calendar, pings owners, pulls required artifacts from CRM/CPQ/billing/BI, tracks completion, and escalates blockers based on SLA rules; adapts sequencing when upstream data is late. Runs multi-step reconciliations (e.g., bookings vs. billings vs. revenue recognition feeds), opens tickets with evidence, assigns to correct teams, and re-runs checks after fixes; maintains audit trail of actions.|Generates plain-language explanations of key variances (QoQ, plan vs. actual, pipeline coverage) using provided metrics and context; produces multiple versions for different audiences (CFO, CRO, audit). Synthesizes exception logs, prior close issues, and policy snippets to suggest hypotheses (e.g., deal desk approval missing, FX rate mismatch) and recommended next actions for humans to validate. Creates checklists, FAQs, and reminders aligned to policies (ASC 606, SOX controls) and internal definitions; helps reduce misinterpretation by field teams.|Requires executive judgment on what is material, acceptable explanations, and whether to certify submissions; accountability cannot be delegated to AI. Handles edge cases (contract terms, side letters, unusual revenue treatment), aligns Sales/Finance/Legal on interpretation, and decides trade-offs under time pressure. Humans must attest to control performance, approve evidence packages, and manage auditor communications; AI can assist but not own responsibility.|Applies deterministic validations (required fields, approval flags, product/region mappings, customer master match, cutoff dates) and flags violations; high precision and auditability. Uses statistical/ML anomaly detection on time series (bookings, churn, discounts, pipeline stages) to surface outliers for review; complements rule checks for novel issues. Performs deterministic aggregations and joins to reconcile CRM opportunities, CPQ quotes, invoices, and GL extracts; produces tie-out tables and variance amounts.

column8: Lead operational risk management for Enterprise Sales by monitoring control performance, tracking remediation actions, and preparing executive updates on key risks, dependencies, and mitigation plans.
column15: The task mixes structured monitoring (well-suited to Traditional AI), workflow execution across tools (best handled by Agentic AI), and executive-ready synthesis (strong fit for Generative AI). However, operational risk management in Enterprise Sales is high-stakes and context-dependent: prioritization, risk acceptance, and executive/board communications require accountable human judgment and governance. Assumptions: access to clean system-of-record data (CRM/CPQ/GRC), defined control library and thresholds, and permissioned integrations. Constraints: data quality, regional policy variance, and changing sales motions limit full automation.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving risk ratings and mitigation plans when revenue impact or compliance exposure is material; (2) interpreting edge cases (strategic deals, one-off contract terms, M&A customers, public-sector constraints) where rules/models may misfire; (3) ensuring communications are accurate, non-misleading, and aligned with SOX/audit expectations; (4) resolving conflicts between functions and enforcing accountability; (5) authorizing escalations, exceptions, and control changes. Humans should also periodically review model/rule performance and update thresholds as sales strategy changes.
column17: Autonomous actions (ticket updates, escalations, notifications) can create noise, mis-route issues, or trigger inappropriate escalations; integration failures can cause silent gaps; emergent behavior if goals are mis-specified; access/privilege risks. Mitigations: least-privilege, sandboxing, explicit action policies, rate limits, human-in-the-loop for escalations above threshold, monitoring/kill switch, and audit trails.
column18: May hallucinate metrics, misstate control status, or over-summarize nuance; can leak sensitive deal/customer data if prompts/logs are mishandled; tone may be inappropriate for audit/board contexts; bias in interpreting qualitative inputs. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, citation requirements, redaction, prompt/response logging, human approval gates, and restricted data scopes.
column19: Rules can become brittle as sales processes change; ML anomaly detection can generate false positives/negatives, especially with seasonality or new product launches; may encode unfairness across regions/segments if thresholds differ. Mitigations: periodic rule reviews, drift detection, segmented baselines, explainability, and governance over threshold changes.
column20: Inconsistent definitions of controls/KRIs across systems, data latency, and identity mismatches can produce conflicting dashboards; GenAI summaries may not match underlying metrics; orchestration loops can amplify errors (bad alert -> agent escalates -> exec report). Mitigations: single source of truth for metrics, data contracts, reconciliation checks, end-to-end testing, versioned control taxonomy, and staged rollout with parallel run.
column22: Orchestrate continuous risk-monitoring workflow across systems. Track remediation actions end-to-end and chase updates|Draft executive risk updates and board-ready narratives. Synthesize qualitative signals into risk themes|Set risk appetite, prioritize top risks, and make trade-off decisions. Validate risk interpretations and approve executive communications. Lead cross-functional alignment and accountability|Control performance analytics and KPI computation. Anomaly detection and rule-based alerting on sales operations risks. Dependency mapping and critical path tracking
column23: Runs scheduled/trigger-based playbooks to pull control evidence from CRM (e.g., Salesforce), CPQ, billing, deal desk, enablement, and GRC tools; checks data freshness; routes exceptions to owners; maintains an auditable activity log. Creates/updates remediation tickets, pings control owners, escalates overdue actions per policy, and updates status dashboards; adapts follow-up cadence based on risk criticality and executive reporting deadlines.|Summarizes current top risks, control performance trends, remediation progress, and dependencies into concise exec-ready language; tailors messaging for CRO/COO/Audit/Risk committees; produces Q&A prep and talking points. Ingests unstructured inputs (call notes, win/loss, Slack/email excerpts, audit findings) to cluster recurring issues (e.g., discounting exceptions, contract deviations) and propose candidate risk statements and mitigations for human validation.|Requires executive judgment to balance revenue goals vs. control rigor, interpret ambiguous signals, and decide escalation/acceptance/mitigation strategies aligned to enterprise risk appetite. Humans must verify factual accuracy, ensure regulatory/compliance alignment (SOX, privacy, antitrust, contracting), and calibrate tone/political sensitivity before sharing with executives/board. Securing commitments from Finance, Legal, RevOps, Security, and regional sales leaders depends on relationship management, negotiation, and authority that AI cannot reliably execute autonomously.|Deterministically calculates KRIs/KPIs (e.g., % deals with approvals, SoD violations, quote-to-cash cycle anomalies), thresholds, and trend lines from structured data; supports explainable drill-downs. Uses rules/ML to flag outliers such as unusual discount levels, atypical payment terms, rapid stage changes, missing approvals, or repeated manual overrides; generates alerts with feature-based explanations. Builds structured dependency graphs between remediation items, system changes, policy updates, and enablement rollouts; computes critical path/at-risk milestones using project data.
