column8: Research and identify enterprise target accounts and key stakeholders using CRM data, intent signals, and public sources to build prioritized outreach lists.
column15: This task mixes structured decisioning (ICP filtering, scoring, dedupe) with unstructured research (public sources, role inference) and workflow execution (multi-source enrichment and refresh). Traditional AI is best for explainable, repeatable segmentation/scoring on CRM + intent features. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step enrichment and keeping lists continuously updated, but should be constrained by policies and approvals. Generative AI accelerates synthesis of unstructured information into briefs and stakeholder hypotheses, but outputs must be treated as suggestions due to hallucination/inference risk. Human input remains essential to set strategy/guardrails, validate top accounts/stakeholders, and ensure compliance-especially in enterprise contexts where errors can waste cycles or create reputational/legal risk. Assumptions: access to CRM APIs, intent provider feeds, and approved enrichment sources; clear territory/account ownership rules; no autonomous outreach without human approval.
column16: Humans must (1) set/adjust ICP and prioritization weights to reflect current GTM strategy and quota realities; (2) approve inclusion of top-tier accounts and any inferred stakeholder mapping before outreach; (3) adjudicate ambiguous entity matches (parent/subsidiary, rebrands, M&A) and ownership conflicts; (4) ensure privacy/compliance (GDPR/CCPA, vendor ToS, internal retention rules) and decide what data can be stored in CRM; (5) review edge cases where intent spikes may be misleading (shared IPs, subsidiaries) or where public info is outdated. Human judgment is irreplaceable for high-stakes prioritization, ethical boundaries, and accountability.
column17: Autonomous workflows can propagate bad data at scale (wrong merges, incorrect enrichment), exceed rate limits, or violate data-use policies if not constrained; may create noisy task queues that reduce SDR productivity; mitigation: guardrails (allowed sources/actions), approval gates for writes to CRM, monitoring/audit logs, rollback mechanisms, and conservative automation (suggest vs act) for high-impact steps.
column18: May hallucinate stakeholders, initiatives, or relationships; can overstate confidence when inferring buying committees; risk of incorporating outdated/incorrect public info; privacy risk if prompts include sensitive CRM notes; mitigation: retrieval with citations, confidence labeling, strict prompt/data controls, human review for Tier-1 accounts, and red-teaming for leakage.
column19: Rules/models can encode bias (e.g., excluding certain regions/industries unfairly), become stale as ICP changes, and perform poorly on sparse/noisy CRM data; entity resolution errors can misattribute activity; mitigation: periodic recalibration, explainability checks, data quality SLAs, bias/fairness reviews, and human sampling audits.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI-inferred stakeholders vs CRM truth), feedback loops (agentic refresh amplifying noisy intent), and provenance loss when merging sources; security risks from multiple vendor integrations and API keys; mitigation: unified data schema with provenance, confidence scores, reconciliation logic, least-privilege access, vendor risk reviews, and end-to-end testing with canary releases.
column22: Orchestrate multi-source data collection and enrichment workflow. Autonomously generate and refresh prioritized account queues on a schedule|Summarize account research into SDR-ready briefs. Extract stakeholder roles and map likely buying committee from unstructured text. Normalize and clean messy text fields|Define ICP, prioritization strategy, and guardrails. Validate high-value accounts and stakeholder accuracy before outreach. Compliance and ethical review of data usage. Handle edge cases and exceptions|Filter and segment candidate accounts using deterministic rules. Score and rank accounts using structured ML / heuristics. Entity resolution and deduplication across CRM and enrichment sources
column23: Plans and executes a multi-step workflow: pulls candidate accounts from CRM, queries intent providers, triggers web/company enrichment (e.g., firmographics, tech stack), deduplicates, and logs provenance; adapts when sources fail (rate limits, missing fields) and retries or switches sources within policy. Runs periodic jobs (daily/weekly) to refresh scores, detect new intent spikes, and update SDR queues; creates tasks in CRM and notifies the rep when thresholds are met, while respecting guardrails (no auto-contact without approval).|Condenses public info (10-K/annual report snippets, press releases, LinkedIn pages) and CRM notes into a short brief: business context, initiatives, likely pain points, and suggested stakeholder functions; cites sources when available. Uses NLP to infer relevant personas (CIO, VP Security, Procurement) from job postings, org pages, and news; proposes hypotheses about reporting lines and influence, flagged as inferred. Standardizes company names, titles, and notes; expands acronyms; helps reconcile variations (e.g., 'Intl.' vs 'International') to improve matching and downstream scoring.|Requires business judgment on what 'good' looks like (strategic verticals, disqualifiers, current GTM focus), and sets thresholds/weights; ensures alignment with leadership and territory plans. Humans verify ambiguous matches, confirm stakeholder relevance/seniority, and sanity-check inferred org maps-especially for top-tier accounts where mistakes are costly. Ensures use of intent/public data complies with privacy laws, vendor terms, and internal policies; decides what can be stored in CRM and what must be excluded. Resolves conflicts (account ownership disputes, subsidiaries vs parent mapping, M&A changes, sensitive industries) and makes final calls when data is contradictory.|Applies ICP rules (industry, employee count, revenue, region, exclusions), territory ownership, and compliance constraints; produces an initial eligible account set with explainable criteria. Combines structured features (fit score, engagement, intent intensity, technographics, past opportunity outcomes) into a ranking model (logistic regression/GBM) or weighted scoring; outputs calibrated scores and feature contributions. Uses fuzzy matching, domain matching, and clustering to merge duplicate accounts/contacts; maintains golden records and confidence scores.
column41: Enterprise Account Planning and Sales Execution

column8: Execute high volume outbound prospecting through email, phone, and social channels, tailoring messaging to enterprise personas and current business priorities.
column15: High-volume outbound prospecting can be substantially accelerated by AI, but cannot be fully automated safely for enterprise contexts. Traditional AI is best for structured segmentation, compliance gates, and measurement. Generative AI is strongest at producing channel-specific, persona-tailored copy and suggested replies, but needs grounded inputs and guardrails to avoid hallucinated claims. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating cadences, monitoring engagement, and triggering next actions across tools; however, autonomous sending and escalation must be constrained by policies and approvals. Humans remain essential for defining positioning/ICP, approving sensitive messaging, and handling nuanced objections and relationship-building. Assumptions: access to CRM and engagement platforms, approved messaging library, and legal permission for data sources; uncertainty remains around data availability/quality and jurisdiction-specific outreach rules.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving messaging that includes product claims, security/compliance statements, pricing, or competitive comparisons; (2) deciding outreach to regulated industries or regions with strict consent rules; (3) handling negative replies, reputational risk, and escalation to AEs; (4) validating personalization facts when signals are weak or conflicting; (5) setting guardrails (frequency, tone, brand) and periodically auditing AI outputs for bias and compliance. Edge cases: prospects requesting data deletion, minors/incorrect contacts, sensitive events (layoffs, breaches), and accounts with contractual restrictions-these require immediate human handling and sequence stoppage.
column17: Over-automation could spam prospects, violate quiet hours, or ignore subtle negative signals; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals (optimize replies at expense of compliance); limited explainability when it changes cadences. Mitigations: hard policy constraints (DNC, rate limits), approval workflows for first-touch/high-value accounts, simulation/testing in sandbox, kill switch, and audit trails for every action.
column18: May hallucinate account facts or overstate capabilities; tone/brand drift across variants; inadvertent inclusion of sensitive personal data in personalization; bias in persona assumptions. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation, approved-claims library, red-team prompts, PII filters, mandatory human review for high-tier accounts, and logging/versioning of prompts/templates.
column19: Brittle rules can mis-segment or suppress incorrectly; ML scoring can encode historical bias (e.g., favoring certain industries/regions); stale data reduces accuracy. Mitigations: periodic rule review, model monitoring, fairness checks, data freshness SLAs, and human override mechanisms.
column20: Toolchain mismatches (CRM vs engagement platform fields), inconsistent identity resolution causing wrong-person outreach, feedback loops where GenAI content affects metrics used by Traditional AI, and security/privacy risks when sharing data across vendors. Mitigations: unified data schema, strong identity matching, least-privilege access, vendor risk reviews, end-to-end testing, and governance for prompt/context data.
column22: Orchestrate multi-channel outreach sequences (cadences) across email/phone/social. Autonomously monitor engagement signals and trigger follow-ups/escalations. Prospect research workflow automation (data gathering + summarization pipeline)|Draft persona- and account-tailored messaging variants (email, LinkedIn, call talk-tracks). Personalization at scale using structured inputs. Reply handling assistance (suggest responses, objection handling, meeting ask). Call preparation and post-call notes drafting|Define ICP, messaging strategy, and approved value claims for enterprise personas. Review/approve high-stakes outreach and handle complex/strategic conversations. Exception handling and data quality correction|Lead list building, enrichment, and segmentation. Deliverability, compliance, and suppression checks. Performance analytics and experimentation measurement
column23: Plans and executes multi-step cadences in sales engagement tools (e.g., sequence enrollment, task creation, channel switching) based on prospect state (opened, replied, bounced) and time windows; adapts next-best action while respecting guardrails (rate limits, do-not-contact, quiet hours). Continuously watches events (opens, clicks, replies, meeting booked, voicemail left) and updates sequence steps, creates SDR tasks, or routes hot leads to AEs per predefined policies; can pause/stop outreach when risk/compliance flags appear. Coordinates calls to data sources (CRM, intent providers, news, LinkedIn snapshots where permitted) to compile a brief on account priorities and persona context; hands off structured context to GenAI for messaging.|Generates outreach copy aligned to enterprise personas (CIO, CISO, VP Ops, Procurement) using provided account context, value props, and approved claims; produces multiple A/B variants and channel-appropriate length/tone. Uses templates plus retrieved snippets (recent initiatives, tech stack, hiring signals) to create individualized openers and relevance hooks while avoiding fabrication; can localize language and adjust formality. Summarizes inbound replies, classifies intent (not now, wrong person, interested, unsubscribe), and drafts compliant responses and meeting scheduling language for human review. Creates call agendas, discovery questions, and concise notes/follow-up emails from human-entered bullet points or call transcripts (where legally recorded), improving throughput.|Requires business judgment and product/market nuance; humans ensure claims are accurate, differentiated, and aligned with positioning and legal/compliance constraints. Enterprise prospects and sensitive topics (security, pricing, competitive displacement) need human judgment, relationship-building, and risk management; humans decide when to escalate to AE/SE. Humans resolve ambiguous matches (wrong contact), conflicting account signals, and CRM hygiene issues that automation cannot reliably disambiguate.|Applies deterministic rules/ML scoring on structured fields (industry, employee count, region, tech stack, intent score) to prioritize accounts and route to the right sequence/persona play. Runs rule-based validation for email syntax, bounce risk heuristics, domain reputation signals, opt-out/do-not-call lists, GDPR/CCPA flags, and contact frequency caps before any send. Computes open/reply rates, step conversion, cohort comparisons, and statistical significance for A/B tests; surfaces best-performing templates and segments without generating content.

column8: Qualify inbound and outbound leads by confirming fit, need, timeline, and buying process, then route qualified opportunities to the assigned Account Executive.
column15: Lead qualification blends structured steps (scoring, routing, data hygiene) with unstructured communication and judgment. Traditional AI is strong for deterministic routing, dedupe, and explainable scoring. Generative AI accelerates drafting outreach, summarizing interactions, and extracting qualification signals but can be unreliable without verification. Agentic AI can orchestrate the end-to-end workflow and execute follow-ups, yet requires tight guardrails to prevent inappropriate outreach or premature handoffs. Humans remain essential for nuanced discovery, handling ambiguity, and approving borderline/high-value decisions. Assumptions: SDR has access to CRM/marketing tools, call recordings (where legal), and defined ICP/routing rules; organization permits AI-assisted drafting and summarization with review policies.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final qualification on ambiguous/borderline leads, (2) any outreach in regulated contexts or where consent is unclear, (3) handling objections/escalations that could create reputational or legal risk, and (4) ensuring AE handoff quality for strategic accounts. Humans should approve changes to ICP, scoring thresholds, and routing rules; review AI-generated summaries before committing critical CRM fields; and intervene when AI confidence is low, data is missing, or signals conflict (e.g., multiple stakeholders, complex procurement).
column17: Over-automation leading to spammy outreach, contacting wrong person, or violating opt-out; unintended workflow loops; opaque decisioning; acting on incorrect data. Mitigations: policy engine/guardrails, rate limits, consent checks, approval gates for sending, audit logs, sandbox testing, and kill switch.
column18: Hallucinated details in summaries or emails; tone/brand mismatch; leakage of sensitive data into prompts; biased language or inappropriate personalization. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from CRM fields, strict templates, redaction, human review for key fields, and confidence/attribution for extracted signals.
column19: Brittle rules causing misrouting; model drift in lead scoring; unfairness if historical data encodes bias; poor handling of novel segments. Mitigations: periodic rule/model review, monitoring conversion by segment, explainability, and fallback queues for low-confidence cases.
column20: Conflicting signals between scoring, GenAI extraction, and agent actions; data sync latency causing duplicate outreach; security/permission misconfigurations across CRM, enrichment, and comms tools. Mitigations: single source of truth in CRM, idempotent workflow design, event-driven updates, role-based access control, end-to-end testing, and unified audit trails.
column22: Orchestrate multi-step lead qualification workflow across systems. Autonomous follow-up sequencing with guardrails|Draft and personalize outreach and qualification questions. Summarize conversations and extract qualification signals (fit/need/timeline/process). Generate objection-handling and next-step recommendations|Handle nuanced discovery, trust-building, and high-stakes qualification calls. Approve qualification outcome and handoff for borderline or high-value leads. Ensure compliance and ethical handling of personal data and consent|Lead scoring and prioritization using rules/ML. Data validation, deduplication, and enrichment matching. Routing logic to assigned Account Executive
column23: Coordinates CRM, marketing automation, enrichment, calendar, and routing steps: pulls lead record, triggers enrichment, selects next-best action (email/call/meeting), updates statuses, and hands off to AE when thresholds are met; adapts sequencing based on responses and SLA timers. Executes approved outreach sequences (email/SMS/LinkedIn where permitted), pauses/escalates on negative signals (unsubscribe, legal keywords, competitor mentions), and schedules meetings when qualification criteria are satisfied; requires policy constraints and human override.|Generates tailored email/call scripts and discovery questions using firmographics, intent signals, and prior interactions; proposes variants for different personas while adhering to brand/compliance templates. Transcribes/summarizes calls or email threads, highlights stated pain points, urgency, stakeholders, and next steps; produces a structured summary for CRM fields and AE handoff notes. Suggests responses to common objections and proposes next questions to clarify gaps (e.g., budget authority, procurement steps), while flagging uncertainty when evidence is weak.|Humans manage rapport, interpret ambiguous needs, and adjust questioning in real time-especially for strategic accounts, complex buying committees, or sensitive industries. Human judgment is needed when signals conflict (e.g., strong fit but unclear timeline), to avoid misrouting or wasting AE time; also ensures compliance with outreach policies. Humans oversee adherence to GDPR/CCPA, opt-in/opt-out, and acceptable use of enrichment/intent data; resolve escalations and customer complaints.|Applies deterministic rules (ICP match, geography, company size) and/or supervised models using historical conversion data to rank leads and set SLA-based queues. Uses fuzzy matching and validation rules to merge duplicates, normalize titles/industries, and map enrichment provider fields into CRM schema; flags low-confidence matches. Deterministic territory/segment rules (region, vertical, account ownership) route qualified leads to the correct AE and create tasks/notifications; provides explainable routing outcomes.

column8: Schedule discovery meetings and product introductions for Account Executives, ensuring agendas, attendee roles, and required context are confirmed in advance.
column15: This task mixes structured coordination (calendar/CRM operations) with nuanced communication and deal judgment. Traditional AI is strong for deterministic scheduling constraints, routing rules, and validation. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step workflows across tools (propose times, book, update CRM, follow up, reschedule) and enforcing readiness checklists with minimal supervision. Generative AI improves quality and speed of agendas, invites, and AE pre-reads, and can extract roles from unstructured threads, but must be grounded to avoid inaccuracies. Humans remain essential for enterprise nuance: deciding stakeholder strategy, handling sensitive exceptions, and approving agendas for high-stakes accounts. Assumptions: organization has integrated CRM/calendar/email APIs, clear scheduling policies, and permissioned access to prospect data; meeting recording/consent policies vary by region and must be enforced.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) deciding whether a meeting should be scheduled given qualification ambiguity, competitive context, or low intent; (2) selecting/confirming the right stakeholders and tailoring positioning for executive audiences; (3) approving agendas and claims to avoid overpromising and to align with AE deal strategy; (4) handling exceptions: executive assistants, procurement/security reviews, NDA/legal constraints, accessibility needs, and cross-border privacy/consent requirements; (5) resolving conflicts when AI outputs disagree (e.g., inferred roles vs. CRM) and when data is incomplete or stale. Humans should also periodically audit automation logs for compliance and relationship-impacting errors.
column17: Over-automation could spam prospects with follow-ups, book wrong attendees, or violate working-hours/consent policies; emergent behavior from poorly constrained agents; opaque decision trails. Mitigations: hard policy constraints, rate limits, approval gates for strategic accounts, simulation/testing in sandbox, and comprehensive audit logs with rollback.
column18: Hallucinated or outdated account context in pre-reads; tone mismatch or culturally inappropriate phrasing; leakage of sensitive data into prompts/logs; incorrect inference of attendee roles leading to misaligned agendas. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations, strict data minimization, redaction, approved templates, and human review for Tier-1 accounts.
column19: Brittle rules may fail for complex availability patterns (travel, holidays, regional calendars) or misroute ownership when org structures change; false positives in validation can block scheduling. Mitigations: continuous rule maintenance, exception handling paths, monitoring of SLA breaches, and periodic calibration with ops teams.
column20: Data inconsistency between CRM and calendar (duplicate contacts, stale titles), permission/scopes misconfiguration, and race conditions causing double-booking; security risks from broad API tokens; unclear source of truth for attendee roles. Mitigations: master-data governance, idempotent booking workflows, least-privilege access, reconciliation jobs, and clear escalation/ownership for failures.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end scheduling workflow across systems (CRM, email, calendar, conferencing). Automated follow-up and rescheduling management. Pre-meeting readiness checklist enforcement|Draft tailored meeting invite copy and agenda options. Summarize account/prospect context for AE briefing. Extract attendee roles and intent from unstructured communications|Confirm strategic fit and stakeholder strategy for high-value/ambiguous accounts. Handle sensitive communications and exceptions. column8: Maintain accurate CRM records for activities, contacts, and opportunity notes, and ensure data hygiene to support forecasting and pipeline reporting.

Agentic AI can reliably run the operational cadence-tracking actions, nudging owners, and maintaining project hygiene-when bounded by playbooks and approval gates. Traditional AI is best for deterministic tracking, routing, and alerting on structured metrics. Assumptions: core systems (CRM, project management, support, finance approvals) are integrated; governance exists for what the AI can send/commit; and stakeholders accept AI-assisted artifacts. Constraints: sensitive commercial/legal decisions remain human-led; data quality and access controls limit automation depth.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final account strategy choices and prioritization when functions disagree; (2) negotiation and conflict resolution across teams with misaligned incentives; (3) approvals for pricing/contractual commitments, roadmap promises, and customer-facing statements; (4) compliance/privacy decisions about sharing customer data internally; (5) edge cases like escalations during outages, executive escalations, or reputational risk where tone and timing are critical. column17: Autonomous follow-ups/escalations can create noise, annoy stakeholders, or escalate prematurely; may take actions based on incomplete context (e.g., scheduling over conflicts, opening incorrect tickets); emergent behavior if goals are mis-specified; auditability challenges. Forecast commitments affect revenue guidance; human accountability is required to reconcile model outputs with ground truth and known deal dynamics.|Applies statistical/ML models on structured CRM data (stage, age, activity counts, product mix) to estimate close probability, expected value, and forecast category; provides explainable drivers. Deterministic rules check pricing/discounts, required fields, and approval matrices; routes to correct approvers and blocks non-compliant submissions. Flags missing/invalid fields, inconsistent dates, duplicate opportunities, and outlier forecasts (e.g., sudden jump in amount) for correction.

column8: Maintain and administer deal desk systems and tools, including CRM and CPQ configurations, user access, and data quality checks to support enterprise quoting workflows.
column15: The task mixes deterministic controls (access, validations, regression tests), operational execution (routine admin runbooks, monitoring), and high-stakes governance (pricing/approval logic, production changes). Traditional AI is best for repeatable rule enforcement and data-quality/anomaly pipelines with clear thresholds and auditability. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step admin workflows across CRM/CPQ/IdP and monitoring/auto-remediation, but must be constrained by change-management guardrails. Generative AI accelerates documentation, troubleshooting narratives, and test-case ideation, yet cannot be trusted to directly implement configs without verification due to hallucination risk. Human effort remains substantial because enterprise quoting controls are compliance-sensitive (SOX), requirements are often ambiguous, and accountability for production changes and exceptions must stay with people. Assumptions: APIs/admin tooling exist for CRM/CPQ (e.g., Salesforce + CPQ), environments support sandbox testing and versioned deployments, and an ITSM system governs approvals; without these, agentic automation share drops and human share rises.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving production-impacting CPQ/CRM configuration changes (pricing rules, approval matrices, product/bundle logic) and any emergency changes; (2) granting/altering privileged access and approving SoD exceptions with documented rationale; (3) interpreting policy/contract intent when requirements conflict or are underspecified; (4) validating that automated anomaly flags reflect legitimate business context (strategic discounts, one-off terms) to avoid blocking revenue; (5) incident command for novel outages, cross-functional coordination (Sales Ops/RevOps/Finance/Legal/IT), and vendor escalation. Edge cases requiring humans: new product launches with incomplete master data, mergers/territory realignments affecting access, multi-entity/multi-currency deals, and regulatory/audit requests where evidence must be curated and attested.
column17: Risks: unintended changes in production (over-automation), cascading failures across integrated systems, acting on incomplete context, and opaque decision traces. Mitigations: least-privilege service accounts, sandbox-first execution, policy-as-code guardrails, mandatory approvals for high-risk actions, rate limits, rollback plans, and immutable audit logs of every action and API call.
column18: Risks: hallucinated configuration guidance, incorrect interpretation of pricing/approval policy, leakage of sensitive deal/customer data in prompts, and non-deterministic outputs that are hard to audit. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation over approved knowledge base, strict redaction/DLP, template-constrained outputs, human review gates, and logging of sources/citations.
column19: Risks: brittle rules that drift from business reality, false positives/negatives in anomaly detection, and maintenance burden as products/pricing evolve. Mitigations: periodic rule/model review with stakeholders, monitoring precision/recall, configurable thresholds, version control for rules, and clear exception-handling workflows.
column20: Risks: inconsistent source-of-truth between CRM and CPQ, feedback loops where agentic remediation fights scheduled jobs, identity/access mismatches, and audit gaps when multiple systems act. Mitigations: unified change-management pipeline, event-driven architecture with idempotency, centralized logging/SIEM, end-to-end reconciliation checks, and clear RACI for ownership of configs, data, and automation.
column22: Orchestrate routine admin workflows across CRM/CPQ/IdP (ticket intake -> validation -> execution -> logging). Continuous monitoring and auto-remediation for quoting workflow health. Guided configuration changes with policy constraints|Draft and maintain admin documentation, runbooks, and release notes. Natural-language support for analysts/admins (querying configs, explaining errors). Generate test cases and data validation rules from policy text|Approve and govern high-impact configuration and access changes. Resolve ambiguous requirements and prioritize trade-offs with stakeholders. Handle novel/edge-case incidents and vendor/escalation management|Rule-based access provisioning/deprovisioning and segregation-of-duties checks. Automated data quality checks and anomaly detection on CRM/CPQ data. Configuration regression testing and validation in sandbox
column23: An agent monitors queues (e.g., ServiceNow/Jira), gathers required context (requester role, impacted objects, change window), runs pre-checks, executes approved runbooks via APIs (create fields, update price rules, assign permission sets), and posts completion notes with audit artifacts; pauses for human approval on high-risk changes. Agent watches signals (failed quote sync jobs, CPQ calculation errors, integration latency, permission errors), correlates incidents to recent changes, rolls back via versioned configs where safe, opens incidents, and routes to owners with suggested fixes. Given a change request (e.g., new discount approval tier), the agent proposes a plan, simulates impact in sandbox, runs regression checks, and prepares a deployment package; executes only after human sign-off and within guardrails (no direct production edits without change record).|GenAI converts tickets, config diffs, and meeting notes into standardized SOPs, step-by-step runbooks, and user comms; can generate role-based quick guides for sales/ops while referencing approved templates. GenAI answers 'why did this quote fail?' by summarizing logs, known issues, and relevant configuration objects; generates troubleshooting checklists and suggested SOQL/SQL queries, reducing time-to-diagnosis. From pricing/approval policies and quoting requirements, GenAI proposes edge-case test scenarios (multi-currency, renewals, bundles) and drafts validation rule logic for review by admins.|Humans must assess business intent, risk, and compliance (SOX, auditability), approve change records, and decide on exceptions (e.g., urgent access for strategic deal) where context and accountability are required. Enterprise quoting often involves conflicting needs (sales speed vs. controls); humans negotiate requirements, define acceptance criteria, and align with finance/legal/revops. When failures are unprecedented (new product model, integration outage, contractual pricing), humans perform root-cause analysis, coordinate cross-functional response, and engage vendors with accountability.|Deterministic rules map job roles to permission sets/CPQ roles; SoD engine flags conflicting entitlements (e.g., ability to edit price books and approve discounts) and enforces approval workflows. Scheduled checks validate required fields, referential integrity, duplicate detection, and outlier detection (e.g., unusually high discounts, negative margins) using statistical thresholds/ML; outputs exception queues. Automated test suites run deterministic CPQ calculations, approval routing, and integration sync tests; compares outputs to baselines to catch breaking changes before deployment.

column8: Prepare and distribute recurring and ad hoc reports for sales leadership on pipeline health, deal cycle time, discounting trends, and approval turnaround performance.
column15: The task is largely a repeatable analytics-and-distribution workflow but requires governed metric definitions and executive judgment. Traditional AI (rules/SQL/BI) is best for reliable KPI computation and standardized dashboards (largest share). Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step workflows, monitoring freshness, and routing exceptions-reducing manual coordination. Generative AI is strongest for translating metrics into leadership narratives and turning ad hoc questions into structured specs, but must be grounded with retrieval/citations to avoid hallucinations. Humans remain essential for KPI governance, interpreting business events, and approving sensitive distributions; hence collaboration rather than full automation. Assumptions: data sources (CRM/CPQ/approval logs) are accessible via APIs/warehouse; BI tooling supports scheduled refresh and RLS; organization permits AI-assisted drafting under security controls.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) KPI definition changes and governance (preventing metric drift and ensuring auditability), (2) approval of sensitive/people-level or compliance-relevant insights before distribution, (3) resolving data disputes and edge cases (e.g., split deals, backdated stage changes, manual approvals outside system), and (4) prioritizing/negotiating ad hoc requests and interpreting causality (AI can suggest drivers but cannot confirm business intent without validation). Humans should also sign off when anomaly checks fail or when agentic workflows propose escalations that could impact reps/regions.
column17: Autonomous orchestration could distribute incorrect or incomplete reports if validation gates are weak; may spam stakeholders or create excessive tickets; opaque decisioning on when to escalate. Mitigations: hard approval checkpoints, allowlists for recipients, rate limits, audit logs, simulation/testing in sandbox, and clear rollback/kill switch.
column18: May hallucinate drivers or misstate numbers if not strictly grounded; can leak sensitive deal/rep data in prompts/outputs; may produce overconfident narratives that imply causality. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with metric citations, constrained templates, redaction/DLP, and mandatory human review for exec-facing commentary and any people-level insights.
column19: Brittle rules and ETL can break with CRM/CPQ schema changes; anomaly detection may miss novel issues or flag false positives; biased baselines if historical data is skewed (e.g., seasonality, territory changes). Mitigations: schema monitoring, unit tests for KPI logic, periodic recalibration, and data lineage documentation.
column20: Mismatch between KPI calculations (Traditional AI) and GenAI narratives; inconsistent definitions across tools; security gaps when moving data between warehouse, LLM, and distribution channels; orchestration failures causing partial refreshes. Mitigations: single source of truth semantic layer, versioned KPI definitions, end-to-end lineage, role-based access controls, encrypted connectors, and automated reconciliation checks before distribution.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end reporting workflow (schedule, run, validate gates, distribute). Monitor data freshness and pipeline anomalies; open follow-up tickets|Draft executive narrative and insights for reports. Generate ad hoc report specifications from stakeholder requests. Create slide/email packaging and Q&A responses|Define KPI definitions, governance, and interpret business context. Approve distribution for sensitive metrics and handle escalations. Stakeholder management and prioritization of ad hoc requests|Extract, transform, and aggregate structured sales/approval data. Data quality checks and anomaly detection on KPIs. Dashboarding and standardized report generation
column23: Plans and executes the multi-step workflow on a cadence (weekly/monthly) and on-demand: triggers data refreshes, runs queries/BI extracts, checks completion, routes exceptions to humans, and distributes outputs to the right leadership groups based on role/region. Continuously monitors source systems/ETL status and KPI thresholds; when anomalies occur (e.g., sudden cycle-time spike), it gathers context, proposes likely causes, and creates Jira/ServiceNow tickets or pings owners for remediation.|Summarizes KPI movements, highlights drivers (e.g., discounting by segment), and produces leadership-ready commentary, including 'what changed' and 'so what' sections, using retrieved metrics and prior-period context. Converts natural-language asks (e.g., 'cycle time by product in EMEA last quarter') into structured requirements: dimensions, filters, time windows, definitions, and acceptance criteria; flags ambiguities for clarification. Produces concise email summaries, slide bullets, and anticipated Q&A with citations to underlying metrics; adapts tone to sales leadership and ensures consistent terminology via templates.|Ensures metrics align with finance/legal policy (e.g., what counts as 'cycle start'), resolves definition disputes, and adds contextual interpretation (product launches, comp plan changes) that AI cannot reliably infer. Reviews outputs for executive sensitivity (e.g., rep-level discounting), approves exceptions, and decides escalation actions when reports reveal compliance or performance issues. Negotiates scope/timelines, balances competing leadership asks, and confirms that outputs answer the decision need; handles edge cases like one-off deal investigations.|Uses deterministic SQL/ETL, rules, and classical analytics to compute pipeline coverage, stage aging, cycle time distributions, discount bands, and approval SLA metrics from CRM/CPQ/approval systems. Applies rule-based validations (nulls, duplicates, out-of-range discounts) and statistical anomaly detection (z-score/seasonality baselines) to flag suspect data before publishing. Generates recurring dashboards and scheduled exports via BI tools (Power BI/Tableau) with parameterized filters and row-level security; produces consistent KPI definitions and visuals.

column8: Review enterprise deal submissions for completeness and accuracy, validating pricing inputs, product configurations, and required documentation before routing for approvals.
column15: This deal-desk task mixes deterministic validation (best handled by Traditional AI via CPQ/CRM rules), unstructured document understanding and communication (best handled by Generative AI), and multi-step workflow coordination (best handled by Agentic AI). However, enterprise deals carry high financial/compliance risk and frequent edge cases (non-standard terms, strategic pricing, novel bundles), so humans must adjudicate exceptions and perform final QA on high-impact submissions. Assumptions: CPQ/CRM data is accessible via APIs; pricing guardrails and approval matrices are codified; document corpus is available with appropriate permissions; organization requires human accountability for exceptions and final routing on large deals.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving/denying pricing or term exceptions, especially where policy allows discretion; (2) resolving conflicting sources of truth (CPQ vs order form vs redlines) and interpreting commercial intent; (3) compliance and risk decisions (export controls, data protection addenda, unusual payment terms) where errors have legal/financial consequences; (4) final sign-off for high-value/strategic accounts and any deal flagged as anomalous by automated checks; and (5) governance-updating rules, approval matrices, and training data when products/policies change. Edge cases: bespoke bundles, channel/partner constructs, multi-entity billing, non-standard revenue recognition triggers, and incomplete/low-quality scans.
column17: Autonomous routing/follow-ups could spam stakeholders, route to wrong approvers, or proceed based on incorrect state, creating compliance breaches. Mitigations: guardrails (policy-as-code), approval gates, rate limits, audit logs, simulation/testing in sandbox, and fallback to human when confidence is low or rules conflict.
column18: May hallucinate terms when summarizing documents, miss subtle legal language, or leak sensitive customer data if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with citations to source passages, strict PII controls, redaction, human review for high-risk summaries, and constrained templates.
column19: Rules can become outdated with new SKUs/policies, causing false positives/negatives; brittle handling of complex pricing constructs; potential bias if anomaly thresholds are poorly set. Mitigations: versioned rule management, monitoring/metrics, periodic audits, exception feedback loops, and clear ownership for rule updates.
column20: Inconsistent data between CRM/CPQ/document repositories can cause conflicting outputs; orchestration failures can create duplicate tasks or missed approvals; unclear accountability when AI outputs disagree. Mitigations: define system of record, implement reconciliation logic, end-to-end observability, human-in-the-loop escalation paths, and unified audit trail tying every flag to evidence and rule/version.
column22: Orchestrate intake workflow and route deals to the right queue/approvers. Automated follow-ups and evidence collection from stakeholders|Extract and summarize unstructured deal documentation for reviewer context. Draft clarification questions and exception justifications. Normalize and classify documents|Adjudicate policy exceptions and ambiguous discrepancies. Final quality assurance and approval routing sign-off. Handle novel products/terms and update policies/rules|Deterministic completeness checks against required-field and required-document rules. Pricing and discount validation against price books and guardrails. Product configuration validation and compatibility checks
column23: Monitors CRM/CPQ intake, creates a checklist instance per deal type/region, assigns tasks, pings owners for missing items, and routes to finance/legal/RevOps based on policy and thresholds; maintains state across steps and escalates when SLAs are at risk. Initiates targeted requests (e.g., to sales rep for missing PO, to solutions for architecture diagram), tracks responses, attaches artifacts to the deal record, and re-runs validations when new data arrives.|Reads order forms, emails, redlines, SOWs, and exception memos; produces a structured summary (commercial terms, key dates, non-standard clauses, discount rationale) and highlights inconsistencies vs CPQ fields. Generates precise questions to sales/finance/legal when fields conflict (e.g., billing terms vs order form) and drafts exception narratives aligned to approval policy, reducing analyst writing time. Classifies uploaded files (MSA, DPA, PO, security addendum), detects missing required docs by deal type, and proposes standardized naming/metadata for downstream routing.|Interprets intent when documents conflict, decides whether an exception is acceptable, and determines risk trade-offs; requires business judgment and accountability. Confirms that automated checks are correct for high-value/strategic deals, ensures compliance with contracting/pricing policies, and signs off before routing to approvers to avoid costly errors. When new SKUs, bundles, or contracting constructs appear, humans define new rules, update playbooks, and provide labeled examples for model/rule tuning.|Applies rule engine/validation schemas by segment, region, product family, and deal motion to flag missing CPQ fields, missing attachments, invalid formats, and stale approvals. Computes list vs net, discount bands, margin floors, multi-year ramp logic, proration, and currency/tax rules; flags out-of-policy pricing and calculates required approval levels. Validates SKU combinations, entitlements, term alignment, renewal/co-term rules, and provisioning constraints using CPQ configuration rules and reference tables; detects anomalies/outliers via simple ML if available.

column8: Coordinate cross functional deal reviews with Sales, Finance, Legal, and Product to align on commercial terms, risk considerations, and execution timelines.
column15: Coordinating cross-functional deal reviews blends structured process work with high-stakes judgment. Traditional AI is best for deterministic policy checks and structured timeline analytics. Generative AI accelerates synthesis of dispersed information (CRM, emails, redlines) into briefs, minutes, and option sets, but must be verified due to hallucination/omissions. Agentic AI can reliably run the workflow-routing, scheduling, reminders, action tracking-when bounded by playbooks and permissions. Humans remain essential for exception approvals, negotiation trade-offs, and conflict resolution across functions. Assumptions: the organization has reasonably clean CRM/CPQ/CLM data, access-controlled document repositories, and defined approval matrices; without these, AI contributions drop and human effort rises.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) approving non-standard terms (discounts beyond threshold, unusual indemnities/liability caps, bespoke SLAs), (2) interpreting ambiguous legal language and business intent, (3) revenue recognition/accounting judgments and compliance determinations (privacy, export controls, sanctions), (4) resolving stakeholder conflicts and setting priorities, and (5) final sign-off that meeting summaries and action items accurately reflect commitments. Edge cases needing humans: novel product bundles, first-of-kind customers/regions, public-sector or regulated-industry deals, and situations where source documents are incomplete or contradictory.
column17: Over-automation could route to wrong approvers, spam stakeholders, or escalate inappropriately; autonomous actions may violate segregation-of-duties or create audit gaps. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, human-in-the-loop gates for escalations/approvals, audit logs, deterministic playbooks, and safe-fail behavior when confidence is low.
column18: May hallucinate terms, omit critical redline details, or mis-summarize decisions; could leak sensitive data if prompts/logs are mishandled; may produce persuasive but non-compliant negotiation language. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations, redline-aware parsing, mandatory human verification for briefs/minutes, PII/contract-data controls, and approved templates/guardrails.
column19: Rules can become outdated as policies change; brittle thresholds may mis-flag legitimate exceptions or miss nuanced risks; ML risk scoring can encode historical bias (e.g., over-penalizing certain regions/segments). Mitigations: policy versioning, regular rule reviews, explainable triggers, monitoring false positives/negatives, and governance for model updates.
column20: Data mismatches across CRM/CPQ/CLM/email can cause inconsistent briefs and wrong routing; identity/access control errors can expose confidential terms; conflicting outputs (GenAI summary vs rule engine flags) can confuse stakeholders. Mitigations: master data management, unified deal ID, role-based access, provenance/citations, reconciliation logic, and clear precedence rules (policy engine overrides narrative summaries).
column22: Orchestrate the deal review workflow (intake -> stakeholder routing -> meeting scheduling -> follow-ups). Maintain a live action-item register and drive closure|Prepare deal review briefing pack from scattered inputs. Draft and normalize meeting notes, decisions, and next steps. Generate scenario narratives and negotiation options|Make final judgment calls on commercial trade-offs and approvals. Resolve cross-functional conflicts and secure alignment. Ensure compliance, ethical handling, and confidentiality for sensitive deals|Detect policy deviations and risk flags against standard deal desk rules. Timeline and capacity analytics for execution readiness

Continuously updates owners/dates from emails/meeting notes, pings owners, escalates overdue items per policy, and prepares a readiness checklist for signature/booking.|Summarizes CRM notes, pricing proposals, redlines, and finance models into a structured brief (commercial terms, deviations, open questions) tailored to each function; drafts agendas and pre-read emails. Given constraints (margin floor, liability caps, delivery dates), proposes alternative term packages and trade-offs to discuss with stakeholders; clearly labels as suggestions, not commitments.|Requires accountability for margin/risk decisions, exception approvals, and prioritization under uncertainty; involves negotiation strategy and business context not fully captured in systems. Humans validate that outputs align with regulatory/contractual obligations (privacy, sanctions, revenue recognition) and decide what can be shared with whom; critical for high-stakes or novel deal structures.|Rule engine/ML classifiers compare proposed terms to approved templates (discount thresholds, payment terms, indemnity, data processing, export controls) and score risk/severity with explainable triggers. Uses structured data (implementation lead times, product availability, legal queue times) to estimate feasible timelines, identify bottlenecks, and recommend earliest close/launch dates.

column8: Ensure adherence to pricing, discount, and approval governance frameworks by applying policy rules, documenting exceptions, and maintaining audit ready approval records.
column15: This deal-desk governance task mixes deterministic checks (best handled by Traditional AI), cross-system workflow execution (Agentic AI), and narrative/audit documentation (Generative AI), but still requires accountable human judgment for ambiguous policy interpretation and high-stakes exception approvals. Assumptions: policies can be codified into decision tables for common cases; CPQ/CRM data is reasonably structured; integrations/APIs exist for routing and logging. Traditional AI provides explainable pass/fail and required approvals; Agentic AI operationalizes the process (routing, monitoring, re-approval triggers); GenAI accelerates drafting and summarization while humans validate facts and make final decisions. Work split reflects that a significant portion remains human due to governance accountability, negotiation, and edge cases, while automation can cover routine checks, routing, and documentation assembly.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving/denying exceptions that materially impact margin, revenue recognition, or regulatory exposure; (2) interpreting policy intent when rules are missing, conflicting, or outdated (new SKUs, bundles, multi-geo deals, non-standard payment/termination terms); (3) validating GenAI-generated narratives against source data to prevent misstatements in audit records; (4) handling escalations involving customer commitments, legal/compliance constraints, or executive approvals; and (5) final sign-off that the approval trail is complete, immutable where required, and defensible under audit. Humans are irreplaceable where accountability, risk acceptance, and negotiation are required.
column17: Autonomous routing could mis-route approvals due to incorrect context, stale org matrices, or unexpected deal changes; could trigger actions (re-approvals, escalations) that disrupt sales cycles; transparency and auditability of agent decisions may be insufficient. Mitigations: constrained action space, approval simulation/dry-run mode, policy-as-code gates, full decision logs, rollback controls, and human-in-the-loop for threshold-crossing actions.
column18: May hallucinate policy requirements or fabricate rationale; may omit critical constraints (e.g., export controls, public sector rules) if retrieval is incomplete; risk of leaking sensitive pricing data if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with citations, locked templates/required fields, redaction/DLP, human verification for high-risk deals, and grounding to structured CPQ fields.
column19: Rules can become brittle/outdated as policies change; deterministic logic may fail on complex bundles or data quality issues; anomaly models can generate false positives/negatives. Mitigations: governance for rule updates, versioning and testing, data validation pipelines, explainability traces, and periodic model recalibration with feedback loops.
column20: Conflicting outputs between rule engine and GenAI summaries; inconsistent identifiers across CPQ/CRM/CLM causing incomplete audit trails; access control gaps when orchestrating across systems; race conditions when deal terms change mid-approval. Mitigations: single source of truth for deal attributes, event-driven architecture with idempotent updates, unified identity/permissions, reconciliation checks, and end-to-end audit logging with artifact versioning.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end governance workflow across systems (CPQ/CRM/CLM/ERP). Dynamic approval routing and exception case management. Continuous compliance monitoring and reminders|Summarize applicable pricing/discount policies for the specific deal context. Draft exception justifications and approval narratives. Compile audit-ready approval packets and meeting notes|Interpret ambiguous policy intent and resolve conflicts between frameworks. Approve/deny high-risk exceptions and negotiate trade-offs. Final audit readiness sign-off and stakeholder alignment|Deterministic policy rule evaluation on structured deal data. Validation, completeness checks, and anomaly detection. Audit log structuring and retention enforcement

Selects the correct approver chain using policy outputs and org matrices; opens/updates exception cases, requests missing inputs from sales, and re-runs checks when revised pricing is submitted. Assembles a coherent record: timeline, approver comments, decision rationale, attachments list; summarizes email/Slack threads into structured audit notes while citing sources/links.|Handles gray areas (new products, bespoke terms, strategic accounts) where rules are incomplete or conflicting; decides how to apply intent and documents rationale. Makes accountable decisions on margin/discount exceptions, risk acceptance, and commercial strategy; balances revenue goals with governance and compliance obligations. Confirms the record is complete, defensible, and compliant; ensures approvers understand implications and that any deviations are explicitly authorized.|Applies codified rules/decision tables (discount thresholds, margin floors, delegation of authority, required attachments) to CPQ/CRM fields; outputs pass/fail and required approver set with explainable traces. Checks required fields/attachments, detects out-of-pattern discounts or margin exceptions via statistical/ML anomaly models, and flags potential data quality issues (currency, term, product eligibility). Automatically timestamps events, hashes/versions key artifacts, enforces retention labels, and ensures immutable logs for approvals and changes (where supported).

column8: Support contract and order form preparation by standardizing templates, confirming key commercial terms, and partnering with Legal on redline tracking and version control.
column15: This task mixes repeatable, policy-driven steps (template selection, term validation, metadata extraction) with high-stakes judgment (negotiation, exception approvals, enforceability). Traditional AI is best for deterministic checks and explainable policy enforcement. Generative AI accelerates drafting, redline summarization, and template documentation but must be constrained to approved clause libraries to reduce hallucinations. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the end-to-end workflow in CLM/CRM, managing versions, routing, and escalations; however, autonomy must be bounded by risk tiers and approval gates. Humans remain essential for interpreting intent, resolving conflicting inputs, negotiating deviations, and final sign-off. Assumptions: organization uses a CLM/document repository with APIs, has a maintained clause library and commercial policy thresholds, and deals can be risk-tiered (e.g., by ACV, region, regulated industry).
column16: Human oversight is essential at (1) any deviation from standard terms beyond pre-approved playbooks (liability, indemnity, data protection, payment/termination), (2) low-confidence extractions or conflicting system-of-record data (CRM vs CPQ vs finance), (3) customer-specific context requiring negotiation strategy, (4) compliance-sensitive clauses (privacy/SCCs, export controls, public sector terms), and (5) final enforceability and signature readiness. Humans must approve all external-facing contract language changes unless explicitly pre-approved, and must arbitrate edge cases such as multi-entity contracting structures, unusual revenue recognition implications, or jurisdictional conflicts.
column17: Over-automation could route/approve incorrectly, miss escalation, or circulate wrong version; emergent behavior in multi-step workflows; auditability challenges if actions aren't logged. Mitigations: strict guardrails (risk-tier gates), least-privilege access, immutable audit logs, simulation/testing in sandbox, and human-in-the-loop checkpoints for exceptions and external sends.
column18: Hallucinated or non-approved clause language; subtle legal meaning drift when paraphrasing; leakage of confidential customer terms if prompts/logs are mishandled; inconsistent formatting that creates ambiguity. Mitigations: retrieval from approved clause library, locked templates, citation of source fields, redline-only suggestions mode, DLP controls, and mandatory human/legal review for external documents.
column19: Brittle rules leading to wrong template/addenda selection when policies change; false positives/negatives in term checks; extraction errors on scanned/complex documents. Mitigations: governance for rule updates, monitoring/QA with feedback loops, confidence thresholds with human review, and periodic policy alignment with Legal/Finance.
column20: Mismatch between CRM/CPQ/CLM data causing incorrect population; conflicting outputs (GenAI draft vs rules engine); version-control race conditions across tools; security/permission misconfigurations. Mitigations: single source-of-truth hierarchy, schema validation, orchestration layer with idempotent actions, consistent document IDs, role-based access, and end-to-end testing with audit trails.
column22: Orchestrate contract/order-form workflow and stakeholder routing. Automated version control and redline cycle management. Exception handling playbook execution (within guardrails)|Populate and draft contract/order-form sections from structured deal inputs. Summarize and compare redlines across versions. Standardize templates and create guidance snippets|Negotiate and approve non-standard commercial/legal positions. Resolve ambiguities and ensure factual correctness of deal inputs. Final quality control and sign-off readiness check|Template selection and clause assembly via rules/decision trees. Commercial term validation and policy checks. Metadata extraction and document classification
column23: Coordinates intake -> template selection -> term validation -> legal review -> redline cycles -> approvals -> signature readiness by triggering tasks in CLM/CRM, assigning owners, setting SLAs, and escalating blockers based on deal stage and risk tier. Monitors document repository/CLM for new versions, enforces naming conventions, locks/unlocks editing, ensures the latest version is circulated, and prompts parties when redlines are pending; can generate a change log and request missing artifacts. When deviations from standard terms are detected, selects the correct fallback clause set, routes to the right approver (Legal/Finance/Security), and gathers required justification fields; stops and requests human input when confidence is low or policy thresholds are exceeded.|Generates first-pass order forms/SOW exhibits using approved clause libraries and deal data (products, pricing, term, renewal, billing, delivery), producing consistent language and formatting while citing source fields. Creates plain-language summaries of changes, highlights commercial impact (e.g., liability cap, payment terms), and produces side-by-side comparisons to speed review; flags ambiguous edits for human/legal confirmation. Assists in updating template instructions, playbooks, and checklists; drafts reusable email/Slack updates to stakeholders and generates FAQs for common deviations, using organization-approved tone and terminology.|Requires judgment on risk appetite, customer relationship context, and trade-offs; only humans (Deal Desk/Legal/Finance leadership) can approve exceptions and negotiate final positions. Humans confirm source-of-truth data (pricing approvals, SKU configuration, customer legal entity) and correct discrepancies that automation cannot reliably reconcile across systems. Human performs final review for enforceability, compliance (export, privacy, procurement), and alignment with internal approvals before sending to customer or initiating signature.|Uses deterministic rules (region, entity type, product line, deal size, channel, data-processing needs) to select the correct base template and required addenda (DPA, SCCs, security exhibits). Validates structured fields against policy thresholds (discount bands, payment terms, auto-renew, termination, liability caps) and detects missing/invalid entries; produces explainable pass/fail results and required remediation steps. Extracts key fields from PDFs/Word (party names, dates, amounts) using OCR/NER models and classifies document type to ensure correct filing and downstream reporting; flags low-confidence extractions for review.

column8: Identify process gaps and recommend improvements to deal desk workflows, automation, and enablement materials to reduce friction and improve seller experience.
column15: The task mixes discovery (quant + qual), diagnosis, and organizational decision-making. Traditional AI is best for deterministic KPI computation, anomaly detection, and process mining on structured event logs. Generative AI accelerates synthesis of unstructured feedback and drafting of enablement artifacts and proposed future-state workflows, but must be grounded in source data to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-system data pulls, iterative root-cause runs, and ongoing monitoring/experiment management-turning one-off analysis into a continuous improvement loop. Human work remains substantial because prioritization, policy trade-offs (risk vs. speed), and stakeholder alignment are high-stakes and context-dependent in an enterprise deal desk. Assumptions: access to CRM/CPQ/ticketing event logs; clear data governance; ability to run limited experiments; and a defined approval matrix/policy baseline. If data access is poor or policies are in flux, human share increases and agentic automation decreases.
column16: Humans are essential to (1) set/approve KPIs and guardrails (e.g., revenue leakage tolerance, compliance requirements), (2) adjudicate ambiguous policy areas and exceptions (strategic accounts, non-standard terms, quarter-end escalations), (3) approve any workflow or automation that changes approval authority, pricing/discount behavior, or legal/commercial commitments, and (4) manage change adoption-training, communications, and enforcement across Sales/RevOps/Legal/Finance. Human review is also required for AI-generated recommendations that could introduce bias (e.g., routing that disadvantages certain regions/segments) or that rely on incomplete logs; and for interpreting causality vs. correlation in bottleneck analyses.
column17: Risks: autonomous actions causing unintended workflow changes (e.g., creating/closing tickets, changing routing rules) or spamming stakeholders; brittle tool integrations; emergent behavior when optimizing for the wrong KPI; limited auditability of multi-step decisions. Mitigations: human-in-the-loop approvals for any write actions; sandbox environments; explicit guardrails and rate limits; comprehensive logging/audit trails; staged rollout with monitoring and rollback.
column18: Risks: hallucinated root causes or incorrect policy statements in drafted enablement; over-generalizing from noisy feedback; leaking sensitive deal/customer data in prompts/outputs; tone or guidance misaligned with current pricing/legal policy. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations; redaction/DLP; human SME review; version control and approval workflows; restrict to internal, least-privilege data.
column19: Risks: biased or misleading metrics due to missing/incorrect timestamps, inconsistent stage definitions, or unlogged work; rigid rules that fail on edge cases; model drift as products/pricing change; false positives in anomaly detection. Mitigations: data quality checks, schema governance, periodic recalibration, explainable thresholds, and exception handling paths.
column20: Risks: inconsistent definitions across systems (CRM vs CPQ vs ticketing) leading to wrong conclusions; feedback loops where agentic actions change the data generating process; security/permission mismatches; conflicting recommendations between GenAI narratives and Traditional AI metrics. Mitigations: canonical data model and metric dictionary; separation of duties (read vs write); end-to-end testing; reconciliation layer that ties GenAI outputs to metric evidence; governance board for changes and KPI ownership.
column22: Continuously monitor deal-desk workflow signals and trigger investigations. Orchestrate cross-system process mining and root-cause runs. Run controlled improvement experiments and rollout workflows|Synthesize qualitative feedback into themes and friction narratives. Draft improved enablement materials and comms. Generate process improvement options and automation ideas|Define success criteria, constraints, and prioritize improvements. Validate root causes and approve policy/process changes. Stakeholder alignment and change management|Quantify bottlenecks and anomalies using structured metrics. Process mining / conformance checking against the intended workflow. Policy/rules validation for automation candidates
column23: Connects to CRM/CPQ/ticketing/Slack to watch for SLA breaches, rework loops, approval bottlenecks, and exception spikes; autonomously opens an 'investigation' run, gathers context, and routes to the right owner with suggested next steps. Coordinates A/B tests or phased rollouts (new intake form, auto-approval thresholds), monitors KPIs, and rolls back if guardrails are breached; drafts change tickets and implementation checklists for ops/IT.|Summarizes seller comments from tickets, call notes, surveys, and chat into structured themes (e.g., 'unclear discount policy', 'duplicate data entry'), with representative quotes and confidence/coverage indicators. Creates first drafts of playbooks, SOP updates, intake form guidance, FAQ entries, and seller-facing announcements tailored to persona/region; proposes examples and decision trees for common deal scenarios. Brainstorms solution patterns (CPQ validations, guided selling prompts, approval matrix simplification) and maps them to expected impact/effort; produces strawman future-state workflows for review.|Requires business judgment to balance seller experience, revenue risk, compliance, and stakeholder priorities; sets KPI targets and decides what 'good' looks like for different segments. Humans must confirm findings with context (edge cases, upcoming product/pricing changes) and approve changes that affect commercial terms, legal/compliance posture, or customer commitments. Needs relationship management with Sales, Legal, Finance, RevOps, and IT; handles objections, training adoption, and ensures accountability for implementation and ongoing governance.|Uses rules/SQL/BI and classical ML to compute cycle times, touch counts, rework rates, approval latency, exception frequency; flags outliers by segment and detects statistically significant shifts. Applies process-mining algorithms to event logs to discover actual paths, measure variant frequency, and identify non-conforming steps (skips, loops, back-and-forth) that correlate with delays. Encodes approval matrices, discount thresholds, and required fields as deterministic checks to estimate how much work could be auto-approved/auto-routed and where rules are ambiguous or conflicting.

column8: Stay current with industry trends and internal performance data to propose innovative ideas that enhance deal desk effectiveness and enterprise sales outcomes.
column15: The task mixes continuous sensing (external + internal), quantitative analysis, and high-stakes prioritization. Traditional AI is best for reliable KPI computation, segmentation, and anomaly detection on structured performance data. Agentic AI adds value by automating the ongoing monitoring and multi-system data refresh/orchestration that would otherwise consume analyst time, and by routing insights into workflows. Generative AI accelerates synthesis and ideation-turning signals into coherent narratives, hypotheses, and experiment/pilot documentation-but must be grounded in verified data to avoid plausible-sounding errors. Human contribution remains largest because proposing 'innovative ideas' that materially affect enterprise deals requires judgment about feasibility, stakeholder incentives, risk/compliance, and organizational readiness. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/CPQ/BI data; permissioned retrieval for external sources; and a governance process where AI outputs are advisory, not auto-enforced. Constraints: data quality gaps, sparse labels for causal inference, and confidentiality of deal terms limit full automation.
column16: Humans must (1) verify that external trend summaries and internal KPI shifts are accurate and relevant before acting; (2) interpret ambiguous signals (e.g., cycle time increase due to a single large customer negotiation) and decide whether to escalate; (3) make final prioritization decisions that trade margin vs. win-rate vs. risk; (4) approve any changes to pricing/discount guardrails, approval matrices, contracting language, or compliance controls; (5) handle edge cases involving strategic accounts, regulatory constraints, or reputational risk where automated recommendations could be inappropriate. Human oversight is also essential for access control (ensuring no sensitive deal data is exposed in prompts), and for validating that proposed experiments won't violate customer commitments or internal policies.
column17: Autonomous monitoring/orchestration can take unintended actions (e.g., creating noisy alerts, routing to wrong stakeholders, pulling data beyond least-privilege); emergent behavior from poorly bounded goals; auditability challenges. Mitigations: sandboxed permissions, explicit action policies, human-in-the-loop approvals for external sharing or policy changes, rate limits, and comprehensive logging/rollback.
column18: Hallucination or overconfident synthesis could misstate competitor moves or misinterpret internal metrics; may leak sensitive deal information if prompts/logs are not controlled; can introduce bias by over-weighting loud/available sources; may generate impractical ideas without operational constraints. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with cited sources, strict data redaction, human fact-checking, and templates requiring assumptions/risks/KPIs.
column19: Brittle rules and models can degrade with changing sales motions, product packaging, or seasonality; risk of spurious correlations driving wrong focus; fairness concerns if segmentation disadvantages certain regions/segments. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, drift monitoring, interpretable models, and governance reviews of features/thresholds.
column20: Data lineage and metric definition mismatches can cause GenAI narratives to conflict with dashboard truth; orchestration failures can propagate stale data into summaries; inconsistent access controls across tools can create privacy/compliance gaps. Mitigations: single source of truth for KPI definitions, automated data validation checks, end-to-end permissioning, and requiring citations/metric IDs in generated briefs.

Negotiates buy-in with sales leadership, finance, legal, and ops; manages adoption, training, and feedback-areas requiring trust and accountability.|Deterministically calculates metrics (cycle time, discount leakage, win rate by segment, approval SLA adherence) and produces standardized dashboards with explainable logic. Uses statistical process control, time-series anomaly detection, and simple predictive models to flag unusual shifts (e.g., spike in exception requests) and quantify confidence/seasonality effects. Applies clustering/decision trees/logistic regression on structured fields (industry, ACV, product mix, region) to surface drivers of delays or margin erosion with interpretable feature importance.

column8: Maintain renewal management systems and tools by ensuring customer contract data, renewal dates, and opportunity stages are accurate, complete, and aligned to enterprise sales processes.
column15: This task is largely structured data hygiene and process alignment across enterprise sales systems, which is well-suited to Traditional AI for deterministic validations and anomaly detection, and Agentic AI for orchestrating multi-step workflows (pull records, validate, create tasks, sync updates, escalate). Generative AI adds value where inputs are unstructured (contracts, emails, notes) and where clear communication and guidance are needed, but it must be constrained due to hallucination risk. Humans remain essential for authoritative decisions when systems disagree, when contract interpretation affects obligations, and for governance of the underlying sales process rules. Assumptions: core systems expose APIs, there is a defined enterprise renewal process with stage/field rules, and audit logging is required. Constraints: data access controls, SOX/finance controls, and legal sensitivity limit full automation of record edits in high-value accounts.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) interpreting nuanced contract terms (auto-renew, termination rights, notice periods) when extraction confidence is low; (2) approving changes that impact ARR, renewal dates, or opportunity stage for strategic/high-ARR accounts; (3) resolving conflicts between systems of record (CLM vs billing vs CRM) and deciding the authoritative value; (4) handling exceptions like co-terming, partial renewals, product swaps, or amendments in-flight; (5) compliance controls (SOX, audit trails, segregation of duties) requiring approvals and periodic reviews; and (6) updating the underlying process rules/playbooks when the business changes. Edge cases: acquisitions/migrations causing missing IDs, multi-entity global contracts with different end dates, and renewals tied to usage-based billing where dates/amounts are variable.
column17: Autonomous workflows could propagate incorrect updates across systems, create noisy task spam, or escalate improperly; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals (e.g., optimizing 'completeness' by filling defaults). Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for write actions above thresholds, simulation/dry-run mode, rate limits, robust audit logs, and clear rollback procedures.
column18: May hallucinate contract terms or misread scanned PDFs; could introduce incorrect renewal dates or clauses if allowed to write back automatically. Privacy/IP risk if contract text is sent to external models. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with approved playbooks, on-prem/tenant-isolated models, confidence scoring with mandatory human verification, and redaction of sensitive fields.
column19: Rules can become brittle/outdated as sales stages or product packaging changes; ML matching can mis-link records causing downstream errors. Mitigations: version-controlled rule sets, monitoring for rule drift, periodic sampling/QA, human review for low-confidence matches, and clear explainability for flags.
column20: Conflicting sources of truth and inconsistent field semantics across CRM/CPQ/CLM/billing can cause oscillating updates; latency can create race conditions; poor identity resolution can duplicate opportunities. Mitigations: master data management strategy, event-driven architecture with idempotent updates, canonical data model, reconciliation jobs, and governance on ownership of each field.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end renewal data hygiene workflow across systems (CRM/CPQ/CLM/billing). Automated exception handling and routing. Governance: define/maintain sales process rules and data standards|Deterministic validation of required fields and business rules. Record matching, deduplication, and anomaly detection on structured data. Forecast-impact scoring for data issues
