column43: CRM Pipeline Forecasting and Data Hygiene

column8: Partner with sales development, solutions engineering, marketing, and customer success to coordinate account plans, handoffs, and follow-up actions that improve win rates.
column15: Coordinating account plans and handoffs is a socio-technical workflow: AI can reduce admin load and improve consistency, but humans must own strategy and relationship-driven alignment. Generative AI is best for synthesizing scattered qualitative inputs (notes, transcripts) into usable briefs and drafting follow-ups, accelerating internal communication. Agentic AI adds value by executing and adapting multi-step workflows (scheduling, task creation, nudging, escalation) across tools, improving handoff timeliness. Traditional AI is well-suited to structured checks-routing, SLA rules, and health scoring-providing explainable guardrails and analytics. Assumptions: the org uses a CRM (e.g., Salesforce/HubSpot), calendar, and collaboration tools with APIs; call recording/transcription is available; governance allows AI access to relevant data with role-based controls. Constraints: customer-facing commitments and strategic decisions remain human-led due to risk and accountability.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) deal strategy decisions (positioning, pricing, concessions, competitive moves) where context and accountability matter; (2) approving customer-facing outputs, especially for enterprise/security/legal topics, to prevent inaccurate or unauthorized claims; (3) resolving cross-functional conflicts and resource trade-offs (SE bandwidth, marketing priorities, CS risk posture); (4) handling exceptions such as non-standard contract terms, escalations, regulated-industry requirements, or ambiguous stakeholder politics. Humans should also periodically audit AI-triggered workflows (routing, escalations) and adjust playbooks/thresholds to avoid over-automation and ensure fairness across territories/segments.
column17: Autonomous actions (creating tasks, sending nudges, scheduling) can spam stakeholders, create conflicting meetings, or escalate prematurely; brittle tool integrations can cause partial execution; emergent behavior if objectives are mis-specified (optimize speed over quality). Mitigations: human-in-the-loop for high-impact actions, rate limits, approval gates by deal stage/value, clear playbooks, audit logs, rollback mechanisms, and sandbox testing.
column18: Hallucination or overconfident summaries can introduce incorrect deal facts (stakeholders, requirements, dates) leading to misaligned execution; tone or confidentiality mistakes in drafts; leakage of sensitive customer data if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from CRM sources, citation/links to evidence, restricted templates, redaction/DLP, mandatory human approval for external messages, and evaluation on factuality.
column19: Rule/ML scoring can be biased toward activity volume over quality, disadvantaging certain segments or sales styles; rules become outdated as process changes; false positives/negatives in health scoring can distract teams. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, transparent feature sets, segment-specific thresholds, monitoring drift, and allowing AE overrides with reason codes.
column20: Data consistency issues across CRM, marketing automation, CS platforms can cause incorrect routing or summaries; identity/permission mismatches can expose data; conflicting recommendations between scoring (Traditional AI) and agent actions (Agentic AI) can confuse teams. Mitigations: master data governance, unified customer/account IDs, RBAC/least privilege, end-to-end observability, conflict-resolution logic (e.g., agent defers to policy rules), and clear ownership of playbooks and metrics.
column22: Orchestrate cross-functional workflow for account planning and handoffs. Automate meeting scheduling, agenda routing, and action-item tracking across teams. Proactive follow-up nudging and exception handling|Draft and refine account plans and internal briefs. Summarize cross-functional inputs and customer interactions into concise updates. Create follow-up communications and enablement requests|Set deal strategy, priorities, and trade-offs across teams. Run high-stakes alignment conversations and resolve cross-functional conflicts. Approve customer-facing messaging and sensitive internal decisions. Handle edge cases and exceptions (non-standard deals, escalations, renewals/expansions complexity)|Opportunity health scoring and win-rate signal detection. Routing, SLA enforcement, and checklist validation for handoffs. Attribution and performance analytics for coordination activities
column23: Monitors CRM/opportunity stage changes and triggers the right playbooks (e.g., schedule internal deal review, request SE discovery, initiate CS risk check), assigns owners, sets deadlines, and adapts the sequence based on deal signals (stage, ICP fit, competitive presence). Coordinates calendars, sends pre-reads, captures decisions/action items from calls (via integrated transcription), opens/updates tasks in CRM/project tools, and follows up until closure with escalation rules. Detects stalled handoffs (e.g., SDR hasn't booked discovery, SE hasn't delivered solution outline) and autonomously pings owners, proposes next-best actions, and escalates to AE when SLA thresholds are breached.|Generates structured account plans (stakeholders, pains, hypotheses, mutual action plan) from CRM notes, call transcripts, and firmographics; tailors versions for SDR/SE/Marketing/CS audiences. Turns meeting notes, Slack threads, and call transcripts into weekly deal summaries, risks, next steps, and asks; highlights inconsistencies for human review. Drafts internal handoff messages (to SE/CS/Marketing) and customer-facing follow-ups (recap emails, next-step confirmations) aligned to tone, value props, and compliance templates.|Requires judgment on positioning, competitive strategy, pricing/packaging, and prioritization when resources conflict; accountable for outcomes and relationship management. Humans negotiate commitments, handle ambiguity, and manage interpersonal dynamics; needed when teams disagree on scope, timelines, or feasibility. Human review needed for accuracy, legal/compliance, security commitments, and executive communications; ensures no hallucinated or unauthorized claims are sent. Complex account contexts (multi-product, procurement/legal hurdles, distressed accounts) require nuanced reasoning and accountability beyond current AI reliability.|Uses deterministic rules/ML on structured CRM data (stage duration, activity counts, stakeholder coverage, MEDDICC fields) to score deal health and flag missing handoff prerequisites. Applies rules to ensure required fields/artifacts exist before handoff (e.g., discovery notes, qualification criteria, security requirements) and routes to correct queue/team based on territory, segment, product line. Analyzes structured data to correlate handoff timeliness, meeting cadence, and content usage with win rates; produces dashboards for continuous process improvement.

column8: Support renewals and expansion by identifying growth opportunities, aligning with customer success on adoption signals, and engaging stakeholders to secure commitments.
column15: The task mixes structured signal processing (best handled by Traditional AI), content synthesis and messaging (Generative AI), and multi-system workflow execution (Agentic AI). However, renewals/expansion outcomes hinge on relationship management, negotiation, and accountable decision-making, keeping humans central. Assumptions: AE has access to CRM/CS/product telemetry; policies exist for outreach/discounting; data quality is moderate. AI complements by (1) prioritizing accounts via risk/propensity scoring, (2) converting noisy adoption data into clear narratives and assets, and (3) orchestrating reminders, task creation, and scheduling. Human effort remains highest due to stakeholder engagement, strategy selection, and approvals.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (a) committing forecast and selecting expansion strategy when signals conflict; (b) any external customer communication beyond low-risk scheduling-especially exec outreach, renewal terms, and competitive positioning; (c) pricing, discounting, and contractual commitments requiring approvals; (d) handling edge cases like customer re-org, security incidents, non-standard legal terms, or multi-year restructures; (e) compliance/privacy checks when using customer data in prompts/outputs; (f) resolving AE/CS misalignment on adoption ownership and escalation paths.
column17: Over-automation could spam stakeholders, create incorrect CRM updates, or trigger actions based on misread context; opaque decisioning and cascading errors across systems. Mitigate with strict permissioning, rate limits, approval gates for outbound actions, audit logs, sandbox testing, and fallback-to-human on low confidence or high ARR accounts.
column18: Hallucinated facts about usage/outcomes, tone mismatch with customer relationship, leakage of sensitive customer data via prompts, and biased/overconfident negotiation advice. Mitigate with retrieval-grounded generation from approved sources, citation requirements, redaction, templates, and mandatory human review for customer-facing content and pricing language.
column19: Scores can be brittle if telemetry is incomplete, leading to false churn risk or missed upsell; drift over time; fairness concerns if proxies correlate with customer segment. Mitigate with monitoring, periodic retraining, explainability (driver breakdown), human override, and data quality checks.
column20: Conflicting recommendations (GenAI narrative vs ML score), identity mismatches across CRM/CS/product systems, and prompt injection/data poisoning via untrusted notes. Mitigate with a single source of truth, entity resolution, governance on what data can be used, confidence/consistency checks, and clear escalation rules when models disagree.
column22: Orchestrate renewal/expansion workflow across systems (CRM, CS platform, billing). Automated stakeholder mapping and outreach sequencing (with guardrails)|Summarize account adoption signals and renewal risks into an executive-ready narrative. Draft personalized expansion hypotheses and messaging assets. Prepare negotiation and objection-handling playbooks|Validate opportunity strategy and choose which expansion path to pursue. Run high-stakes stakeholder conversations and secure commitments. Approve external communications, pricing/concessions, and compliance-sensitive steps. Coordinate with Customer Success on adoption plan ownership and accountability|Predict renewal likelihood and expansion propensity from structured signals. Detect adoption anomalies and trigger alerts. Segment accounts and recommend next-best-actions based on rules
column23: Monitors renewal dates and health signals, creates/updates CRM opportunities, assigns tasks, triggers playbooks, and routes exceptions to the AE/CSM based on policy; adapts sequencing (e.g., exec outreach before legal) using context like deal size and risk. Builds a contact graph from CRM/email/calendar, proposes multi-threading plan, schedules meetings, and drafts task lists; can execute low-risk steps (meeting scheduling, reminders) while requiring human approval for external messaging.|Turns product usage, support tickets, NPS/QBR notes, and CS updates into concise summaries, highlighting value realized, gaps, and renewal/expansion implications; produces QBR/EBR briefs. Generates tailored talk tracks, email sequences, mutual action plans, and proposal language aligned to the customer's industry, prior outcomes, and stakeholder personas; suggests discovery questions to validate needs. Creates scenario-based responses (pricing, procurement, competitor, budget freeze) and suggests concession strategies consistent with policy inputs; flags where legal/compliance review is needed.|Requires judgment on customer politics, competitive context, and strategic fit; accountable for forecast and prioritization trade-offs. Humans build trust, read nuance, handle conflict, and negotiate terms; needed for executive alignment, procurement/legal navigation, and closing. Ensures accuracy, brand tone, contractual correctness, and adherence to discounting/approval policies; mitigates reputational and legal risk. Cross-functional alignment involves ambiguity and organizational dynamics; humans resolve disagreements and set clear owners/timelines.|Uses explainable ML/scorecards on usage trends, seat growth, ticket volume, payment history, and engagement to produce risk/upsell scores and key drivers for prioritization. Applies rules/anomaly detection to identify drops in usage, stalled onboarding milestones, or champion churn; triggers alerts and playbook entry criteria. Segments by ARR, renewal window, health tier, and product mix; recommends standardized plays (exec sponsor call, training, add-on pitch) with deterministic logic for consistency.

column8: Contribute to scalable sales processes by sharing best practices, participating in enablement, and providing feedback on playbooks, tools, and messaging improvements.
column15: This task blends qualitative judgment (what should become a standard process) with repeatable analysis and content work. Generative AI accelerates synthesis and drafting of playbooks/messaging, but requires grounding in approved assets and human validation to avoid incorrect claims or tone drift. Traditional AI is strong for deterministic measurement (adoption, funnel metrics) and scalable tagging/compliance checks. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the end-to-end improvement loop-monitoring signals, routing feedback, and managing pilots-yet needs guardrails to prevent over-automation or misaligned experiments. Humans remain essential for strategic prioritization, cross-functional alignment, and final approvals, hence a collaboration classification and the largest work share for humans.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) deciding which 'best practices' are truly transferable across segments/territories and not artifacts of a few deals; (2) prioritizing changes against quarterly strategy, capacity, and product roadmap; (3) approving any customer-facing messaging, competitive claims, pricing/terms, and regulated-industry language; (4) resolving conflicts between stakeholder goals (e.g., speed vs compliance, standardization vs flexibility); (5) handling edge cases like atypical enterprise-like mid-market deals, sensitive customer escalations, or markets with different legal constraints. Humans should also review AI-generated summaries/drafts for factual accuracy and ensure data privacy policies are followed when using call transcripts and customer data.
column17: Autonomous orchestration could spam stakeholders, create excessive tickets, or run experiments that harm pipeline if guardrails are weak. Potential for emergent behavior (optimizing for proxy metrics like activity volume) and unclear accountability. Mitigations: constrained action spaces, approval workflows for rollouts, rate limits, audit logs, KPI guardrails (e.g., win rate not just activity), and kill switches.
column18: May hallucinate 'best practices' or fabricate evidence; can introduce unapproved claims, incorrect competitive positioning, or inconsistent brand voice. Risk of leaking sensitive customer data if prompts include PII or confidential deal details. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation grounded in approved repositories; redaction/PII controls; mandatory citations; human review gates; style/claims guardrails.
column19: Brittle rules and models can misclassify feedback or misread performance due to data quality issues, seasonality, or territory mix changes; may encode historical bias (e.g., favoring certain industries). Mitigations: periodic recalibration, monitoring for drift, transparent metrics definitions, and data governance/validation routines.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs dashboard metrics), feedback loops that amplify noise, and identity/permission mismatches across systems can cause incorrect recommendations or data exposure. Mitigations: single source of truth for metrics, unified taxonomy, role-based access control, end-to-end auditability, and staged deployment with user acceptance testing.
column22: Continuously monitor sales execution signals and surface process improvement opportunities. Orchestrate enablement workflows and feedback loops. Run controlled rollouts of playbook/tool changes|Synthesize best practices from unstructured inputs. Draft and iterate playbook sections, messaging, and enablement content. Convert qualitative feedback into structured recommendations|Validate and contextualize best practices before institutionalizing them. Provide nuanced frontline feedback and stakeholder alignment. Approve final playbook/tool/messaging changes for compliance and brand risk|Quantify funnel/process performance and adoption metrics. Detect data quality issues and enforce process compliance. Classify and tag feedback at scale
column23: Connects to CRM, call-recording, email, and enablement platforms; autonomously tracks leading indicators (stage conversion, cycle time, objection frequency) and flags anomalies/opportunities, then proposes prioritized experiments (e.g., new discovery checklist) with expected impact. Automatically routes drafts and surveys to the right stakeholders (RevOps, Enablement, Product Marketing), schedules enablement sessions, collects acknowledgements, and follows up; adapts cadence based on participation and performance. Manages A/B or phased rollouts (pilot cohort vs control), ensures correct assets are delivered in the right moments (in-app guidance), and compiles outcome reports; pauses/rolls back if guardrails are breached.|Summarizes call transcripts, win/loss notes, Slack threads, and deal reviews into reusable patterns (talk tracks, objection handling, discovery questions) tailored to mid-market AE context. Generates first drafts of playbook updates, email sequences, battlecards, and training snippets; proposes variants by persona/industry while maintaining brand voice when grounded in approved messaging. Transforms AE feedback into categorized issues (tool UX, messaging clarity, competitive gaps), writes concise problem statements, and suggests acceptance criteria for updates.|AEs/enablement leaders judge whether patterns are causal vs coincidental, ensure fit for ICP/territory, and avoid over-generalizing from small samples. Humans negotiate trade-offs across Product, Marketing, RevOps, and Sales leadership; incorporate strategic priorities, competitive context, and customer sentiment. Human review is required for legal/compliance (claims, privacy), pricing/contract language, and high-stakes messaging to prevent reputational or regulatory harm.|Uses dashboards, rules, and classical ML to compute stage conversion, activity-to-outcome correlations, content usage, and training completion; provides explainable trend and cohort analyses. Rule-based checks for missing fields, inconsistent stage definitions, and SLA breaches; triggers alerts and creates tickets for remediation to keep scalable processes reliable. Supervised models or keyword/rule systems label feedback by theme, product area, persona, and severity to support prioritization and reporting.

column8: Maintain CRM and sales enablement tools by ensuring data hygiene, accurate account ownership, and consistent pipeline stage definitions to support reliable forecasting and execution.
column15: This task mixes deterministic governance (best handled by Traditional AI), ongoing monitoring and workflow execution across systems (Agentic AI), and communication/enablement artifacts plus summarization (Generative AI). Humans remain essential for defining the operating model (stage definitions, ownership policy), adjudicating exceptions, and approving high-impact changes. Assumptions: CRM has robust APIs/audit logs; clear RevOps policies exist; access controls and approval workflows are available. Constraints: mid-market AE context means high volume of records and frequent stage/close-date changes, making automation valuable but requiring guardrails to avoid disrupting selling motion.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) setting/maintaining pipeline stage definitions and required fields (process design), (2) approving exceptions and high-impact actions (bulk ownership changes, merges, stage overrides) to prevent revenue attribution errors, (3) resolving ambiguous territory/account disputes, and (4) compliance/privacy review of what data can be ingested from emails/call notes. Humans also validate model/rule changes and handle edge cases like acquisitions, parent-child hierarchies, and partner-sourced opportunities.
column17: Autonomous actions (reassignments, merges, stage enforcement) can cause widespread reporting/comp errors if misconfigured; emergent loops (repeated retries) can spam users or thrash records. Mitigate with least-privilege access, approval gates, rate limits, dry-run modes, and comprehensive audit logs/rollback plans.
column18: May hallucinate field values or infer incorrect stages from incomplete notes; could leak sensitive customer data if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigate with retrieval from CRM as source of truth, constrained templates, PII redaction, and human-in-the-loop for critical updates.
column19: Rules can become brittle as process evolves; fuzzy matching may create false-positive merges; anomaly models can drift seasonally. Mitigate with versioned rule management, periodic calibration, confidence thresholds, and sampling-based QA.
column20: Conflicting sources of truth across CRM and enablement tools can cause oscillating updates; identity/permission mismatches can break workflows; inconsistent field mappings can corrupt data. Mitigate with master-data governance, contract tests for integrations, centralized schema registry, and staged rollouts with monitoring.
column22: Continuously monitor CRM for hygiene violations and trigger remediation workflows. Orchestrate account ownership change workflows. Automate pipeline stage governance enforcement. Coordinate cross-tool sync checks (CRM  sales enablement  marketing automation)|Draft and maintain human-readable data hygiene playbooks and in-app guidance. Summarize data quality issues and forecast-impact narratives for stakeholders. Assist reps with natural-language CRM updates and field completion. Generate exception justifications and approval requests|Define and approve pipeline stage definitions, entry/exit criteria, and required fields. Resolve ambiguous ownership disputes and strategic account exceptions. Approve high-impact data changes and merges. Investigate root causes of recurring data quality issues and drive change management|Rule-based validation of required fields, formats, and stage-entry criteria. Duplicate detection and record linkage. Anomaly detection on pipeline movements and forecast inputs. Territory/ownership assignment via deterministic routing rules
column23: Runs scheduled/near-real-time checks across CRM objects (accounts, contacts, opps, activities) for missing/invalid fields, stale stages, duplicate records, and policy breaches; opens tickets, pings owners in Slack/Email, and routes items based on severity and SLA. When signals indicate ownership mismatch (territory change, rep departure, reassignment request), agent gathers required context (territory rules, activity history), proposes reassignment, requests approvals, executes changes via CRM APIs, and logs audit trails. Detects stage transitions that violate definitions (e.g., skipping required steps, missing MEDDICC fields), blocks or flags updates, and guides reps through required updates; escalates exceptions to RevOps/Sales Ops. Runs reconciliation jobs, identifies drift (field mapping mismatches, failed syncs), retries safe operations, and creates incident reports with suggested fixes.|Generates concise SOPs, tooltips, and enablement snippets tailored to the org's pipeline stages and required fields; updates documentation when policies change. Turns dashboards/anomaly outputs into executive summaries (what changed, likely causes, impacted deals/segments) for weekly pipeline reviews and QBRs. From call notes/emails, suggests structured updates (next step, close date rationale, stage, key fields) while prompting for missing required info; reduces manual entry burden. Creates standardized messages for stage/ownership exceptions, including evidence links and risk notes, to speed manager/RevOps approvals.|Requires business judgment and alignment across Sales/RevOps; impacts comp, forecasting, and process adherence. Edge cases (multi-region accounts, partner-led deals, named accounts) need negotiation, relationship context, and leadership decisions. Merges/reassignments can affect reporting, customer comms, and compliance; humans validate before irreversible actions. Requires cross-functional coordination, training, and policy enforcement beyond automation (e.g., incentives, enablement, system redesign).|Implements deterministic checks (picklist validity, date ranges, mandatory MEDDICC fields per stage, close date windows) and prevents saves or flags records. Uses fuzzy matching/classical ML on names, domains, emails, and addresses to identify duplicates; proposes merges with confidence scores. Statistical/ML models flag unusual changes (sudden close date pushes, stage aging outliers, atypical discounting) to prioritize hygiene work that affects forecast accuracy. Applies territory matrices (geo, segment, industry, account tier) and round-robin logic to recommend or auto-assign ownership where policy is clear.
column41: CRM Data Hygiene and Pipeline Forecasting
column43: CRM Pipeline Forecasting and Data Hygiene

column8: Prospect and qualify mid-market accounts using defined ICP criteria, outbound sequences, and inbound follow-up to build a healthy pipeline of sales opportunities.
column15: This task mixes repeatable workflow (sequencing, routing, enrichment, prioritization) with high-judgment selling (qualification calls, strategy, compliance). Traditional AI is best for deterministic ICP checks, scoring, and data hygiene on structured CRM/enrichment data. Generative AI accelerates creation of personalized outreach, call prep, and summarization, but must be grounded in approved content to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step sequences and inbound follow-up across tools, adapting cadence based on engagement signals; however, autonomy must be bounded with guardrails and approvals. Humans remain essential for ICP/territory strategy, nuanced qualification, and final go/no-go decisions that affect pipeline integrity and forecast accountability. Assumptions: access to CRM/SEP, enrichment providers, call recording, and a maintained enablement knowledge base; uncertainty: data quality and regulatory constraints vary by segment and region.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) setting and revising ICP/qualification policy and resolving conflicts between pipeline volume and quality; (2) approving outreach in regulated industries, to strategic accounts, or when messaging includes claims (ROI, security, compliance) that require verification; (3) handling ambiguous or politically complex buying committees, negotiating next steps, and interpreting soft signals (tone, urgency, internal alignment); (4) deciding SQL/opportunity creation and forecast implications; (5) exception handling when AI flags anomalies (possible spam traps, do-not-contact, mismatched account hierarchy, suspected hallucinated facts).
column17: Over-automation may spam prospects, violate consent/opt-out rules, or contact wrong personas; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals (optimize meetings at expense of quality); opaque decisioning and difficult rollback; tool misfires (wrong sequence, wrong timing). Mitigations: rate limits, compliance gates, sandbox testing, explicit stop conditions, audit logs, approval workflows for new sequences, and conservative autonomy (recommend/queue vs. auto-send) until proven.
column18: Hallucinated firmographic facts or fabricated trigger events can damage credibility; tone/brand drift across segments; leakage of sensitive prospect data if prompts/logs are mishandled; biased language or non-compliant claims. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, strict templates/claim libraries, PII redaction, human approval for high-risk sends, and automated fact-checking against CRM/enrichment.
column19: Brittle ICP rules become outdated as product/market shifts; scoring models can encode historical bias (over-prioritizing certain industries) and miss new segments; false positives/negatives from noisy CRM data. Mitigations: scheduled rule/model reviews, monitoring drift, explainability, and data quality SLAs with dedupe/validation.
column20: Inconsistent source-of-truth across CRM/SEP/enrichment leads to wrong personalization or routing; feedback loops where GenAI content affects engagement metrics that drive agentic decisions; permissioning gaps expose data; latency causes missed inbound SLAs. Mitigations: unified identity/account matching, event-driven architecture with retries, role-based access control, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and end-to-end monitoring with audit trails.
column22: Orchestrate multi-channel outbound sequences and inbound SLA follow-up. Autonomous lead routing, enrichment, and next-best-action planning|Personalized messaging and talk-track generation for ICP segments. Summarize inbound context and discovery notes into qualification insights. Objection handling and competitive positioning suggestions|Define/adjust ICP, qualification thresholds, and territory/account strategy. Conduct high-stakes qualification conversations and stakeholder mapping. Approve sensitive outreach and ensure compliance/brand alignment. Final qualification decision and opportunity creation/forecast impact|ICP scoring and lead prioritization using rules/ML. Data hygiene, deduplication, and contact/account matching. Sequence performance analytics and alerting
column23: Coordinates steps across CRM/SEP (e.g., Salesforce + Outreach/Salesloft), queues tasks, adapts cadence based on engagement signals (opens/replies/meetings booked), and enforces SLAs for inbound leads; escalates exceptions to the AE. Pulls leads from forms/intent sources, triggers enrichment (firmographics/technographics), checks ICP fit, and proposes next actions (call/email/LinkedIn) with timing; can pause/stop sequences when risk/compliance flags appear.|Drafts emails/LinkedIn messages/call scripts tailored to persona, industry, and trigger events using approved value props and case studies; produces variants for A/B tests while adhering to brand and compliance guardrails. Condenses website activity, prior threads, meeting notes, and call transcripts into concise briefs; extracts pain points, stakeholders, and suggested qualification questions aligned to MEDDICC/BANT. Generates recommended responses and battlecard snippets based on known competitor narratives and internal enablement content; flags when claims require verification.|Requires market judgment, alignment with leadership, and nuanced trade-offs (pipeline coverage vs. focus); humans validate that ICP reflects current product/packaging and competitive reality. Humans build trust, read nuance, handle ambiguity, and navigate politics; critical for validating pain, urgency, budget, and decision process beyond what signals/data show. Human review needed for regulated industries, edge-case claims, pricing/contract statements, and to prevent reputational damage or policy violations. AE must decide whether to convert to SQL/opportunity, set next steps, and commit to forecast; accountability and judgment remain human-led.|Applies deterministic rules (employee count, region, industry exclusions) plus lightweight predictive scoring (propensity-to-meet) to rank accounts/leads; outputs explainable reasons for score. Uses fuzzy matching and validation to merge duplicates, standardize fields, and ensure correct account hierarchies-critical for accurate sequencing and reporting. Computes deliverability, reply rates, step conversion, and cohort performance; triggers alerts when metrics deviate (e.g., spam risk, low reply rate) and recommends experiments.
column43: Mid-Market Sales Pipeline and Renewal Insights

column8: Run discovery calls to understand customer needs, map stakeholders, and define success criteria, then translate findings into a clear solution approach and next steps.
column15: The core of discovery-live conversation, trust-building, and strategic judgment-remains human-led, especially for a Senior Mid-Market AE where deal outcomes depend on nuanced qualification and stakeholder management. Generative AI is well-suited to accelerate preparation (question/talk-track tailoring) and convert unstructured call content into structured artifacts (summaries, success criteria drafts, recap emails), but requires review due to hallucination and context drift. Agentic AI can reliably orchestrate the surrounding workflow (research aggregation, scheduling, task creation, follow-up execution) when bounded by guardrails and approval steps; fully autonomous customer interaction is not mature or acceptable for most sales contexts. Traditional AI contributes best on structured elements: CRM field validation, deduplication/entity resolution, stakeholder graphing from metadata, and predictive scoring/alerts. Assumptions: access to CRM/calendar/email/call recording tools with appropriate permissions; organization allows AI-assisted transcription and drafting; mid-market deals have moderate complexity but still require human negotiation and tailoring. Edge cases (regulated industries, sensitive security discussions, multi-threaded buying committees) increase required human share.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) conducting the live discovery call and adapting in real time to tone, objections, and politics; (2) validating AI-generated summaries, stakeholder inferences, and success metrics before they become commitments; (3) deciding qualification (go/no-go), pricing/packaging positioning, and any concessions; (4) ensuring legal/compliance requirements-recording consent, privacy constraints, and accurate product claims-are met; (5) handling high-stakes or ambiguous situations (conflicting stakeholder goals, competitive displacement, security/compliance objections) where AI confidence is low. Mandatory approval gates should exist before any external email is sent, before CRM stage changes that trigger downstream processes, and before commitments on timelines, integrations, or custom work are communicated.
column17: Autonomous actions (scheduling, emailing, task creation) can create customer friction if it contacts the wrong person, sends incorrect agendas, or over-automates follow-ups; emergent behavior from poorly scoped goals could leak sensitive info across accounts. Mitigations: strict permissioning, sandboxed tools, allow-list actions, confidence thresholds, human-in-the-loop approvals for external communications, and audit logs.
column18: May hallucinate requirements, misattribute statements, or overstate product capabilities in recaps; can miss subtle cues (sarcasm, hesitation) and may introduce biased or overly generic discovery questions. Mitigations: use retrieval from approved product/contract sources, enforce citation/quote linking to transcript, require human review for customer-facing outputs, and maintain persona/tone guidelines.
column19: Brittle rules can misclassify discovery elements or enforce the wrong CRM requirements; predictive scores can be misleading if trained on biased historical data or if market conditions shift. Mitigations: periodic model/rule reviews, monitoring for drift, explainability on key drivers, and avoid using scores as sole decision criteria.
column20: Data quality and identity resolution issues can propagate across systems (wrong stakeholder map, incorrect account context). Toolchain failures (transcription errors, CRM sync delays) can cause inconsistent artifacts. Privacy/compliance risks arise when combining call recordings with CRM/email data. Mitigations: master data management, robust deduplication, end-to-end audit trails, consent tracking, least-privilege access, and clear ownership for final artifacts.
column22: Pre-call research orchestration and briefing pack creation. Workflow coordination for discovery process. Post-call next-step execution (bounded)|Discovery question generation and talk-track tailoring. Real-time note-taking, summarization, and insight extraction. Drafting stakeholder map narrative and success criteria document. Drafting customer-facing recap and internal handoff materials|Lead the live discovery conversation and build trust/rapport. Interpret ambiguous needs and align on problem framing. Make strategic decisions on qualification, positioning, and concessions. Compliance, privacy, and customer consent management|Structured data capture and field validation in CRM. Stakeholder identification and relationship graphing from structured signals. Scoring and alerts for deal risk and next-best-action prompts
column23: Autonomously pulls and synthesizes account signals from CRM, prior emails/calls, product usage (if available), firmographics, news, and intent data; drafts a call plan (hypotheses, risks, tailored agenda) and updates it as new info arrives. Manages multi-step workflow: schedules calls, sends agenda, assigns internal SMEs, creates tasks for follow-ups, and ensures artifacts (notes, stakeholder map, success criteria) are completed; escalates to human when confidence is low or deal risk is high. After human approval, triggers agreed actions: send recap email, create CRM opportunities/fields, open solution engineering requests, and schedule next meetings; uses guardrails and approval gates for external communications.|Generates role- and industry-specific question banks, objection-handling prompts, and value hypothesis statements aligned to the prospect's context; adapts language to mid-market buyer personas. Transcribes calls (via integrated tools), summarizes key pains, desired outcomes, constraints, and buying signals; extracts action items and open questions; flags inconsistencies for human review. Turns raw notes into structured artifacts: stakeholder roles/influence, decision process, success metrics, timeline, risks, and assumptions; produces a clear solution approach draft and mutual action plan. Creates recap email, meeting minutes, and internal summaries for SE/CS/Legal; maintains consistent tone and includes agreed next steps and owners, pending human approval.|Requires nuanced interpersonal skills, empathy, adaptive questioning, and credibility; humans handle sensitive moments, negotiate access to stakeholders, and manage power dynamics. Humans reconcile conflicting stakeholder inputs, read between the lines, and decide what is truly driving urgency; avoids over-reliance on imperfect AI inferences. Humans decide whether to pursue, how to position value, what to commit to, and when to involve executives/SEs; accountable for revenue outcomes. Humans ensure recording consent, data handling, and representations are compliant; approve any external-facing summaries and commitments.|Uses rules/ML classifiers to tag topics (security, pricing, integrations), enforce required fields, detect missing MEDDICC elements, and validate data formats (dates, stages, contacts). Applies entity resolution and graph algorithms to link contacts across systems, deduplicate stakeholders, and infer reporting lines or influence based on interaction metadata (email/calendar/CRM). Predictive models score likelihood-to-close, churn/competition risk, and stage progression; triggers alerts when discovery gaps exist (e.g., no economic buyer identified).

column8: Develop and deliver tailored product demonstrations and presentations that align capabilities to customer requirements, addressing objections and confirming value throughout the cycle.
column15: The core value of the task-persuasive, trust-building delivery and real-time objection handling-remains human-led due to high stakes, relationship dynamics, and accountability for commitments. Generative AI is strong for producing tailored narratives, slides, scripts, and rehearsal scenarios quickly, but must be grounded in approved messaging to avoid hallucinated claims. Agentic AI adds leverage by orchestrating prep workflows (pulling CRM context, assembling assets, scheduling dry-runs) and keeping a living demo plan updated across the sales cycle; however, autonomy should be bounded with approval gates. Traditional AI is best for deterministic requirement-to-capability matching via controlled taxonomies and for analytics on engagement signals; it is less suited to nuanced persuasion. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/discovery notes, an approved messaging/claims library, and governance that restricts AI from making unapproved roadmap/pricing promises. Edge cases (regulated industries, security/compliance reviews, competitive bake-offs) increase required human share and reduce autonomous actions.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) deciding the demo strategy (what to emphasize, what to defer, and how to navigate internal politics), (2) live delivery and improvisation based on stakeholder reactions, (3) approving any statements about pricing, contractual terms, SLAs, security posture, and product roadmap, and (4) handling ethical boundaries (avoiding deceptive comparisons or overpromising). Mandatory human checkpoints should include: final review of slides/talk track for factual accuracy; approval of any competitive claims; confirmation that demo environment reflects current GA functionality; and sign-off before any agentic system sends customer-facing materials or schedules meetings. In edge cases-public sector, healthcare/finance, or when a customer requests written commitments-human legal/compliance review is required.
column17: Autonomous actions (scheduling, asset sharing, updating CRM) can create customer friction or compliance issues if wrong stakeholders/assets are used; may propagate incorrect context from CRM; emergent behavior in multi-step workflows can bypass intended controls. Mitigations: least-privilege access, explicit approval gates for customer-facing actions, audit logs, sandboxed execution, policy constraints (no sending without approval), and monitoring/rollback.
column18: May hallucinate features, integrations, certifications, or roadmap timelines; may produce overly confident language that becomes an implicit commitment; tone/persona mismatch can reduce trust; risk of leaking sensitive customer data if prompts include confidential info. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, claim-checking against a controlled knowledge base, red-team prompts, PII/secret scrubbing, and mandatory human approval for external-facing artifacts.
column19: Brittle taxonomies and rules can mis-map requirements to features, especially for novel use cases; models trained on historical wins may encode bias (e.g., deprioritizing certain industries); analytics may overfit and mis-score small-sample mid-market deals. Mitigations: periodic taxonomy governance, human-in-the-loop corrections, explainability, and calibration with holdout evaluation.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs. Traditional AI capability mapping) can create inconsistent messaging; data quality issues in CRM can cascade through agentic workflows; security risks from connecting multiple systems (CRM, email, enablement, demo environments). Mitigations: single source of truth for approved claims, schema validation, provenance tracking for generated content, centralized policy engine, and staged rollout with A/B testing and incident response procedures.
column22: Orchestrate demo preparation workflow across systems (CRM, calendar, enablement assets). Maintain a living demo plan and objection log per opportunity|Draft tailored demo narrative, agenda, and talk track aligned to customer pains and desired outcomes. Create presentation materials and demo scripts (slides, speaker notes, FAQs). Simulate Q&A and objection scenarios for rehearsal|Conduct discovery validation and choose the strategic demo angle. Deliver the live demo/presentation and handle high-stakes objections in real time. Approve claims, pricing/roadmap statements, and compliance-sensitive content|Match customer requirements to product capabilities using structured tagging and retrieval. Analyze engagement and demo effectiveness signals
column23: Plans and executes a multi-step prep checklist: pulls opportunity context from CRM, identifies stakeholders and stage, schedules internal dry-run, assembles relevant assets, and tracks completion; adapts steps based on deal stage and customer persona with human approval gates. Continuously updates a structured plan (agenda, success criteria, mapped requirements, risks, next steps) by monitoring new emails/notes/call transcripts; prompts AE when new objections appear and suggests follow-ups.|Generates a persona- and industry-specific storyline, opening, transitions, and value framing using provided discovery notes, ICP playbooks, and product messaging; produces multiple variants for different stakeholders (economic buyer, champion, IT). Produces slide outlines, speaker notes, and concise feature-to-benefit explanations; drafts FAQ and objection-handling responses; rewrites for tone, brevity, and clarity while keeping claims constrained to approved sources. Role-plays as different buyer personas to stress-test messaging, surface gaps, and generate rebuttals and clarifying questions; helps AE practice concise responses and confirm value.|Interprets nuanced business context, politics, and buying dynamics; decides what to show/avoid, sets success criteria, and ensures alignment with mutual action plan. Requires credibility, rapport, improvisation, and ethical judgment; adapts to verbal/non-verbal cues, negotiates tradeoffs, and avoids overpromising. Ensures accuracy, legal/compliance adherence, and alignment with company positioning; accountable for commitments made to the customer.|Uses rules/ML classifiers over a controlled taxonomy (requirements, features, integrations, compliance) to recommend relevant modules, case studies, and demo environments; provides deterministic traceability for why an asset was suggested. Applies analytics to meeting attendance, poll/Q&A counts, follow-up actions, and stage progression to score demo impact and predict risk; flags anomalies (e.g., low engagement from economic buyer).
column41: Tailored Product Demos and Value Presentations
column43: Customized Product Demos and Value Messaging

column8: Create proposals and negotiate commercial terms, coordinating with legal and finance as needed to close new business and expansion deals within governance guidelines.
column15: This task mixes content creation, structured governance, and high-stakes judgment. Generative AI is strong for drafting proposals, summarizing redlines, and producing term-sheet variants, but must be grounded in approved templates and deal facts to avoid hallucinations. Traditional AI/CPQ is best for deterministic pricing, margin math, and governance checks where rules and thresholds are explicit and auditable. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the multi-party workflow (AE, legal, finance, deal desk) and managing iterations, but should not autonomously commit the company to terms. Humans remain essential for negotiation strategy, relationship management, and approving exceptions. Assumptions: the org has a CPQ/CRM, a clause library/playbooks, and defined approval matrices; AI has access to sanitized deal data and approved templates; external communications require human approval for non-standard/high-value deals.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) final commercial commitments (pricing, liability caps, indemnities, SLAs) and any deviation from standard paper; (2) exception approvals per governance (discounts beyond thresholds, unusual payment terms, bespoke clauses); (3) live negotiation and relationship management where intent, tone, and concessions must be calibrated; (4) validating factual inputs (scope, deliverables, timelines) and ensuring promises are feasible; (5) edge cases: regulated customers, cross-border data/privacy terms, public sector procurement, channel/partner deals, and situations where AI outputs conflict with policy or stakeholder guidance.
column17: Autonomous workflow actions could route wrong documents, miss critical approvals, or over-escalate/under-escalate; emergent behavior could spam stakeholders or create conflicting versions. Mitigations: constrained action space, approval gates, audit logs, role-based permissions, simulation/testing, and kill-switches.
column18: May hallucinate deal facts, misstate pricing/discounts, or generate non-compliant clause language; could leak sensitive customer or pricing data if prompts/logs are mishandled; tone or commitments may be inappropriate. Mitigations: retrieval from approved sources (RAG), template locking, redline-to-playbook grounding, PII controls, and mandatory human review before sending externally.
column19: Rules can become outdated (policy changes), leading to false approvals/blocks; models may encode historical bias (e.g., risk scoring penalizing certain segments) and can be brittle with novel deal structures. Mitigations: governance for rule updates, monitoring/QA, explainability, and periodic recalibration with human review.
column20: Data mismatches between CRM/CPQ/CLM can cause inconsistent terms; unclear source of truth can lead to sending wrong versions; compounded errors if GenAI drafts from stale data and Agentic AI propagates it. Mitigations: single source of truth, version control, schema validation, end-to-end audit trails, and staged rollouts with human-in-the-loop checkpoints.
column22: Orchestrate deal desk workflow and stakeholder coordination. Automate iterative redline cycle management (process, not decisions)|Draft proposal narrative and value justification aligned to customer context. Generate first-pass commercial term sheets and negotiation playbook suggestions. Summarize and explain contract language/redlines for non-legal stakeholders|Set negotiation strategy and make trade-off decisions. Approve exceptions and ensure ethical/compliant commitments. Conduct live negotiations and manage stakeholder relationships|Governance and policy compliance checks (discounts, approvals, clause deviations). Pricing/quote calculations and margin guardrails. Deal risk scoring and anomaly detection
column23: Monitors deal stage in CRM, triggers tasks (legal review, finance approval), routes the right template/MSA/SOW, schedules reminders, and tracks SLA/approvals; adapts next steps based on deal attributes (region, product, discount level) while keeping humans in the loop for approvals. Coordinates version control, compares redlines, highlights deltas, requests clarifications from counterparties, and prepares a consolidated issues list for the AE/legal; can propose next actions (e.g., escalate indemnity clause) but does not finalize positions.|Generates tailored executive summary, scope narrative, implementation approach, and ROI/value messaging using approved product positioning and customer notes; produces multiple variants for different stakeholders (CFO/IT/Procurement). Creates term sheet drafts (pricing structure, payment terms, renewal, usage assumptions) and suggests negotiation levers/counters based on internal playbooks and prior deal patterns; flags missing inputs and inconsistencies. Produces plain-language summaries of key clauses (liability, DPA, termination), extracts obligations, and drafts questions for legal/finance; helps AE communicate implications to the customer.|Requires judgment about relationship, competitive dynamics, concessions, and long-term account strategy; accountable for final positions and escalation choices. Humans (AE leadership/legal/finance) must approve non-standard terms, pricing exceptions, and representations to the customer; ensures commitments match actual deliverability and regulatory obligations. High-stakes persuasion, reading counterpart signals, and trust-building are not reliably automated; humans handle sensitive conversations and final agreement alignment.|Applies deterministic rules/thresholds to validate discount bands, required approvers, non-standard terms, and required attachments (DPA, security addendum); generates pass/fail and required next steps. Uses CPQ logic and forecasting models to compute pricing, proration, ramp deals, multi-year uplifts, and margin impact; flags out-of-bounds configurations and suggests compliant alternatives. Scores deals for risk (unusual payment terms, high discount, non-standard liability) using structured features; alerts AE/deal desk to prioritize review.

column8: Collaborate on cross-functional projects with marketing, product, and customer success to improve messaging, refine processes, and support smooth handoffs after close.
column15: This AE task blends coordination, content refinement, and operational rigor across marketing/product/CS. Generative AI is strong for synthesizing qualitative inputs and drafting messaging/process artifacts, but needs human validation for accuracy, tone, and compliance. Agentic AI can reliably run the project 'operating system' (scheduling, task chasing, playbook triggers) and keep handoffs moving, yet should not autonomously decide priorities or customer commitments. Traditional AI is best for structured measurement (dashboards, anomaly detection) and deterministic governance (CRM validation). Humans remain essential for cross-functional negotiation, final decisions, and relationship management. Assumptions: the org has reasonably instrumented CRM/CS tools, meeting transcripts/call notes are available, and integrations/permissions are configured; without these, AI leverage drops and human work increases.
column16: Human oversight is required for: (1) prioritization and trade-offs (e.g., whether to change messaging vs. adjust qualification vs. request product changes); (2) approvals for any externally-facing messaging, pricing/claims, competitive statements, and regulated-industry language; (3) exception handling in handoffs (non-standard contracts, security/legal constraints, escalations, at-risk implementations); (4) stakeholder management-resolving conflict between teams, aligning incentives, and setting expectations with customers/executives; (5) verifying AI outputs against source-of-truth systems to prevent propagation of incorrect assumptions. Edge cases: sparse or contradictory feedback, new product launches with limited historical data, strategic accounts where a 'standard playbook' is inappropriate, and regions with different compliance requirements.
column17: Over-automation could spam stakeholders, create/close tickets incorrectly, or trigger handoffs prematurely; autonomy may obscure why actions were taken; permission misconfiguration could expose data across teams. Mitigations: constrained action space (allowlists), approval for high-impact actions, rate limits, audit logs, simulation/testing in sandbox, and clear escalation policies.
column18: May hallucinate product capabilities, misquote customer feedback, or drift from approved positioning; can introduce biased or non-compliant language; may leak sensitive customer/contract data if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigations: retrieval with citations, approved messaging guardrails, redaction/DLP, human approval gates, and model/prompt evaluations on known scenarios.
column19: Rules/metrics can become stale as processes/products change; may optimize for measurable proxies (speed) over outcomes (customer success); brittle validation can block legitimate exceptions. Mitigations: periodic rule reviews, exception workflows, monitoring for metric gaming, and governance ownership (RevOps/CS Ops).

column8: Prepare and present weekly pipeline, forecast, and performance reports for leadership, highlighting risks, mitigation plans, and actions to achieve targets.
column15: Weekly reporting combines structured computation with high-stakes judgment and communication. Traditional AI is best for deterministic rollups, variance analysis, and data-quality/anomaly checks on CRM data. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the end-to-end workflow (scheduled pulls, refreshes, validations, assembling packs) and monitoring material pipeline movements between reporting cycles. Generative AI is strongest for converting metrics and deal notes into leadership-ready narratives, risk/mitigation write-ups, and presentation artifacts. However, forecast ownership, contextual overrides, and live leadership interaction remain human-led due to accountability, nuanced deal reality, and compliance considerations. Assumptions: access to CRM/BI APIs, standardized opportunity stages/fields, and a defined forecast methodology (commit/best-case). Uncertainty: maturity of agentic integrations and data cleanliness in test_dhina's stack; higher noise increases human effort.
column16: Humans must (1) approve final forecast numbers and any overrides to model outputs; (2) validate sensitive deal-level claims (e.g., customer intent, competitive position) and correct AI misinterpretations; (3) decide mitigation actions that require trade-offs (discounting, resource allocation, exec escalation) and manage stakeholder relationships; (4) ensure audience-appropriate disclosure (pricing, legal terms, customer PII) and comply with internal policies; (5) handle edge cases such as atypical deal structures, one-off renewals/expansions, channel conflicts, or sudden macro/customer events where historical patterns fail.
column17: Autonomous workflow actions can propagate bad data (refreshing reports from incorrect snapshots), spam stakeholders with alerts, or trigger unintended changes if write-access exists. Opaqueness in decision logic and brittle integrations can cause silent failures. Mitigations: least-privilege (read-only by default), approval gates for outbound actions, audit logs, monitoring/rollback, and clear runbooks.
column18: May hallucinate drivers or overstate certainty; can mis-summarize deal notes, omit critical caveats, or produce misleading narratives. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, citation/traceability, redaction, constrained templates, and mandatory human review for deal-level statements.
column19: Forecast models can be biased by historical periods, fail under regime change, or be brittle to stage-definition drift; rule-based checks may create false positives/negatives. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, feature/definition governance, explainability dashboards, and threshold tuning with feedback loops.
column20: Data lineage mismatches between CRM, BI, and spreadsheets can yield inconsistent numbers; conflicting outputs (model vs narrative) can erode trust. Mitigations: single source of truth, metric definitions catalog, automated reconciliation tests, versioned reporting packs, and clear ownership for final numbers.

column8: Manage end-to-end sales operations workflows for the mid-market segment, ensuring accurate order processing, renewals, and contract administration with clear ownership and timely issue resolution.
column15: The task mixes high-volume, structured operations (order/renewal processing) with policy-driven controls and occasional high-stakes exceptions. Traditional AI is best for deterministic validations, anomaly detection, and explainable scoring on structured CRM/CPQ/ERP data. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-system workflows, enforcing SLAs, and coordinating follow-ups/escalations-work that is procedural but multi-step and context-dependent. Generative AI accelerates unstructured work: drafting communications, summarizing contracts/tickets, and producing SOP artifacts, but must be grounded to avoid hallucinations. Humans remain essential for approvals, negotiation-sensitive escalations, and governance. Assumptions: systems (CRM/CPQ/ERP/CLM) have APIs, clear process guardrails exist, and the organization accepts human-in-the-loop controls for nonstandard cases. Edge cases (complex legal redlines, disputed invoices, multi-entity billing) increase human share and reduce automation.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) any deviation from standard pricing/terms (discount exceptions, bespoke clauses, nonstandard payment terms), (2) final approval gates tied to SOX/revenue recognition controls and signature authority, (3) ambiguous or conflicting data where source-of-truth is unclear (CRM vs ERP), (4) customer escalations involving relationship risk, churn risk, or reputational impact, and (5) legal/compliance interpretations (privacy, data residency, indemnity) where errors carry material risk. Humans should also review AI-proposed resolutions when confidence is low, when anomalies are detected, or when actions would trigger external communications or contractual commitments.
column17: Over-automation could execute incorrect actions (e.g., creating orders with wrong entities, triggering renewals prematurely), cascading errors across systems; opaque decision paths and emergent behaviors in exception handling. Mitigations: hard guardrails, least-privilege access, simulation/sandbox runs, step-level audit logs, human approval for high-impact actions, and circuit breakers on anomaly signals.
column18: Hallucination or incorrect extraction of contract terms; tone/policy drift in customer communications; leakage of sensitive customer/contract data if prompts are not governed. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from approved sources, strict templates, redaction/DLP, approval workflows for external messages, and evaluation on contract-summary accuracy.
column19: Brittle rules can break with new SKUs/process changes; ML risk scores may encode historical bias (e.g., deprioritizing certain segments) or degrade with drift; false positives create operational noise. column20: Inconsistent data definitions across CRM/CPQ/ERP/CLM can cause conflicting recommendations; orchestration failures (API downtime) can leave workflows half-complete; compounded errors when GenAI summaries feed agent actions. Mitigations: master data management, idempotent workflows, reconciliation jobs, confidence/verification checks before action, and clear RACI/ownership with escalation paths.

column17: Over-automation may route/approve incorrectly if permissions or policies are misconfigured; autonomous actions across systems can create cascading errors (e.g., closing stages without approvals); limited transparency in decision paths. column19: Rules can become outdated as policies change; brittle logic may generate false positives/negatives; anomaly models may encode bias (e.g., flagging certain regions/teams disproportionately). Mitigations: policy-change management, versioned rules, periodic calibration, explainability, and fairness reviews.
column20: Inconsistent data across CRM/CPQ/e-sign can cause mismatched evidence; identity/role mapping errors can break segregation-of-duties; conflicting outputs between rule engine and GenAI summaries can confuse auditors. Mitigations: master data governance, unified identity/SSO, reconciliation checks, single source of truth for control status, and end-to-end audit trails.

Generative AI accelerates summarization, drafting, and policy Q&A, reducing coordination overhead. Humans should also periodically audit AI-triggered escalations to prevent alert fatigue and ensure fairness/consistency across vendors.
column17: Autonomous actions (opening tickets, sending escalations) could over-escalate, spam vendors, or miss context (e.g., planned maintenance). Emergent behavior and tool errors can cause cascading workflow issues. Mitigations: guardrails (approval gates, rate limits), clear playbooks, simulation/testing, least-privilege tool access, and rollback mechanisms.
column18: May hallucinate SLA clauses, misstate metrics, or produce overly aggressive/soft tone in escalations; could leak confidential pricing/terms if prompts include sensitive data. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved contract/KPI sources, strict templates, redaction, and mandatory human approval for high-stakes outputs; logging and access controls.
column19: Brittle rules or biased scoring weights can misclassify vendor performance; data quality issues (missing timestamps, inconsistent severity) can yield incorrect compliance calculations. Mitigations: data validation, periodic recalibration of thresholds/weights, explainable scoring, and exception handling with human review.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs. Traditional AI metrics), identity/access mismatches across systems, and poor provenance can undermine trust. Mitigations: single source of truth for metrics, provenance links in summaries, idempotent workflows, unified vendor master data, and end-to-end monitoring/audit trails.

Coordinate QBR preparation and action-plan tracking|Summarize vendor performance and incident narratives for stakeholders. Draft vendor communications (requests, escalations, corrective action, renewals). Extract and interpret contract/SLA language into plain-English obligations|Set vendor strategy, success criteria, and negotiation positions. Handle high-stakes escalations and relationship management. Approve contractual interpretations, penalties, and compliance actions. Decide on vendor renewal/termination and remediation acceptance|KPI/SLA compliance calculation and alerting. Anomaly detection on vendor performance metrics. Vendor scorecarding and trend reporting
column23: Connects to CRM, ticketing, telephony/enablement tools, and finance systems; schedules data pulls, checks KPI/SLA dashboards, and triggers next actions (e.g., request RCA, open internal task) based on thresholds with minimal human prompting. When incidents breach severity/SLA thresholds, creates/updates tickets, notifies vendor contacts and internal stakeholders, schedules follow-ups, and tracks commitments to closure; adapts cadence based on vendor responsiveness and business impact. Builds agendas from prior issues, pulls open actions, assigns owners, and monitors completion; proposes priorities based on impact and recurrence patterns, while leaving final decisions to the manager.|Drafts concise weekly/monthly summaries from KPI trends, ticket histories, and meeting notes; highlights drivers, risks, and recommended talking points tailored to Sales Ops leadership vs. frontline managers. Generates emails/letters using approved templates and context (issue description, SLA clause references, desired remediation); maintains professional tone and consistency, with human review for high-stakes messages. Parses contract sections to produce obligation checklists (response times, credits, reporting duties) and maps them to measurable KPIs; flags ambiguous clauses for legal/procurement review.|Requires business judgment about trade-offs (cost vs. service), prioritization aligned to revenue goals, and negotiation tactics; accountability cannot be delegated to AI. Sensitive conversations (executive-to-executive), conflict resolution, and trust-building require empathy, context, and authority; humans manage reputational and partnership risks. Legal/procurement and the sales manager must validate interpretations, approve credits/penalties, and ensure actions comply with policy/regulation and do not create unintended liabilities. Final decisions involve risk tolerance, switching costs, and organizational constraints; humans must weigh qualitative factors and sign off.|Deterministically computes uptime, response/resolve times, backlog aging, and penalty/credit eligibility from structured logs; triggers alerts when thresholds are crossed; provides auditable calculations. Uses statistical/ML models to detect unusual spikes in incident volume, latency, or cost-to-serve; reduces noise vs. simple thresholds and surfaces early warning signals. Applies weighted scoring rules to produce standardized scorecards across vendors; supports benchmarking and quarter-over-quarter comparisons with consistent methodology.

column8: Analyze sales and operational data to identify trends, anomalies, and root causes, then translate findings into actionable recommendations for leadership and frontline teams.
column15: For a Mid-Market Sales Manager, the heavy lift is reliable data prep, KPI computation, and statistical detection of trends/anomalies-best handled by Traditional AI/BI automation (0.30). Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-system data refreshes and running iterative investigative loops that would otherwise consume analyst time, but must be constrained due to access, compliance, and error propagation risks (0.20). Generative AI is strongest at translating outputs into leadership/frontline-ready narratives and drafting recommendations and scenarios, but should not be the source of truth for numbers (0.20). Humans remain essential to set the right questions, interpret findings with market/context knowledge, and approve/own actions that affect revenue, customers, and people (0.30). Assumptions: data access exists via governed connectors; metric definitions are documented; organization tolerates AI-drafted narratives with human review; sensitive actions require approval gates.
column16: Human oversight is required to (1) choose which KPIs and segments matter this quarter (strategy/OKRs), (2) adjudicate ambiguous root causes where external context dominates (competitor pricing, product incidents, rep turnover), (3) approve actions with legal/financial/people impact (pricing/discount policy, territory changes, comp implications), (4) validate anomalies before escalation to avoid false alarms, and (5) ensure compliance with data governance (PII, customer confidentiality) and prevent misuse of insights in performance management. Edge cases needing humans: sparse data for new products/regions, one-off enterprise deals skewing mid-market metrics, data pipeline outages, and conflicting metric definitions across teams.
column17: Autonomous workflows can propagate bad data (schema changes, partial refresh) into downstream analyses; may take unintended actions (creating noisy tickets, triggering playbooks) or exceed least-privilege access; mitigation: approval gates for actions, constrained tool permissions, audit logs, sandbox testing, and anomaly thresholds with rate limits.
column18: May hallucinate drivers or misstate metrics if not grounded in verified tables; can oversimplify uncertainty and present speculative causality as fact; privacy risk if prompts include PII/deal notes; mitigation: retrieval-grounded generation, numeric citation/links to dashboards, redaction, and mandatory human review for exec outputs.
column19: Models can be brittle under distribution shift (new GTM motion, pricing changes), produce biased segment insights (territory/rep comparisons), and miss qualitative drivers; mitigation: monitoring, periodic retraining, explainability checks, and pairing with qualitative validation.
column20: Mismatch between metric definitions across systems can cause inconsistent narratives; chaining outputs (Traditional->Agentic->GenAI) can amplify errors and create false confidence; workflow failures (API limits, auth expiry) can silently degrade; mitigation: single source of truth semantic layer, data contracts, end-to-end tests, confidence/lineage metadata passed to GenAI, and clear RACI for approvals.
column22: Orchestrate data pulls and refreshes across systems (CRM, ERP, BI, support, marketing). Iterative investigative analysis loop (hypothesis generation -> drill-down -> validation). Alerting and routing recommendations into workflows|Narrative synthesis and executive-ready storytelling. Insight explanation, scenario framing, and recommendation drafting. Ad-hoc natural-language querying and semantic search over documentation|Define business questions, success criteria, and decision thresholds. Validate root-cause plausibility with ground truth and context. Make and communicate high-stakes decisions; manage change|Data cleaning, validation, and metric computation. Trend detection, forecasting, and driver decomposition. Anomaly detection and segmentation analytics
column23: Plans and executes multi-step workflows (authenticate, query, join, schedule), monitors failures, and adapts to schema/API changes with guardrails; escalates when access/quality issues arise. Autonomously proposes hypotheses for anomalies (e.g., pipeline drop due to lead source change), runs successive queries/segmentations, and selects next-best analyses based on results, while logging rationale for review. Creates tickets/tasks for regional managers or ops (e.g., pricing approval bottleneck), attaches evidence, and tracks resolution SLAs; can trigger playbooks (enablement, process fixes) subject to approval.|Turns analytical outputs into clear narratives (what happened, why, impact, confidence), tailored to leadership vs frontline; drafts QBR slides, emails, and talking points. Generates actionable options (e.g., adjust territory coverage, tighten qualification), outlines pros/cons, assumptions, and expected KPI impact; helps translate technical findings into operational actions. Enables NL questions over metric definitions, past postmortems, enablement docs; summarizes relevant context to avoid misinterpretation of KPIs and processes.|Requires domain judgment to prioritize analyses (e.g., bookings vs margin), set materiality thresholds, and align to strategy and current quarter commitments. Humans confirm whether patterns reflect reality (field feedback, policy changes, competitor moves) and prevent spurious correlations from driving decisions. Leadership/manager approval needed for actions affecting pricing, comp, territories, customer commitments; humans handle stakeholder alignment, coaching, and accountability.|Deterministic rules/ETL and classical ML handle deduping, missing-value checks, currency normalization, cohorting, and standardized KPI calculations (conversion, cycle time, win rate). Time-series models and regression/causal proxy methods quantify trends and key drivers (seasonality, channel mix, rep capacity) with confidence intervals and explainable feature contributions. Statistical process control, isolation forest, and clustering flag outliers (sudden churn spike, discounting drift) and identify segments where issues concentrate (region, product, lead source).

column8: Drive process improvements through standardization and automation, documenting current and future state workflows and managing change adoption across the team.
column15: The task mixes socio-technical change leadership with documentation and automation. Generative AI is strong for drafting SOPs, future-state options, and enablement content, reducing time spent on writing and synthesis. Agentic AI can coordinate multi-step discovery and rollout operations (data pulls, ticketing, reminders, monitoring) but should operate within guardrails and approvals due to cross-system impact. Traditional AI/BI is best for deterministic KPI baselining and compliance/anomaly detection using structured CRM/CPQ data. Humans remain essential for prioritization, stakeholder alignment, exception handling, and leading adoption-areas requiring context, authority, and nuanced judgment. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/RevOps data, approved integrations, and a defined sales methodology; without these, AI contributions shift toward drafting and analysis rather than automation.
column16: Human oversight is required to (1) choose which processes to standardize first and set success metrics, (2) approve future-state designs that affect customer commitments, comp plans, or legal/privacy obligations, (3) validate that documentation reflects reality across regions/roles, (4) decide when to enforce vs allow exceptions (e.g., strategic accounts, atypical deal cycles), and (5) manage change adoption through coaching and accountability. Humans must approve any automation that changes CRM stages, routing, discount approvals, or customer-facing communications, and must handle escalations when KPIs worsen or reps report workflow friction.
column17: Autonomous actions across CRM/ticketing can create unintended workflow changes, spam users with nudges, or reinforce flawed metrics; transparency and auditability challenges. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for write actions, sandbox testing, rate limits, clear escalation rules, and audit logs.
column18: May hallucinate steps/policies, misrepresent current practices, or produce overly generic workflows; risk of leaking sensitive deal/customer data if prompts include PII. Mitigations: use retrieval from approved internal sources, redaction, human review, and version control; constrain outputs to templates and cite sources.
column19: Rules/ML can be brittle when processes change, leading to false positives/negatives in compliance flags; biased metrics if data quality is poor (missing fields, inconsistent stage definitions). Mitigations: data governance, periodic rule/model recalibration, monitoring for drift, and explainable thresholds.
column20: Conflicting definitions of stages/SLAs across tools can cause inconsistent dashboards and automation loops; identity/permission mismatches and data latency can trigger wrong actions. Mitigations: single source of truth for definitions, integration testing, event-driven architecture with idempotency, and cross-functional governance (Sales Ops/RevOps/IT/Security).
column22: Orchestrate process discovery workflow across systems (CRM, email, call logs, CPQ). Automate rollout tasks and governance (tickets, enablement nudges, KPI monitoring)|Draft current-state workflow documentation from interviews/notes. Design future-state process options and standard templates. Create change-management communications and training materials|Set improvement priorities and success criteria. Validate workflows with stakeholders and ensure policy/compliance alignment. Lead change adoption (coaching, handling resistance, accountability)|Baseline and track process performance metrics. Detect anomalies and non-compliance with standardized steps
column23: Connects to approved data sources/APIs, schedules data pulls, prompts stakeholders for missing inputs, and maintains a living backlog of process issues/opportunities; adapts the plan as new constraints emerge.
