Generates alternative process designs (e.g., lead handoff, qualification, forecasting cadence), standard operating templates, and automation ideas aligned to sales methodology and tooling constraints. Produces rollout emails, FAQs, enablement decks, micro-learning scripts, and objection-handling guidance tailored to AEs/SDRs/RevOps; localizes tone and terminology for the org.|Requires business judgment to balance revenue impact, rep capacity, customer experience, and strategic goals; resolves trade-offs and defines what 'good' looks like. Humans must confirm accuracy, feasibility, and alignment with legal/privacy, compensation, and customer commitments; handles political/organizational nuances. Adoption depends on trust, motivation, and situational leadership; humans address edge cases, negotiate exceptions, and reinforce behaviors.|Uses deterministic rules/BI models to compute cycle times, stage conversion, SLA adherence, forecast accuracy, and activity-to-outcome ratios; provides explainable dashboards. Applies rules/ML classification to flag missing CRM fields, skipped stages, unusual discounting, or outlier deal velocity; routes findings for review.

column8: Support period-close activities and recurring reporting, ensuring data accuracy, timely submissions, and reconciliation of key metrics used for forecasting and performance reviews.
column15: Period-close reporting is a repeatable, system-driven process with clear controls (good fit for Traditional AI validation/reconciliation) and many coordination steps (good fit for Agentic AI orchestration). Generative AI adds value in drafting narratives, communications, and documentation but cannot be trusted to originate factual numbers. Humans remain essential for final attestation, policy interpretation, and high-stakes judgment on adjustments. Assumptions: access to CRM (e.g., Salesforce), finance system, and BI layer; stable metric definitions; role has authority to coordinate cross-functional inputs. Constraints: data access/privacy, audit requirements, and varying regional processes reduce full automation feasibility.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) final sign-off/attestation of close numbers and forecast inputs; (2) approving manual adjustments, deal crediting, and timing decisions that affect compensation and external reporting; (3) resolving conflicts between systems of record (finance vs CRM) and interpreting policy exceptions; (4) reviewing GenAI-written narratives to prevent unsupported causal claims; (5) handling edge cases like mergers of accounts, mid-period territory changes, retroactive contract amendments, and late-arriving invoices. Humans should also approve any changes to validation rules, anomaly thresholds, and agent permissions.
column17: Autonomous actions (sending escalations, triggering refreshes, opening tickets) can create noise, mis-route requests, or act on stale data; permission overreach could expose or modify sensitive records; emergent loops (retries/escalations) may disrupt close. Mitigations: least-privilege access, action allowlists, rate limits, human-in-the-loop gates for external communications, and robust logging/rollback.
column18: May hallucinate drivers or misstate figures if not grounded in retrieved, approved data; can introduce confidentiality leaks if prompts include sensitive deal details; risk of persuasive but incorrect variance narratives. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from governed sources, numeric grounding (no free-form numbers), redaction, and mandatory human review for leadership/audit outputs.
column19: Rules can become brittle as definitions change; anomaly models may generate false positives/negatives, especially during seasonality or business changes; reconciliation depends on accurate mapping tables. Mitigations: version-controlled rule sets, periodic calibration, monitoring drift, and clear ownership for master data management.
column20: Mismatch of metric definitions across tools can cause AI components to optimize different 'truths'; pipeline from validation->agent actions->GenAI narratives can propagate errors if upstream checks fail; auditability may be lost if transformations aren't logged end-to-end. Mitigations: single semantic layer/metric store, end-to-end lineage, consistent IDs, staged approvals, and automated test suites for close workflows.
column22: Orchestrate period-close workflow (task checklist, dependencies, reminders, escalations). Automate data pulls and refreshes across systems. Coordinate reconciliation loop and evidence collection|Draft close summary narratives and variance explanations. Generate stakeholder communications (submission requests, clarifications). Create ad-hoc Q&A and documentation on metric definitions|Approve final numbers and sign-off on submissions. Resolve ambiguous discrepancies and make judgment calls. Interpret insights for forecasting and performance actions|Validate data quality with deterministic rules. Anomaly detection on key close metrics. Reconcile metrics across sources (CRM vs finance vs BI)
column23: Connects to CRM/ERP/BI tools and calendars to generate a close checklist, assign owners, monitor completion, send reminders, and escalate blockers based on SLA rules; adapts sequencing when upstream data arrives late. Runs scheduled/triggered jobs to extract required close-period datasets (pipeline, bookings, renewals, activities), refresh BI datasets, and log run status; retries on transient failures and notifies humans on hard errors. When discrepancies are detected, opens tickets, requests supporting evidence from owners, tracks responses, and compiles an audit trail package for review; proposes likely root causes based on patterns.|Turns metric deltas (MoM/QoQ, vs forecast, vs target) into readable commentary for leadership decks and performance reviews; suggests plausible drivers while clearly flagging uncertainty and requiring human confirmation. Creates tailored messages to regional managers/ops/finance requesting missing inputs, clarifying definitions, and summarizing required actions; maintains consistent tone and includes links to source reports. Answers questions like 'what counts as committed pipeline' by retrieving approved definitions and producing concise guidance; drafts SOP updates after process changes.|Accountable for accuracy and business impact; must validate exceptions, approve adjustments, and attest to compliance/audit requirements. Performs deterministic matching/aggregation (account/opportunity IDs, mapping tables) and computes reconciliation deltas; generates tie-out tables for auditability.

column8: Develop and maintain comprehensive process documentation and training materials, enabling consistent execution, onboarding support, and continuous knowledge transfer within the team.
column15: The task mixes high-volume content production and upkeep (well-suited to Generative AI) with ongoing coordination and lifecycle management (best handled by Agentic AI) and governance/measurement on structured signals (Traditional AI). However, the Mid-Market Sales Manager remains accountable for the canonical process, policy/compliance correctness, and change management. Assumptions: documentation lives in a central KB (e.g., Confluence/Notion/SharePoint), training in an LMS, and core process data in a CRM; integrations/APIs are available. Work split reflects that drafting/updating materials is substantial (GenAI), but approvals, nuance, and adoption leadership remain human-heavy; agentic orchestration reduces admin overhead but should not autonomously publish policy changes without review.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving any process/policy changes (stage definitions, discounting, legal language), (2) validating factual accuracy against current tooling and real rep workflows, (3) ensuring compliance with privacy, customer communications rules, and competitive/claims guidance, (4) resolving edge cases where process differs by segment/region or exceptions are allowed, and (5) leading change management-communicating rationale, coaching, and handling performance conversations. Mandatory human gates: publish/major revision approval, quarterly process review sign-off, and any externally-facing template updates.
column17: Over-automation could push incorrect updates, spam stakeholders with tasks, or change training assignments inappropriately; opaque decision logic for prioritization; permission creep when connected to CRM/LMS/KB. Mitigations: least-privilege access, human-in-the-loop approvals for publishing/assignments, audit logs, rate limits, sandbox testing, and clear rollback/versioning.
column18: Hallucinated steps, incorrect tool instructions, or fabricated policy rationale; tone that conflicts with brand/leadership; leakage of sensitive customer/comp data if prompts include raw CRM notes; copyright risk if it reproduces proprietary playbooks. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, redaction/DLP, style guides, SME review gates, and automated fact-check prompts against CRM fields/process maps.
column19: Brittle rules can mis-tag content, miss nuanced exceptions, or create false positives in stale/duplicate detection; dashboards may drive wrong conclusions if data quality is poor (e.g., incomplete CRM fields). Mitigations: periodic rule review, data validation checks, confidence thresholds, and pairing metrics with qualitative feedback.
column20: Inconsistent source-of-truth across CRM, KB, and LMS can cause conflicting guidance; identity/role mapping errors can assign wrong training; model outputs may not align with template enforcement rules; security risks from multiple connectors. Mitigations: define a single canonical process map, schema/version governance, staged environments, automated regression checks on templates, centralized access control, and incident response/rollback procedures.
column22: Continuously monitor source-of-truth systems for process changes and trigger doc updates. Orchestrate onboarding/training delivery and knowledge checks. Run periodic documentation audits and compliance checks|Draft and refresh SOPs, playbooks, and templates from inputs. Create training assets (slides, scripts, FAQs, scenario role-plays). Summarize SME interviews and deal/post-mortem learnings into reusable knowledge|Define/approve the canonical sales process and policy decisions. Validate accuracy, compliance, and contextual nuance of documentation/training. Facilitate change management and coaching|Template enforcement, metadata tagging, and version control rules. Search, retrieval, and duplicate/outdated content detection. Training analytics and KPI tracking for adoption
column23: Connects to CRM (e.g., Salesforce), enablement LMS, ticketing/Slack/Teams, and SOP repositories; detects changes (new fields, stage definitions, approval steps), opens update tasks, assigns owners, and proposes timelines; orchestrates review workflows with reminders/escalations. Builds role-based learning paths, schedules sessions, pushes micro-learning, administers quizzes, tracks completion, and nudges managers/new hires; adapts cadence based on performance signals (quiz scores, ramp metrics). Executes scheduled audits (broken links, outdated screenshots, missing owners, stale pages), compares docs against current process telemetry, and generates exception reports for human resolution.|Transforms meeting notes, process outlines, and CRM screenshots into structured SOPs (purpose, scope, steps, SLAs, RACI), call scripts, email templates, and checklists; rewrites for clarity and consistent tone. Generates slide outlines, facilitator guides, objection-handling scenarios, quizzes, and knowledge-base FAQs tailored to mid-market sales motions and tools; can localize/translate content if needed. Condenses recordings/transcripts into key takeaways, best practices, and 'what changed' notes; proposes updates to existing modules and highlights contradictions needing SME review.|Requires managerial judgment, alignment with leadership, and accountability for trade-offs (e.g., qualification criteria, discounting guardrails, legal/compliance constraints). Humans must verify that steps match real-world execution, edge cases (exceptions, escalations), and that guidance is compliant (pricing, claims, privacy) and culturally appropriate. Driving adoption needs leadership behaviors: communicating 'why', handling resistance, coaching reps, and reinforcing standards-areas where human credibility and empathy are essential.|Applies deterministic rules for required sections, naming conventions, ownership fields, review dates, and approval states; auto-tags docs by process area (prospecting, qualification, pricing, contracting). Uses keyword/semantic indexing plus heuristics (similarity thresholds, last-updated dates) to surface duplicates, stale pages, and missing cross-links; improves findability in the knowledge base. Computes dashboards for completion rates, time-to-productivity, stage conversion changes post-training, and correlation analyses using structured data; flags anomalies for investigation.

column8: Prospect and research mid-market target accounts using defined ICP criteria, intent signals, and firmographic data to build a prioritized outreach list for pipeline creation.
column15: The task mixes structured filtering/scoring (best handled by Traditional AI) with multi-step data gathering and continuous refresh (Agentic AI) and synthesis of unstructured research into usable briefs (Generative AI). However, ICP definition, exception handling (territory/account ownership, sensitive industries), and final accountability for who gets targeted remain human responsibilities-especially for an entry-level mid-market SDR where coaching and judgment are part of the workflow. Assumptions: access to reliable data sources/APIs, clear territory rules, and a CRM as system of record; without these, human effort increases and agentic automation should be constrained.
column16: Human oversight is essential to (1) set and periodically recalibrate ICP and scoring weights based on pipeline outcomes and GTM shifts; (2) approve outreach targeting for ambiguous entities (parent/subsidiary, rebrands), regulated/sensitive segments, and accounts with existing relationships or contractual constraints; (3) ensure compliance with privacy/consent rules (GDPR/CCPA), internal policies, and platform ToS for data sourcing; (4) spot-check AI-generated briefs for factual accuracy and relevance, especially when sources conflict or are outdated; (5) provide feedback/labels to correct systematic errors (bad merges, biased scoring). Edge cases: stealth startups with sparse data, international subsidiaries with shared domains, intent spikes driven by students/researchers, and companies with similar names/domains.
column17: Autonomous workflows can over-collect data, violate rate limits/ToS, or propagate errors at scale (e.g., wrong entity resolution leading to widespread mis-targeting). Emergent behavior risk if optimization targets volume over quality. Mitigations: strict guardrails (allowed sources/actions), approval gates for list changes, throttling, audit logs, sandbox testing, and KPI alignment to quality metrics (meeting rate, conversion) not just list size.
column18: May hallucinate company facts, misinterpret intent from noisy text, or produce overconfident rationales; can introduce bias in how it frames 'fit'. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with citations, constrained templates, confidence scoring, and mandatory links to sources; human spot checks on top-ranked accounts; avoid generating sensitive personal data.
column19: Rules/models can become stale as ICP evolves; weighted scoring may encode historical bias (over-prioritizing certain industries/regions) and miss novel good-fit accounts. Entity resolution errors can be hard to detect. Mitigations: scheduled recalibration, monitoring drift, explainable scoring, holdout evaluation against downstream outcomes, and human review of ambiguous merges.
column20: Data inconsistencies across CRM and enrichment tools can cause conflicting outputs; feedback loops may amplify errors (GenAI summaries based on wrong merged entities; agentic refresh overwriting human edits). Security/privacy risks when moving data between vendors. Mitigations: single source of truth, schema/versioning, human-locked fields, provenance tracking, role-based access, and automated tests for pipelines plus reconciliation reports.
column22: Orchestrate multi-source data collection for target accounts. Autonomously expand and refine the account universe within guardrails. Maintain a living prioritized list and trigger refreshes|Summarize account research into SDR-ready briefs. Normalize messy unstructured signals into structured tags. Draft hypotheses for ICP fit and outreach priority rationale|Define/adjust ICP, weighting, and disqualification rules. Review and approve final prioritized outreach list for edge cases. Resolve conflicting signals and provide feedback to improve systems|Apply deterministic ICP filtering and data validation. Score and rank accounts using weighted models. Deduplicate and entity-resolve accounts across systems
column23: Plans and executes a workflow that queries CRM, sales intelligence (e.g., ZoomInfo/Clearbit), intent providers (e.g., Bombora/G2), LinkedIn/company sites, and news sources; handles retries, deduping, and logging; adapts the sequence based on data availability and rate limits. Starting from seed lists/territory rules, iteratively discovers lookalike accounts, applies ICP constraints, and proposes additions/removals; flags uncertain matches for human review rather than auto-including. Monitors for changes (funding, hiring spikes, tech stack changes, intent surges), schedules periodic refresh, and updates rankings while preserving audit trails and rollback.|Generates concise account snapshots (what they do, likely pain points, relevant triggers, competitors, suggested angles) from gathered sources; produces citations/links to reduce hallucination impact. Extracts entities and signals from webpages/news/job posts (e.g., 'migrating to Salesforce', 'hiring RevOps', 'security compliance') and converts them into standardized fields for scoring. Produces a short, human-readable justification for why an account is prioritized (e.g., intent + firmographics + trigger), enabling faster human validation and coaching for entry-level SDRs.|Requires business judgment and alignment with Sales/Marketing strategy; humans set what 'good' looks like, manage tradeoffs, and update criteria when GTM priorities shift. Humans validate high-stakes or ambiguous accounts (regulated industries, subsidiaries, competitors, existing relationships), ensuring accuracy, compliance, and territory fairness. When intent/firmographics disagree or data is stale, humans decide inclusion and feed corrections back (labeling, rule updates) to reduce future errors.|Uses rules/SQL to filter by firmographics (employee count, revenue band, region, industry), exclude existing customers/duplicates, validate required fields, and enforce territory/account ownership constraints. Combines firmographic fit, intent scores, engagement, and recency into a transparent weighted score or classical ML ranking model; supports explainability and consistent prioritization. Performs fuzzy matching and entity resolution (domain, company name variants) to merge records and prevent double outreach; flags ambiguous merges for review.

column8: Execute multi-channel outbound outreach including email, phone, and social touches to engage prospects, qualify interest, and generate discovery meetings for Account Executives.
column15: The task mixes high-volume repeatable execution (sequence scheduling, queue management, logging) with high-judgment interactions (live calls, qualification, relationship building). Generative AI is strong for drafting personalized multi-channel copy, summarizing context, and suggesting objection responses, but needs guardrails due to hallucinations and tone/compliance risks. Agentic AI can automate orchestration across sales tools and adapt sequences based on engagement signals, yet should operate under strict policies and approval thresholds to avoid unintended outreach. Traditional AI is best for deterministic compliance enforcement, segmentation, and performance analytics on structured data. Assumptions: the org uses a CRM + sales engagement platform with APIs, has approved messaging frameworks, and can implement consent/DNC controls; entry-level SDRs still own live conversations and final approvals for sensitive accounts.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final approval of outreach to strategic/high-value accounts, regulated industries, or when personalization references uncertain facts; (2) live calling and qualification where rapport, negotiation, and real-time adaptation matter; (3) interpreting ambiguous replies (legal threats, procurement/security questionnaires, competitor traps) and deciding whether to disengage; (4) ensuring compliance with regional outreach laws and internal brand standards when automation proposes actions; (5) validating meeting quality before handing off to AEs to protect AE time and pipeline integrity. Humans should also periodically audit AI outputs, update playbooks, and set escalation thresholds (e.g., confidence scores, restricted phrases, max touches).
column17: Over-automation could spam prospects, violate frequency caps, or take unintended actions in CRM (wrong status changes, premature meeting booking). Emergent behavior from optimization (e.g., prioritizing easy meetings over ICP quality). Mitigations: hard policy constraints, rate limits, simulation/testing, approval gates, audit logs, and rollback mechanisms.
column18: Hallucinated personalization (wrong role/company facts), tone mismatch, over-claiming product capabilities, or generating non-compliant language (privacy/opt-out). Mitigations: retrieval from approved sources, constrained templates, banned-claims lists, human approval for low-confidence/high-stakes sends, and continuous QA sampling.
column19: Brittle rules or stale models can mis-score leads, exclude good prospects, or fail on new segments; may encode historical bias in targeting. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, explainability checks, monitoring drift, and human review of scoring criteria.
column20: Data quality issues (duplicate records, incorrect consent flags) can cascade across systems; conflicting recommendations between scoring, agentic orchestration, and GenAI messaging; security/privacy risks when sharing PII across vendors. Mitigations: single source of truth for consent, robust identity resolution, end-to-end logging, least-privilege access, vendor risk reviews, and clear precedence rules (compliance blocks override all).
column22: Orchestrate multi-channel outreach sequences across CRM/sales engagement tools. Autonomously manage inbox triage and routing with guardrails. Dynamic prioritization of daily outreach queue|Draft and personalize outreach copy (email, LinkedIn messages, call talk tracks/voicemails). Objection handling suggestions and response drafting. Summarize prospect research and interaction history for quick prep|Define targeting strategy, messaging positioning, and escalation rules. Conduct live calls and nuanced qualification conversations. Monitors replies, classifies intent (positive/neutral/objection/unsubscribe), drafts suggested actions, creates/updates CRM records, and escalates high-risk or high-value threads to the SDR for approval before sending. Continuously re-ranks prospects using engagement signals, ICP fit, SLA to AEs, and capacity constraints; proposes a daily plan and reallocates effort when meetings are booked or prospects go cold.|Generates channel-appropriate messaging using ICP, persona pain points, account context, and prior interactions; produces variants for A/B tests and maintains brand/voice guidelines. Creates concise rebuttals, clarifying questions, and follow-up emails based on common objections (timing, budget, authority, competitor) while referencing approved enablement content. Condenses CRM notes, website/news snippets, and prior threads into a brief with hypotheses, relevant triggers, and recommended questions for qualification.|Requires business judgment about ICP nuances, competitive context, and what constitutes acceptable risk; sets guardrails for automation and approves playbooks. Real-time rapport building, negotiation, and adaptive questioning are still best handled by humans, especially with ambiguous answers, multi-threaded stakeholders, or sensitive topics. Humans must review messages to strategic accounts, regulated industries, or when AI confidence is low; ensures factual accuracy, tone, and compliance before sending/committing. Human ensures discovery meeting is properly qualified, notes are accurate, and expectations are set; coordinates with AE on context and next steps.|Uses rules or classical ML on structured fields (industry, size, tech stack, intent signals) to score fit and segment lists; provides explainable criteria for why a lead is prioritized. Deterministically enforces contactability rules, regional regulations, and internal policies; blocks sends/calls when constraints are violated and logs reasons. Tracks open/reply rates, bounce rates, spam indicators, and call connect rates; flags statistically significant winners and anomalies using standard analytics.
column41: Enterprise Account Planning and Sales Execution

column8: Qualify inbound leads by validating fit, need, timing, and buying process, then route or convert them into meetings while meeting response-time SLAs.
column15: This task mixes structured triage (well-suited to Traditional AI), content and summarization (best for Generative AI), and multi-step execution across tools with SLA-driven prioritization (Agentic AI). However, qualification quality depends on real-time conversational judgment, exception handling, and accountability for routing/meeting commitments-requiring humans. Assumptions: the org has a CRM/MAP, defined ICP/routing rules, approved messaging, and consent/compliance constraints; AI is integrated with role-based access and audit logs. Work split reflects that AI can accelerate first response, prep, scoring, and workflow execution, but humans still conduct many live interactions and own final decisions for ambiguous or sensitive leads.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving/handling sensitive communications (pricing promises, legal/security claims, regulated industries), (2) resolving low-confidence or conflicting signals (e.g., high intent but poor fit, or strategic exceptions), (3) live discovery where tone, negotiation, and stakeholder mapping matter, and (4) governance: monitoring AI outputs, correcting CRM data, and updating ICP/routing rules. Humans should be in-the-loop for any autonomous meeting booking beyond predefined guardrails, and for any lead where consent, identity, or intent is uncertain.
column17: Over-automation could spam leads, mis-route accounts, or book meetings incorrectly; emergent behavior from tool actions; limited explainability for why it chose an action. Mitigations: strict action guardrails (rate limits, approval gates), confidence thresholds, sandbox testing, audit trails, and kill switches/escalation paths.
column18: Hallucinated facts (e.g., incorrect product capabilities), tone/brand drift, leakage of sensitive lead data in prompts, and biased language. Mitigations: retrieval from approved knowledge base, constrained templates, redaction, human review for high-risk segments, and automated claim-checking against allowed statements.
column19: Brittle rules and model drift can mis-score leads, disadvantaging certain segments; false positives/negatives in duplicate/bot detection. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, fairness checks, monitoring precision/recall, and easy rule overrides with feedback loops.
column20: Data inconsistency across CRM/MAP/enrichment, permissioning errors, and cascading failures (bad score -> wrong agent action -> poor outreach). Mitigations: unified data contracts, idempotent workflows, validation layers, observability (SLA dashboards), and staged rollout with A/B testing and human QA.
column22: Orchestrate inbound lead triage workflow across systems (CRM, marketing automation, calendar, routing). Autonomously follow up for missing qualification data within guardrails|Summarize inbound inquiry and synthesize lead context for the SDR. Draft compliant, on-brand first-response and qualification messages. Generate call scripts and objection-handling prompts for entry-level SDRs|Handle nuanced qualification and buying-process discovery in ambiguous/high-stakes cases. Make final routing/meeting decisions for exceptions and ensure policy compliance. Relationship-building and persuasion during live conversations|Lead scoring and fit classification using structured signals. SLA monitoring, queue prioritization, and anomaly detection. Duplicate detection and data validation/enrichment checks
column23: Monitors inbound queues, pulls lead context from CRM/MAP/enrichment tools, selects the next best action (auto-reply, request info, route to SDR/AE), and executes multi-step workflows (create tasks, assign owners, book meetings) while tracking SLA timers and escalating when at risk. If key fields (company size, use case, authority) are missing, agent sends a short clarification email/chat, waits for response, updates CRM, and re-runs qualification logic; stops and hands off when confidence is low or lead is sensitive (regulated industries, enterprise exceptions).|Reads form fills, emails, chat transcripts, and website intent signals to produce a concise brief (pain points, requested product, urgency, stakeholders mentioned) and highlights gaps against MEDDICC/BANT-style criteria. Generates personalized replies and discovery questions tailored to the lead's industry/use case, using approved templates, tone guidelines, and allowed claims; can produce variants for email vs. chat to meet response-time SLAs. Creates talk tracks and suggested next questions based on the lead's signals and common objections, improving consistency and ramp time while keeping the SDR in control of the conversation.|Humans are needed to interpret unclear intent, navigate multi-stakeholder dynamics, and adjust questioning in real time-especially when signals conflict or the lead's process is complex. Human judgment is required for edge cases (strategic accounts, partner leads, regulated customers, pricing/contract discussions) and to ensure adherence to legal/compliance and internal territory rules. Entry-level SDRs still need to build trust, read tone, and adapt to objections live; AI can assist but cannot fully replace human rapport and accountability.|Applies deterministic rules and/or supervised ML on firmographics (industry, employee count), technographics, geography, and intent/engagement to assign fit tiers and prioritize response order. Tracks time-to-first-response and time-in-stage; triggers alerts/escalations when thresholds are breached; detects unusual spikes (bot submissions, duplicate leads) using heuristics and statistical methods. Uses fuzzy matching and validation rules to merge duplicates, verify email/domain, and flag suspicious or incomplete records before outreach or routing.

column43: Customer Engagement and Delivery Risk Management

column8: Maintain accurate CRM hygiene by logging activities, updating lead and account fields, managing sequences, and ensuring data quality for reporting and forecasting.
column15: CRM hygiene for an entry-level mid-market SDR is repetitive but high-impact: much can be automated, yet errors propagate into forecasting and compliance. Traditional AI is best for deterministic validation, dedupe, and anomaly flags on structured data. Agentic AI adds the most leverage by autonomously capturing activities, executing trigger-based sequence actions, and coordinating remediation workflows across systems-while still requiring guardrails and approvals. Generative AI is valuable for converting unstructured notes/emails into structured suggestions and standardizing notes, but should generally propose rather than commit changes due to hallucination/context risks. Humans remain essential for ambiguous record linkage, sensitive updates (merges/opt-outs/ownership), and for defining evolving hygiene standards. Assumptions: CRM has APIs, activity sources are integrated, and governance allows AI-suggested updates with audit logs; without these, human share increases.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) compliance-sensitive fields and actions: consent/opt-out, email legality, data retention, and any automated outreach sequence changes that could violate policy; (2) high-impact data operations: record merges, account/contact hierarchy changes, ownership reassignment, stage changes that affect pipeline and compensation; (3) low-confidence entity resolution: matching activities to the correct contact/account when signals conflict; (4) exception handling: unusual deal structures, partner-led motions, or strategic accounts where standard rules don't apply; (5) governance: periodically reviewing AI performance, updating validation rules/taxonomies, and approving new data sources/enrichment vendors.
column17: Autonomous actions can create cascading errors (wrong sequence enrollment, incorrect activity-to-record linking) and may be hard to audit if decision logic is opaque. Risk of over-automation leading to compliance breaches (contacting opted-out leads) or damaging customer experience. Mitigations: policy engine with hard constraints, sandbox/testing, approval gates for sensitive actions, rate limits, comprehensive audit logs, and rollback mechanisms.
column18: May hallucinate details when summarizing notes/emails, misclassify dispositions, or introduce biased/incorrect inferences (e.g., industry/persona). Privacy risk if prompts include sensitive PII without proper controls. Mitigations: retrieval/citation to source text, constrained output to allowed picklists, confidence thresholds, redaction, and 'suggest-only' mode with human approval for critical fields.
column19: Rules can become brittle as GTM processes change; duplicate detection may produce false positives/negatives, leading to bad merges or missed duplicates. Anomaly detection can generate alert fatigue. Mitigations: periodic rule review, human-in-the-loop merge approval, threshold tuning, monitoring precision/recall, and clear exception workflows.
column20: Conflicts between systems (CRM vs sequencing tool vs enrichment) can cause data thrash (fields overwritten), inconsistent source-of-truth, and race conditions in automation. Security risks from broad API permissions. Mitigations: define field-level ownership, use event-driven architecture with idempotency, implement permission scoping/least privilege, maintain audit trails, and establish a governance process for changes to workflows and schemas.
column22: Auto-capture and log sales activities across channels (email, calendar, calls) into CRM. Orchestrate sequence enrollment/pausing based on CRM state changes and engagement signals. Workflow execution for data remediation tickets|Normalize and summarize unstructured interaction notes into structured CRM fields. Draft standardized activity notes and disposition reasons. Assist SDR with CRM field guidance and validation prompts|Approve/override high-impact CRM changes (merges, ownership, stage changes, opt-out status). Resolve ambiguous identity/linking and nuanced context. Define and maintain CRM hygiene standards and exception handling|Deterministic validation rules and required-field enforcement. Duplicate detection and record matching. Anomaly detection for reporting-critical fields
column23: Connects to email/calendar/telephony systems, detects relevant interactions, creates/updates CRM activity records, and links them to the correct lead/contact/account/opportunity using identity resolution; escalates low-confidence matches for review. Monitors triggers (reply received, meeting booked, bounce, unsubscribe, stage change) and autonomously pauses/resumes/removes prospects from sequences per policy; creates tasks for SDR when manual follow-up is required. When data-quality checks fail, opens a remediation task, gathers missing info from approved sources (e.g., enrichment vendor, website), proposes updates, and routes for approval; maintains audit trail.|Transforms call notes/emails into concise summaries and extracts entities (pain points, next steps, stakeholders) to suggest field updates (e.g., next step, persona, use case) with confidence and citations to source text. Generates consistent, policy-compliant notes (e.g., call outcome, objection category) from minimal SDR inputs, reducing variability and improving reporting quality. Acts as an in-CRM copilot that explains required fields for the current stage/sequence, flags inconsistencies in plain language, and suggests corrections without directly committing changes.|These actions can affect pipeline attribution, compliance (CAN-SPAM/GDPR), and downstream automation; human judgment is needed to resolve ambiguity and ensure policy adherence. When multiple contacts share domains/names or when context is subtle (e.g., partner vs prospect), humans decide correct associations and interpret intent. Humans set the taxonomy (dispositions, stages), decide what 'good data' means for the business, and update rules as GTM strategy changes.|Applies schema checks (required fields by stage), picklist validation, formatting rules (phone/email), and prevents saving invalid records; produces explainable error messages. Uses fuzzy matching/classical ML on names, domains, emails, and addresses to detect duplicates and suggest merges; provides match scores and rationale. Flags outliers and drift (e.g., sudden spike in 'Unknown' industry, missing lead source, unrealistic close dates) using statistical checks; routes exceptions for review.

column8: Collaborate with Account Executives to align on account plans, messaging, and handoff notes so meetings are well-prepared and opportunities progress efficiently.
column15: The task mixes structured coordination (well-suited to agentic orchestration and traditional validation) with high-context communication and strategy (human-led) plus drafting/summarization (GenAI). GenAI can reliably accelerate creation of talk tracks, agendas, and handoff summaries from transcripts, but must be grounded in approved sources to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI adds value by ensuring the alignment process actually happens-triggering workflows before meetings, collecting inputs, and tracking completion-yet should operate with guardrails and approvals for external outputs. Traditional AI is best for deterministic checks (required fields, freshness, taxonomy) and prioritization scoring. Humans remain essential for final positioning, risk acceptance, and relationship-driven alignment with AEs. Assumptions: SDR has access to CRM, call recordings/transcripts, and brand/compliance guidelines; integrations exist or can be built; data quality is moderate. If data access is limited or transcripts are unavailable, GenAI/agentic contributions drop and human work increases.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final account plan decisions (target personas, competitive positioning, qualification thresholds, next-step commitments), (2) approval of any customer-facing or high-stakes internal messaging to prevent inaccurate claims, (3) handling edge cases like regulated industries, security/legal commitments, pricing/contract discussions, and named strategic accounts, (4) resolving conflicting signals (e.g., transcript vs CRM vs AE intuition), and (5) ensuring ethical/compliant use of customer data (PII, call recording consent). Humans should approve: the final handoff note for late-stage/high-value deals, any outbound agenda email, and any changes to CRM fields that affect forecasting. Humans must also intervene when AI flags low confidence, missing sources, or anomalies (sudden stage change, unusually high deal value, or negative sentiment).
column17: Over-automation could spam AEs/prospects, create incorrect tasks, or update CRM improperly; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals (optimizing 'completion' over quality); permission creep across systems; brittle integrations causing silent failures. Mitigations: least-privilege access; explicit action policies (no external sends without approval); audit logs; sandbox/testing; rate limits; fallback to human when data missing; monitoring/alerts for workflow failures.
column18: Hallucinated facts in handoff notes (wrong stakeholders, pains, commitments); tone/positioning misalignment with AE strategy; leakage of sensitive data if prompts include PII; bias in suggested messaging; overconfident summaries that omit uncertainty. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations to CRM/transcript snippets; templates with required sections; confidence/uncertainty flags; redaction of PII; human approval gates for strategic accounts; maintain an approved messaging library.
column19: Rules become outdated as sales process changes; scoring models may encode historical bias (favoring certain industries/personas); false positives/negatives in completeness checks leading to busywork; entity matching errors merging accounts incorrectly. Mitigations: quarterly rule review with RevOps; transparent thresholds; human override; continuous evaluation against outcomes; conservative merge policies and manual review for merges.
column20: Inconsistent source-of-truth between CRM, conversation intelligence, and docs can propagate errors; identity/permission mismatches across tools; prompt injection via email/transcript content; duplicated or conflicting tasks from multiple automations; lack of end-to-end observability. Mitigations: define system of record per field; use signed connectors and scoped tokens; sanitize untrusted text before feeding GenAI; idempotent workflow design; centralized logging and reconciliation jobs; clear RACI for SDR/AE/RevOps ownership.
column22: Orchestrate alignment workflow across tools (CRM, calendar, Slack/Teams). Auto-compile account-plan packet and keep it current. Manage handoff task execution and follow-ups|Draft and refine aligned messaging (value prop, talk tracks, objection handling). Summarize discovery and produce structured handoff notes. Generate meeting agenda and pre-read for AE + prospect|Decide final account strategy and trade-offs (who to pursue, positioning, concessions). Approve customer-facing messaging and sensitive claims. Resolve ambiguous/conflicting information from sources. Conduct live alignment conversation with AE (relationship + nuance)|Validate completeness/quality of account plan and handoff fields. Prioritize which accounts need alignment attention. Detect duplicates and enforce taxonomy/standardization
column23: Monitors upcoming meetings/opportunity stage changes, triggers a pre-meeting alignment workflow, pings AE/SDR with a checklist, gathers required fields (ICP fit, pains, stakeholders, next steps), and routes for approval; adapts sequencing based on deal stage and time-to-meeting. Pulls latest CRM fields, recent emails/calls, intent signals, and open tasks; assembles a single 'meeting prep' packet; refreshes when new activity occurs; flags missing/contradictory info for human resolution. Creates/updates handoff tasks, assigns owners, sets due dates, and follows up until completion; escalates when deadlines are missed; logs completion back to CRM with audit trail.|Generates stage-appropriate messaging variants tailored to the account's industry, persona, and known pains; proposes discovery questions and objection responses; keeps tone consistent with brand guidelines when provided. Transforms call transcripts, emails, and SDR notes into concise handoff sections (context, pains, stakeholders, qualification, risks, next steps); highlights uncertainties and suggests clarifying questions for AE. Creates a proposed agenda, desired outcomes, and role assignments (who covers what); drafts a short internal pre-read and optional external agenda email for AE review.|Requires contextual judgment, commercial accountability, and nuanced understanding of competitive dynamics and internal capacity; errors have revenue impact. Humans must ensure accuracy, compliance (e.g., security/legal), and appropriateness for high-stakes accounts; prevents overpromising and brand risk. When CRM, transcripts, and stakeholder intel conflict, humans interview stakeholders, interpret intent, and choose what is true/most relevant. Real-time collaboration, negotiation of responsibilities, and trust-building are best handled by humans; AI can support but not replace interpersonal dynamics.|Uses deterministic rules and scoring (required fields by stage, MEDDICC/qualification checklist, formatting constraints) to flag missing data, stale timestamps, or inconsistent entries. Applies heuristic/ML scoring using structured signals (meeting date proximity, deal stage, engagement, ICP fit, pipeline value) to rank accounts for SDR/AE alignment time. Performs entity matching for accounts/contacts and enforces picklist/taxonomy normalization so handoff notes map cleanly into CRM fields and dashboards.

column8: Tailor value messaging and objection handling to mid-market personas by using product knowledge, competitive context, and approved talk tracks to improve conversion rates.
column15: The task mixes creative language tailoring (best handled by Generative AI) with structured segmentation/measurement and compliance checks (Traditional AI) and multi-system workflow coordination plus iterative optimization (Agentic AI). However, because messaging and objection handling can create legal/commercial risk and requires real-time interpersonal judgment, humans must approve claims/positioning and make final decisions in live interactions. Assumptions: an approved claims/talk-track library exists; CRM data quality is moderate; tooling can enforce guardrails (RAG over approved content + deterministic policy checks).
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving any new or modified claims, competitive statements, and pricing/security language; (2) reviewing AI-generated variants before external use when stakes are high (strategic accounts, regulated industries, competitor landmines); (3) deciding in real time how to respond to nuanced objections, emotional cues, or ethical concerns; (4) resolving conflicts between AI recommendations and current GTM strategy. Edge cases: ambiguous persona/title, incomplete CRM data, prospects asking for contractual commitments, or competitor misinformation-AI should defer and route to AE/SE/legal.
column17: Autonomous actions could propagate unapproved messaging at scale, run uncontrolled experiments, or create feedback loops optimizing for short-term replies over long-term brand/compliance. Mitigations: human-in-the-loop approvals for rollout, scoped permissions, audit logs, rate limits, and objective constraints (quality/compliance gates).
column18: May hallucinate product capabilities, overstate ROI, or invent competitive facts; tone may mismatch persona or brand; could leak sensitive internal positioning if prompts include confidential data. Mitigations: retrieval only from approved sources, claim-level allowlists, red-teaming, mandatory citations, and pre-send policy scanning.
column19: Rules/models can be brittle with new industries/titles, causing wrong persona routing; analytics may misattribute lift due to confounders or small samples. Mitigations: periodic rule/model refresh, confidence thresholds with human review, and robust experimentation design.
column20: RAG + policy engine + CRM orchestration can fail silently (stale docs, mismatched versions), leading to inconsistent guidance; identity/permission issues may expose restricted battlecards; conflicting recommendations between optimization agent and compliance rules. Mitigations: single source of truth for approved content, versioning, end-to-end monitoring, access control, and automated regression tests for guardrails.
column22: Orchestrate persona-specific messaging workflow across CRM, enablement, and call tools. Continuously optimize talk-track selection using outcome feedback loops|Draft persona-tailored value propositions using approved claims and product knowledge. Generate objection-handling responses aligned to approved talk tracks. Summarize competitive context into battlecard-ready snippets. Role-play simulations for practice and coaching|Define/approve final messaging, claims, and competitive positioning. Apply situational judgment in live conversations. Handle escalations and sensitive objections|Persona/segment classification and routing to the correct playbook. Performance analytics and attribution on messaging/objection tags. Compliance guardrails via deterministic checks
column23: Plans and executes a multi-step workflow: pulls account/persona context from CRM, retrieves the latest approved talk tracks from enablement repositories, selects the right playbook for the persona and funnel stage, and prepares a recommended messaging pack for the SDR; can trigger follow-up tasks (e.g., request competitive intel update) when gaps are detected. Monitors downstream signals (reply rates, meeting set rate, objection outcomes) and adapts which approved snippets to recommend per persona/industry; proposes experiments (A/B variants within approved boundaries) and routes results for human approval before broad rollout.|Generates concise value messaging variants (email, call opener, LinkedIn) conditioned on persona pains, industry, and stage, constrained to an approved claims library; can produce multiple tones and lengths for SDR use. Produces suggested rebuttals and question-based frameworks (e.g., clarify-validate-reframe) for common mid-market objections (price, timing, build vs buy), grounding responses in approved competitive positioning and proof points. Condenses long competitive docs into quick-hit differentiators, landmines, and discovery questions tailored to the prospect's likely alternatives; highlights what is and isn't approved to say. Acts as a prospect persona to simulate calls, surface likely objections, and provide feedback on clarity, compliance, and alignment to talk tracks; useful for entry-level SDR skill building.|Requires product marketing/legal/compliance judgment to ensure accuracy, differentiation, and adherence to policy; humans own the source-of-truth talk tracks and what can be promised. In-call nuance (tone, empathy, negotiation, reading intent) and ethical judgment are needed to decide when to push, pivot, or disqualify; AI suggestions may be inappropriate in edge cases. For security, legal, pricing exceptions, or competitor-specific landmines, humans must respond to avoid misrepresentation and to coordinate with AE/SE/legal.|Uses rules/ML on structured CRM fields (industry, employee count, tech stack, title) to assign persona and segment, ensuring consistent selection of the right approved messaging framework. Computes conversion metrics by persona, template, and objection category; flags statistically significant lifts/declines and detects drift when a talk track underperforms. Applies rule-based scanning for prohibited claims, missing disclaimers, competitor naming rules, and regulated-language triggers before content is used externally.
column41: Mid-Market Sales Messaging and Objection Handling
column43: Mid-Market Sales Messaging and Objection Handling

column8: Track performance against KPIs and OKRs, analyze outreach results, and run small experiments on messaging and cadence to improve meeting set and pipeline metrics.
column15: This SDR task mixes structured analytics (best handled by Traditional AI), iterative workflow execution (Agentic AI), and insight/messaging ideation (Generative AI), but still needs human judgment for goal-setting, compliance/brand risk, and deciding which changes to operationalize. Traditional AI covers metric computation, cohorting, and experiment statistics reliably. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating recurring data refreshes, experiment setup/monitoring, and anomaly triage across tools-reducing manual ops work. GenAI accelerates qualitative response analysis and converts metrics into narratives and test ideas, but should not be the source of truth for numbers. Assumptions: SDR has access to clean CRM/engagement data, pre-approved messaging guardrails/templates exist, and experimentation is limited-scope (small variants) rather than major positioning changes.
column16: Humans must (1) set/adjust KPI and OKR targets and resolve metric-definition disputes across CRM/engagement/BI; (2) approve outbound experiments that could impact brand, legal compliance (GDPR/CAN-SPAM), or deliverability, especially for regulated verticals and high-value accounts; (3) adjudicate edge cases like conflicting signals (reply rate up but meeting quality down), seasonality/territory shifts, list-quality changes, and small-sample noise; (4) make final decisions to roll out, pause, or revert cadences and to coach reps-areas where accountability and contextual business judgment are required.
column17: Autonomous actions could launch/modify sequences incorrectly, spam prospects, or pause revenue-critical campaigns due to mis-set thresholds; opacity in decision logic. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for send/launch, hard guardrails (rate limits, complaint thresholds), audit logs, and safe-mode fallbacks when data quality checks fail.
column18: May hallucinate causal explanations or misread tables if not grounded; can generate non-compliant or off-brand copy; may overfit to anecdotal reply snippets. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded prompting with metric citations, locked style/compliance constraints, human approval for external-facing copy, and red-teaming for sensitive segments.
column19: Brittle metric pipelines when schemas change; misleading results from confounding, selection bias, or incorrect cohort definitions; statistical misuse (peeking, multiple comparisons). Mitigations: data validation tests, versioned metric definitions, experiment pre-registration, correction for multiple testing, and monitoring for drift in segments.
column20: Data mismatches across CRM/engagement tools can cause inconsistent KPIs; feedback loops where agentic automation acts on GenAI-generated hypotheses without sufficient validation; privacy risks when sharing PII across systems. Mitigations: single source of truth for metrics, explicit handoffs (GenAI suggests -> Traditional AI evaluates -> Human approves -> Agentic executes), PII minimization/tokenization, and end-to-end observability with lineage.
column22: Automate KPI/OKR data pulls and refresh dashboards on a schedule. Orchestrate experiment workflow (setup -> launch -> monitor -> stop). Continuous monitoring and anomaly triage|Summarize performance narratives and insights for weekly readouts. Generate hypotheses and variant messaging/cadence ideas. Qualitative analysis of outreach responses|Define KPI/OKR targets, metric definitions, and business context. Approve experiments that affect brand, compliance, or deliverability. Interpret results and decide operational changes|Compute KPI/OKR metrics and funnel conversion rates. A/B test statistics and significance/credible-interval estimation. Segmentation and cohort analysis
column23: Connects to CRM (e.g., Salesforce/HubSpot), sales engagement (Outreach/Salesloft), and BI sheets; plans and executes recurring ETL jobs, validates schema changes, and refreshes dashboards/alerts with minimal human prompting. Creates experiment tickets, applies pre-approved templates in sequencing tools, randomizes assignment, monitors guardrails (unsubscribe/spam/complaints), and pauses variants when thresholds are breached; escalates to human for approvals/exceptions. Watches leading indicators (reply rate, bounce rate, meeting rate) and detects sudden shifts; gathers context (list source change, deliverability issues) and proposes likely causes and next actions.|Turns KPI tables and charts into concise narratives (what changed, why it matters, confidence level), tailored to SDR manager vs. marketing ops audiences; drafts slides/notes with citations to source metrics. Proposes testable hypotheses (e.g., value prop framing, CTA, personalization depth, step timing) and drafts compliant message variants aligned to ICP, industry, and product positioning; flags assumptions and required inputs. Clusters and labels reply themes (objections, interest signals, wrong person) from email/LinkedIn/call notes; extracts common language to inform new variants and enablement snippets.|Requires judgment about what success means (e.g., meeting quality vs. quantity), alignment with GTM priorities, and resolving ambiguous definitions across systems. Humans must review for legal/compliance (GDPR/CAN-SPAM), brand voice, sensitive industries, and risk tradeoffs; also decide when to stop/scale tests. Determines whether observed lifts are meaningful given seasonality, territory changes, and pipeline quality; chooses coaching, list strategy, or messaging pivots and communicates change management.|Deterministic calculations and classical analytics on structured data (activity counts, stage conversions, time-to-meeting, pipeline created); produces reproducible metric definitions and audit trails. Applies standard statistical tests or Bayesian models to compare variants, control for sample size, and output lift estimates with confidence; enforces minimum sample and duration rules. Rule-based or ML clustering to segment by persona, industry, region, list source, and channel; supports apples-to-apples comparisons and reduces confounding.

column8: Participate in enablement and team routines including training, call reviews, and feedback sessions to improve skills, share learnings, and support scalable SDR processes.
column15: This task is inherently social and developmental: humans must participate, practice, and deliver nuanced feedback. AI can materially reduce overhead and increase consistency. Traditional AI is best for reliable measurement (call metrics, completion tracking) that feeds coaching. Generative AI accelerates synthesis (summaries, playbook snippets, drafted feedback) but needs verification to avoid hallucinations and tone issues. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating routines (scheduling, nudges) and maintaining personalized coaching loops, but should not autonomously change standards or evaluate performance without oversight. Assumptions: calls are recorded/transcribed with consent; enablement content is accessible; a defined coaching rubric exists; data privacy controls are in place.
column16: Humans must (1) validate any AI-generated call feedback before it is delivered, especially for sensitive topics (performance management, DEI, compliance); (2) interpret metrics in context (lead quality, seasonality, new product changes) and set coaching priorities; (3) run/participate in role-plays and live coaching where empathy, motivation, and real-time adaptation matter; (4) approve any changes to SDR processes/playbooks to ensure alignment with ICP, brand voice, and legal/compliance requirements (e.g., recording consent, claims, regulated industries). Edge cases: poor transcript quality, multilingual calls, sarcasm/nuance, or atypical deal contexts can mislead AI-human review is mandatory.
column17: Over-automation could create meeting fatigue, spammy nudges, or mis-prioritized coaching; autonomous actions (posting feedback, assigning training) may be perceived as surveillance; opaque decision logic can erode trust. Mitigations: limit permissions (suggest vs. execute), configurable frequency caps, audit logs, opt-outs, and manager approval for performance-impacting actions.
column18: May hallucinate call details or misattribute intent; can produce overly generic or misaligned coaching; risk of leaking sensitive customer/employee data when summarizing; tone may feel punitive or biased. Mitigations: ground outputs in transcripts, require citations/timestamps, use approved templates, redact PII, human review before sharing, and maintain a style/feedback rubric.
column19: Brittle rules can mis-tag objections or penalize nonstandard but effective styles; models can drift as messaging/products change; metrics can be gamed (script adherence over genuine discovery). Mitigations: periodic recalibration, holdout QA with human reviewers, combine quantitative metrics with qualitative review, and monitor for disparate impact.
column20: Data pipeline issues (bad transcripts, mismatched IDs) can cascade into wrong coaching; conflicting signals between GenAI narrative feedback and Traditional AI scores can confuse SDRs; privacy/compliance risks increase when combining call recordings, HR performance data, and knowledge bases. Mitigations: single source of truth, data validation checks, role-based access, clear precedence rules (metrics vs. narrative), and governance for retention/consent.
column22: Coordinate enablement cadence and logistics (calendar holds, agendas, pre-reads). Personalized coaching plan orchestration|Summarize training content and create SDR-ready playbook snippets. Call review assistance: transcript summarization and feedback drafting. Knowledge sharing: synthesize learnings into internal posts/FAQs|Active participation in training and role-plays (practice, peer learning). Manager/peer feedback delivery and calibration. Final judgment on coaching priorities and process changes|Call analytics and rubric scoring (keyword/behavioral signals). Track enablement completion and performance correlations
column23: Monitors team calendars/time zones, proposes recurring sessions, drafts agendas based on recent performance gaps, and routes pre-reads; adapts schedule when conflicts arise with minimal human input. column8: Prepare and deliver renewal quotes and proposals, including pricing, term options, and required approvals, while ensuring alignment with commercial policy and governance frameworks.
column15: This renewal-quote task mixes deterministic computation (pricing/configuration), policy governance (threshold-based approvals), and high-stakes commercial judgment plus customer negotiation. Humans are irreplaceable where accountability, negotiation, and nuanced trade-offs are required.
column17: Autonomous orchestration could route approvals incorrectly, trigger actions on wrong opportunities, or over-escalate/under-escalate exceptions; emergent behavior may cause unintended customer communications. Generates clear, role-tailored messaging (CS playbooks, Sales enablement, customer FAQs) while adhering to tone and approved claims; suggests variants for different segments. Clusters and summarizes free-text feedback from CS notes, call transcripts, and survey comments to surface themes (e.g., legal turnaround, billing disputes) and propose hypotheses for fixes.|Requires relationship management, organizational context, and negotiation across Sales/Finance/Legal/CS; humans resolve conflicting incentives (e.g., speed vs. risk) and set shared goals. Humans must own accountability for contractual/legal exposure, margin impact, and customer relationship strategy; many decisions require authorized sign-off. Humans test proposed workflows with nuanced scenarios (strategic accounts, disputes, regulated customers) and ensure changes improve experience without unintended harm.|Uses BI/ML on timestamps and stage transitions to compute lead times, queue times, SLA adherence, and identify statistically significant bottlenecks by segment, region, deal size, or product. Applies deterministic rules (approval matrices, discount thresholds, required clauses, data completeness) to flag exceptions early and reduce rework; produces explainable pass/fail outputs. Time-series/forecasting models predict renewal volume and staffing needs by week/month to prevent backlogs that increase cycle time.

column8: Ensure adherence to governance and compliance requirements by following approval matrices, documenting exceptions, and supporting internal reviews of renewal transactions.
column15: The task mixes deterministic compliance enforcement (best handled by Traditional AI via rule engines and validations), content-heavy documentation and summarization (well-suited to Generative AI), and multi-step coordination across systems and stakeholders (where Agentic AI can automate routing, reminders, and re-checks). However, governance/compliance for renewals includes high-stakes judgment, discretionary exception decisions, and accountability that require human oversight and final sign-off-especially for non-standard terms, regulated customers, or large ARR impact. Assumptions: approval matrices are codified into decision tables; CRM/CPQ and document repositories expose APIs; GenAI is deployed with retrieval over current policies and strong guardrails; and the organization permits automated gating but not automated approval decisions.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving/denying exceptions and non-standard terms where risk tolerance and business strategy must be weighed; (2) final sign-off on high-value/high-risk renewals and any legal/regulatory interpretations; (3) resolving conflicts between policy guidance and real-world constraints (e.g., urgent renewal timing, customer demands); (4) validating GenAI-generated narratives for factual accuracy and ensuring no confidential data is inappropriately disclosed; and (5) handling edge cases such as novel contract clauses, cross-border regulatory requirements, or incomplete/contradictory source records. Humans also remain accountable for audit outcomes and must be able to explain decisions to internal reviewers.
column17: Autonomous orchestration could route to wrong approvers, spam stakeholders, or incorrectly block/unblock deals due to misinterpreted context; emergent behavior from tool use could create unauthorized changes in CRM/CPQ; transparency and controllability challenges. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, deterministic policy checks as gates, human-in-the-loop for unblock actions, simulation/testing in sandbox, explicit action logs, and kill-switch/rollback mechanisms.
column18: May hallucinate policy requirements or fabricate rationale/approval history; can omit critical details or misstate numbers/terms if source data is messy; risk of leaking sensitive customer/pricing data in prompts/outputs; potential bias in how exceptions are framed. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with citation requirements, strict grounding to deal data, redaction/DLP, template constraints, human review for high-risk cases, and logging of prompts/outputs for audit.
column19: Rule engines can become outdated as policies change; brittle handling of edge cases (new products, regions, clause types) may cause false blocks or missed approvals; anomaly models may generate false positives/negatives. Mitigations: policy versioning, governance for rule updates, monitoring/metrics on overrides, periodic audits, and clear exception pathways with human review.
column20: Inconsistent data across CRM/CPQ/CLM/document repositories can cause incorrect compliance decisions; conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs rule-engine requirements) may confuse reviewers; identity/permission mismatches can break audit trails; over-automation may reduce human vigilance. Mitigations: master-data alignment, single source of truth for approval logic, end-to-end workflow testing, unified logging/audit trail, and UI that clearly distinguishes 'computed requirements' from 'generated suggestions' with confidence/citations.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end compliance workflow for each renewal (routing, reminders, status tracking). Proactively detect missing artifacts and trigger corrective actions|Draft compliance documentation and exception narratives. Summarize renewal transaction history for internal reviewers/auditors. Answer policy Q&A and provide guidance with citations|Interpret ambiguous policy situations and make judgment calls on exceptions. Perform final compliance sign-off and accountability for high-risk renewals. Handle stakeholder negotiation and obtain discretionary approvals|Deterministic approval-matrix evaluation and gating. Compliance checklist validation and anomaly detection on structured fields. Audit trail capture and immutable logging
column23: Monitors renewal stage in CRM/CPQ, determines required approvals based on deal attributes, routes to approvers, sends reminders/escalations, and blocks progression until required steps are complete; adapts to changes (e.g., pricing updates) and re-evaluates approval needs. Checks whether required documents (quote, redlines, approvals, exception forms) are present; if gaps exist, opens tasks/tickets, requests inputs from owner, and re-runs checks before submission.|Generates first-draft exception justifications, risk statements, and approval request summaries using structured deal data and policy snippets; formats to internal templates and highlights required fields. Produces concise, traceable summaries of key changes (pricing, terms, concessions), rationale, and approval trail from CRM notes, emails, and contract redlines; flags ambiguous areas for human confirmation. Acts as a governed assistant over the policy knowledge base to explain approval matrix rules, required evidence, and common pitfalls; returns cited excerpts and suggests next steps.|Required when policies conflict, deal context is nuanced (strategic account, competitive pressure), or risk trade-offs must be weighed; humans decide whether to pursue/accept exceptions. Humans must approve material deviations, ensure ethical/legal compliance, and accept accountability; required for regulated customers, unusual clauses, or large financial impact. Securing approvals often involves persuasion, context-setting, and negotiation with finance/legal/sales leadership that AI cannot authoritatively perform.|Applies rule engine/decision tables to compute required approvers based on thresholds (discount %, ARR, term length, non-standard clauses, region, customer segment) and enforces CPQ/CRM stage gates with auditable logic. Validates mandatory fields, compares entered values to allowed ranges, detects inconsistencies (e.g., discount vs margin floor, payment terms vs policy), and flags outliers for review using rules and simple ML anomaly models. Automatically timestamps approvals, captures approver identity, versioning of quotes/terms, and stores artifacts in the correct repository locations to support later internal reviews.

column8: Stay current with industry trends and competitive renewal practices, and propose process or tooling improvements that increase team efficiency and renewal performance.
column15: The task mixes ongoing intelligence gathering, synthesis, quantitative diagnosis, and change-management. Generative AI is strong for summarization, drafting briefs/playbooks, and ideation but must be grounded in verified sources. Agentic AI adds value by continuously monitoring approved channels and orchestrating approved rollouts/measurement workflows, reducing manual overhead. Traditional AI is best for structured-data analytics (renewal KPIs, bottlenecks, causal impact) and deterministic change detection. Humans remain essential for source governance, interpreting competitive nuance, prioritizing initiatives, and approving changes that affect policy, customer experience, or compliance. Assumptions: access to clean renewal data in BI/CRM; a curated, legally approved source list; and tooling integrations (CRM/Slack/Jira/Confluence). Constraints: competitive intel collection must respect ToS and privacy; mid-market scope implies moderate complexity but high volume, making automation valuable.
column16: Human oversight is required to (1) approve/curate external sources and ensure ToS, privacy, and competitive-intel ethics; (2) validate high-impact claims (e.g., competitor pricing/terms) before dissemination; (3) choose which process/tool changes to implement given organizational constraints; (4) approve customer-facing guidance, discounting/terms recommendations, and any workflow that could alter contractual outcomes; (5) handle edge cases such as regulated industries, atypical deal structures, or conflicting signals across sources; and (6) arbitrate when AI outputs disagree (e.g., GenAI narrative vs. BI findings).
column17: Autonomous monitoring/automation could violate ToS or scrape disallowed sources; alert fatigue from poorly tuned thresholds; unintended workflow actions (creating tickets, updating docs) without proper approvals; model drift leading to irrelevant monitoring. Mitigations: allowlist sources, rate limits, approval gates, sandbox environments, audit logs, and kill switches; periodic review of monitoring rules and outcomes.
column18: Hallucination or overconfident synthesis of trends; citation/grounding gaps; leakage of confidential data if prompts include sensitive renewal terms; biased recommendations based on incomplete sources; tone or policy misalignment in drafted enablement assets. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with approved corpora, mandatory citations, redaction/DLP, human review for external sharing, and prompt templates with policy constraints.
column19: Brittleness if rules/classifiers are not updated; misleading analytics due to data quality issues (missing fields, inconsistent stages), confounding in impact measurement, and fairness concerns if models influence renewal prioritization. Mitigations: data validation, model monitoring, explainability, periodic recalibration, and causal inference best practices.
column20: Inconsistent definitions of KPIs across BI and GenAI narratives; feedback loops where GenAI-generated hypotheses bias what data is collected; permissioning gaps across CRM/BI/wiki leading to overexposure; orchestration failures causing stale insights or duplicate actions. Mitigations: shared metric dictionary, governance for prompts and datasets, role-based access control, end-to-end testing, and human-in-the-loop checkpoints for publish/execute steps.
column22: Continuously monitor and collect signals from approved sources. Orchestrate experiments and rollout tasks for approved improvements|Summarize and synthesize trend intelligence into renewal-relevant insights. Draft proposals for process/tooling improvements and enablement assets. Generate hypotheses and KPI definitions for efficiency/performance gains|Source curation, compliance gating, and interpretation of ambiguous signals. Prioritization and decision-making on which improvements to pursue. Stakeholder alignment and final approval of recommendations. Exception handling and ethical judgment|Quantitative analysis of renewal performance and operational bottlenecks. Competitive/pricing change detection via rules and classifiers. A/B test and causal impact measurement for implemented changes
column23: Runs scheduled workflows to pull updates from curated RSS/newsletters, analyst portals, competitor release notes, pricing pages (where permitted), community forums, and internal win/loss notes; maintains a change-log and alerts when thresholds are met (e.g., competitor renewal policy change). After human approval, creates tickets, assigns owners, updates enablement docs, schedules trainings, and tracks adoption/impact metrics across systems (CRM, CS tools, BI) with guardrails and audit logs.|Condenses long-form content into executive summaries focused on renewal levers (pricing, terms, discounting, procurement tactics), highlights implications for mid-market renewals, and drafts 'what changed/so what/now what' briefs. Generates first drafts of SOP updates, playbooks, talk tracks, competitive battlecards, and experiment plans; tailors to the Sales Renewals context and Grade 5/6 scope. Suggests measurable hypotheses (e.g., reduce time-to-quote, increase on-time renewals), proposes leading/lagging indicators, and drafts measurement plans and dashboards requirements.|Selects trustworthy sources, ensures ToS/privacy compliance, and interprets nuanced market moves (e.g., bundling strategy) that require domain judgment and context beyond data. Balances strategic fit, customer impact, change-management capacity, and stakeholder alignment; decides trade-offs and sequencing across Sales Ops, CS, Finance, Legal. Communicates recommendations, secures buy-in, and approves customer-facing or policy-impacting changes; accountable for outcomes and risk acceptance. Handles edge cases (regulated customers, sensitive competitive intel, atypical contract terms) and ensures recommendations align with company values and legal constraints.|Uses BI/ML on structured data to identify drivers (segment, product, term length), forecast renewal risk, detect anomalies in discounting/approval cycle times, and quantify baseline metrics. Applies deterministic rules and lightweight classifiers to detect changes in scraped/ingested competitor artifacts (e.g., keyword diffs, pricing table deltas) and route to review queues. Runs statistical tests, uplift modeling, and causal impact analyses to estimate effect sizes of new processes/tools while controlling for seasonality and segment mix.
