column8: Partner with Sales, Marketing, Customer Success, and Operations stakeholders to align regional mid-market priorities, coordinate execution, and deliver measurable outcomes against business objectives.
column15: The task is inherently cross-functional and high-context: humans must negotiate priorities, resolve conflicts, and own outcomes. Generative AI accelerates synthesis, documentation, and communication; Traditional AI provides reliable measurement, segmentation, and forecasting from structured data; Agentic AI improves execution discipline by orchestrating cadences, maintaining plans, and triggering playbooks. Assumptions: access to integrated systems (CRM/MA/CS/BI), clear OKRs, and governance for approvals. Constraints: data quality, tool permissions, and organizational readiness limit automation depth, keeping humans as primary decision-makers.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final prioritization and trade-offs when objectives conflict (e.g., growth vs retention vs margin), (2) commitments that affect headcount/budget and inter-team SLAs, (3) customer-facing escalations and exception handling, (4) compliance/privacy review of data used in summaries and any outbound messaging, and (5) edge cases like sudden market shocks, executive directives, or strategic accounts where relationship nuance dominates. Humans should approve any agentic actions that change spend, customer entitlements, or external communications.
column17: Over-automation could spam stakeholders, create meeting overload, or trigger workflows that conflict with current strategy; permission misconfiguration could expose data or take unauthorized actions; emergent behavior in multi-step planning. Mitigate with least-privilege access, approval gates, rate limits, audit logs, and constrained playbooks.
column18: Hallucinated metrics or misattributed stakeholder positions; tone/brand mismatch; leakage of sensitive customer or employee data when summarizing notes; bias in prioritization narratives. Mitigate with retrieval from approved sources, citation requirements, redaction, and human review for exec/customer artifacts.
column19: Model drift and brittle rules can mis-rank accounts or mis-forecast in regime changes; fairness issues in scoring; false positives/negatives in anomaly alerts. Mitigate with monitoring, periodic recalibration, explainability, and human validation of scoring thresholds.
column20: Inconsistent definitions of KPIs across systems, data latency, and identity resolution errors can cause conflicting recommendations; feedback loops where agentic triggers amplify noisy signals; unclear ownership when AI outputs drive decisions. Mitigate with a single metrics layer, data governance, versioned OKR definitions, end-to-end testing, and RACI for AI-assisted workflows.
column22: Orchestrate cross-functional operating cadence (agenda, pre-reads, action logs, reminders). Maintain a live regional execution plan and dependency map. Trigger playbooks based on signals (pipeline gaps, churn risk, inventory/fulfillment constraints)|Synthesize stakeholder inputs into aligned regional priorities and narratives. Draft cross-functional communications and enablement artifacts. Scenario planning and hypothesis generation|Set final regional priorities and make trade-off decisions across functions. Stakeholder management, conflict resolution, and executive alignment. Approve budgets, customer-impacting actions, and compliance-sensitive messaging|Quantify performance vs objectives and forecast outcomes. Segment accounts/territories and identify priority cohorts. Detect anomalies and operational bottlenecks
column23: Connects to calendar/email/PM tools to propose meeting times, generate agendas from open items, distribute pre-reads, capture decisions/action items, and follow up automatically; adapts cadence based on attendance, slippage, and priority changes. Continuously syncs updates from CRM, marketing automation, CS platform, and ops tickets; detects blocked tasks and proposes re-sequencing or escalation paths; drafts weekly status updates for review. Monitors defined thresholds and initiates workflows (e.g., request campaign support, schedule QBRs, open ops escalation) while keeping humans in approval loops for customer-facing or budget-impacting actions.|Summarizes notes, emails, and dashboards into a coherent set of priorities, trade-offs, and rationale tailored to mid-market context; drafts alignment docs and one-pagers. Creates briefs, FAQs, talk tracks, internal announcements, and customer-facing messaging drafts consistent with brand and regional strategy; localizes by segment/industry. Generates plausible scenarios (e.g., budget cuts, competitor moves) and proposes experiments/OKRs; highlights assumptions and data needed to validate.|Requires judgment under ambiguity, accountability for revenue/brand/customer impact, and negotiation of competing incentives that AI cannot legitimately arbitrate. Relies on trust-building, political context, and nuanced communication; humans handle sensitive escalations and secure commitments. Humans must ensure legal/compliance adherence, ethical considerations, and risk acceptance for high-stakes decisions and external communications.|Uses deterministic BI metrics and classical forecasting (time-series/regression) on pipeline, conversion, retention, and campaign performance to estimate attainment and identify gaps. Applies rules/ML clustering on firmographics, intent, engagement, and product usage to rank accounts and regions; produces explainable scoring for stakeholder review. Runs anomaly detection on lead flow, SLA adherence, renewal cycles, and fulfillment times to flag issues early with clear thresholds and alerts.

column8: Develop and execute regional sales strategy and territory plans, including account segmentation, target lists, and coverage models to drive pipeline growth and revenue attainment.
column15: This task mixes quantitative planning (segmentation, scoring, capacity/coverage analytics) with high-stakes judgment (territory/quota impacts, strategic account handling) and cross-functional alignment. Traditional AI is well-suited for structured segmentation, propensity scoring, forecasting, and optimization inputs (25%). Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating recurring multi-system workflows and continuously re-optimizing recommendations as conditions change, then executing approved updates in CRM and enablement systems (25%). Generative AI accelerates the creation of strategy narratives, hypotheses, and enablement artifacts but must be grounded in verified data to avoid hallucinations (15%). Humans remain essential for setting direction, approving consequential changes, and managing stakeholder dynamics and accountability (35%). Assumptions: access to reasonably clean CRM/BI data; clear governance for territory changes; integration APIs available; and a defined ICP/segment taxonomy to anchor models.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) defining objectives/constraints (quota strategy, strategic account protections, partner/channel rules), (2) approving territory realignments that affect compensation, customer experience, or contractual obligations, (3) adjudicating edge cases (sparse-data accounts, political/regulated customers, one-time mega-deals, channel conflict), (4) compliance and fairness review (non-discriminatory segmentation, privacy/consent for intent data), and (5) stakeholder alignment and communication. Humans should also sign off on any automated system changes (CRM ownership/assignment, routing, outreach cadences) and review model drift or unexpected performance drops.
column17: Autonomous workflows can make cascading changes (mis-assign territories, trigger outreach to excluded accounts) if guardrails fail; opaque decision paths and emergent behaviors under changing constraints. Mitigations: approval gates for high-impact actions, least-privilege access, simulation mode, audit logs, rollback, and monitoring with anomaly alerts.
column18: May hallucinate market facts or overstate performance drivers; can introduce biased or non-compliant messaging; risk of leaking sensitive customer/price data if prompts are not governed. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from approved sources, redaction/DLP, template constraints, human review for exec outputs and customer-facing content.
column19: Models can encode historical bias (e.g., under-investing in emerging segments), degrade with drift, or be brittle to missing/incorrect CRM fields; optimization may overfit to short-term pipeline at expense of retention. Mitigations: data quality checks, periodic retraining, fairness/coverage constraints, explainability reports, and holdout validation tied to revenue outcomes.
column20: Conflicting outputs between models (GenAI narrative vs ML metrics), inconsistent definitions (segment taxonomy), and brittle API integrations can cause planning errors. Mitigations: single source of truth for metrics, schema/version control, orchestration layer with validation rules, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and end-to-end testing in sandbox before production.
column22: Orchestrate data pulls and recurring planning workflows across CRM/BI tools. Continuously optimize territory/coverage recommendations under constraints. Execute approved actions in systems (task creation, routing, cadences)|Draft regional strategy narrative and executive-ready plan artifacts. Generate hypotheses for segmentation variables and ICP refinements. Create messaging frameworks aligned to segments and targets|Set strategic direction, constraints, and success criteria. Validate data quality, interpret edge cases, and approve territory changes. Stakeholder alignment and change management|Account segmentation and scoring using structured data. Target list generation and prioritization. Coverage model analytics and capacity planning
column23: Sets up and runs multi-step workflows (e.g., weekly pipeline/whitespace refresh), triggers data extraction from CRM, marketing automation, finance, and support systems; monitors for failures and requests human input when data quality thresholds are breached. Runs iterative what-if simulations (capacity, travel time, partner coverage, SLA tiers), adapts plans as new signals arrive (rep attrition, competitor moves), and proposes rebalancing actions with audit trails for approval. After human approval, updates CRM territory assignments, creates rep task queues, launches outreach sequences, and coordinates handoffs with SDR/CS teams while enforcing guardrails (no changes to strategic accounts without sign-off).|Synthesizes inputs (market trends, performance gaps, product priorities) into coherent strategy docs, QBR slides, and territory plan write-ups tailored to leadership and field teams; highlights assumptions and open risks. Proposes segmentation dimensions (industry micro-verticals, tech stack, intent signals), suggests proxy features when data is sparse, and produces testable hypotheses for analysts to validate. Produces segment-specific value propositions, talk tracks, and enablement snippets to support coverage models; ensures consistency with brand/compliance templates provided.|Defines regional goals, competitive posture, pricing/discount guardrails, and non-negotiables (strategic accounts, partner commitments); requires judgment, accountability, and alignment with corporate strategy. Reviews anomalies (one-off deals, channel conflicts, political accounts), resolves disputes, and approves changes that impact compensation, customer relationships, or legal commitments. Negotiates with Sales Ops, Marketing, Finance, and field leaders; communicates plan, manages rep buy-in, and handles escalations-activities requiring trust and nuanced human interaction.|Applies clustering/classification/regression on firmographics, historical win rates, product fit, and engagement to produce segment labels and propensity scores with measurable performance metrics. Ranks accounts/leads using deterministic rules + ML scoring (propensity, whitespace, churn risk), enforces exclusion lists, and outputs prioritized call lists by territory and rep capacity. Uses optimization/forecasting models to estimate required touches, rep capacity, pipeline coverage ratios, and quota attainment probabilities; outputs explainable dashboards and sensitivity analyses.

column43: Mid-Market Sales Pipeline and Renewal Insights

column8: Lead, coach, and develop mid-market sales representatives through regular 1:1s, deal reviews, and skill-building plans to improve performance, consistency, and career progression.
column15: The core of the task-coaching, motivation, trust-building, and accountable people decisions-remains human-led, especially at Grade 9/10 where leadership judgment and HR/compliance accountability are central. Generative AI adds leverage by turning scattered signals (notes, calls, CRM) into usable coaching artifacts (prep briefs, scripts, development plans) and by accelerating documentation. Agentic AI is best used to run the operating cadence (scheduling, reminders, assembling deal packets, executing approved enablement plans) and to trigger workflows from CRM signals, but should not autonomously decide outcomes. Traditional AI provides reliable, auditable measurement (KPIs, segmentation, rubric scoring) that grounds coaching in consistent data. Assumptions: access to CRM/call-recording/enablement systems with appropriate consent; a defined competency framework; and governance that requires human approval for sensitive actions (PIP, promotions, pricing exceptions).
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) interpreting rep intent, morale, and interpersonal dynamics during 1:1s; (2) delivering sensitive feedback and ensuring psychological safety; (3) any employment-impacting decisions (PIP, termination recommendations, promotion readiness, compensation inputs) requiring HR/legal compliance; (4) approving deal strategies involving pricing/contract risk, ethical considerations, or customer commitments; and (5) resolving edge cases where data is sparse, biased, or contradictory (e.g., new reps with limited history, territory changes, atypical verticals, or CRM hygiene issues). Humans must also approve any AI-generated coaching guidance before it is used, and periodically audit models/rules for fairness across gender/region/tenure.
column17: Over-automation could create 'managerial spam' (too many nudges), schedule inappropriate meetings, or trigger interventions that feel punitive. Autonomous actions in CRM (editing fields, routing tasks) can cause workflow disruption if logic is wrong. Emergent behavior risk if goals are mis-specified (optimize completion metrics over coaching quality). Mitigations: human-in-the-loop approvals for impactful actions, rate limits, clear escalation paths, sandbox testing, and audit logs for every action taken.
column18: May hallucinate details about deals or rep behavior, misattribute quotes from call transcripts, or produce coaching language that is culturally insensitive or misaligned with company policy. Privacy risk if it ingests call recordings/HR notes without proper consent and retention controls. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with source citations, strict data access controls, redaction of HR-sensitive fields, human review before sharing with reps, and tone/policy guardrails.
column19: Brittle thresholds can misclassify rep risk (e.g., seasonal territories) and reinforce bias if historical performance reflects unequal opportunity. Models may drift when GTM motions change. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, fairness checks (opportunity-adjusted metrics), explainable features, and allowing manager overrides with rationale capture.
column20: Data quality and identity resolution across CRM, call tools, and LMS can lead to incorrect coaching recommendations. Conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs KPI dashboard) can erode trust. Security/compliance risks increase with more connectors and automated actions. Mitigations: master data management, provenance/citation requirements, unified permissioning, staged rollout, and governance defining which system is source-of-truth for each field.
column22: Orchestrate coaching cadence and workflow (1:1 scheduling, agendas, reminders, follow-ups). Trigger deal-review interventions based on signals. Personalized enablement plan execution (tasking + resource assignment)|Summarize rep performance narratives for 1:1 preparation. Generate coaching scripts, role-play scenarios, and feedback phrasing. Draft individualized skill-building plans and career progression narratives. Create deal-review artifacts|Conduct high-trust 1:1 conversations and relationship-based coaching. Make final decisions on performance management and career moves. Approve deal strategy in high-stakes/exception cases. Validate data quality and resolve ambiguous/conflicting signals|Performance analytics and KPI tracking. Rep segmentation and risk flagging. Competency scoring from structured rubrics
column23: Connects to calendar/CRM/LMS to schedule recurring 1:1s, generates agenda templates based on pipeline and recent activity, sends reminders, captures action items, and nudges reps/managers until completion with minimal manual coordination. Monitors CRM events (stage changes, stalled deals, discount requests, competitor mentions) and autonomously proposes/initiates a deal-review workflow: requests missing fields, assembles a deal packet, and routes to the manager for review/approval. Given a manager-approved development plan, assigns micro-tasks (call shadowing, role-play sessions, course modules), tracks completion, adapts sequencing based on progress, and escalates blockers.|Synthesizes notes from prior 1:1s, call transcripts, emails, and CRM activity into concise prep briefs: wins, risks, coaching themes, and suggested questions; flags uncertainty when sources conflict. Creates tailored talk tracks for objection handling, discovery, negotiation, and executive messaging; drafts constructive feedback in the manager's tone and aligned to competency frameworks. Produces draft development plans (goals, behaviors, milestones) mapped to mid-market sales competencies and internal leveling; proposes measurable outcomes and learning resources for manager review. Generates deal review templates, MEDDICC-style summaries, mutual action plans, and email follow-ups based on structured deal inputs; highlights assumptions and missing data.|Requires empathy, psychological safety, and nuanced judgment; humans interpret context (motivation, burnout, personal constraints) and adapt coaching in real time. Promotion, PIP, compensation, territory changes, and sensitive feedback require accountable human judgment, legal/HR alignment, and ethical consideration. Humans must decide on pricing exceptions, risk acceptance, and strategic trade-offs; accountability and cross-functional alignment cannot be delegated to AI. CRM/call data can be incomplete or misleading; humans arbitrate disputes, correct records, and ensure coaching is fair and accurate.|Uses deterministic dashboards and classical ML to compute attainment, pipeline coverage, conversion rates, activity metrics, forecast accuracy, and trend deltas; provides explainable metrics for coaching focus. Rules/ML classify reps by ramp stage, performance tier, and risk (e.g., declining activity, low win rate, long sales cycles) to prioritize coaching time; thresholds are configurable and auditable. Applies rubric-based scoring (checklists) from manager inputs and assessment results to track skill progression over time; produces consistent, comparable scores.
column41: Sales Leadership and Sales Ops Execution

column8: Manage forecasting and pipeline hygiene by reviewing CRM data, validating deal stages, and communicating risks and upside to leadership with clear, timely updates.
column15: This task mixes structured data hygiene (well-suited to Traditional AI), ongoing monitoring and workflow execution (Agentic AI), and narrative synthesis for leadership updates (Generative AI). However, forecast ownership, stage/commit decisions, and executive-facing accountability remain human-led due to high stakes, incomplete/biased CRM data, and the need for judgment from real customer interactions. Assumptions: CRM (e.g., Salesforce) is the system of record; stage definitions and required fields are documented; integrations allow read access and controlled write-back with approvals; leadership expects weekly forecast cadence with ad-hoc escalations near quarter-end.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving any CRM write-backs that change stage, amount, or close date when confidence is low or impact is material; (2) final forecast/commit calls and exception handling (e.g., strategic deals, multi-region accounts, end-of-quarter pulls); (3) interpreting ambiguous signals (political changes, budget freezes) and reconciling off-CRM intel; (4) compliance/privacy checks when using customer communications/notes (PII, contractual restrictions); (5) executive messaging where tone, accountability, and organizational context matter. Edge cases needing humans: conflicting rep vs. CRM signals, deals with sparse history, channel/partner-sourced opportunities, and sudden macro events affecting multiple accounts.
column17: Autonomous workflows can spam reps, create incorrect tasks, or escalate unnecessarily; if granted write access, could change CRM data incorrectly; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals (optimize hygiene at expense of rep time). Mitigations: least-privilege permissions (read-only by default), approval gates for writes/escalations, rate limits, audit logs, simulation/testing in sandbox, and clear policies for when to notify leadership.
column18: May hallucinate deal facts or overstate confidence when summarizing; can misinterpret sarcasm/nuance in notes; risk of leaking sensitive customer/PII into prompts or outputs; tone may be inappropriate for leadership. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with citations to CRM fields, strict grounding/"no new facts" policies, redaction/PII controls, and mandatory human review for exec updates and strategic deals.
column19: Rules can become outdated as sales process changes; models may encode historical bias (e.g., under-forecasting new segments) and fail under distribution shifts (new pricing, new product); brittle handling of atypical deal structures. Mitigations: governance for rule updates, periodic model recalibration, explainability dashboards, and exception pathways for humans to override with rationale.
column20: Data mismatches between CRM, BI, and enablement tools can produce inconsistent numbers; latency can cause stale alerts; conflicting recommendations between models and GenAI summaries; security risks from multiple connectors and prompt injection via untrusted notes. Mitigations: single source-of-truth definitions, metric reconciliation checks, end-to-end monitoring, role-based access, content sanitization, and versioned prompts/models with change control.
column22: Orchestrate recurring pipeline hygiene workflow (data pulls, reminders, task routing). Autonomously monitor pipeline changes and trigger alerts/escalations|Draft leadership-ready forecast narratives (risks, upside, deltas). Normalize and interpret unstructured deal notes for risk signals. Generate rep-facing coaching prompts and data-quality questions|Make final judgment on forecast calls (commit/best case) and deal stage overrides. Conduct rep deal reviews and challenge assumptions. Communicate sensitive risks/upside to leadership and align on actions|Validate CRM field completeness and enforce stage-entry rules. Forecast rollups and anomaly detection on pipeline metrics. Deduplicate/clean CRM records and detect stale opportunities
column23: Runs a scheduled workflow that pulls CRM snapshots, opens tasks for reps on stale/at-risk deals, routes exceptions to the Regional Sales Manager (RSM), and tracks completion SLAs; adapts cadence by quarter-end/segment and escalates non-response. Continuously watches for material changes (stage regression, close-date push, amount changes, inactivity) and triggers alerts to owners and the RSM; can propose next-best actions (e.g., request MEDDICC fields) while keeping humans in approval loops for any CRM writes.|Summarizes CRM signals and rep notes into concise weekly/monthly updates, highlighting key movements, top risks, upside candidates, and asks; produces multiple formats (email, slide bullets, QBR notes) with consistent tone. Extracts entities and cues from call notes/emails (e.g., competitor mentioned, procurement delay, champion change) and converts them into structured risk tags and suggested questions for reps; flags uncertainty when evidence is weak. Creates tailored prompts per deal (e.g., 'What is the mutual close plan?') based on missing fields and stage criteria; helps standardize hygiene without the manager writing repetitive guidance.|Requires contextual judgment from live conversations, market knowledge, and accountability; humans arbitrate conflicting signals, approve stage changes, and own the number communicated upward. Humans probe credibility of close plans, validate customer intent, and coach reps; this relies on interpersonal dynamics and tacit knowledge not present in CRM. Executive communication involves nuance, political context, and responsibility; humans decide what to escalate, how to frame uncertainty, and what commitments to make.|Uses deterministic rules/validation (required fields by stage, close-date windows, next-step required) and simple classifiers to flag non-compliant opportunities; produces explainable pass/fail outputs. Applies statistical models/ML to compute weighted pipeline, trend lines, seasonality-adjusted expectations, and detect anomalies (sudden spike in commit, unusually old opps, rep-level outliers) for manager review. Uses matching rules and heuristics to identify duplicates, inconsistent account/opportunity mappings, and inactivity thresholds; recommends closure or requalification actions.

column8: Drive best-practice adoption across the team by standardizing sales processes, playbooks, and cadences that improve efficiency, quality, and scalability in the region.
column15: Standardizing sales processes is partly content and analysis (well-suited to GenAI and Traditional AI) and partly organizational change (requires human leadership). GenAI accelerates creation and harmonization of playbooks, templates, and enablement assets, and helps distill qualitative best practices. Traditional AI provides reliable, explainable measurement and enforcement in structured systems like CRM (validation rules, dashboards, process mining). Agentic AI adds value by coordinating rollout and continuously monitoring adherence, triggering nudges and follow-ups across systems; however, autonomy must be bounded to avoid over-automation and misaligned interventions. Humans remain essential for setting the standard, securing buy-in, coaching, and approving policies-especially in a Grade 9/10 regional leadership context. Assumptions: access to CRM/call-recording data, a knowledge base/LMS, and clear governance for what AI can publish or automate; without these, AI contributions drop and human work increases.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) defining the target operating model and deciding which process elements are mandatory vs optional by segment; (2) approving customer-facing claims, pricing guidance, and compliance-sensitive language in playbooks; (3) interpreting analytics in context (territory differences, seasonality, product changes) before enforcing consequences; (4) handling exceptions for strategic accounts and atypical deal cycles; (5) managing change-communicating rationale, addressing resistance, and coaching managers/reps; (6) setting guardrails for agentic actions (who gets nudged, escalation thresholds, and when automation must pause).
column17: Unintended workflow actions (publishing wrong version, spamming reps with nudges), misinterpreting signals (e.g., low activity due to PTO) and escalating incorrectly, and opaque decision logic. Mitigations: strict permissions, sandbox/testing, rate limits, human-in-the-loop for escalations, audit logs, and clearly defined policies for interventions.
column18: Hallucinated or outdated guidance (e.g., incorrect pricing/positioning), inconsistent tone, leakage of sensitive customer data if prompts include PII, and bias toward practices that worked in different segments. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, human approval workflow, red-teaming for claims, prompt/data minimization, and version control with expiry dates.
column19: Brittle rules that don't fit edge cases, dashboard gaming, and model drift if segments/products change; fairness issues if benchmarks penalize certain territories. Mitigations: periodic rule review, exception codes, monitoring for drift, and benchmarking that controls for segment/territory factors.
column20: Conflicting sources of truth (playbook vs CRM rules), feedback loops where agentic nudges drive metric gaming, and security/identity issues across CRM/LMS/wiki. Mitigations: governance with single owner per artifact, unified taxonomy, end-to-end auditability, staged rollout, and access control/SSO with least privilege.
column22: Orchestrate rollout workflow across tools (CRM/LMS/wiki). Monitor adherence and trigger interventions|Draft and standardize sales playbooks, talk tracks, and templates. Synthesize best practices from call notes, win/loss, and rep feedback. Create enablement content and assessments|Set strategic direction and define the 'non-negotiables' of the sales process. Stakeholder alignment, change management, and coaching conversations. Governance, approvals, and exception handling|Define and enforce CRM process rules and data validation. Process mining and KPI analytics for process effectiveness. Segmentation and benchmarking dashboards
column23: Plans and executes a multi-step rollout: creates project plan, assigns tasks, opens enablement tickets, publishes playbooks to the knowledge base, schedules trainings, and nudges reps/managers based on completion signals; adapts sequencing by region/team capacity. Continuously watches leading indicators (CRM hygiene, stage progression, activity cadence compliance) and autonomously triggers actions: reminders, micro-coaching tasks, manager alerts, or creates follow-up enablement sessions when drift is detected.|Generates first-draft playbooks (discovery, qualification, objection handling, MEDDICC/Challenger variants), email/call scripts, and meeting agendas aligned to regional ICP and product lines; rewrites for consistency and localization. Summarizes qualitative inputs (call transcripts, deal reviews, QBR notes) into repeatable patterns, anti-patterns, and recommended cadences; produces concise guidance and FAQs. Produces training decks, role-play scenarios, quizzes, and certification rubrics mapped to the standardized process; tailors content by role (AE/BDR/CSM) and segment.|Requires judgment about regional market realities, product strategy, and trade-offs; decides which steps are mandatory vs flexible and aligns with leadership priorities. Adoption depends on trust, motivation, and nuanced coaching; humans handle resistance, tailor messaging, and run high-stakes enablement and manager coaching. Humans approve final playbooks/cadences for compliance, pricing/claims, and brand; adjudicate edge cases (strategic accounts, atypical procurement) where rigid standards may harm deals.|Implements deterministic rules/ML checks: required fields by stage, validation of close dates, activity logging thresholds, duplicate detection, and routing rules; provides explainable compliance flags. Uses structured analytics to compare funnel conversion, cycle time, stage aging, and activity-to-outcome ratios before/after standardization; detects anomalies by team/territory. Builds dashboards that benchmark reps/teams against the standard cadence (e.g., touches per week, meeting-to-opportunity rate) and controls for segment/territory differences using classical models.

column8: Support strategic deal execution by guiding pricing, packaging, and negotiation approaches, coordinating internal approvals, and ensuring proposals meet customer and company requirements.
column15: This Grade 9/10 Regional Sales Manager task is high-stakes and policy-constrained: humans remain accountable for commercial judgment, exceptions, and relationship outcomes, so the task cannot be fully automated. Generative AI accelerates drafting/summarization for proposals, redlines, and justification memos but must be grounded in approved facts to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step deal-desk workflows (routing approvals, collecting artifacts, tracking SLAs) and running structured checklists across systems; however it should operate within strict guardrails and require human confirmation before external sends or policy exceptions. Traditional AI is best for deterministic guardrails (discount bands, margin floors, approval matrices), template/clause selection, and predictive analytics from historical structured data. Assumptions: CRM/CPQ data is reasonably clean; approval policies are codified; integrations to contract repository and ticketing exist; customer requirements are available in text form. Edge cases (public sector, export controls, unusual data-processing terms, channel conflicts) increase human share and reduce autonomy.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) final pricing/packaging and any exception to floors, margin, or channel rules; (2) interpreting ambiguous customer requirements and balancing legal/compliance risk vs. revenue; (3) approving customer-facing commitments (SLAs, security, roadmap statements) and ensuring no unauthorized promises; (4) live negotiation, escalation handling, and relationship management; (5) mandatory approvals where policy/regulation requires accountable signatories (legal, finance, security). Humans must also review AI-produced summaries and drafts for factual accuracy, confidentiality, and alignment with company positioning-especially for strategic accounts, regulated industries, and cross-border data/export scenarios.
column17: Autonomous workflow actions could route to wrong approvers, miss a required compliance step, or spam stakeholders; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals could prioritize speed over compliance; auditability challenges. Mitigations: explicit guardrails, step-level approvals for high-risk actions, least-privilege access, comprehensive logging, simulation/testing, and kill-switches.
column18: May hallucinate deal facts (pricing, terms), misstate product capabilities/roadmap, or produce non-compliant language; can leak sensitive data if prompts include confidential info; tone may harm negotiations. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from approved sources, redaction/DLP, locked templates, citation requirements, and mandatory human review before external sharing.
column19: Rules can become outdated as pricing policies change; models trained on biased historical deals may reinforce discounting patterns or disadvantage segments; brittle handling of novel deal structures. Mitigations: governance for rule updates, monitoring drift, periodic retraining with fairness checks, and clear explainability with override paths.
column20: Conflicting outputs (e.g., GenAI draft contradicts rule-based guardrails), data quality issues across CRM/CPQ/CLM, and access-control mismatches can cause compliance breaches. Mitigations: single source of truth for pricing/terms, validation layers that block inconsistent proposals, role-based access, end-to-end audit trails, and human-in-the-loop checkpoints at key gates (pricing approval, legal redlines, final proposal release).
column22: Orchestrate deal desk workflow and internal approvals routing. Run multi-step compliance/feasibility checks across systems. Scenario planning for pricing/packaging with constraints|Draft and tailor proposal narrative, packaging rationale, and negotiation talk-tracks. Summarize customer requirements and contract redlines into actionable issues. Generate approval-ready deal justification memos|Set negotiation strategy and make final pricing/packaging decisions. Approve non-standard terms and manage stakeholder alignment. Conduct live negotiations and relationship management|Discount/margin guardrail checks and exception detection. Win-rate and price-sensitivity analytics from historical deals. Template and clause selection based on deal attributes
column23: Monitors deal stage signals in CRM, triggers approval workflows (legal, finance, security, product), routes packets to the right approvers based on deal attributes (region, discount band, data-processing terms), follows up on SLAs, and maintains an auditable timeline; adapts routing when requirements change. Executes a plan to gather required artifacts (customer requirements, redlines, security questionnaires), queries internal policy repositories and prior deal precedents, flags missing items, and proposes next actions (e.g., request DPA, initiate export-control review). Given guardrails (floor price, margin targets, channel rules), autonomously evaluates multiple configurations, simulates approval likelihood and revenue impact, and recommends a short list for human selection; updates recommendations as new info arrives.|Generates customer-facing proposal sections (value narrative, packaging justification, ROI framing) and internal negotiation playbooks aligned to the account context; can rewrite for tone, industry, and stakeholder personas. Extracts key obligations, deviations from standard terms, and open questions from emails, notes, and redlined documents; produces structured summaries for legal/finance review and highlights negotiation levers. Creates concise business-case writeups (discount rationale, competitive context, risk mitigations) using provided facts and templates, reducing manager time while keeping humans in control of final assertions.|Requires judgment on competitive dynamics, relationship context, and risk appetite; accountable for revenue/margin trade-offs and exceptions beyond policy. Humans (RSM, legal, finance leadership) must approve deviations, interpret ambiguous requirements, and resolve conflicts between internal teams; accountability and regulatory obligations demand human sign-off. Real-time persuasion, trust-building, and reading counterpart signals remain human-led; humans handle sensitive escalations and adapt to nuanced interpersonal dynamics.|Applies deterministic rules and/or classical ML on structured deal data to validate pricing against floors, margin thresholds, and approval matrices; flags anomalies (e.g., unusual payment terms) with explainable reasons. Uses regression/classification models on CRM history to estimate probability of win by discount band, package mix, and segment; provides quantitative inputs to negotiation strategy without generating new policy. Rule-based selection of proposal templates, SKUs, and standard clauses (e.g., data residency addendum) based on region/industry/product, ensuring consistency and reducing omissions.

column8: Monitor regional performance metrics and market signals to identify trends, prioritize initiatives, and adjust tactics based on data-driven insights and competitive context.
column15: This task blends quantitative monitoring with contextual interpretation and accountable decision-making. Traditional AI is best for reliable KPI computation, anomaly detection, and forecasting on structured data (high maturity, low variance). Generative AI adds value by turning multi-source signals into readable insights, drafting RBR narratives, and accelerating hypothesis generation, but must be constrained due to hallucination and source-traceability limits. Agentic AI can automate the operational cadence-data refresh orchestration, alerting, and playbook execution-and can draft prioritized initiative options via multi-step workflows, but should operate under strict guardrails and require approvals before executing impactful actions. Humans remain essential for setting priorities/guardrails, validating insights against market nuance, and approving/owning tactical changes. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/BI data, defined KPI taxonomy, and permissible external data sources; organization has change-management capacity to act on recommendations. Constraints: competitive intel may be incomplete; some markets have sparse data, increasing uncertainty.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) defining KPI definitions, thresholds, and strategic guardrails (discount limits, channel rules, compliance constraints); (2) adjudicating conflicting signals (e.g., forecast up but margin down) and incorporating qualitative field intelligence; (3) approving high-impact actions-pricing changes, territory/account reassignments, partner escalations, headcount shifts-where accountability, ethics, and legal/commercial risk apply; (4) ensuring compliance with privacy/competition law when using external market/competitor data; (5) handling edge cases like one-off mega-deals, data outages, or sudden macro events where models extrapolate poorly.
column17: Autonomous workflows can over-trigger alerts, create ticket noise, or recommend actions that violate commercial guardrails (e.g., excessive discounting) due to mis-specified objectives. Emergent behavior and limited explainability can reduce trust. Mitigations: hard policy constraints, approval gates for impactful actions, simulation/sandbox testing, audit logs, rate limits, and continuous monitoring of outcomes/bias.
column18: May hallucinate drivers or competitor claims, misstate numbers if not grounded to a single source of truth, or leak sensitive customer/price info if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations, numeric locking from BI outputs, redaction/DLP, approved templates, and mandatory human review for external-facing or high-stakes briefs.
column19: Brittle KPI rules and models can drift as products/segments change; forecasts may underperform in sparse-data territories or during regime shifts. Risk of biased prioritization if historical data reflects past coverage inequities. Mitigations: data quality SLAs, periodic recalibration, drift detection, interpretable models where possible, and fairness checks across territories/segments.
column20: Inconsistent metric definitions across systems can cause GenAI narratives to contradict dashboards; agentic orchestration may act on stale/partial data; feedback loops can amplify errors (bad forecast -> wrong initiative -> worse data). Mitigations: single metric layer/semantic model, lineage tracking, freshness checks, human-in-the-loop approvals, end-to-end testing, and clear ownership for each data source and decision point.
column22: Orchestrate recurring data refreshes and monitoring workflows across systems. Trigger and manage exception-handling playbooks. Run multi-step scenario analysis and initiative prioritization drafts|Synthesize insights from mixed structured/unstructured signals. Draft executive-ready performance and competitive context briefs. Generate hypotheses and questions for field validation|Set strategic priorities, guardrails, and success criteria. Validate insights with field reality and customer context. Make and approve high-stakes tactical adjustments. Manage stakeholder alignment and communication|Compute and validate core performance metrics and KPI dashboards. Detect anomalies and emerging trends in time-series KPIs. Forecast near-term sales outcomes and leading indicators
column23: Sets up and runs scheduled pipelines to pull CRM (pipeline, win/loss), ERP (shipments), finance (revenue/margin), marketing (leads), and external market/competitor feeds; monitors job health, retries failures, and alerts stakeholders when data is stale or anomalies exceed thresholds. When KPIs breach thresholds (e.g., sudden churn, ASP drop, competitor price move), autonomously opens investigation tickets, requests missing inputs from owners, assembles a brief, and proposes next-best actions aligned to predefined guardrails (e.g., discount approval limits, channel conflict rules). Combines forecasts, constraints (inventory, headcount), and objectives (quota, margin) to generate ranked initiative options; iterates through what-if scenarios (pricing, territory focus, promo timing) and produces a recommendation package for human approval.|Summarizes dashboards plus unstructured inputs (sales call notes, customer emails, analyst reports, news) into coherent narratives: what changed, why it matters, and likely drivers; highlights confidence levels and cites sources where available. Generates weekly/monthly regional business review (RBR) write-ups, including KPI commentary, competitor moves, risks/opportunities, and suggested talking points tailored to leadership and cross-functional partners. Proposes plausible explanations for metric movements (e.g., mix shift, channel leakage, competitor bundling) and produces targeted questions for account teams to validate, reducing time to root cause.|Requires business judgment to balance growth vs. margin, brand considerations, channel relationships, and corporate strategy; defines what 'good' looks like and acceptable trade-offs. Humans confirm whether signals reflect real market dynamics (e.g., one-off deal timing, distributor stocking) and incorporate qualitative intel not captured in systems. Decisions like pricing exceptions, territory reassignments, partner escalations, and resource shifts carry financial/legal/reputational risk and require accountable leadership approval. Negotiates with marketing, product, finance, and sales teams; resolves conflicts and ensures initiatives are adopted-work that depends on trust, persuasion, and organizational context.|Uses deterministic ETL, BI calculations, and rules to standardize definitions (quota attainment, pipeline coverage, conversion rates, churn, margin), perform data quality checks, and produce consistent dashboards. Applies classical ML/statistical methods (change-point detection, seasonal decomposition, control charts) to flag unusual movements by territory/segment/product and quantify trend strength. Uses regression/gradient boosting or probabilistic models on historical pipeline and activity data to predict bookings/revenue and identify leading indicators (e.g., stage velocity, activity-to-opportunity ratios).

column8: Collaborate with enablement and product partners to surface field feedback, improve training content, and influence roadmap priorities that address mid-market customer needs.
column15: The task is cross-functional and high-context: influencing roadmap and improving enablement depends on human judgment, stakeholder management, and accountability, so it cannot be fully automated. Generative AI is well-suited to converting unstructured field inputs into usable summaries and drafting training/briefing artifacts, accelerating content iteration. Agentic AI adds value by running the ongoing, multi-step operational loop (collect->triage->route->follow up->report) across tools with minimal manual coordination. Traditional AI contributes best on structured quantification-segmenting and trending tagged feedback to support prioritization. Assumptions: access to CRM/call transcripts/LMS/wiki data via secure connectors; clear taxonomy for mid-market segments; governance for what data can be processed by AI.
column16: Humans must (1) verify that synthesized feedback is accurate and representative (avoid over-weighting loud anecdotes), (2) make final prioritization calls that balance revenue, strategy, and product constraints, (3) manage stakeholder alignment and negotiate trade-offs, (4) approve externally/field-facing training and any roadmap statements to avoid compliance issues and overcommitments, and (5) handle edge cases like escalations from strategic accounts, regulated industries, or sensitive competitive claims. Human oversight is also required for any automated routing/escalation rules and for monitoring drift in themes as the market changes.
column17: Autonomous workflows can spam stakeholders, mis-route sensitive feedback, or create unintended escalations; opaque decision logic may reduce trust; integration permissions could overreach. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval steps for escalations, rate limits, audit logs, sandbox testing, and clear stop conditions/fallback to human.
column18: May hallucinate product capabilities or misattribute customer quotes; can oversimplify nuanced objections; risk of leaking sensitive customer data if prompts/logs are not governed; tone/positioning may conflict with official messaging. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, citation requirements, redaction, human review gates, and style/claims checklists.
column19: Brittle tagging/rules can mis-segment mid-market vs SMB/enterprise; trend metrics can be biased by logging behavior and seasonality; models may lag behind new product terms. Mitigations: periodic taxonomy reviews, calibration against ground truth, monitoring for data quality, and human-in-the-loop relabeling.
column20: Conflicting outputs (e.g., GenAI themes vs dashboard rankings), feedback loops that amplify biased signals, and inconsistent data definitions across CRM/support/product tools. Mitigations: single source of truth for taxonomy, reconciliation layer with confidence scores, governance on data lineage, and defined decision forums where humans arbitrate.

Segment feedback by customer attributes and sales context

Maintains a queue of feedback items, schedules recurring syncs, drafts agendas, tracks decisions/actions, and nudges owners; updates a shared dashboard and notifies the field when items are accepted, deferred, or need more evidence.|Transforms messy notes/transcripts into structured summaries (problem, impact, segment, urgency, evidence), extracts quotes, and produces theme-level insights tailored to mid-market personas. Generates first drafts aligned to product positioning and common objections; proposes examples, role-plays, and knowledge checks; adapts tone for sales enablement audiences and formats for LMS/wiki. Drafts concise documents that connect field evidence to customer outcomes, revenue impact, and competitive context; proposes prioritization arguments and trade-offs for product review forums.|Regional Sales Manager judges credibility, representativeness, and business impact; reconciles conflicting anecdotes; decides escalation thresholds and aligns with regional strategy and quotas. Requires relationship management, persuasion, and real-time negotiation with product/enablement leaders; handles political dynamics and resource constraints. Ensures content matches current product behavior, legal/compliance guidance, and go-to-market strategy; prevents overpromising and manages sensitive customer/competitive information.|Uses dashboards, keyword/topic frequency counts, sentiment scoring, and time-series trend detection on structured tags to rank issues by volume, ARR impact, stage, and churn risk. Applies deterministic rules/ML classification to map feedback to industry, size band, product line, region, deal stage, and competitor; supports slice-and-dice analysis for prioritization.

column8: Execute outbound and inbound prospecting across assigned SMB segments, qualify leads against ICP criteria, and build a healthy pipeline to consistently meet weekly activity KPIs and monthly revenue targets.
column15: Prospecting and qualification combine high-volume repeatable work (list hygiene, scoring, cadence execution, drafting messages) with high-stakes judgment (fit, compliance, relationship). Traditional AI is strong for structured ICP scoring, segmentation, and KPI analytics. Generative AI accelerates personalized messaging, call prep, and summarization but must be grounded to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI can orchestrate sequences, triage inbound, and maintain pipeline hygiene, yet needs guardrails and approvals to prevent inappropriate outreach or incorrect CRM updates. Humans remain essential for ICP governance, live discovery, and final qualification/forecast commitments. Assumptions: AE uses a modern CRM + sales engagement platform with APIs; call recording/transcription is available; approved messaging libraries exist; SMB segments have moderate variability allowing templated personalization.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) approving/maintaining ICP criteria, disqualification rules, and any changes that affect targeting; (2) reviewing GenAI-generated claims to ensure product accuracy, pricing/terms correctness, and brand tone; (3) compliance decisions (consent, opt-out handling, regional privacy laws, sensitive industries) and exception handling (VIP accounts, complaints); (4) conducting and steering live discovery where ambiguity is high (multi-stakeholder dynamics, competitive positioning, negotiation); (5) final stage changes, close dates, and forecast commitments; (6) resolving edge cases like conflicting enrichment data, duplicate accounts across territories, or leads with mixed signals (high intent but non-ICP firmographics).
column17: Over-automation leading to spammy outreach, contacting opted-out leads, or escalating sequences incorrectly; unintended actions in CRM (wrong stage updates), and opaque decision logic. Mitigations: strict policy engine (DNC/consent), rate limits, approval gates for first-touch and stage changes, audit logs, sandbox testing, and kill-switches.
column18: Hallucinated product capabilities, incorrect personalization (wrong company/persona), tone or cultural mismatch, leakage of sensitive prospect/customer data into prompts, and inconsistent adherence to approved messaging. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved collateral, prompt templates with required fields, red-team tests, PII controls, and human review for first-touch/high-risk segments.
column19: Brittle rules or biased historical data causing systematic exclusion of certain SMB types, stale scoring models as market shifts, and false confidence from explainable but incomplete features. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, fairness checks, monitoring drift, and allowing human overrides with feedback loops.
column20: Data sync issues between CRM/SEP/enrichment tools causing wrong personalization or duplicate outreach; conflicting recommendations (GenAI suggests one persona while scoring flags non-ICP); security and access-control gaps across tools; and accountability ambiguity when automation acts. Mitigations: single source of truth for contact status, event-driven architecture with idempotency, unified consent management, role-based access, and clear RACI plus end-to-end monitoring.
column22: Orchestrate multi-channel prospecting sequences (email/LinkedIn/calls) within guardrails. Automate inbound lead triage and routing with SLA management. Pipeline hygiene automation and follow-up nudging|Draft and personalize outreach messaging and call talk-tracks. Summarize inbound conversations and extract qualification signals. Generate ICP-fit hypotheses and discovery questions|Define/adjust ICP, messaging positioning, and compliance constraints. Conduct live qualification/discovery and relationship-building. Final qualification decisions and pipeline commitments|Lead scoring and ICP rules evaluation on structured data. List segmentation, deduplication, and data quality checks. KPI tracking, forecasting baselines, and anomaly detection
column23: Plans and executes step-by-step outreach workflows in the CRM/SEP (e.g., Outreach/Salesloft), selects next-best action per lead based on engagement signals, schedules tasks, and adapts cadence timing; requires policy constraints (do-not-contact, frequency caps) and human approval for high-risk sends. Monitors inbound queues (web forms, chat, demo requests), enriches records, assigns priority, routes to AE/SDR, and triggers follow-ups; escalates exceptions (missing data, high-value accounts) to humans. Detects stale opportunities/leads, proposes updates, creates follow-up tasks, and prompts for disposition reasons; can auto-update low-risk fields but should request confirmation for stage changes/close reasons.|Generates first-touch and follow-up emails/LinkedIn messages tailored to SMB segment, persona, and pain points using approved value props and snippets; produces call openers, objection handling lines, and voicemail scripts; must be grounded in verified product/offer facts. Summarizes chat/email threads and call transcripts, highlights pain, timeline, budget cues, stakeholders, and next steps; proposes BANT/MEDDICC-style notes for CRM entry with citations to transcript where available. Given limited firmographic/technographic data, proposes likely use cases and targeted discovery questions to validate fit; helps entry-level AE prepare quickly while avoiding overconfident claims.|Requires business judgment and market context; humans set what 'good fit' means, approve claims, and ensure adherence to brand, legal (GDPR/CCPA), and industry regulations. Humans handle nuanced conversations, trust-building, negotiation, and reading tone; they validate ambiguous signals (budget authority, urgency) and manage objections ethically. Humans own stage progression, disqualification reasons, and commit/forecast inputs because errors impact revenue targets and customer experience; they arbitrate edge cases and exceptions.|Applies deterministic rules and/or classical ML on firmographics (industry, headcount), intent signals, engagement metrics, and historical conversion to score leads and flag ICP matches/non-matches; outputs explainable reasons (e.g., 'Headcount < 10'). Uses rules/record linkage to dedupe contacts/accounts, validate emails, normalize fields, and segment lists by territory/vertical; reduces wasted outreach and improves reporting accuracy. Computes weekly activity KPIs (calls, emails, meetings), conversion rates, and pipeline coverage; flags anomalies (sudden drop in connect rate) and suggests where to focus based on historical patterns.

column8: Run discovery calls to understand customer goals, current workflows, budget, and decision process, then map needs to product capabilities and document next steps in the CRM.
column15: Discovery calls combine interpersonal influence and nuanced qualification (human-led) with heavy information processing before/during/after the call (AI-assisted). Generative AI is best for language-heavy outputs: question sets, summaries, needs-to-capability drafts, and follow-up emails. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step workflows across tools (research, agenda, task creation, scheduling) and providing real-time gap coverage prompts, but should operate with guardrails and approvals. Traditional AI is well-suited for structured enrichment, scoring, entity extraction, and deterministic CRM validation/compliance flags. Assumptions: call recording/transcription is permitted; product knowledge base is available; CRM has APIs; AE at entry level benefits from coaching prompts. Constraints: privacy rules, variable audio quality, and incomplete data reduce automation reliability.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) live conversation steering, empathy, and trust-building; (2) interpreting ambiguous or political decision dynamics and validating true budget authority; (3) making commitments on pricing, timelines, security posture, and contractual terms; (4) approving any external-facing message and any CRM stage/forecast change; (5) handling edge cases like regulated industries, procurement/legal escalations, or when AI flags potential compliance issues. Humans must also verify AI summaries against the transcript to prevent incorrect CRM records that could mislead downstream teams.
column17: Autonomous actions could create incorrect CRM updates, spam prospects with premature follow-ups, or schedule meetings without proper context; real-time prompts may distract AE; emergent behavior from tool chaining can be hard to audit. Mitigations: approval gates for external actions and stage changes, least-privilege access, audit logs, rate limits, sandbox testing, and clear playbooks/stop conditions.
column18: May hallucinate requirements, mis-summarize budget/timeline, or overconfidently map needs to capabilities not actually supported; tone may be inappropriate for buyer persona; privacy risk if sensitive data is sent to external models. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved product docs, citation/quote grounding, constrained templates for CRM fields, redaction, and mandatory human review before saving/sending.
column19: Scoring/enrichment can be biased or stale, leading to misprioritization; rigid rules may fail on new segments or messy data; entity extraction errors can propagate. Mitigations: periodic model/rule recalibration, monitoring for drift, explainable thresholds, and validation against required fields with human confirmation.
column20: Data leakage across CRM/transcription/email tools; inconsistent customer identifiers causing wrong account updates; conflicting recommendations between models; latency or API failures breaking workflows mid-process. Mitigations: unified identity resolution, robust error handling and retries, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, environment separation (prod vs sandbox), and governance for data retention and consent.
column22: Pre-call orchestration (research, agenda creation, and question plan). In-call assistance workflow (real-time prompts and next-best-question). Detects keywords/topics (pricing, security, competitors), sentiment trends, talk-time ratios, and required disclosure phrases; produces explainable flags for AE review.

column8: Deliver product demos and tailored presentations for SMB buyers, address common objections, and position value and ROI using approved messaging and competitive context.
column15: The core of the task-delivering a persuasive live demo and handling objections-remains human-led due to trust, accountability, and real-time stakeholder nuance. Generative AI is strong for drafting tailored narratives, talk tracks, and objection responses, but must be constrained to approved messaging to avoid hallucinated claims. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating pre/post workflows and providing real-time prompts, yet autonomy must be limited because incorrect next-best-actions or unsanctioned follow-ups can create compliance and customer-experience risk. Traditional AI is best for deterministic retrieval (battlecards, disclaimers), segmentation, and ROI calculations/validation. Assumptions: the org has an approved messaging library, competitive battlecards, and structured CRM data; meeting transcription is permitted; and AE tools integrate with CRM and content repositories.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) any externally facing claims about product capabilities, security, pricing, or competitor comparisons; (2) tailoring strategy when buyer requirements are ambiguous, politically sensitive, or involve procurement/legal; (3) deciding concessions, timelines, and commitments; (4) handling edge cases like regulated industries, data residency, or requests for roadmap features; and (5) approving AI-assembled follow-ups before sending when content is non-standard or high-risk. Humans must also monitor for bias or inappropriate language and ensure the demo reflects the live product environment (not a simulated or outdated flow).
column17: Autonomous actions (sending follow-ups, updating CRM, recommending pivots) can be wrong or premature; may optimize for speed over compliance; opaque decisioning can reduce AE situational awareness. Mitigations: human-in-the-loop approvals, scoped permissions, audit logs, conservative policies (suggest vs act), and rollback/undo for CRM changes.
column18: May hallucinate features, ROI numbers, or competitor facts; can drift from approved messaging or adopt an overly aggressive tone. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, strict claim templates, citation requirements, red-team objection prompts, and mandatory human approval for competitive/pricing/security statements.
column19: Rules/models can become stale (new competitors, updated messaging), misclassify persona/industry, or enforce overly rigid gating that blocks legitimate statements. Mitigations: regular rule/model refresh, monitoring for false blocks, clear override workflows with logging, and validation against ground-truth outcomes.
column20: Data leakage between CRM, call transcripts, and GenAI prompts; inconsistent source-of-truth (old battlecards vs new); cascading errors where mis-segmentation leads to wrong deck and then wrong ROI framing; latency during live demos. Mitigations: centralized content governance, versioning, access controls, prompt/data minimization, end-to-end testing in sandbox, and graceful degradation (offline decks, manual mode).
column22: Pre-demo discovery orchestration and agenda planning. Real-time demo assistance and next-best-action prompts. Post-demo follow-up package assembly|Tailored slide/script drafting using approved messaging. Objection-handling response suggestions. Value/ROI storyline and examples|Live relationship management and trust-building in the demo. High-stakes judgment on positioning, concessions, and commitments. Final approval of claims, competitive statements, and ROI assertions|Prospect segmentation and persona/industry classification. Competitive battlecard retrieval and compliance gating. ROI calculator and metrics validation

Drafts ROI framing (time saved, risk reduced, revenue impact) and proposes customer-like scenarios; can summarize case studies into relevant proof points, with citations to internal sources when available.|Builds rapport, reads nuanced stakeholder dynamics, and adjusts tone/strategy in ways that require human empathy and accountability. Decides what to promise, how to handle edge-case requirements, and when to escalate (security, legal, pricing exceptions); accountable for outcomes. Ensures accuracy and compliance with company policy and local regulations; verifies that AI-generated content matches the actual product and current roadmap.|Uses rules/ML on firmographics and CRM fields to classify the buyer (industry, size band, tech stack) and map to the correct demo path and approved deck variant. Deterministically retrieves the latest approved competitive snippets, pricing guardrails, and legal disclaimers; blocks or flags disallowed claims and unapproved competitor statements. Runs spreadsheet/ML-based calculators with structured inputs (seats, hours, conversion rates) and validates ranges/outliers to reduce arithmetic errors before sharing outputs.

column8: Manage the full sales cycle from first meeting through close, including proposal creation, pricing and packaging alignment, and coordinating internal approvals to close deals on time.
column15: The end-to-end sales cycle for an entry-level SMB AE mixes repeatable process work with high-judgment customer interaction. Generative AI is strong for drafting and summarizing (proposals, recaps, talk-tracks) but must be reviewed due to hallucination and policy constraints. Traditional AI/CPQ is best for deterministic pricing, packaging eligibility, and completeness gates, providing auditability. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step workflows across CRM, CPQ, and approval systems-creating tasks, routing approvals, and tracking deadlines-yet should operate under guardrails and require confirmation before external actions. Humans remain essential for discovery, negotiation, and accountable commitments. Assumptions: the org has a CRM/CPQ stack, approved templates, and clear discount/approval policies; call recordings/notes are available for summarization; integrations allow read/write with role-based access. Uncertainty: maturity of agentic orchestration and integration depth in test_dhina; if integrations are weak, agentic share drops and human coordination rises.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) qualification and strategy: deciding ICP fit, stakeholder mapping, and whether to advance/exit; (2) customer-facing commitments: ensuring proposals, timelines, and product claims are accurate and compliant; (3) negotiation and concessions: approving discounts, non-standard terms, and tradeoffs with margin/precedent risk; (4) exception handling: unusual procurement/legal clauses, data privacy/security concerns, or escalations; (5) compliance/accountability: humans must approve final quotes/contracts and any outbound communications triggered by automation. Humans should also periodically audit AI outputs (summaries, drafts, routing decisions) and adjust playbooks/guardrails.
column17: Autonomous actions can create incorrect approvals, spam stakeholders, or advance stages prematurely; may optimize for speed over compliance; opaque decision paths complicate audits; integration errors can cascade (wrong approver, wrong quote version). Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, human-in-the-loop confirmations for external sends and stage changes, policy-based constraints, simulation/testing in sandbox, rate limits, and clear rollback procedures.
column18: May hallucinate product capabilities, pricing, or legal language; can drift from approved templates or include unapproved claims; risk of leaking sensitive customer data if prompts/logs are mishandled; tone or localization mismatches can harm trust. Mitigations: retrieval from approved knowledge base, template locking, redline/claim checkers, PII controls, mandatory human review for external docs, and logging/versioning.
column19: Rules/CPQ brittleness when pricing policies change; ML deal scoring can encode historical bias (e.g., under-scoring certain segments) and may be gamed via CRM field manipulation; false positives can waste AE time. Mitigations: governance for rule updates, monitoring drift, explainability, periodic recalibration, and using scores as advisory not determinative.
column20: Data inconsistency across CRM/CPQ/contract tools can cause mismatched versions and approval loops; unclear source of truth for pricing and terms; security risks from broad API tokens; conflicting recommendations between ML scoring and agentic playbooks. Mitigations: master-data management, document/version control, unified audit logs, strong IAM, staged rollouts, and reconciliation checks before sending final quotes/contracts.

Initiates approval requests, gathers required artifacts (redlines, discount justification), routes to correct approvers based on rules and context (deal size, region), tracks SLA timers, and nudges approvers; pauses and requests human input when exceptions occur.|Summarizes notes/call transcripts into MEDDICC/BANT-style fields, highlights pain points, stakeholders, objections, and agreed next steps; drafts recap emails for AE review. Generates first-draft proposals using approved templates, product descriptions, and customer requirements; tailors value messaging to SMB persona while enforcing brand/tone guidelines; flags missing inputs. Produces option sets (good/better/best), concession strategies, and objection handling aligned to policy; suggests language for discount justification and procurement responses, requiring human validation.|Requires nuanced judgment, trust-building, and real-time adaptation to customer cues; AE decides whether the opportunity is real and worth pursuing. High-stakes tradeoffs (margin vs. win probability), competitive dynamics, and ethical considerations require accountable human decision-making and authority. Humans must ensure accuracy of promises, align with legal/compliance, and handle edge cases (non-standard terms, escalations, sensitive accounts) where AI may be unreliable.|Applies deterministic CPQ/rules engines to compute quotes, enforce floor pricing, bundle eligibility, tax/VAT, and approval thresholds; outputs explainable rule traces for audit. Uses classical ML on CRM fields (stage age, activity counts, close dates, past win rates) to predict slippage risk and recommend pipeline hygiene actions; provides interpretable drivers. Validates that mandatory CRM fields, security questionnaires, and approval artifacts are present; runs checklist-based gating before advancing stages.

column8: Maintain accurate CRM hygiene by logging activities, updating opportunity stages, forecasting close dates, and producing weekly pipeline and performance reports for sales leadership.
column15: The task mixes structured data operations (best handled by Traditional AI), unstructured-to-structured summarization and narrative reporting (best handled by Generative AI), and multi-step workflow execution across tools with reminders/escalations (best handled by Agentic AI). However, opportunity stage, forecast category, and close-date changes are revenue-critical and often ambiguous; humans must remain accountable for final decisions and for what is appropriate to record/share. Assumptions: CRM has APIs, activity sources (calendar/call platform) are integrated, and governance allows AI-assisted drafting with audit logs. Constraints: model outputs must be reviewable; auto-write actions should be limited to low-risk fields or require approval. The split reflects that much of the mechanical work (checks, aggregation, drafting) can be automated, but final deal truth and compliance require substantial human time.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final approval of stage/forecast/close-date changes, especially for commit deals, end-of-quarter, or when signals conflict; (2) resolving edge cases like multi-threaded stakeholders, verbal-only commitments, procurement/legal delays, channel/partner deals, and partial product scope changes; (3) compliance/privacy decisions-what from transcripts/emails can be stored in CRM, redaction of PII, and adherence to retention policies; (4) correcting AI misinterpretations and providing missing context; (5) accountability to leadership-humans must stand behind the forecast and explain variances. Humans should also approve any automated outbound distribution lists and ensure metrics definitions match leadership expectations.
column17: Autonomous actions could write incorrect CRM updates, spam stakeholders with reminders, or trigger workflows at the wrong time; emergent behavior from tool chaining can cause cascading errors; auditability may be weak. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for high-impact fields, sandbox/dry-run mode, rate limits, explicit policies, comprehensive logging, and rollback/versioning of CRM changes.
column18: May hallucinate meeting outcomes, misattribute statements, or overstate deal confidence; can introduce biased or overly optimistic narratives; may leak sensitive info if prompts include PII or confidential terms. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations to source snippets, redaction/PII filters, constrained templates, confidence/uncertainty flags, and mandatory human review for revenue-impacting fields and leadership narratives.
column19: Rules can become brittle as sales process changes; anomaly models can generate false positives/negatives, leading to alert fatigue or missed issues; may encode historical bias (e.g., segment assumptions). Mitigations: periodic rule/model review, monitoring precision/recall, configurable thresholds by segment/quarter, and clear metric definitions with data lineage.
column20: Data mismatches across calendar/call tools/CRM can create incorrect joins; inconsistent field definitions can corrupt reports; prompt injection via emails/notes could manipulate GenAI outputs; latency or API failures can lead to partial updates. Mitigations: master data management, schema mapping tests, idempotent writes, reconciliation jobs, secure connectors, content sanitization, and end-to-end observability with alerts.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end CRM hygiene workflow (collect signals -> propose updates -> route for approval -> execute updates). Automated follow-up and exception handling for missing/late CRM updates. KPI computation and standardized reporting tables

Runs on a cadence, pulls latest CRM data, triggers report generation (tables + narrative), posts to Slack/Email/BI workspace, and confirms delivery/reads; adapts timing to leadership meeting schedules.|Transforms unstructured text (call transcripts, meeting notes, email threads) into concise summaries with fields like pain points, stakeholders, next steps, MEDDICC/BANT elements, and suggested tasks-flagging uncertainty when evidence is weak. Produces short justifications for stage changes, close-date shifts, and forecast category changes (commit/best case/pipeline) based on recent activity and deal signals; generates leadership-ready commentary. Converts KPI deltas (coverage, slippage, stage conversion, activity) into plain-language insights, risks, and recommended actions; tailors to SMB leadership expectations and entry-level AE context.|Requires human accountability for revenue-impacting commitments, nuanced context (politics, procurement, competitor moves), and ethical/compliance considerations; humans resolve ambiguity and ensure updates reflect reality. Humans decide what can be logged from calls/emails (PII, confidential terms), ensure adherence to company policy and regulations, and approve any sharing of insights beyond need-to-know. Leadership-facing interpretation and prioritization (what to do next, resource asks) depends on human judgment; AE aligns narrative with real-world constraints and commitments.|Applies deterministic rules and schema constraints (required fields, picklist validity, date logic), detects duplicates, enforces formatting, and produces error lists for correction. Uses statistical/ML models to flag outliers (e.g., close dates repeatedly pushed, unusually large deal for segment, stage duration anomalies) and prompts review rather than auto-changing. Reliably aggregates CRM data into metrics (pipeline by stage, weighted pipeline, activity counts, win rate, aging) and exports to dashboards/spreadsheets with consistent definitions.
