column8: Collaborate with marketing, SDRs, and customer success to coordinate handoffs, align on campaign follow-up, and ensure smooth onboarding and early adoption for new customers.
column15: This task mixes structured coordination (well-suited to automation) with relationship management and judgment-heavy alignment. Traditional AI covers deterministic routing, prioritization, and SLA monitoring. Generative AI reduces manual effort by summarizing context, drafting follow-ups, and producing agendas/recaps, but needs guardrails due to hallucination and tone risk. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step workflows across CRM/CS tools and proactively initiating remediation when onboarding risk signals appear; however, customer-facing actions and commitments should remain human-approved. Humans remain essential for negotiating expectations, resolving cross-team conflicts, and handling sensitive communications. Assumptions: the org uses a CRM (e.g., Salesforce/HubSpot), has defined handoff playbooks, and can integrate product usage/CS data; without these, agentic/traditional contributions drop and human work increases.
column16: Human oversight is required for: (1) approving any customer-facing message that includes commitments, timelines, pricing, or legal/security statements; (2) resolving conflicts between marketing/SDR/CS priorities and setting final ownership; (3) handling escalations, churn-risk conversations, and exceptions (non-standard onboarding, regulated customers, complex stakeholders); (4) validating that handoff briefs accurately reflect what was sold (SOW scope, success criteria). Humans should also periodically audit automation rules/playbooks, review edge cases (multi-product bundles, partner-led deals), and ensure compliance with privacy/consent for campaign follow-up.
column17: Over-automation may trigger wrong playbooks, spam internal teams, or schedule customer meetings without proper context; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals could prioritize SLA metrics over customer experience. Mitigations: strict policy constraints, human-in-the-loop for external actions, sandbox testing, rate limits, audit logs, and clear rollback/override mechanisms.
column18: Hallucinated or incomplete handoff summaries can cause CS to miss commitments; tone or claims in follow-up emails may be inappropriate or non-compliant; leakage of sensitive customer data if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from approved sources, citations/links to CRM fields, redaction, approval workflows, and template constraints for regulated statements.
column19: Brittle routing rules or stale ML scores can mis-prioritize follow-up, creating gaps in onboarding; anomaly detection may generate false positives/negatives. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, monitoring precision/recall, simple explainable rules for SMB segments, and feedback loops from CS outcomes.
column20: Data silos and inconsistent definitions (e.g., what counts as 'onboarded') can cause conflicting actions; identity/permission issues across CRM, marketing automation, and CS tools can expose data or break workflows; duplicated outreach if systems aren't deduped. Mitigations: shared data dictionary, single source of truth for lifecycle stage, idempotent workflow design, consent management, and end-to-end testing with observability.

Continuously watches product usage/CS tickets/meeting outcomes; when risk thresholds are met, it proposes or initiates actions (schedule enablement session, alert AE/CSM, open internal case) while keeping humans in approval loops for customer-facing commitments.|Generates a concise handoff brief from CRM notes, emails, call transcripts, and proposal/SOW, highlighting key commitments, success criteria, and open questions for CS/SDR/marketing. Creates first-touch onboarding emails, campaign follow-up templates, and in-app/FAQ snippets aligned to the customer's use case and persona; adapts tone and content for SMB context and channel (email, LinkedIn, CS ticket). Produces structured agendas for kickoff and internal alignment meetings and converts notes into action items with owners/dates to reduce coordination overhead.|Requires judgment to reconcile conflicting priorities, interpret nuance from conversations, and ensure promises made in sales are feasible and aligned with delivery. Facilitation, persuasion, and accountability-setting are interpersonal and context-heavy; humans ensure buy-in and resolve ambiguity that AI cannot reliably close.|Uses deterministic rules or classical ML scoring (industry, size, intent, engagement) to prioritize which new customers get which follow-up sequence and which team owns next action. Tracks timestamps across pipeline stages and flags deviations (e.g., no CS kickoff scheduled within X days) using rules/anomaly detection; sends reminders and dashboards.

column8: Negotiate commercial terms within guardrails, prepare order forms, and support contract execution by partnering with legal and finance to resolve standard terms and billing requirements.
column15: This task is high-stakes and policy-bound: humans must own negotiation strategy, approvals, and accountability for contractual risk. Generative AI accelerates drafting, redline summaries, and order-form text creation but cannot be trusted to be legally correct without review. Agentic AI is well-suited to orchestrating the end-to-end workflow (routing, reminders, exception handling) and ensuring steps happen in sequence, but must be constrained by permissions and guardrails. Traditional AI provides reliable deterministic validation (pricing math, required fields, template selection) and reduces clerical errors. Assumptions: the org has a maintained playbook/clause library, CRM+CPQ/CLM integrations, and clear approval matrices; without these, AI contributions drop and human work increases.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) any concession outside discount/payment guardrails, (2) acceptance of legal risk (liability caps, indemnities, data protection, governing law), (3) revenue recognition/billing structure decisions (multi-year, ramp deals, usage-based, split invoicing), (4) interpreting ambiguous customer requests and relationship/competitive context, and (5) final document verification before signature. Edge cases needing humans: conflicting contract documents (MSA vs order form), customer-provided paper with unusual clauses, public sector/procurement mandates, sanctions/export controls, and situations where AI confidence is low or source data is incomplete/outdated.
column17: Could take unauthorized actions (sending incorrect versions, triggering wrong approvals), create workflow loops, or over-optimize for speed vs risk; emergent behavior if goals are mis-specified. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, deterministic guardrails, human-in-the-loop checkpoints for exceptions, comprehensive logging, and sandbox testing with rollback/version control.
column18: May hallucinate clause meanings, invent approvals, or draft non-compliant language; can miss subtle legal nuances or jurisdictional requirements; risk of leaking confidential deal terms if prompts/logs are not controlled. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved clause library, strict citation to sources, redline-only mode, PII/secret scrubbing, and mandatory human/legal review for any contractual language.
column19: Rules can become outdated as pricing/policy changes; brittle handling of novel deal structures; false positives/negatives in anomaly detection. Mitigations: governance for rule updates, monitoring/QA, configurable thresholds, and clear escalation paths.
column20: Data mismatches between CRM/CPQ/CLM, version-control errors, and inconsistent sources of truth can cause incorrect order forms or approvals; compounded errors when GenAI drafts from stale data and agentic workflows propagate it. Mitigations: single source of truth, schema validation, document versioning, end-to-end audit trails, and reconciliation checks before signature.
column22: Orchestrate contract workflow across CRM/CLM and stakeholders. Automated guardrail checks and exception routing|Draft and refine negotiation language within approved playbooks. Summarize redlines and create issue lists for legal/finance. Populate and draft order forms/SOW text from deal inputs|Lead live negotiation and make trade-off decisions. Approve non-standard terms and ensure compliance. Final review and signature coordination|Validate structured deal data and pricing calculations. Template selection and clause library retrieval
column23: Monitors deal stage, triggers tasks (e.g., request legal review, finance approval), routes documents, follows up on blockers, and maintains an audit trail; adapts next steps based on exceptions (redlines, non-standard billing) while staying within configured permissions. Runs multi-step checks (discount thresholds, payment terms, data privacy addenda needs) and, when outside policy, opens the right approval path and compiles the required context for reviewers.|Generates customer-facing responses and redline suggestions aligned to a clause library/playbook; summarizes positions and proposes compromise options while flagging uncertainty when inputs are incomplete. Produces structured summaries of contract changes, highlights deviations from standard terms, and drafts questions/decision points for internal stakeholders to accelerate review. Transforms CRM quote data and customer requirements into draft order form language (products, quantities, term, billing cadence) and generates checklists for missing fields.|Requires judgment on concessions, relationship dynamics, competitive context, and risk appetite; accountable for final commercial position. Legal/finance and AE must approve deviations (liability, indemnity, payment terms, revenue recognition) and ensure adherence to company policy and regulations. Humans confirm accuracy of final documents, resolve last-mile ambiguities, and manage executive/customer sign-off where accountability and escalation are needed.|Deterministic rules/ML validate SKU eligibility, discount math, tax/VAT fields, billing start/end dates, and required metadata; flags anomalies and missing mandatory fields. Rules-based selection of correct order form template, jurisdictional addenda, and standard clauses based on customer attributes (region, segment, procurement requirements).

column8: Develop product and industry knowledge through enablement sessions, call coaching, and peer shadowing, then apply feedback to improve conversion rates and repeatable sales motions.
column15: The task is fundamentally developmental: building judgment, product conviction, and situational selling skill. Humans (rep, manager, peers) remain central for interpreting context, delivering nuanced coaching, and making live decisions. Generative AI is strong for summarizing enablement materials, extracting themes from call transcripts, and creating practice scenarios, accelerating learning but requiring validation to avoid hallucinations or misaligned guidance. Traditional AI reliably measures conversion and motion adherence using structured CRM/call signals, enabling objective before/after tracking. Agentic AI adds value by coordinating the learning workflow (scheduling, nudges, task creation) and closing the loop from feedback to execution, but should not autonomously change messaging or customer-facing actions without approval. Assumptions: call recording/transcription is available; CRM hygiene is adequate; enablement content is accessible; governance exists for using customer data in AI tools.
column16: Human oversight is essential to (1) validate AI-derived coaching insights against deal context and company strategy, (2) approve any customer-facing messaging changes, especially around pricing, security, compliance, and competitive claims, (3) prioritize which skill gaps to address given limited time and territory realities, and (4) manage edge cases like atypical industries, regulated customers, or novel objections where AI may generalize incorrectly. Managers should review transcript-based feedback before it becomes part of performance evaluation, and reps must retain discretion in live calls to adapt ethically and authentically.
column17: Over-automation could spam reps with tasks, optimize for proxy metrics (activity) over learning quality, or schedule actions that conflict with manager priorities; opaque decision logic. Mitigate with guardrails (approval steps), rate limits, explainable planning, and manager-configurable objectives.
column18: Hallucinated product facts or incorrect competitive claims; tone that conflicts with brand; overgeneralized coaching that ignores deal context; privacy risks if transcripts/notes are sent to non-approved models. Mitigate with approved data boundaries, retrieval from vetted enablement sources, mandatory citations, and human review for external messaging.
column19: Brittle rules for motion adherence (false positives/negatives), biased metrics if CRM data is incomplete, and misleading causal attribution (coaching vs seasonality). Mitigate with data quality checks, periodic rule/model recalibration, and using metrics as directional signals not sole truth.
column20: Feedback loops can amplify errors (GenAI insight -> Agentic tasks -> behavior change) if not validated; inconsistent definitions of stages/motions across systems; security/compliance gaps when moving transcripts between vendors. Mitigate with a single source of truth for playbooks, audit logs, human approval gates, and end-to-end data governance.
column22: Orchestrate a personalized enablement plan and weekly learning cadence. Automate capture and routing of coaching actions into CRM/task systems|Summarize enablement content into role-specific cheat sheets and talk tracks. Analyze call transcripts for coaching insights and generate improvement suggestions. Simulate role-plays and generate scenario-based practice drills|Attend enablement, shadow peers, and internalize nuanced product/market context. Deliver and calibrate coaching feedback; decide what to change in live selling. Handle high-stakes customer interactions and ensure compliant messaging|Track conversion metrics and funnel movement pre/post coaching. Detect adherence to defined sales motions using structured signals
column23: Builds a multi-week plan based on role (SMB AE entry level), current pipeline stage mix, observed skill gaps, and upcoming enablement calendar; schedules sessions, assigns modules, and adapts the plan as performance signals change. After coaching/shadowing, converts agreed actions into tasks, reminders, and playbook checklists in tools like CRM/LMS; follows up for completion and escalates blockers to the manager.|Condenses product updates, competitive intel, and industry narratives into concise messaging, objection handling, and discovery question banks tailored to SMB personas. Uses conversation context to highlight missed discovery, weak value articulation, pricing/competitor moments, and proposes alternative phrasing; produces a coaching-ready summary for manager/rep review. Creates realistic SMB prospect scenarios by industry, generates objections, and provides iterative feedback on rep responses to reinforce repeatable sales motions.|Requires judgment to interpret ambiguous customer context, prioritize what matters for territory, and build authentic conviction; learning is social and situational. Applies rules/ML classifiers on call metadata (talk/listen ratio, keyword hits, checklist completion) to flag whether required steps (agenda, discovery, next steps) occurred.

column8: Prospect and qualify SMB leads using outbound calls, emails, and social outreach, documenting fit, needs, and next steps in the CRM for consistent pipeline creation.
column15: The task mixes high-volume, semi-structured work (list hygiene, scoring, drafting, logging) with high-variance human interaction (calls, qualification judgment). Traditional AI is best for deterministic data operations (enrichment, dedupe, rules-based scoring, compliance gating). Generative AI accelerates personalization, call prep, and summarization but requires review due to hallucination/tone risk. Agentic AI adds value by coordinating cadences, monitoring signals, and keeping CRM workflows synchronized, yet must operate within strict guardrails to avoid unintended outreach. Humans remain essential for live conversations, trust, and accountable qualification decisions. Assumptions: CRM supports APIs/workflows; call recording/transcription is permitted; outreach policies (opt-in/DNC) are available as structured rules; SMB segment tolerates templated-but-personalized messaging. Constraints: deliverability limits, privacy laws, and brand risk reduce automation of sending without review.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) any outbound calling and real-time discovery where empathy, negotiation, and situational awareness matter; (2) approving or spot-checking AI-generated outreach to ensure factual accuracy (no false claims about integrations/pricing), appropriate tone, and compliance with marketing policies; (3) final qualification/disqualification and committing next steps (meeting set, handoff) because errors directly impact pipeline quality and customer trust; (4) handling opt-outs, complaints, sensitive industries, and ambiguous consent/DNC cases; (5) resolving edge cases such as conflicting firmographic data, multi-entity accounts, or when AI suggests aggressive follow-ups that could harm reputation. Recommended control points: human-in-the-loop approval for first-touch messages and any high-risk segments; mandatory review of CRM summaries before saving; escalation workflow for compliance flags.
column17: Unintended autonomous outreach (sending to wrong contacts, violating frequency caps), feedback loops that over-prioritize noisy engagement signals, and opaque decisioning. Mitigations: hard policy constraints (DNC/consent gates), rate limits, approval steps for first-touch or new segments, simulation/testing in sandbox, and audit trails with rollback.
column18: Hallucinated facts (wrong company details, product capabilities), tone mismatch, over-personalization using sensitive attributes, and leakage of confidential CRM data into prompts. Mitigations: retrieval from approved sources, strict templates/guardrails, redaction, no free-form claims, human review, and logging of prompts/outputs.
column19: Brittle rules and stale models causing mis-scoring, exclusion of good leads, or inclusion of non-ICP leads; bias via proxy variables (zip code, industry). Mitigations: periodic recalibration, explainable scoring, fairness checks, and monitoring drift/coverage.
column20: Data sync errors between CRM, sequencing tools, and enrichment providers; inconsistent lead IDs causing duplicate outreach; compounding errors where GenAI drafts based on incorrect enriched data and Agentic AI executes at scale. Mitigations: master data management, idempotent workflows, validation layers before send, unified consent source of truth, and end-to-end monitoring with alerting.
column22: Orchestrate multi-channel outreach sequences (cadences) and task queues. Automate CRM workflow updates and follow-up scheduling. Monitor engagement signals and reprioritize leads|Research-assisted personalization for outreach. Call preparation and talk tracks. Summarize interactions and draft CRM notes|Live calling, rapport building, and real-time discovery. Final qualification judgment and next-step commitment. Compliance-sensitive decisions and exception handling|Lead list building, enrichment, and de-duplication. Rules-based lead scoring and qualification checks. Performance analytics and anomaly detection on outreach metrics

Continuously watches opens/clicks/replies/website intent (where available) and adjusts priority, suggesting next-best-action (call now vs. wait vs. nurture) using policies and learned patterns.|Drafts tailored email/LinkedIn messages using approved data sources (CRM fields, firmographics, public website snippets) and rep-provided value props; produces variants for A/B testing while maintaining brand tone. Turns call transcripts, email threads, and chat logs into structured summaries (pain points, current tools, timeline, authority, next steps) and drafts CRM entries for human review.|Humans handle nuanced conversation, trust-building, and adaptive questioning; they interpret tone, negotiate access to decision-makers, and manage sensitive objections that AI cannot reliably navigate. Rep decides whether the lead is truly qualified (budget/authority/need/timing), sets meeting commitments, and determines when to disqualify-requiring accountability and contextual judgment. Humans must approve outreach in regulated contexts, handle opt-out/escalations, and ensure claims are accurate; they resolve edge cases like conflicting data or potential reputational risk.|Uses deterministic matching and classical ML to merge duplicates, validate emails, standardize company names, and enrich with firmographics (industry, size) from structured providers. Applies transparent scoring rules (ICP fit thresholds, geography, employee count) and compliance checks (DNC lists, consent flags) to gate who can be contacted and how. Tracks conversion rates by segment/cadence and flags anomalies (sudden bounce spikes, deliverability drops) using statistical methods; supports continuous improvement.

column8: Run discovery conversations to understand customer goals, current tools, budget, and decision process, and translate findings into a clear solution recommendation.
column15: Discovery for SMB sales is high-context and relationship-driven: humans must lead the live conversation, interpret nuance, and make accountable qualification/recommendation decisions. Generative AI is strong for drafting (questions, summaries, recommendation narratives) and converting unstructured conversation into usable artifacts, but needs verification due to hallucination/context drift. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating pre-call research, real-time prompts, and automating post-call workflows; however, autonomy must be constrained (approval gates) to avoid incorrect CRM updates or inappropriate outreach. Traditional AI is best for structured enforcement (required fields, routing) and stable predictive scoring/compliance checks. Assumptions: calls are recorded/transcribed with consent; CRM and product/price knowledge bases are available; guardrails restrict autonomous sending/updates without review for sensitive accounts. Edge cases: multi-stakeholder buying committees, regulated industries, unclear budget authority, or competitive displacement scenarios increase human share and reduce autonomy.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) live rapport-building and handling emotional/strategic cues; (2) confirming inferred facts (budget, authority, timeline) before they become CRM truth; (3) making qualification and pricing/packaging decisions that affect revenue recognition and customer expectations; (4) compliance-sensitive situations (recording consent, regulated data, security claims) where misstatements create legal risk; (5) edge cases like conflicting stakeholder goals, procurement/legal objections, or when AI flags low confidence/contradictions. Mandatory approvals recommended before: sending external follow-ups, changing opportunity stage, committing to capabilities/roadmap, or sharing security/compliance statements.
column17: Autonomous actions could create incorrect CRM records, spam prospects, or schedule meetings without proper context; real-time prompts may distract reps or bias questioning; emergent behavior if tools are over-permissioned. Mitigations: least-privilege tool access, approval workflows, rate limits, audit logs, sandbox testing, and clear stop conditions when confidence is low.
column18: May hallucinate customer details or mis-map needs to capabilities; can overconfidently infer budget/authority from weak signals; tone may be inappropriate for SMB owner/operators; privacy risk if transcripts/PII are sent to external models. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from approved product/pricing KB, confidence/attribution, redaction, human review for outbound content, and structured templates.
column19: Scoring/rules can be brittle as SMB segments shift; may encode historical bias (e.g., under-scoring certain industries); deterministic compliance rules can miss novel phrasing. Mitigations: periodic retraining/rule reviews, fairness testing, monitoring drift, and human override paths.
column20: Data synchronization errors between transcript, GenAI summaries, and CRM fields can propagate wrong assumptions; conflicting recommendations between scoring models and GenAI narratives; security risks from broad integrations (calendar/email/CRM). column8: Deliver product demos and tailored presentations that map features to SMB use cases, address common objections, and confirm value with key stakeholders.
column15: The core value of the task is persuasive, trust-building communication in a live setting, which remains human-led-especially for an entry-level SMB rep who must manage stakeholder dynamics and be accountable for commitments. Generative AI is strong for drafting tailored narratives, demo scripts, and objection talk tracks, accelerating prep and improving consistency, but it must be grounded in approved content to avoid incorrect claims. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step preparation and providing in-meeting retrieval/next-best-action suggestions, yet autonomy must be constrained to avoid unapproved actions. Traditional AI is reliable for structured profiling, asset recommendation, compliance gating, and post-demo triggers. Assumptions: access to a curated, up-to-date knowledge base; meeting transcription is permitted; and the org has approved messaging/claims libraries. Constraints: privacy/compliance limits on using customer data; variability in SMB contexts; and the need for human accountability in sales conversations.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) validating discovery inputs and ensuring the demo aligns with the prospect's true priorities and buying process; (2) delivering the live demo, reading stakeholder reactions, and adapting in real time beyond what transcript-only systems can infer; (3) approving any product claims, security statements, ROI calculations, timelines, pricing/discount discussions, and contractual implications; (4) handling edge cases such as regulated industries, atypical integrations, competitive takeouts, or when the prospect challenges credibility. Humans should also review AI-generated decks/talk tracks before use, and explicitly confirm that any generated sample data is synthetic and policy-compliant.
column17: Over-automation (sending materials, scheduling, or updating CRM incorrectly), acting on ambiguous signals during live calls, or retrieving/using non-approved assets; opaque decision paths and difficulty auditing actions. Mitigations: tight permissioning (read-mostly), human-in-the-loop approvals for outbound actions, action logs, sandboxed demo environments, and guardrails limiting suggestions to approved playbooks.
column18: Hallucinated features/roadmap, inaccurate security/compliance statements, or overconfident ROI claims; tone mismatch for SMB owners vs IT; leakage of sensitive prospect info if prompts are logged; and inadvertent use of copyrighted/third-party content. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, claim-checking against product/Legal FAQs, red-teaming objection responses, prompt/data handling policies, and mandatory human review for external-facing materials.
column19: Brittle rules leading to mis-segmentation or wrong asset recommendations when CRM data is incomplete/outdated; bias in lead/stakeholder scoring; and poor handling of novel objections. Mitigations: data quality checks, periodic model/rule recalibration, explainable outputs, and fallback to human judgment when confidence is low.
column20: Inconsistent source-of-truth between CRM, content repositories, and AI knowledge bases; compounding errors (bad profiling -> wrong narrative -> poor demo); security/privacy risks when piping transcripts and CRM data across vendors; and workflow failures during live demos (latency, retrieval errors). Mitigations: unified governance for approved content, versioning, end-to-end audit trails, least-privilege access, offline fallback decks, and monitoring/SLAs for real-time components.

column8: Prepare and send quotes and proposals, coordinate pricing approvals, and negotiate commercial terms within policy to close new business and renewals.
column15: This task mixes structured steps (CPQ entry, approvals, policy checks) with high-stakes judgment (negotiation, concessions, compliance). Generative AI can draft and summarize content quickly but must be grounded in approved templates and verified facts. Agentic AI is well-suited to orchestrating the multi-system workflow and chasing approvals, reducing cycle time, but should not autonomously commit to commercial terms. Traditional AI provides reliable guardrails (discount/margin thresholds, required approvals) and analytics from historical data. Humans remain essential for final accountability, relationship management, and exception handling. Assumptions: SMB sales uses CRM+CPQ, has a clause/term library, and clear approval matrices; without these, automation share drops and human share rises.
column16: Human oversight is essential at (1) requirements confirmation when inputs are ambiguous or conflicting; (2) any deviation from standard pricing/terms, including exceptions to discount floors, non-standard payment terms, or unusual liability clauses; (3) final outbound review to prevent incorrect totals, wrong legal entity, or misstatements; (4) negotiation decisions where context (competitive pressure, strategic logos, churn risk) matters; (5) compliance checkpoints (regulated industries, sanctions, privacy, export controls) and ensuring approvals are properly obtained. Edge cases: customer requests terms outside clause library; multi-entity billing; reseller/channel deals; currency/tax complexities; urgent end-of-quarter deals where process shortcuts are tempting-humans must enforce policy.
column17: Could route approvals incorrectly, spam stakeholders, or progress stages prematurely; may take unintended actions across systems if permissions are broad; opaque decisioning in exception handling. Mitigations: least-privilege access, action confirmation for binding steps, sandbox testing, clear escalation rules, audit logs, and kill switch.
column18: May hallucinate pricing, scope, or legal language; could produce inconsistent totals vs CPQ; tone may overpromise or misrepresent commitments; privacy risk if customer data is sent to external models. Mitigations: retrieval from CPQ/CRM, strict templates, redline limited to approved clause library, PII controls, mandatory human review before sending.
column19: Rules can become outdated as pricing/policy changes; brittle handling of novel deal structures; biased win-rate models if historical data reflects past discounting practices. Mitigations: governance for rule updates, monitoring drift, periodic model retraining, and human override with documented rationale.
column20: Mismatch between CPQ numbers and GenAI-generated text; inconsistent source-of-truth across CRM/CPQ/contracting; workflow deadlocks when agentic orchestration meets manual approvals; security/compliance gaps in data flows. Mitigations: single source-of-truth (CPQ) for commercial fields, automated reconciliation checks, robust API contracts, end-to-end audit trail, and staged rollout with exception reporting.
column22: Orchestrate quote-to-cash workflow across systems (CRM->CPQ->approvals->e-sign->order handoff). Approval coordination and follow-up automation. Negotiate and agree final terms within policy; decide concessions. Final review and approval of outbound quote/proposal (especially exceptions). Handle exceptions and escalations (legal/finance/product)|Policy/guardrail checks on pricing and terms. Pricing analytics and win-rate guidance. Data validation and document assembly from structured fields

Runs multi-step analysis of concessions, renewal history, and policy guardrails to propose counteroffer options and recommended sequencing (e.g., extend term vs discount); does not commit without human confirmation.|Generates tailored proposal text (value summary, scope, assumptions, exclusions) and outreach emails using product, industry, and opportunity notes; adapts tone for SMB buyers and ensures required sections are present via templates. Converts call transcripts/notes into structured fields (products, quantities, term, billing, special terms) and highlights ambiguities/questions for the rep before CPQ entry. Human must verify accuracy, compliance, and customer-specific nuances; mandatory for non-standard terms, high discounts, regulated customers, or ambiguous requirements. Cross-functional alignment and accountability for risk acceptance cannot be delegated fully to AI; humans resolve conflicts and approve deviations.|Deterministic rules/ML classifiers validate discount thresholds, margin floors, required approvals, renewal uplift rules, and prohibited terms; produces explainable pass/fail and required approver list. Uses historical deal data to estimate probability of win at different price points, recommend standard bundles, and detect outlier discounts; outputs structured recommendations for the rep/deal desk. Ensures required fields are complete (tax, entity name, address, SKU mapping), merges CPQ outputs into approved proposal templates, and checks for inconsistencies (dates, totals, currency).

column8: Manage the sales pipeline in the CRM by updating stages, forecasting close dates, and reporting weekly progress to support accurate team forecasting.
column15: Pipeline management mixes structured data operations (best handled by Traditional AI), multi-step workflow execution across CRM/Slack/email (Agentic AI), and narrative summarization/standardization of notes (Generative AI). However, forecast accuracy ultimately depends on human judgment about deal reality, internal politics, and commitment levels, especially for SMB where signals can be noisy and deal cycles volatile. Assumptions: CRM has reliable activity logging; integrations/APIs exist; governance allows AI-assisted updates with audit trails; rep remains accountable for final forecast inputs.
column16: Humans must approve any stage/close-date changes that affect forecast commitments, especially for late-stage deals, quarter-end, or high-value opportunities; validate AI-suggested rationales against real buyer conversations; handle exceptions (partner-led deals, split opportunities, procurement delays) and ensure compliance with data policies (no sensitive personal data in notes). Human oversight is essential for resolving conflicting signals (e.g., positive emails but no meeting scheduled), preventing over-optimistic automation, and providing managerial context and escalation decisions.
column17: Over-automation could change CRM fields incorrectly, spam reps with prompts, or create cascading workflow errors across systems; emergent behavior from poorly constrained goals (e.g., optimizing 'hygiene' by closing deals). Mitigate with least-privilege permissions, approval gates for write actions, rate limits, sandbox testing, and full audit logs/rollback.
column18: May hallucinate deal facts or infer intent not supported by CRM data; can introduce tone/wording that misrepresents certainty; privacy risk if it ingests emails/call transcripts without proper consent and redaction. Mitigate via retrieval-grounded generation, citations to source fields, redaction/DLP, and mandatory human review for forecast-impacting statements.
column19: Rules can become outdated as sales process changes; anomaly models may flag too many false positives or miss novel patterns; probability tables can encode bias or miscalibration. Mitigate with periodic rule reviews, monitoring precision/recall, recalibration by segment, and transparent explainability.
column20: Data mismatches between CRM, email, and BI can cause inconsistent reporting; unclear ownership of 'source of truth'; feedback loops where AI suggestions influence human inputs and degrade model validity. Mitigate with data contracts, single-source governance, versioned metrics definitions, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and separation between recommendation and committed forecast fields.
column22: Orchestrate pipeline hygiene workflow (monitor -> prompt -> update). Autonomously compile weekly pipeline pack and route for review|Draft weekly progress narrative and deal-by-deal commentary. Suggest close-date and stage adjustments with rationale (decision support). Normalize and rewrite CRM notes into consistent, searchable updates|Make final judgment on stage, probability, and close date for each deal. Resolve ambiguous/edge-case deals and ensure compliance. Communicate risks and mitigation plans to manager/team|Detect stale or inconsistent CRM records (rules/anomaly detection). Forecast rollups and probability-weighted pipeline calculations. Data validation and deduplication
column23: Runs a scheduled workflow that checks for stale opportunities, missing fields, or overdue next steps; pings the rep in Slack/CRM, collects confirmations, and (with permissions) applies updates or creates tasks, adapting prompts based on deal stage and prior behavior. Pulls CRM data, applies agreed logic (e.g., stage definitions, forecast categories), generates a draft report, routes it to the rep/manager for approval, and posts to the team channel or BI tool after sign-off.|Summarizes changes since last week (stage moves, slipped dates, new risks) and produces a concise narrative for managers; can generate per-deal notes from call summaries/emails when provided as grounded context. Given structured signals (last activity, next meeting date, buyer intent notes), proposes likely close-date ranges and stage changes, explaining assumptions; outputs are recommendations requiring human confirmation. Transforms messy rep notes into standardized MEDDICC/BANT-style fields and concise next-step entries to improve downstream forecasting and handoffs.|Requires contextual understanding of buyer politics, competitive dynamics, and real-time signals not fully captured in CRM; accountable for forecast commitments. Handles exceptions (multi-entity deals, renewals vs expansion, channel influence), ensures notes don't include prohibited personal data, and approves any externally shared reporting. Human-to-human alignment is needed to negotiate resources, escalate issues, and commit to actions; AI can draft but not own accountability.|Uses deterministic rules (e.g., no activity in 14 days, close date in past, stage-age thresholds) and simple ML anomaly detection to flag deals needing attention; produces explainable alerts. Computes forecast totals by stage/category, weighted pipeline, and week-over-week deltas using predefined probability tables or calibrated logistic regression; outputs feed dashboards. column8: Coordinate with onboarding, support, and account management to ensure smooth handoffs, set customer expectations, and reduce early churn risk after close.
column15: This task mixes relationship-critical judgment with repeatable coordination. Traditional AI is best for structured checks (required fields, segmentation, churn-risk flags) that are deterministic and auditable. Generative AI accelerates drafting and summarization (handoff briefs, expectation-setting emails, agendas/notes) but needs verification due to hallucination/context drift. Agentic AI adds value by executing and monitoring the multi-step workflow across tools (CRM, ticketing, project management, calendar, Slack) and escalating blockers; however, autonomy must be bounded by policies. Humans remain essential for negotiating expectations, handling escalations, and approving commitments because errors directly impact trust and churn. Assumptions: SMB sales rep has access to CRM notes/call transcripts; onboarding/support/AM use standard systems with APIs; playbooks exist or can be defined. Constraints: regulated customers or bespoke contracts increase required human oversight and reduce automation.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) validating any customer-facing promises (timelines, scope, integrations, SLAs) against contract and delivery capacity; (2) resolving discrepancies between sales narrative and implementation reality; (3) handling escalations, dissatisfaction, or executive communications where empathy and negotiation are required; (4) approving exceptions to standard playbooks (discount-driven urgency, custom security reviews, data processing terms). Edge cases needing humans: incomplete/contradictory CRM data, multi-product bundles with dependencies, customers with legal/security constraints, and situations where AI-generated summaries could omit a critical commitment. Humans should also approve any agentic actions that change ownership, commit dates, or send external messages beyond templates.
column17: Could take unintended actions (wrong ticket routing, spamming stakeholders, scheduling without consent) or amplify errors at scale; limited transparency in decision paths. column18: May hallucinate commitments, misstate contract terms, or produce tone-inappropriate messages; could leak sensitive data if prompts include PII or confidential pricing. Mitigations: retrieval from approved sources (contract/CRM), constrained templates, redaction, mandatory human approval for external sends, and logging.
column19: Rules/models can be brittle or biased (e.g., discount depth as proxy for risk), leading to false positives/negatives; requires ongoing maintenance as products/playbooks change. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, monitoring drift, explainable features, and human override.
column20: Data inconsistency across CRM/onboarding/support tools can cause incorrect summaries or workflow triggers; identity matching errors (wrong account/contact) and race conditions (status updates) can break handoffs. Mitigations: master data management, idempotent workflow design, validation layers, clear system-of-record definitions, and end-to-end monitoring/alerts.

Finds mutually available times, proposes agendas, sends calendar invites, and issues reminders; reschedules when conflicts arise; ensures required attendees (onboarding lead, CSM/AM, support specialist) are included based on customer needs.|Drafts tailored communications using deal notes, product scope, timelines, and known constraints; produces clear milestones, responsibilities (RACI-lite), and success criteria; offers variants by tone and channel. Creates a concise handoff brief from call transcripts, emails, and CRM notes: objectives, stakeholders, use cases, integrations, commercial terms, and any commitments; highlights ambiguities for human confirmation. Generates structured agendas for kickoff/handoff calls and converts meeting transcripts into decisions, action items, owners, and dates; drafts follow-up messages to keep momentum.|Requires judgment, negotiation, and accountability-especially when commitments are ambiguous, timelines are unrealistic, or scope needs adjustment; humans manage relationship risk and approvals. Humans must manage high-stakes conversations (executive sponsors, dissatisfied customers), interpret nuance, and make trade-offs; ensures empathy and brand alignment. Human review is needed to prevent inaccuracies, ensure compliance (privacy/contractual terms), and validate that internal teams accept responsibilities and timelines.|Uses deterministic rules and/or classical ML on structured signals (time-to-close, discount depth, product fit tags, missing onboarding prerequisites, low engagement, support history) to flag accounts needing tighter expectation management. Applies rules to ensure required fields are present (billing, technical contacts, provisioning details, scope, start date); detects inconsistencies (SKU vs entitlement, contract dates) and routes exceptions. Classifies customers into onboarding tracks (standard vs complex integration, regulated industry, multi-site) using structured attributes to select the correct checklist, SLAs, and required roles.

column8: Maintain compliance with internal sales policies and industry standards by following approval workflows, keeping records complete, and completing required training.
column15: This compliance task mixes deterministic checks (best for Traditional AI), content understanding and drafting (best for Generative AI), and multi-step workflow coordination across CRM/CPQ/LMS (best for Agentic AI). However, accountability for approvals, attestations, and nuanced ethical/legal judgment remains with humans, especially for exceptions and high-risk deals. Assumptions: the SMB sales stack includes CRM/CPQ, document repository, and LMS with APIs; policies are codified into rules and maintained; GenAI is retrieval-grounded with citations; agentic actions are constrained by guardrails and approval gates.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving exceptions/non-standard terms where policy assigns accountable signatories; (2) interpreting ambiguous situations (gifts/entertainment, public sector rules, data privacy constraints, suspected fraud) and deciding escalation; (3) validating AI-generated summaries/drafts before submission to approvers to avoid misrepresentation; (4) maintaining policy intent when rules conflict or are outdated; and (5) ensuring the rep personally completes training/attestations and applies compliant conduct in customer interactions. Edge cases: cross-border deals with conflicting regulations, new products without established rules, and urgent end-of-quarter pressure where bypass attempts may occur.
column17: Could over-enforce or mis-route approvals, block legitimate deals, or take unintended actions (e.g., sending requests to wrong approver) due to brittle integrations or goal mis-specification; mitigation: least-privilege permissions, action allowlists, simulation/testing, human-in-the-loop gates for irreversible actions, and comprehensive audit logs.
column18: May hallucinate policy requirements, omit jurisdiction-specific constraints, or produce persuasive but incorrect justifications; privacy risk if sensitive customer data is included in prompts; mitigation: retrieval-augmented generation with citations, strict data minimization, redaction, and mandatory human review for submissions/attestations.
column19: Rules can become outdated as policies change, causing false positives/negatives; rigid validation may not handle novel deal structures; mitigation: governance for rule updates, versioning, exception pathways, monitoring of override rates, and periodic audits.
column20: Inconsistent source-of-truth across CRM/CPQ/LMS can create gaps (e.g., training completion not reflected), and conflicting outputs between rule engine and GenAI guidance can confuse reps; mitigation: unified policy taxonomy, event-driven synchronization, clear precedence (rules > guidance), end-to-end audit trails, and fallback procedures when systems are unavailable.
column22: Orchestrate approval workflows for quotes/discounts/contract terms. Proactive compliance task management for the rep|Summarize and explain relevant policies/standards in context. Draft compliant documentation and record notes. Create micro-learning and quiz prep from training materials|Make judgment calls on exceptions and ethical/compliance gray areas. Provide final approvals where policy requires accountable sign-off. Complete training attestations and apply learning in real interactions|Policy rule checks and field validation in CRM/CPQ. Compliance monitoring dashboards and anomaly detection
column23: Monitors CRM/CPQ opportunities for policy-triggering conditions (e.g., discount thresholds, non-standard terms), routes items to the correct approvers, tracks SLA timers, nudges stakeholders, and updates status fields; escalates when deadlines are missed while keeping an auditable trail. Builds a personalized compliance checklist per deal and per period (e.g., quarterly attestations), creates tasks in CRM, reminds the rep, and blocks downstream steps (e.g., sending order form) until required approvals/fields are complete, subject to configured guardrails.|Given the deal context (industry, product, region), retrieves policy snippets and generates plain-language guidance, highlighting what approvals/clauses/training apply; provides links/citations to source policy docs to reduce misinterpretation. Generates first-draft call notes, justification memos for exceptions, and approval request narratives using structured inputs; formats to internal templates and prompts the rep for missing required details. Turns internal training PDFs/videos into short study guides, flashcards, and scenario questions tailored to the rep's role and recent mistakes (e.g., missing fields), improving completion and retention.|Humans must interpret intent and context (e.g., potential bribery red flags, customer pressure, unusual contract terms) and decide whether to proceed, escalate to Legal/Compliance, or halt the deal. Managers/Legal/Finance must approve non-standard terms and high-risk deals; accountability and regulatory expectations typically require a human approver. The rep must personally complete required training/attestations and demonstrate compliant behavior in customer communications; AI can assist but cannot assume responsibility.|Deterministic rules validate mandatory fields, required attachments, approval thresholds, and prohibited combinations (e.g., discount > X without VP approval); produces explainable pass/fail results and prevents submission when invalid. Aggregates structured data to report completion rates (training, approvals, record completeness) and flags anomalies (e.g., unusually high discounts, repeated exceptions) using simple statistical/ML models with clear thresholds.
column41: Sales Ops Governance, Documentation, and Systems

column8: Contribute to process improvement by sharing win-loss insights, updating talk tracks, and participating in team enablement sessions to improve conversion rates.
column15: The task blends analysis (what changed), synthesis (why it changed), and organizational change (updating messaging and enabling reps). Traditional AI is best for structured funnel metrics and consistent tagging. Generative AI excels at summarizing unstructured win/loss evidence and drafting talk tracks/materials quickly, but needs grounding and review. Agentic AI adds value by automating the end-to-end cadence-pulling data, triggering drafts, routing for approvals, tracking adoption, and monitoring impact-yet should be constrained by guardrails. Humans remain essential for interpreting nuance, making final messaging decisions, and delivering coaching. Assumptions: access to CRM/call data, a standardized win/loss taxonomy, and an approval workflow; entry-level reps contribute inputs while enablement/manager owns final decisions.
column16: Human oversight is required to (1) confirm causal narratives vs. correlations (e.g., seasonality, territory changes), (2) approve any externally used talk tracks/battlecards for accuracy, brand, and legal/compliance (claims, competitor comparisons, pricing/discount guidance), (3) handle edge cases like strategic accounts, sensitive losses, or escalations, and (4) run live enablement/coaching where interpersonal dynamics and real-time judgment matter. Humans should also audit data access/privacy (call recordings, PII) and decide when insights warrant process changes (routing, qualification criteria).
column17: Autonomous workflows could spam stakeholders, create incorrect tickets, or propagate flawed recommendations at scale; may take actions beyond intent if permissions are broad. Mitigations: least-privilege access, explicit action approvals (human-in-the-loop) for external-facing changes, rate limits, audit logs, and sandbox testing with rollback.
column18: May hallucinate win/loss reasons, misquote customers, or overgeneralize from small samples; can introduce non-compliant claims or inappropriate competitor language; risk of leaking sensitive customer info in prompts/outputs. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations to source snippets, redaction/PII controls, approved messaging libraries, and mandatory human review before publication.
column19: Brittle tagging rules or biased classifiers can mislabel reasons, masking true drivers; dashboards can incentivize gaming metrics; models degrade with process changes. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, human spot-checking, clear definitions, and monitoring for drift and fairness across segments/reps.
column20: Mismatched taxonomies between CRM fields and GenAI summaries can create inconsistent reporting; feedback loops (AI-generated talk tracks influencing outcomes) can confound measurement; data pipeline failures can lead to stale insights. Mitigations: single source of truth for reason codes, versioning of talk tracks, controlled experiments (A/B), end-to-end monitoring, and governance over data/permissions.
column22: Orchestrate win/loss data collection across systems. Run continuous improvement workflow and route recommendations|Summarize win-loss narratives from unstructured sources. Draft and iterate talk tracks, objection handling, and battlecards. Create enablement materials and session agendas|Validate insights and provide contextual judgment. Approve final talk tracks and ensure compliance/brand fit. Facilitate enablement sessions and coach behavior change|Quantify funnel and conversion metrics; detect shifts. Classify and tag win/loss reasons in structured fields
column23: Connects to CRM, call-recording, email, and meeting tools; pulls relevant opportunity, activity, and outcome data on a schedule; flags missing fields and requests completion from reps via workflow prompts. Plans a recurring cadence (weekly/monthly) to generate insights, open tickets for talk-track updates, notify enablement owners, and track adoption/impact; adapts priorities based on observed conversion changes.|Transcribes/summarizes call snippets, notes, and emails into concise win/loss reasons, objections, competitor mentions, and decision criteria; produces rep- and segment-level themes. Generates updated messaging variants tailored to SMB personas/industries, incorporating discovered themes; proposes A/B versions and coaching tips while keeping within approved brand/compliance constraints. Drafts slide outlines, role-play scripts, quizzes, and recap emails; converts insights into teachable modules for entry-level reps.|Humans interpret nuance (politics, relationship dynamics, true decision drivers), challenge spurious correlations, and ensure insights reflect reality rather than data artifacts. Managers/enablement/legal approve customer-facing messaging, especially around claims, pricing, competitors, and regulated industries; humans own accountability. Live coaching, role-play feedback, and culture-building require human empathy and credibility; humans handle sensitive performance discussions and tailor coaching in real time.|Uses dashboards, statistical tests, and anomaly detection on structured CRM data to identify conversion-rate changes by segment, channel, rep cohort, and stage; outputs explainable trend reports. Applies rules/ML classifiers to map notes/tags to standardized reason codes (price, product gap, timing, competitor, no decision) to enable consistent reporting and benchmarking.

column8: Partner with Sales, Marketing, Product, and Operations stakeholders to align SMB sales priorities, coordinate execution, and deliver measurable outcomes that support quarterly business objectives.
column15: This task is primarily cross-functional leadership: aligning priorities, negotiating trade-offs, and ensuring accountable execution toward quarterly objectives. AI can materially reduce coordination overhead and improve measurement rigor, but cannot fully replace human judgment, authority, and relationship management. Generative AI is best for synthesis and drafting (alignment memos, agendas, KPI definitions). Agentic AI is best for ongoing orchestration (action registers, nudges, status collection, escalation) within guardrails and approval steps. Traditional AI is best for deterministic KPI computation, dashboards, and forecasting from structured CRM/BI data. Assumptions: the org has reasonably integrated systems (CRM, project management, BI) and allows AI access under governance; stakeholders accept AI-generated drafts and automated reminders. Constraints: data quality and metric definitions vary across functions; decisions often require executive context not present in systems.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) final prioritization and trade-offs (e.g., pipeline vs retention vs product debt) because accountability and strategic context sit with the lead; (2) negotiation and conflict resolution across Sales/Marketing/Product/Ops, where trust, incentives, and politics matter; (3) approvals for commitments that affect customers, pricing, legal/compliance, or brand messaging; (4) interpreting ambiguous or contradictory data (e.g., attribution disputes, noisy forecasts) and deciding when to override AI recommendations; (5) edge cases such as major launch slips, outages, or reputational risks where escalation paths and communications must be carefully managed.
column17: Over-automation could spam stakeholders, create meeting churn, or escalate incorrectly; unintended actions if connected to calendars/project tools without strict permissions; opaque prioritization logic leading to mistrust; feedback loops where it optimizes for easy-to-measure KPIs at the expense of strategy. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for scheduling/escalations, rate limits, audit logs, and explicit objective/constraint configuration.
column18: Hallucinated facts/metrics in alignment docs; tone or positioning misaligned with brand/compliance; leakage of sensitive pipeline/customer data if prompts are not governed; over-summarization that drops dissenting stakeholder views. Mitigations: retrieval from approved sources, citation requirements, redaction, human review for exec/customer-facing artifacts, and prompt templates with metric definitions.
column19: Brittle rules and models degrade with process changes (new stages, new segments); biased forecasts if historical data reflects past territory/lead allocation inequities; false alerts from noisy data leading to alert fatigue. Mitigations: monitoring/retuning, periodic rule reviews, data quality checks, and fairness/segment performance audits.
column20: Metric mismatches across BI/CRM/marketing attribution can cause conflicting 'truth'; GenAI may draft narratives inconsistent with dashboard numbers; agentic workflows may act on stale data; security/compliance gaps when connecting multiple SaaS systems. Mitigations: single source of truth for KPIs, shared metric dictionary, data freshness SLAs, end-to-end access governance, and automated reconciliation checks with human sign-off on quarterly targets.
column22: Cross-functional workflow orchestration (cadence, owners, dependencies). Automated status collection and escalation. Scenario planning and re-prioritization proposals|Synthesize stakeholder inputs into aligned priorities and narratives. Draft cross-functional communications and enablement artifacts. Define measurable outcomes and draft KPI/OKR wording|Set final priorities and make trade-off decisions. Relationship management, negotiation, and alignment in live forums. Approve commitments, messaging, and compliance-sensitive actions. Handle edge cases and escalations (customer-impacting, reputational risk)|KPI tracking, dashboards, and variance detection. Forecasting and pipeline health analytics. Stakeholder/task routing rules
column23: Creates and maintains an execution plan across Sales/Marketing/Product/Ops: proposes meeting cadence, assigns draft owners, tracks dependencies/risks, nudges stakeholders, and updates a shared action register based on signals from calendars, project tools, and CRM-while routing exceptions to the lead. Polls systems (Jira/Asana, CRM, marketing automation, support ops) for progress vs plan, detects slippage, and escalates blockers with recommended next actions (e.g., schedule a decision meeting, request resources). Given constraints (capacity, pipeline coverage, launch dates), runs what-if plans and proposes re-prioritization options with trade-offs; does not finalize decisions without human approval.|Summarizes notes, emails, and docs into a coherent set of SMB sales priorities, articulating rationale tied to quarterly OKRs, and drafts alignment memos that reflect each function's goals. Generates agendas, pre-reads, decision logs, QBR/MBR slides, launch briefs, and internal FAQs; adapts tone for Sales vs Product vs Ops and highlights open questions. Proposes KPI definitions (e.g., pipeline creation, conversion, CAC payback, activation), measurement approaches, and instrumentation questions; flags ambiguous metrics for clarification.|Requires judgment to balance competing functional goals, interpret ambiguous signals, and make accountable decisions under uncertainty (budget, headcount, strategic bets). Trust-building, conflict resolution, and influencing peers/executives are inherently human; nuanced context and organizational politics are hard to automate safely. Humans must approve externally impactful commitments (pricing, promises to customers), ensure legal/compliance alignment, and validate that plans match real-world constraints. When outcomes affect customers or revenue materially, human oversight is needed to assess risk, choose mitigations, and communicate decisions.|Uses BI/analytics models and rules to compute weekly KPI performance vs targets, segment by channel/region/rep, and trigger alerts when thresholds are breached. Applies classical ML/time-series and rules (stage-weighting, seasonality) to forecast SMB bookings/pipeline coverage and identify leading indicators (win rate, cycle time). Deterministic rules map requests to the right owner/team (e.g., pricing exceptions to RevOps, collateral to Marketing) and enforce SLAs for follow-ups.

column8: Lead day-to-day team execution by setting weekly goals, assigning territories and accounts, and removing blockers so the team consistently meets activity and pipeline targets.
column15: This Sales Team Lead task mixes judgment-heavy leadership (goal setting trade-offs, fairness in assignments, coaching/escalation) with data-heavy operational cadence (KPI tracking, scoring, monitoring). Traditional AI is well-suited for reliable KPI computation, anomaly detection, and constrained allocation recommendations using structured CRM/BI data. Generative AI adds value by turning data and qualitative inputs into clear briefs, rationales, and blocker summaries, but must be grounded to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI can run the weekly operating rhythm and monitoring/alerting loops across tools, but should operate within guardrails (no unilateral reassignment or customer-impacting actions). Humans remain accountable for commitments, exceptions, and cross-functional influence. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/activity data, defined territory rules, and integration with Slack/Email/CRM; without these, AI contributions drop and human workload rises.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving weekly goals that affect commitments to leadership and compensation-linked targets; (2) final territory/account ownership decisions, especially when reallocations impact rep morale, customer relationships, or contractual constraints; (3) handling sensitive exceptions (VIP accounts, legal/privacy constraints, partner/channel conflicts, discrimination/fairness concerns); (4) coaching and performance management, where tone, trust, and individualized development plans matter; (5) approving any agentic actions that could change CRM ownership, trigger outreach at scale, or escalate to customers. Humans should also adjudicate when data is incomplete (new reps, new markets) or when models conflict with frontline reality.
column17: Could over-automate workflow steps (e.g., reassign accounts, spam reps with nudges), amplify bad data, or create cascading actions across systems; limited transparency in why it took actions. Mitigations: permissioning/guardrails (read-only by default), approval gates for ownership changes and mass actions, rate limits, audit logs, simulation mode, and clear rollback procedures.
column18: May hallucinate KPI explanations or invent account context; can produce biased or inappropriate messaging for reps; risk of leaking sensitive customer/employee data if prompts include PII. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from approved sources, strict redaction/PII controls, templates with required citations/links to dashboards, and human review for high-stakes briefs.
column19: Brittle rules and models can become stale as ICP/territory strategy changes; optimization may encode unfairness (e.g., consistently giving best accounts to top reps) and can mis-handle edge cases like split territories. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, fairness constraints, explainability dashboards, and exception handling workflows.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs KPI truth), data latency between CRM/BI, identity/permission mismatches, and feedback loops where agentic actions change the data used for scoring. Mitigations: single source of truth for metrics, event-driven architecture with timestamps, consistent role-based access control, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and monitoring for drift and unintended incentives.
column22: Orchestrate weekly execution workflow (data pulls -> draft goals -> draft assignments -> publish -> monitor). Continuous monitoring and proactive escalation of blockers. Automated task assignment and follow-up nudges within guardrails|Draft weekly goals narrative and team brief. Generate account/territory rationale and talking points for rep enablement. Summarize blockers and propose resolution options|Set strategic priorities and approve weekly goals/targets. Finalize territory/account assignments and handle exceptions. Remove high-impact blockers via cross-functional influence and coaching. Compliance, ethics, and customer-impact review|Compute activity and pipeline KPIs; detect anomalies vs targets. Territory/account scoring and capacity-based allocation recommendations. Blocker classification from structured signals
column23: Connects to CRM, sales engagement, and BI tools to run a repeatable weekly cadence: pulls latest pipeline/activity data, triggers analyses, drafts plans, routes for approvals, publishes to team channels, and sets monitoring/alerts. Adapts steps based on exceptions (e.g., rep PTO, territory changes). Watches leading indicators (activity shortfalls, stalled opp stages, SLA breaches on leads, meeting no-show rates) and opens tickets/Slack threads, schedules coaching slots, or requests enablement/ops support; proposes next-best actions while keeping humans in the loop for approvals. Creates/updates tasks in CRM (call lists, follow-ups, sequence enrollments) based on approved territory/account plans and rep capacity; sends reminders and rebalances suggestions when capacity changes, without unapproved reassignment.|Generates a clear weekly plan summary (targets, focus segments, key plays, risks) tailored to SMB context using structured inputs (dashboards, prior week outcomes). Produces manager-ready messaging for standups and written updates. Creates concise rationales for why accounts are prioritized, suggested outreach angles, and objection-handling prompts based on firmographics, intent signals, and past interactions; helps reps execute consistently. Turns qualitative inputs (rep notes, call snippets, Slack threads) into categorized blocker summaries (product, pricing, process, lead quality) and suggests resolution paths and owners; flags uncertainty when evidence is thin.|Requires judgment on trade-offs (pipeline vs activity, strategic accounts, quarter-end pushes), alignment with leadership, and accountability for commitments; humans validate assumptions and adjust for context not in data. Humans must manage fairness, rep development, relationship continuity, and sensitive account ownership changes; also handle edge cases (key customer risk, partner conflicts) and ensure policy compliance. Many blockers require negotiation, escalation, and coaching (performance issues, interpersonal conflicts, bespoke deal strategy) that depend on trust, nuance, and organizational authority. Ensures assignments and outreach plans comply with privacy/consent, anti-discrimination, and internal policies; approves any automated actions that could affect customers or rep compensation.|Uses deterministic rules/SQL and classical ML for forecasting/propensity to calculate weekly targets, attainment, conversion rates, and to flag outliers (e.g., sudden drop in meetings set) with explainable thresholds. Applies scoring models (lead/opportunity propensity, ICP fit, recency) and optimization heuristics (round-robin with constraints, workload balancing) to recommend assignments under defined business rules (geo, vertical, named accounts). Uses rule-based tagging (e.g., stage aging, missing next step, pricing approval pending) to categorize common blockers and route them to the right queue (RevOps, enablement, product).
column41: Accounting Team Leadership and Performance Management

column8: Coach and develop SMB sales representatives through call reviews, deal strategy sessions, and skill-building plans to improve conversion rates and overall sales performance.
column15: This task blends qualitative leadership with repeatable analysis and workflow. Traditional AI is strong for reliable KPI computation, funnel diagnostics, and explainable scoring from structured data and speech analytics. Generative AI adds leverage by summarizing calls, drafting coaching materials, and producing structured deal briefs-high value but requires verification due to hallucination/tone risks. Agentic AI is useful to orchestrate the end-to-end coaching cadence (selecting calls/deals, scheduling, tasking, nudging, tracking), but autonomy should be bounded by policies and approvals. Humans remain essential for trust-based coaching delivery, interpreting nuance, making accountable commercial decisions, and ensuring fairness/compliance. Assumptions: calls are recorded with consent; CRM data quality is moderate; playbooks/rubrics exist; tooling integrations are available. If data quality or consent is lacking, AI contributions drop and human work increases.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final coaching feedback in sensitive/high-impact situations (new hires, underperformance, PIPs), (2) decisions that affect pricing, commitments, or customer relationships, (3) validating call summaries/insights before they are used in evaluations, (4) monitoring bias in speech analytics (accent, dialect, gendered communication styles) and ensuring equitable coaching, (5) approving any agentic actions that contact reps/customers or change CRM/forecast fields, and (6) handling edge cases like incomplete recordings, sarcasm, multi-party calls, regulated disclosures, or confidential customer data. Humans should set rubrics, thresholds, and escalation rules, and periodically audit outputs.
column17: Over-automation leading to inappropriate scheduling/nudges, mis-prioritization of reps, or unintended CRM updates; opaque decision logic; cascading errors if it acts on flawed summaries; security risks from broad tool permissions. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for high-impact actions, sandbox testing, clear policies/guardrails, audit logs, and fallback to manual workflows on anomalies.
column18: Hallucinated call details or incorrect attribution of who said what; tone mismatch that demotivates reps; leakage of sensitive customer/employee data if prompts include PII; bias in generated feedback (e.g., overemphasis on style vs substance). Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation grounded in transcripts/CRM, citation of timestamps, redaction/PII controls, approved coaching templates, and mandatory human review for performance documentation.
column19: Brittle rules that don't generalize across segments; model drift as messaging/playbooks change; speech analytics inaccuracies for accents/noisy audio; false confidence from dashboards when data entry is inconsistent. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, data quality checks, confidence intervals, human spot-checks, and allowing reps to contest/annotate scores.
column20: Feedback loops where GenAI summaries feed agentic actions without verification; inconsistent definitions of KPIs across systems; privacy/compliance gaps when moving call data between vendors; conflicting recommendations (dashboard vs narrative). Mitigations: single source of truth for metrics, schema/ontology alignment, end-to-end governance (consent, retention), human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and monitoring for outcome regressions after rollout.

Personalized coaching plan execution and nudging|Summarize call recordings and extract coaching moments. Generate role-play scripts, objection-handling drills, and skill-building content. Make final judgments on deal strategy and commercial trade-offs. Validate AI outputs and ensure fairness/compliance in coaching. Handle edge cases and sensitive conversations|Compute and monitor KPI dashboards and conversion funnels. Speech/interaction analytics and rule-based compliance checks. Rep segmentation and coaching prioritization scoring
