Maintains per-rep development plans, monitors leading indicators (activity, stage conversion, talk ratios), and triggers adaptive interventions (micro-lessons, practice prompts, manager check-ins) based on observed gaps and timelines.|Transcribes/summarizes calls, highlights objection handling, discovery depth, next-step setting, and tone; drafts annotated snippets and coaching notes aligned to the team's playbook. Creates tailored practice scenarios by segment/industry, produces talk tracks, question banks, and feedback rubrics; adapts difficulty based on rep level and recent performance. Synthesizes CRM notes, stakeholder maps, and call summaries into a structured deal review doc (risks, gaps, mutual plan, next actions) and proposes an agenda for strategy sessions. Turns metrics and qualitative observations into clear coaching narratives, strengths/areas to improve, and suggested experiments; drafts recap emails and action plans for reps.|Requires empathy, trust-building, and situational leadership; humans interpret context (rep confidence, burnout, interpersonal dynamics) and tailor delivery beyond what AI can reliably do. High-stakes decisions (discounting, positioning, escalation, account strategy) require accountability, ethical judgment, and alignment with business priorities and risk tolerance. Humans must review for hallucinations, mis-scoring, bias (accent/gender), and ensure coaching feedback is equitable and compliant with HR/legal policies. Performance issues, PIPs, conflicts, customer complaints, and privacy-sensitive call content require discretion and policy-aware handling.|Uses deterministic analytics/BI to calculate stage conversion, cycle time, activity-to-meeting ratios, win rates by segment, and cohort trends; flags statistically significant changes. Applies classifiers/rules to detect talk-listen ratio, interruptions, filler words, keyword coverage (pricing, timeline), and required disclosures; produces explainable scores and alerts. Uses ML/rules to rank reps/deals for review based on risk signals (stalled stages, low engagement, forecast slippage) and to allocate coaching time where ROI is highest.

column8: Manage the SMB pipeline and forecast by reviewing CRM hygiene, validating deal stages, and communicating risks and upside to sales leadership with clear action plans.
column15: The task mixes structured analytics (best served by Traditional AI), cross-system workflow execution (Agentic AI), and narrative synthesis/action-plan drafting (Generative AI), but the forecast ultimately carries managerial accountability and requires contextual judgment and coaching. Traditional AI can reliably compute roll-ups, hygiene scores, and anomaly flags. Agentic AI can operationalize the cadence (pull data, create tasks, chase updates) with guardrails. Generative AI accelerates summarization and drafting but must be grounded in CRM data to avoid hallucinations. Humans remain essential for final stage/commit decisions, exception handling, and leadership communication. Assumptions: CRM data is accessible via APIs; governance allows AI to read notes/call summaries; a defined sales methodology (e.g., MEDDICC) and stage exit criteria exist; SMB deal cycles are short enough for weekly cadence to be meaningful.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving any changes to forecast category/commit for material deals, (2) interpreting ambiguous signals (politics, competitor moves, procurement nuance) not captured in CRM, (3) ensuring compliance/privacy when using call notes/emails, (4) handling edge cases like channel deals, multi-entity buying groups, or atypical payment terms, and (5) making resource/discount decisions and communicating accountable commitments to leadership. Humans should also review low-confidence AI suggestions, override automation when reps dispute data, and periodically audit models/rules for drift.
column17: Autonomous actions could spam reps, create incorrect CRM updates, or escalate prematurely; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals (e.g., optimizing hygiene score at expense of accuracy). Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, action limits, approval gates for writes/escalations, sandbox testing, clear policies/SLAs, and full audit logs with rollback.
column18: May hallucinate deal facts or overstate confidence; can misinterpret unstructured notes, leak sensitive customer data in prompts, or produce inconsistent tone. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from CRM/BI, strict citation of source fields, redaction/DLP, confidence scoring, and mandatory human approval for exec communications and CRM field writes.
column19: Rules/models can be brittle (new products, pricing changes, seasonality) and may encode historical bias (e.g., underestimating certain segments). Mitigations: periodic rule review, monitoring for drift, explainability dashboards, fairness checks, and allowing human overrides with feedback loops.
column20: Data mismatches between CRM/BI, inconsistent definitions of stages/forecast categories, and feedback loops where AI-written notes contaminate training data. Mitigations: single source of truth for metrics, schema/definition governance, provenance tagging for AI-generated content, robust identity/access management, and end-to-end testing of orchestrated workflows.
column22: Orchestrate recurring pipeline inspection workflow across CRM, email, calendar, and BI tools. Automate rep follow-ups and task creation for hygiene fixes and stage validation|Draft leadership-ready forecast narrative (risks, upside, deltas vs last week). Generate deal-level risk/upsides and recommended action plans from structured signals. Normalize and summarize unstructured inputs (call notes, emails) into CRM-ready updates|Final validation of deal stages and commit calls for high-impact deals. Coach reps and negotiate corrective actions with stakeholders. Communicate final forecast, risks, and upside to leadership with accountability|CRM hygiene scoring and rule-based validation of required fields. Forecast roll-up, trend analytics, and anomaly detection on pipeline metrics. Stage progression and probability calibration using historical win/loss data
column23: Runs a scheduled multi-step workflow: pulls latest pipeline snapshots, checks for missing fields/next steps, pings owners for updates, and compiles a ready-to-review pack; adapts follow-ups based on responses and deadlines. Creates CRM tasks/Slack nudges, routes exceptions (e.g., stalled deals, missing MEDDICC fields) to owners, and escalates to the lead when SLAs are missed; keeps an audit trail of actions taken.|Summarizes pipeline movements, highlights key drivers, and produces concise exec updates with consistent structure; can tailor messaging to VP/Director preferences and include suggested talking points. Uses CRM fields, activity notes, and call summaries to propose risk statements (e.g., no economic buyer identified) and next-best actions (e.g., schedule mutual plan review), while flagging low-confidence items for human review. column8: Drive adoption of best practices and scalable sales processes by documenting playbooks, standardizing workflows, and reinforcing consistent use of tools across the team.
column15: The task mixes content creation (playbooks), operational enforcement (standard workflows), and behavioral change (reinforcing tool use). Generative AI is strong for drafting/maintaining documentation, templates, and synthesizing qualitative feedback, but requires human validation to prevent inaccuracies and ensure alignment with positioning and compliance. Traditional AI is best for deterministic workflow standardization (CRM validation rules) and reliable adoption analytics. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating rollout tasks and continuously monitoring adherence across tools, but should be constrained with approvals and limited autonomy to avoid disruptive or non-compliant actions. Human leadership remains essential for defining best practices, making trade-offs, and driving change management and coaching-hence the largest share of work. Assumptions: SMB sales stack includes a CRM, conversation intelligence, and a knowledge base/LMS; data quality is sufficient for analytics; organization permits limited automation within internal systems.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) defining/approving the canonical sales process and playbook content (positioning, pricing/discount guidance, qualification standards), (2) compliance and risk sign-off (claims, privacy, call-recording consent, data retention), (3) resolving edge cases where strict workflows harm deal strategy (strategic exceptions, unusual procurement, partner-led deals), and (4) coaching and accountability conversations that require empathy and context. Humans should approve any changes that affect compensation, forecasting, customer-facing messaging, or CRM stage definitions; and review AI-generated playbook updates before publication.
column17: Autonomous nudges/escalations can create noise, reduce trust, or inadvertently change configurations/workflows; may optimize for proxy metrics (field completion) over true selling quality. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, approval workflows for config changes, rate limits, audit logs, clear success metrics, and safe-fail design (no customer outreach without approval).
column18: May hallucinate steps, misstate product capabilities/pricing, or produce inconsistent guidance; can leak sensitive customer or internal strategy if prompts/docs are mishandled. Mitigations: retrieval from approved sources, citation requirements, red-teaming, human review gates, PII redaction, and role-based access.
column19: Rules can become brittle as process evolves; dashboards can incentivize gaming (checkbox compliance) and may miss qualitative selling improvements. Mitigations: quarterly rule reviews, exception handling paths, balanced scorecards, and periodic sampling audits of deal notes/calls.
column20: Data inconsistencies across CRM, enablement, and call tools can cause incorrect adherence flags; conflicting recommendations between GenAI drafts and enforced rules can confuse reps. Mitigations: single source of truth for process definitions, schema/version control for playbooks and workflows, integration testing, monitoring, and a change advisory board for coordinated releases.

Standardize messaging and templates across channels. Summarize feedback and synthesize best practices|Set the operating model and approve playbook/process changes. Change management, coaching, and handling exceptions. Governance of data access, privacy, and tool configuration|Define and enforce CRM/workflow validation rules. Adoption and performance analytics (dashboards, anomaly detection)
column23: Connects to CRM/enablement tools (e.g., Salesforce/HubSpot, Gong, Slack, LMS) to create rollout plans, schedule enablement tasks, assign owners, send reminders, and adapt sequencing based on completion signals and performance telemetry; escalates blockers to the lead. Runs periodic checks across systems (CRM hygiene, stage progression, required fields, activity logging) and automatically opens tickets, pings reps/managers, or recommends coaching modules when deviations persist; uses guardrails and approval gates for any customer-facing actions.|Transforms call transcripts, win/loss notes, existing docs, and SME inputs into structured playbooks (discovery, qualification, MEDDICC, objection handling), SOPs, checklists, and templates; proposes updates when new patterns emerge. Generates consistent email/LinkedIn/call talk tracks aligned to ICP, value props, and compliance constraints; produces variants by segment while preserving approved claims and tone. Clusters qualitative inputs from reps (retro notes), customer calls, and manager observations into themes; drafts 'what changed' release notes and FAQs for process updates.|Sales lead defines what 'best practice' means for the business, aligns with leadership, legal/compliance, and product; approves final playbooks and workflow changes to avoid misalignment and unintended incentives. Humans drive buy-in, address resistance, coach nuanced behaviors (negotiation, discovery depth), and decide when exceptions are warranted (strategic accounts, atypical deals) that rigid automation cannot judge reliably. Ensures appropriate permissions, retention policies, and ethical use of call recordings/customer data; validates that automations comply with internal policies and regional regulations.|Implements deterministic rules (required fields, stage-entry criteria, SLA timers, routing, duplicate detection) and dashboards; provides explainable pass/fail outputs for compliance with the standardized process. Uses BI/ML to track tool usage, funnel conversion, cycle time, and adherence metrics; flags outliers (teams/reps/stages) and correlates adherence with outcomes to prioritize interventions.

column8: Support key customer and prospect engagements by joining strategic calls, handling escalations, and guiding negotiation and pricing discussions within approved guardrails.
column15: This task is high-stakes and externally facing: it combines relationship management, real-time negotiation, and escalation handling where accountability and nuanced judgment are essential. Generative AI is best for preparing briefs, drafting talk tracks, and producing follow-ups, but must be grounded in CRM/contract data to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the end-to-end workflow (prep, live prompts, routing, follow-up) and ensuring nothing falls through, yet should not autonomously commit pricing or remedies. Traditional AI is well-suited for deterministic guardrails (discount thresholds, approval matrices) and structured risk scoring. Assumptions: the org has a CRM, call recording/transcription with consent, defined pricing guardrails/approval policies, and secure access controls. Constraints: regulated industries and bespoke contracts increase required human oversight and reduce automation.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) any binding commitments on pricing, contract terms, SLAs, remedies, or timelines; (2) approving exceptions outside discount/term guardrails and interpreting ambiguous policy cases; (3) managing emotionally charged escalations, legal threats, or reputational incidents; (4) ensuring compliance with privacy/recording consent, competition law, anti-bribery, and truthful marketing claims; (5) resolving conflicts between AI recommendations and real-world context (e.g., strategic logo value, channel partner constraints). Edge cases: multi-year enterprise-like SMB deals, public-sector procurement rules, customers in sanctioned regions, and situations where transcript quality is poor or data in CRM is stale-humans must validate before action.
column17: Autonomous workflow actions could route escalations incorrectly, spam stakeholders, or trigger approvals based on misclassification; emergent behavior risk if allowed to act across systems (CRM/CPQ/email). Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, approval gates for external sends and pricing changes, deterministic policies for critical steps, simulation/testing, and continuous monitoring with rollback.
column18: May hallucinate customer facts, misquote pricing/terms, or produce overly aggressive negotiation language; can leak sensitive data if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from approved sources (CRM, CPQ, contract repo), strict redaction, template constraints, human review for external comms, and audit logging.
column19: Rules can become outdated as pricing policies change; models may encode historical bias (e.g., offering worse terms to certain segments) and can be brittle to new products/bundles. Mitigations: policy-as-code with versioning, regular calibration, fairness checks, and clear exception handling paths.
column20: Data inconsistency across CRM/CPQ/support tools can cause wrong recommendations; identity/access misconfigurations can expose sensitive deal data; conflicting outputs (GenAI suggestion vs rule engine) can confuse reps. Mitigations: single source of truth for pricing/terms, strong data governance, unified approval workflow, confidence/traceability indicators, and UI that clearly distinguishes suggestions from enforceable rules.
column22: Orchestrate engagement workflow (prep -> live support -> follow-up). Real-time call assistance and action capture. Escalation triage and routing|Pre-call research synthesis and briefing pack. Negotiation talk tracks and objection handling suggestions. Post-call recap, customer-facing follow-up drafts, and internal notes|Lead strategic calls and manage executive presence. Make final negotiation decisions and approve exceptions. Handle sensitive escalations and conflict resolution. Ensure ethical/compliant conduct and accurate commitments|Guardrail enforcement for pricing/discounts and approval thresholds. Deal scoring and escalation risk detection from structured signals. Compliance checks and red-flag detection in structured fields
column23: Coordinates multi-step playbooks: pulls latest CRM context, creates call agenda, assigns owners, triggers approvals for exceptions, and schedules follow-ups; adapts steps based on deal stage/escalation type while keeping humans in the loop for approvals. During calls, listens (with consent) to detect intents (pricing pushback, competitor mention), surfaces next-best actions, and creates tasks/tickets automatically; can route escalations to legal/finance based on detected triggers. Monitors inbound escalation channels (email/Slack/tickets), classifies severity, gathers required artifacts (logs, contract, prior comms), and routes to the right resolver group with SLA tracking; pauses and requests human decision when policy thresholds are hit.|Summarizes account history, stakeholder map, recent usage/health signals, open issues, and prior negotiation notes into a concise brief; drafts tailored discovery questions and value messaging aligned to the prospect's industry. Generates role-specific scripts, concession ladders, and responses to common objections (budget, competitor, security) grounded in approved messaging; proposes alternative packaging options to stay within guardrails. Produces accurate-when-grounded summaries from transcripts/notes, drafts follow-up emails, mutual action plans, and updates CRM fields for human review; highlights risks and open questions.|Requires relationship-building, trust, and nuanced judgment; humans read room dynamics, power structures, and adapt messaging ethically in real time. Concession strategy, pricing exceptions, and trade-offs involve business judgment, accountability, and cross-functional alignment; humans must approve anything outside guardrails. High-stakes escalations (service failures, legal threats, reputational risk) require empathy, accountability, and authority to commit resources or remedies. Humans must ensure statements made on calls are truthful, non-misleading, and compliant with contractual/regulatory constraints; AI outputs must be validated.|Applies deterministic rules and/or supervised models to validate proposed discounts, term lengths, and bundles against policy; flags exceptions and calculates required approvers based on region/segment/product. Uses structured CRM features (stage age, activity, support tickets, NPS/health, payment history) to predict churn/escalation likelihood and prioritize leadership involvement. Validates required fields (DPA status, security questionnaire completion, export controls) and triggers checklists; ensures mandatory steps are completed before quote/contract progression.

column8: Analyze performance metrics and market signals to identify trends, prioritize improvements, and run targeted initiatives that increase efficiency and revenue productivity.
column15: This SMB Sales Team Lead task mixes structured analytics (best handled by Traditional AI/BI), unstructured market sensing and communication (well-suited to Generative AI), and operational execution/monitoring of initiatives (where Agentic AI can automate workflows). However, prioritization and trade-offs (e.g., revenue vs. margin, fairness across reps/territories), interpretation under shifting market conditions, and approvals for customer/comp-impacting actions require human judgment and accountability. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/product data, defined KPI taxonomy, and permissioned integrations; agentic actions are constrained by approval gates for high-risk changes.
column16: Humans must (1) set KPI definitions/targets and interpret them with context (seasonality, one-off deals), (2) approve initiatives that affect customer experience, pricing/discounting, territories, or compensation, (3) validate causal claims before acting (avoid acting on noisy correlations), (4) ensure compliance with privacy, outreach regulations, and internal policies, and (5) handle edge cases like strategic accounts, reputational risk, or conflicting signals between data sources.
column17: Autonomous workflows could spam prospects/reps, change CRM fields incorrectly, or optimize for the wrong objective (e.g., short-term bookings harming retention). Mitigate with least-privilege access, sandboxing, approval gates, rate limits, audit logs, and clear objective/constraint specification.
column18: May hallucinate drivers or misstate numbers when summarizing; can overgeneralize from limited context; risk of leaking sensitive customer/rep performance data in prompts/outputs. Mitigate with retrieval from governed sources, numeric grounding, redaction, and human review for exec-facing narratives.
column19: Models can encode historical bias (territory/rep tenure), degrade with data drift, or produce brittle rules that miss new segments/competitors. Mitigate with monitoring, periodic retraining, explainability checks, and governance on feature use.
column20: Mismatched metric definitions across systems, feedback loops (agent actions altering data used for evaluation), and inconsistent permissions can cause incorrect conclusions or unauthorized actions. Mitigate with a single metric layer, data lineage, end-to-end testing, and separation of duties between analysis and execution.
column22: Orchestrate recurring data refreshes and KPI monitoring across systems. Execute and iterate targeted initiatives (closed-loop experimentation)|Narrative synthesis of trends and drivers for leadership/team consumption. Hypothesis generation and initiative design options. Anomaly detection and forecasting for pipeline and productivity. Lead/account scoring and prioritization signals
column23: Connects to CRM, billing, product analytics, and marketing sources; schedules pulls, validates freshness, and triggers alerts/workflows when thresholds are breached (e.g., pipeline coverage drops, win-rate anomaly). Proposes plausible explanations (e.g., segment mix shift, competitor pricing) and suggests initiative playbooks (enablement, messaging tests, routing tweaks) tailored to SMB sales context; produces experiment briefs and comms. Summarizes competitor updates, customer feedback, call transcripts, and news; extracts themes (pricing, feature gaps) and maps them to pipeline stages/segments.|Sets what 'good' looks like (targets, acceptable trade-offs), ensures alignment with strategy, seasonality, and constraints (budget, headcount, territories). Applies judgment to avoid spurious correlations, considers qualitative context (rep morale, partner dynamics), and approves changes affecting compensation, territories, pricing, or customer commitments. Communicates priorities, handles objections, reinforces behaviors, and ensures initiatives are executed ethically and compliantly; escalates edge cases.|Uses deterministic rules/SQL/BI and classical ML to compute core metrics (conversion rates, cycle time, ACV, CAC proxy), segment by industry/geo/channel/rep tenure, and compare cohorts over time. Uses supervised models or rules to score propensity-to-buy, churn risk, and upsell likelihood; outputs ranked lists and routing recommendations for SMB motions.

column8: Coordinate onboarding and ongoing enablement by partnering with Sales Enablement to deliver training, certify readiness, and ensure continuous learning for the SMB sales team.
column15: The task mixes operational coordination (highly automatable), content/communication (well-suited to GenAI), measurement (best handled by Traditional AI), and high-stakes judgment/people leadership (human-led). Agentic AI can run the end-to-end enablement workflow-enrollment, scheduling, reminders, evidence collection-within predefined guardrails, reducing manual coordination. Generative AI accelerates creation of role-play scenarios, talk tracks, recaps, and practice coaching, but must be grounded in approved enablement materials to avoid drift. Traditional AI provides explainable readiness scoring and compliance dashboards from structured LMS/CRM data. Humans remain essential for defining standards, making final certification calls, and managing stakeholder and people dynamics. Assumptions: access to LMS/CRM APIs, approved content sources, clear rubrics, and governance for automated actions (e.g., who can enroll/schedule). Constraints: privacy rules on call recordings, varying rep roles/regions, and change management adoption.
column16: Human oversight is required to (1) set/approve certification rubrics, pass/fail thresholds, and recertification policy; (2) approve any customer-facing or policy-sensitive training content and ensure alignment with legal/compliance (claims, pricing, competitive positioning); (3) make final readiness/certification decisions, especially when signals conflict (strong numbers but poor ethics/process) or when accommodations are needed; (4) intervene on edge cases such as new product launches, territory changes, underperformance requiring PIP, or data quality issues in CRM/LMS; (5) supervise agentic actions that affect calendars, enrollments, or performance records-using approval gates, audit logs, and escalation paths.
column17: Over-automation could spam reps, schedule inappropriate sessions, or enroll wrong cohorts; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals; opaque decisioning when adapting plans; permission misuse across systems. Mitigations: strict guardrails (allowed actions), human-in-the-loop approvals for high-impact steps, rate limits, audit trails, sandbox testing, and role-based access control.
column18: Hallucinated or outdated product/competitive claims in training materials; tone/culture mismatch; biased coaching feedback; leakage of sensitive internal/customer data if prompts include call transcripts. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved enablement docs, content approval workflow, red-teaming for bias, PII redaction, and restricted data handling policies.
column19: Brittle rules or models may mis-score readiness due to incomplete data, gaming (checkbox completion), or shifting sales motions; fairness issues if metrics disadvantage certain cohorts; false confidence from dashboards. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, data quality checks, incorporate qualitative manager inputs, monitor disparate impact, and keep metrics explainable and reviewable.
column20: Inconsistent data between LMS/CRM/HRIS causing wrong assignments; feedback loops where GenAI coaching influences metrics and agentic triggers (runaway nudging); security/privacy gaps when moving transcripts across tools; unclear ownership when AI outputs conflict. Mitigations: master data management, event-driven architecture with validation, unified identity/permissions, clear RACI for approvals, and monitoring/alerting for automation anomalies.
column22: Orchestrate onboarding/enablement workflow across systems (LMS, calendar, CRM). Personalize learning plans and nudges based on performance signals. Coordinate certification logistics and evidence collection|Create and localize training content and practice materials. Summarize training sessions and extract action items. Coach reps via simulated role-plays and feedback drafts|Define enablement strategy, success criteria, and certification standards. Deliver high-stakes coaching and evaluate nuanced performance. Stakeholder management with Sales Enablement and leadership. Handle exceptions and people-related issues|Readiness scoring and compliance tracking dashboards. Knowledge checks and assessment item analytics. Cohort segmentation for targeted enablement
column23: Plans and executes multi-step workflows: enrolls reps into role-based learning paths, schedules sessions, sends reminders, opens/updates enablement tickets, and adapts timelines based on completion status and start dates; escalates blockers to the lead. Monitors CRM activity (calls, pipeline hygiene), quiz results, and manager feedback to propose next-best modules/coaching; autonomously triggers nudges or schedules practice sessions within guardrails set by Sales Enablement. Produces concise recaps from meeting notes/transcripts, highlights FAQs, and generates follow-up quizzes/flashcards; flags unclear areas for Enablement to refine. Runs interactive role-play simulations (buyer personas) and generates feedback aligned to rubric; suggests improvements while avoiding final pass/fail decisions.|Requires business judgment to set what "ready" means for SMB selling, align with GTM priorities, and ensure standards are fair, role-appropriate, and compliant. Humans assess soft skills (rapport, ethics, judgment), handle sensitive feedback, and make final certification decisions-areas where AI feedback may be unreliable or biased. Negotiates priorities, secures resources, resolves conflicts, and communicates tradeoffs; requires trust, context, and accountability. Manages accommodations, performance concerns, cultural/team dynamics, and policy exceptions that require discretion and HR/legal awareness.|Uses deterministic rules/ML on structured data (module completion, quiz scores, attendance, time-in-role) to compute readiness status, SLA breaches, and cohort comparisons; provides explainable metrics. Automatically grades objective quizzes, detects anomalous patterns (guessing, rapid completion), and reports item difficulty/discrimination to improve assessments. Clusters or rule-segments reps by tenure, territory, product focus, and performance bands to assign appropriate training cohorts and measure uplift.

column8: Design and implement SMB sales processes and operating rhythms that improve pipeline coverage, forecast accuracy, and rep productivity across the team.
column15: Designing SMB sales processes and operating rhythms is socio-technical: humans set strategy, incentives, and adoption plans, while AI accelerates analysis, documentation, and execution. Traditional AI is best for structured forecasting, coverage, and hygiene scoring with auditable logic. Generative AI speeds creation of playbooks, templates, and synthesis of qualitative feedback but needs verification. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating recurring workflows (agenda creation, nudges, task routing) and monitoring exceptions, yet should operate under guardrails and approvals for major changes. Assumptions: CRM data is reasonably available; integrations (CRM, calendar, comms, BI) are permitted; SMB motion has repeatable stages. Constraints: data quality and change management limit automation; compliance requires approvals for policy-impacting changes.
column16: Humans must (1) set stage definitions, qualification standards, and forecast policy because they encode business risk tolerance; (2) approve changes affecting comp, quotas, customer commitments, or legal/compliance (privacy, retention, auditability); (3) arbitrate conflicts between metrics (e.g., pushing hygiene may reduce selling time) and handle edge cases like atypical enterprise-like SMB deals, channel deals, or distressed renewals; (4) validate model outputs and agent actions when confidence is low or when anomalies suggest gaming or data drift; (5) lead training, coaching, and enforcement to ensure adoption and ethical use.
column17: Autonomous nudging/task creation can overwhelm reps, create perverse incentives, or modify CRM records incorrectly; emergent behavior in multi-step workflows may cause unintended escalations or missed exceptions; auditability challenges. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for write actions, rate limits, sandbox testing, clear rollback, and comprehensive logging/monitoring.
column18: May hallucinate process details, misstate policy, or produce generic playbooks that don't fit the company's SMB ICP; risk of leaking sensitive customer/rep data if prompts include PII; may introduce biased coaching language. Mitigations: retrieval from approved internal docs, redaction, human review, style/brand guardrails, and evaluation against KPI outcomes.
column19: Forecast models can be brittle under GTM changes (new pricing, territories), suffer from biased historical data, and give false confidence; rule-based hygiene can be gamed and may penalize legitimate deal patterns. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, drift detection, segmented models, transparent features, and governance for rule updates.
column20: Conflicting signals between models (Traditional AI) and agent actions (Agentic AI) can create inconsistent guidance; data sync issues across CRM/BI lead to wrong agendas and alerts; security/privacy risks increase with more integrations. Mitigations: single source of truth definitions, data contracts, end-to-end testing, unified identity/access management, and human-in-the-loop escalation paths.
column22: Orchestrate operating rhythm workflows (cadences, reminders, agenda generation, follow-ups) across tools. Continuous monitoring and exception management for pipeline hygiene. Generate meeting artifacts and coaching prompts|Define strategy, guardrails, and success metrics for SMB motion. Stakeholder alignment and change management. Approve high-impact process changes and handle edge cases|Forecasting and pipeline coverage analytics. Rule-based pipeline hygiene scoring and alerts. Capacity and productivity measurement dashboards

column8: Analyze funnel performance and key sales metrics to identify bottlenecks, quantify impact, and prioritize process improvements with clear success measures.
column15: This task mixes structured analytics (best handled by Traditional AI) with workflow automation (Agentic AI) and communication/ideation (Generative AI), but still requires human judgment to set goals, interpret causality vs. correlation, and lead organizational change. Traditional AI does the heavy lifting on KPI computation, cohorting, anomaly detection, and impact math. Agentic AI reduces manual effort by orchestrating data pulls and running investigation playbooks, but must be constrained to avoid unintended actions. GenAI accelerates synthesis into narratives, metric documentation, and experiment framing, yet cannot be trusted as the source of truth for numbers. Humans remain essential for strategic prioritization, approvals, and change management. Assumptions: SMB Sales Manager has access to CRM/BI data; metric definitions exist or can be established; privacy/compliance constraints limit autonomous actions affecting customers or compensation.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) defining success measures and trade-offs (e.g., prioritize pipeline quality vs. volume), (2) validating metric definitions/attribution and resolving data disputes, (3) interpreting root causes where confounders exist (seasonality, territory changes, pricing updates), (4) approving actions that affect people or spend (rep coaching, lead routing changes, budget reallocations), and (5) coordinating cross-functional process changes and ensuring adoption. Edge cases needing human review: sparse data segments, major CRM process changes, one-off enterprise-like deals in SMB funnel, and any analysis used for performance management or compensation.
column17: Autonomous workflows can propagate bad data (refreshing dashboards with incorrect joins), spam stakeholders with false alerts, or take unintended actions if given write access (e.g., changing routing rules). Mitigations: read-only by default, sandbox testing, explicit guardrails, human-in-the-loop approvals for any system changes, and audit logs.
column18: May hallucinate drivers or misstate numeric results if not grounded in retrieved data; can oversimplify causality; risk of leaking sensitive CRM data in prompts. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation, numeric citation/links to source dashboards, redaction, and approval workflows.
column19: Brittle rules and models can break with schema/process changes; anomaly detection may trigger false positives during seasonality or campaign launches; prioritization scoring can encode biased weights. Mitigations: monitoring, periodic recalibration, seasonality controls, transparent scoring, and governance over metric definitions.
column20: Mismatch between metric definitions across tools, inconsistent time windows, and compounding errors when GenAI narrates unverified outputs from other systems. Orchestration failures (API limits, partial refresh) can create conflicting dashboards. Mitigations: single source of truth in BI layer, data contracts, end-to-end tests, lineage tracking, and requiring citations in generated reports.
column22: Orchestrate recurring funnel analytics workflow across systems. Automated bottleneck investigation playbooks|Narrative insight generation and executive-ready reporting. Hypothesis generation and experiment design support. Metric definition alignment and documentation|Set business context, targets, and constraints. Validate data/assumptions and approve decisions. Drive cross-functional change management|Data preparation, KPI computation, and funnel cohorting. Anomaly detection and trend change-point alerts. Impact quantification and prioritization scoring
column23: Plans and executes a scheduled pipeline: pulls data from CRM/marketing automation/BI, refreshes dashboards, runs checks, and notifies stakeholders; adapts steps when schemas or sources change (within guardrails). When anomalies are detected (e.g., stage conversion drop), autonomously runs a predefined investigation: slices by segment/rep/channel, checks recent process changes, compiles evidence, and proposes likely root causes and next actions for human review.|Turns metric outputs into clear narratives: what changed, where in the funnel, likely drivers, and recommended focus areas; drafts weekly/monthly performance readouts tailored to SMB sales leadership. Suggests plausible bottleneck causes (messaging, lead quality, rep behavior, pricing friction) and proposes A/B tests or process experiments with success metrics, sample size heuristics, and instrumentation needs. Drafts and maintains a metric dictionary (stage definitions, SLA rules, attribution assumptions) and translates technical BI logic into manager-friendly language to reduce misinterpretation.|Defines revenue goals, segment strategy, acceptable trade-offs (growth vs. margin), and operational constraints; ensures analysis aligns with current GTM priorities. Reviews metric definitions, investigates data quality edge cases, and approves high-stakes conclusions (e.g., rep performance actions, budget shifts) to avoid misinformed decisions. Coordinates with Marketing/RevOps/Product to implement process changes, secure buy-in, and ensure frontline adoption-work that requires leadership, negotiation, and accountability.|Deterministically computes core KPIs (stage conversion, velocity, win rate, ACV, CAC proxy, SLA adherence) using SQL/rules; builds cohorts by segment, channel, rep, and time window. Uses statistical process control, time-series change-point detection, and outlier models to flag significant deviations in conversion/velocity and identify where the funnel shifted. Applies decomposition (e.g., volume  conversion  price), sensitivity analysis, and weighted scoring (impact, effort, confidence) to rank improvement opportunities; outputs explainable calculations.

column8: Present performance insights, risks, and recommendations to senior leadership, translating data into decisions on targets, resources, and execution plans.
column15: This is a high-stakes, executive-facing task where AI can substantially reduce analysis and preparation time but cannot own accountability for targets, resourcing, or organizational commitments. Traditional AI is best for reliable KPI computation, forecasting, and rule/anomaly-based risk flags. Generative AI is best for translating outputs into executive narratives, decision memos, and presentation drafts, but must be grounded in verified numbers to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-system data refreshes, running repeatable scenario workflows, and maintaining an actions tracker post-meeting; however, autonomy must be constrained due to governance and change-management risks. Humans remain essential for setting decision criteria, validating assumptions, and delivering/defending recommendations. Assumptions: SMB sales manager has access to CRM/BI tools with APIs; metric definitions are documented; leadership expects weekly/monthly cadence; data quality is imperfect and requires oversight.
column16: Human oversight is essential at (1) metric definition and source-of-truth decisions (e.g., bookings vs billings, pipeline inclusion rules), (2) approval of forecasts/targets and any headcount or territory changes (legal/HR/comp implications), (3) interpretation of external context (competitive intel, macro shifts, product readiness) not captured in systems, (4) handling sensitive performance discussions and stakeholder negotiation, and (5) edge cases like one-off large deals, channel conflicts, or data outages where automated conclusions could mislead. Humans must approve final decks/memos and any downstream actions created by agents.
column17: Autonomous workflows could pull wrong datasets, overwrite dashboards, spam stakeholders, or create unauthorized tasks/changes; emergent behavior and limited auditability can erode trust. Mitigations: least-privilege access, human-in-the-loop approvals for actions, sandbox testing, audit logs, rate limits, and clear stop conditions/guardrails.
column18: May hallucinate numbers/causal claims, overstate confidence, or produce persuasive but incorrect narratives; risk of leaking sensitive revenue/employee data if prompts are not governed. Mitigations: retrieval grounded in approved datasets, citation of metric sources, constrained templates, red-teaming for misleading language, and strict data handling policies.
column19: Models can drift with seasonality or GTM changes; rules can become brittle and miss novel risks; biased training data can skew forecasts (e.g., underestimating new segments). Mitigations: monitoring, periodic recalibration, explainability checks, backtesting, and governance over metric definitions.
column20: Mismatch between metric definitions across systems can cause inconsistent narratives; GenAI may summarize outdated outputs if orchestration fails; agents may act on unvalidated anomaly flags. Mitigations: single semantic layer/metric store, data lineage and timestamps in outputs, validation gates before narrative generation, and end-to-end testing with rollback procedures.
column22: Orchestrate data pulls and refreshes across sales systems (CRM, pipeline, bookings, activity, finance). Run scenario workflows and propose draft operating plans. Maintain an insights-to-actions tracker and follow-up automation|Narrative synthesis for leadership: insights, risks, and recommendations. Translate analysis into target/resource trade-offs and decision options. Create presentation artifacts (slide outlines, speaker notes, visuals descriptions)|Set context, priorities, and decision criteria with leadership. Validate assumptions, resolve data disputes, and approve final recommendations. Deliver the presentation and manage stakeholder dynamics|Compute core performance KPIs and trends. Forecasting and variance decomposition. Risk detection via anomaly/threshold rules
column23: Plans and executes multi-step workflows to fetch the latest metrics from connected tools/APIs on a schedule or on-demand, handles retries, and logs lineage; flags missing/late data for follow-up. Given constraints (headcount, budget, capacity, territory coverage), agent iterates through what-if scenarios (e.g., target uplift, conversion improvements) and assembles candidate execution plans with assumptions and sensitivity notes. After leadership review, agent creates/updates action items, owners, and timelines in project tools, monitors leading indicators, and prompts stakeholders when risks trigger thresholds.|Transforms quantitative outputs into an executive-ready storyline (what happened, why, so what, now what), tailored to senior leadership expectations; drafts talking points and Q&A prep. Generates structured decision memos with options, pros/cons, and implications (quota, coverage, enablement, pricing, channel mix), explicitly stating assumptions and confidence levels. Drafts slide structure, titles, key messages, and suggested charts; can generate concise summaries for pre-reads and meeting minutes.|Requires organizational judgment about strategic priorities, acceptable risk, and constraints (market conditions, competitive moves, board expectations) that AI cannot authoritatively determine. Human accountability is needed to verify contentious numbers, adjudicate conflicting sources of truth, and sign off on targets/resources that impact compensation and employment. Persuasion, negotiation, and handling sensitive questions (performance, headcount, customer escalations) require human credibility and real-time judgment.|Deterministically calculates attainment, pipeline coverage, stage conversion, win rate, ASP, cycle time, activity-to-outcome ratios, cohort trends; produces consistent metric definitions. Uses classical statistical/ML models (e.g., time-series, regression) to forecast bookings and identify drivers of variance vs plan (volume, conversion, deal slippage). Applies rules and anomaly detection to flag pipeline hygiene issues, concentration risk, stalled deals, rep underperformance, territory gaps, and data quality anomalies.

column8: Coach and manage SMB account executives through regular 1:1s, deal reviews, and skill development plans to improve win rates and quota attainment.
column15: The core of coaching SMB AEs is interpersonal leadership: building trust, diagnosing motivation/behavior, and making accountable decisions on deal strategy and performance management-these remain human-led. Traditional AI is mature for structured KPI computation, segmentation, and deal health scoring, providing consistent prioritization and measurement. Generative AI adds leverage by summarizing interactions, drafting coaching plans, and producing practice materials, but must be grounded in accurate CRM/context to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI can automate the operational layer (cadence, reminders, task routing) and continuously monitor signals to propose interventions, yet requires guardrails and approvals to prevent overreach. Assumptions: access to CRM/call recordings is permitted; data quality is moderate; organization has defined sales methodology (e.g., MEDDICC) and enablement content library.
column16: Humans must lead sensitive 1:1 discussions (performance issues, compensation/career, conflict), approve any pricing/contract or escalation actions, and validate coaching recommendations when data is incomplete or biased (new reps, new product, atypical territories). Human oversight is essential for compliance (privacy of call recordings, HR policies), fairness in performance evaluation, and edge cases where CRM data is stale or reps sell in non-standard motions. Managers should review AI-generated summaries/action items before they become official performance records.
column17: Over-automation could spam reps, create surveillance culture, or trigger inappropriate escalations; autonomous actions may violate approval chains or customer communication policies. Mitigate with strict permissioning, human-in-the-loop approvals for external actions, rate limits, audit logs, and clear opt-in/communication norms.
column18: Hallucinated deal facts or misattributed rep behaviors from noisy transcripts; tone that undermines trust; leakage of sensitive customer/HR data if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigate with grounding to CRM fields, citation/traceability, redaction, and human review before storing in HR/performance systems.
column19: Brittle rules or biased models can mis-score deals or misclassify rep performance (e.g., territories with longer cycles), leading to unfair coaching focus. Mitigate with periodic recalibration, cohort-aware benchmarks, explainability, and monitoring for drift.
column20: Conflicting signals between dashboards (Traditional AI) and GenAI narratives; agentic workflows acting on hallucinated summaries; identity/permission mismatches across CRM, calendar, and call tools. Mitigate with a single source of truth (CRM), schema/ontology alignment, end-to-end testing, RBAC, and provenance tracking for any recommendation/action.
column22: Orchestrate coaching cadence and workflows (1:1 scheduling, agendas, reminders, follow-ups). Proactive deal-risk monitoring and escalation routing. Personalized enablement plan execution (assignments, nudges, progress tracking)|Summarize 1:1 inputs and generate coaching notes/action plans. Create role-play scripts, objection-handling drills, and messaging improvements. Draft deal review narratives and win/loss learning briefs|Conduct high-trust 1:1 conversations (motivation, accountability, career coaching). Make final decisions on deal strategy, concessions, and ethical/compliance boundaries. Observe live selling behaviors and deliver nuanced feedback. Calibrate coaching approach to team culture and individual differences|Performance analytics and KPI dashboards (quota, win rate, pipeline coverage, activity). Rep segmentation and skill-gap detection using structured signals. Deal health scoring using rules/ML on structured CRM fields
column23: Connects to calendar/CRM/LMS to automatically schedule recurring 1:1s, generate agenda templates based on pipeline changes, send reminders, capture action items, and trigger follow-up tasks (e.g., role-play session, enablement module) while adapting cadence based on rep performance signals. Continuously monitors CRM activity, stage aging, next-step gaps, stakeholder coverage, and competitive signals; autonomously flags at-risk deals, proposes interventions (exec sponsor, pricing review), and routes to manager for approval before contacting others. Builds and runs a multi-week skill plan per AE by selecting relevant content, scheduling practice sessions, and nudging completion; updates plan based on observed outcomes (call scores, win/loss) with human-set guardrails.|Transcribes/summarizes call recordings, Slack/email context, and CRM notes into concise 1:1 briefs; drafts SMART action items and coaching feedback tailored to each AE's deals and behaviors. Generates scenario-based role plays for SMB personas, competitive objections, pricing pushback; drafts talk tracks and email templates aligned to product positioning and rep skill gaps. Turns structured deal data plus qualitative notes into a coherent deal story (MEDDICC-style), highlights gaps, and drafts win/loss summaries with hypotheses and experiments to test.|Requires empathy, psychological safety, and nuanced judgment to address morale, conflict, and personal development; humans interpret context that is not reliably captured in systems. Pricing exceptions, contract terms, and competitive/ethical considerations are high-stakes and require accountable human approval and risk judgment. Humans can assess subtle communication cues, relationship dynamics, and situational appropriateness; AI signals can inform but not replace judgment. Adapting coaching style, setting expectations, and managing performance requires leadership discretion and organizational alignment beyond current AI reliability.|Uses deterministic BI/ML to compute metrics, trends, cohort comparisons, and alerts (e.g., stage conversion anomalies) from CRM and sales engagement data; provides explainable, auditable outputs. Clusters reps by tenure, segment, product mix, and KPI patterns; identifies statistically significant gaps (e.g., low discovery-to-demo conversion) to prioritize coaching focus. Applies scoring models (stage age, next step date, multi-threading, champion presence) to rank deals for review; supports consistent prioritization and reduces manager bias.
column41: SMB Sales Coaching and TAM Playbooks

column8: Own forecasting and pipeline management, including weekly reviews, hygiene standards, and corrective actions to ensure reliable revenue commitments.
column15: Forecasting and pipeline management for an SMB Sales Manager (Mgmt Grade 9/10) combines structured rollups (well-suited to Traditional AI), ongoing workflow execution (best handled by Agentic AI), and narrative synthesis/coaching artifacts (strong fit for Generative AI). However, the final forecast commitment, policy setting, and rep/customer-facing judgment remain human-accountable due to business risk, ambiguity, and the need to challenge assumptions. Assumptions: CRM is the system of record; call notes/emails are accessible with proper permissions; hygiene standards are codified; automation is constrained by approval gates for high-impact field changes. Under these constraints, AI can materially reduce manual prep and improve consistency, but cannot fully replace managerial judgment and accountability-hence Human-AI Collaboration and a human plurality of work.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final commit/best-case calls and any external communication of forecast to leadership/finance; (2) setting/adjusting definitions, thresholds, and incentives (e.g., what counts as commit, acceptable slippage) to avoid gaming; (3) approving automated edits to high-value deals, close dates, amounts, or stages, especially near quarter-end; (4) coaching and performance management actions triggered by hygiene non-compliance; (5) compliance/privacy decisions about which unstructured data (emails/calls) can be analyzed; and (6) resolving edge cases like strategic accounts, partner/channel conflicts, legal/procurement delays, and macro events where context is not captured in CRM. Humans must also audit model outputs periodically for drift and bias.
column17: Over-automation could spam reps, create incorrect CRM updates, or trigger inappropriate escalations; emergent behavior from tool chains may cause unintended actions; auditability can be weak. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval workflows for write actions, rate limits, sandbox testing, clear runbooks, and immutable audit trails for every action taken.
column18: May hallucinate deal facts or misattribute reasons for slippage; can over-summarize and miss critical nuances; risk of leaking sensitive customer data if prompts/logs are mishandled; tone may be inappropriate for coaching. Mitigations: retrieval with citations to CRM fields/snippets, strict grounding, redaction, role-based access, human review for exec-facing narratives, and prompt/response logging with privacy controls.
column19: Rules can become brittle/outdated as sales process changes; ML scores can drift with seasonality or GTM shifts and may encode bias (e.g., territory/segment effects); false positives in anomaly detection can create alert fatigue. Mitigations: quarterly rule reviews, model monitoring/calibration, explainability, threshold tuning, and feedback loops from managers to adjust alerts.
column20: Conflicting signals between ML scores, rule-based flags, and GenAI narratives can confuse users; data latency/inconsistency across CRM, BI, and comms tools can produce wrong conclusions; permission mismatches can expose data; orchestration failures can break weekly cadence. Mitigations: single source of truth hierarchy (CRM fields > derived metrics > narratives), data quality SLAs, unified identity/RBAC, end-to-end testing, and UI that shows provenance and timestamps for every metric and summary.
column22: Orchestrate weekly pipeline review workflow (agenda, data pulls, reminders, follow-ups). Automated corrective-action execution for hygiene gaps (within guardrails). Continuous monitoring and exception management|Generate narrative forecast commentary and weekly review summaries. Deal risk/next-step reasoning support from unstructured signals. Scenario framing and QBR-style insights|Set forecasting policy, definitions, and hygiene standards; align with finance leadership. Run the weekly review conversation and make final revenue commitments. Approve/override corrective actions and coach reps. Handle edge cases (strategic deals, legal/procurement complexity, channel conflicts)|Forecast rollups, coverage metrics, and KPI dashboards. Rule-based hygiene validation and anomaly detection. Predictive scoring using classical ML on structured CRM history
column23: Connects to CRM/calendar/chat tools to automatically compile the weekly review pack, schedule meetings, send reminders to reps, and track completion of pre-work; adapts cadence based on deal volume and quarter stage. When predefined issues are detected (e.g., missing next step, stale close date), it opens tasks, pings owners, proposes updates, and can apply low-risk fixes (e.g., set required fields) after human approval; escalates repeated non-compliance. Runs daily checks on pipeline changes, flags material movements (slippage, stage regression, sudden amount changes), and routes exceptions to the manager with suggested actions and owners.|Drafts concise manager-ready narratives (what changed, why, risks, asks) from CRM deltas, call notes, and rep inputs; produces exec-ready summaries and rep-specific coaching notes. Synthesizes emails, call transcripts, and notes to suggest likely deal health, missing stakeholders, and recommended next steps; highlights uncertainty and cites source snippets to reduce hallucination risk. Creates what-if scenarios (best/base/worst), identifies coverage gaps vs quota, and proposes focus areas for the week; outputs are advisory and require validation against actual CRM data.|Requires organizational judgment, cross-functional alignment, and accountability for definitions (commit/best case), governance, and incentives. High-stakes judgment under uncertainty; must challenge reps, incorporate market/context not in systems, and own the communicated forecast to leadership. Ensures actions are appropriate for customer relationships and rep development; handles sensitive accounts and exceptions where automation could harm trust. Nuanced stakeholder management and risk trade-offs are not reliably automatable; requires experience and accountability.|Deterministically aggregates CRM data into forecast categories, pipeline coverage, stage conversion, aging, and attainment metrics; produces consistent dashboards for weekly reviews. Applies required-field rules, stage-exit criteria, and statistical thresholds to detect outliers (e.g., close date pushed >X days, amount change >Y%); generates alerts with explainable triggers. Uses logistic regression/GBM-style models to estimate win probability and expected close timing based on historical patterns; provides calibrated scores and feature importance for transparency.
