column8: Partner with Marketing, RevOps, and Customer Success to align lead flow, handoffs, and lifecycle processes that increase conversion and retention.
column15: This task is cross-functional process design and governance: humans must align incentives, define success, and approve changes. Traditional AI is strong for structured KPI computation, lead scoring/segmentation, and data quality-foundational to diagnosing funnel issues. Generative AI accelerates synthesis of qualitative inputs and creation of documentation/enablement materials, but cannot be the source of truth for metrics or policy. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-system workflows (SLAs, routing ops, escalations) and continuously monitoring/acting on agreed rules, yet requires guardrails and approvals for impactful changes. Assumptions: SMB stack includes CRM + marketing automation + CS platform; data access is permissioned; change management requires auditability.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) agreeing on lifecycle definitions (MQL/SQL/SAL, onboarding complete, churn risk) and prioritizing KPIs; (2) approving any changes that affect customer communications, routing fairness, privacy/consent, or revenue risk; (3) resolving conflicts between teams' incentives and capacity constraints; (4) interpreting ambiguous signals (e.g., macro seasonality vs. process failure) and handling strategic-account exceptions. Humans should gate agentic actions via approval workflows, maintain an escalation path, and periodically review model/rule performance and drift.
column17: Over-automation could change routing/cadence in ways that harm conversion or customer experience; emergent behaviors from poorly specified goals (optimize conversion at expense of retention); permission misuse across systems. Mitigate with least-privilege access, approval gates for high-impact actions, sandbox testing, rate limits, and comprehensive audit logs/rollback.
column18: May hallucinate process details or misstate KPI definitions; can leak sensitive customer/lead data if prompts/logs are mishandled; may produce persuasive but incorrect enablement content. Mitigate with retrieval from approved docs, redaction, human review, and version-controlled sources of truth.
column19: Lead scoring and segmentation can encode bias (e.g., disadvantaging certain industries/regions) and degrade with drift; brittle rules can break when fields change. Mitigate with fairness checks, periodic retraining/validation, monitoring, and robust data contracts.
column20: Metric mismatches across tools (different timestamps/stage definitions) can cause conflicting recommendations; feedback loops where scoring influences outreach which influences labels; inconsistent identity resolution leads to broken handoffs. Mitigate with a canonical data model, governance for definitions, end-to-end testing, and clear ownership (RevOps) for system-of-record decisions.
column22: Orchestrate cross-functional workflow execution (tickets, tasks, reminders, SLAs) across CRM/marketing automation/CS tools. Monitor funnel and handoff health continuously and propose/execute corrective actions. Run controlled experiments on lifecycle changes (A/B, holdouts) with guardrails|Synthesize stakeholder inputs into a unified lifecycle narrative and process documentation. Draft enablement and communication assets for process changes. Generate hypotheses and root-cause analysis prompts from KPI patterns|Set shared definitions, strategic priorities, and negotiate trade-offs across functions. Approve lifecycle policy changes that impact customer experience, compliance, or revenue risk. Handle edge cases and relationship management with key stakeholders/customers|Define and compute funnel/lifecycle KPIs and anomaly detection on structured data. Lead scoring, segmentation, and routing optimization using structured features. Data quality checks and deduplication for lead/contact/account records
column23: Connects to systems (e.g., CRM, MAP, CS platform) to create and route tasks, enforce SLA timers, trigger escalations, and keep stakeholders aligned; adapts plans when bottlenecks or exceptions occur (e.g., reroute leads when owner OOO). Runs scheduled checks on lead aging, stage conversion, MQL->SQL acceptance, onboarding milestones; when thresholds breach, it opens investigations, pings owners, drafts action plans, and can implement approved changes (e.g., adjust routing rules) with audit logs. Plans experiment steps, coordinates with stakeholders, deploys changes in a sandbox/feature-flag manner, monitors KPIs, and rolls back if negative impact is detected-subject to human approval gates.|Summarizes interviews/notes, extracts requirements, drafts end-to-end lifecycle maps, RACI, SLA definitions, and handoff checklists tailored to SMB sales motions; highlights ambiguities and conflicting definitions. Creates playbooks, internal announcements, training scripts, call-out examples, and FAQ for Sales/Marketing/CS; adapts tone per audience and generates role-specific job aids. Given dashboards/exports, proposes plausible drivers (e.g., lead source quality, speed-to-lead, missing fields), suggests analyses to validate, and drafts questions for RevOps/Marketing/CS to resolve data/process gaps.|Requires authority and judgment to align incentives (pipeline vs. retention), resolve conflicts (e.g., MQL definition), and decide what to optimize given capacity and market context. Humans must review high-stakes changes (routing, messaging, outreach cadence, CS touchpoints) for brand, legal/privacy, fairness, and commercial risk; accountable sign-off is needed. Complex exceptions (strategic accounts, escalations, sensitive churn risks) need contextual judgment, empathy, and real-time negotiation that AI cannot reliably perform.|Uses deterministic metric definitions and classical ML/statistics to calculate conversion rates, time-in-stage, cohort retention, and detect anomalies (e.g., sudden drop in SQL acceptance) with explainable thresholds. Applies rules and/or supervised models to score leads, segment by firmographics/behavior, and recommend routing/priority queues; outputs are auditable and can be constrained by business rules. Runs rule-based validation (required fields, format), fuzzy matching for duplicates, and consistency checks across systems to reduce handoff failures caused by bad data.
column41: Cross-Functional Lead Flow and Messaging Alignment

column8: Drive continuous improvement initiatives by piloting new tools, workflows, and incentives, then scaling what works with documented change management.
column15: The task mixes strategic judgment (what to improve, whether to change incentives) with repeatable operational work (running pilots, collecting data, producing artifacts). Traditional AI/BI is best for rigorous measurement and auditability of cohort selection and incentive calculations. Generative AI accelerates ideation and documentation (charters, comms, SOPs) and synthesizes qualitative feedback, but must be grounded in source data to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-system pilot operations and rollout steps with monitoring and adaptive nudges, yet requires guardrails and approvals due to potential business impact. Humans remain essential for prioritization, compliance/ethics, stakeholder alignment, and final scaling decisions. Assumptions: SMB sales stack includes CRM (e.g., Salesforce/HubSpot), enablement/LMS, BI, and ticketing; pilots can be run in sandboxes/feature flags; data instrumentation exists or can be added. Constraints: limited rep time, change fatigue, and compensation governance reduce automation ceiling.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) prioritization and trade-offs (revenue vs rep burden vs customer experience), (2) any incentive/compensation changes (fairness, labor law, finance controls), (3) policy/security approvals for new tools and data access, (4) interpreting ambiguous results (small samples, confounders, seasonality), (5) stakeholder buy-in and conflict resolution, and (6) final go/no-go and accountability for scaling. Edge cases needing humans: pilots that negatively impact protected groups/territories, tool changes that alter customer communications, metric movements driven by external shocks (pricing changes, market events), and data integrity incidents.
column17: Over-automation could change workflows in production without adequate approvals; emergent behaviors (optimizing for proxy metrics) may harm customer experience or rep morale; integration failures could spam reps or mis-provision access. Mitigations: sandbox-first, feature flags, least-privilege permissions, explicit stage gates, rate limits, audit logs, and rollback plans; constrain objectives and require human approval for high-impact actions.
column18: Hallucinated metrics or incorrect causal claims in summaries; leakage of sensitive customer/employee data when drafting artifacts; biased or inappropriate incentive suggestions; tone/policy mismatches in comms. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations, redaction/DLP, approved templates, human review for leadership-facing and compensation-related docs.
column19: Brittle rules for cohort selection/incentives can create unfairness or gaming; statistical analyses can be misleading with small samples, selection bias, or untracked confounders; dashboards may encode wrong definitions. Mitigations: periodic rule reviews, fairness checks, pre-registered metrics, robust experiment design, data dictionary governance, and monitoring for gaming.
column20: Inconsistent metric definitions across BI/CRM/enablement; feedback loops where agentic actions change the data being measured; identity/permission mismatches across tools; unclear ownership of decisions produced by AI. Mitigations: single source of truth for KPIs, experiment governance board, end-to-end audit trails, clear RACI, and automated validation tests for pipelines and workflows.
column22: Orchestrate pilot execution across systems (CRM, enablement, ticketing). Automate experiment operations and monitoring. Scale rollout playbooks with gated approvals|Generate pilot hypotheses, experiment designs, and success metrics drafts. Create change-management artifacts. Synthesize qualitative feedback and lessons learned|Select improvement priorities aligned to strategy and constraints. Approve experiment ethics, compliance, and compensation changes. Stakeholder management and adoption leadership. Final go/no-go decisions and accountability for scaling|Baseline and pilot performance analytics (dashboards, cohort comparisons). Rules-based eligibility and routing for pilot cohorts/incentives. Anomaly detection and data quality checks
column23: Plans and runs multi-step pilot workflows (e.g., create pilot cohorts, provision tool access, trigger enablement tasks, collect feedback) via integrations/APIs; adapts schedules and reminders based on completion signals while keeping humans in approval loops for policy-impacting actions. Continuously monitors leading indicators (activity, conversion, cycle time) during pilots, detects deviations, and proposes/executes corrective actions (e.g., adjust routing rules in sandbox, nudge reps, open support tickets) within predefined guardrails. After a pilot is approved, executes rollout steps (training assignments, CRM field deployment, workflow activation) using checklists and stage gates; ensures dependencies are met and logs evidence for audit/change records.|Drafts structured experiment charters (problem statement, hypothesis, target segments, KPIs, sample size heuristics, risks) tailored to SMB sales context; proposes incentive structures and workflow variants for A/B tests. Produces comms plans, enablement content, FAQs, SOPs, release notes, and manager talk-tracks; adapts tone for reps vs leadership; summarizes what changed and why. Summarizes rep/manager feedback from calls, surveys, and tickets; clusters themes, extracts pain points, and drafts recommendations and decision memos while citing sources where available.|Requires business judgment to balance revenue goals, rep capacity, customer impact, and organizational strategy; involves trade-offs and accountability. Incentives and workflow changes can create fairness, labor-law, and policy risks; humans must review for compliance, equity, and unintended behaviors. Driving buy-in across sales, ops, finance, HR, and enablement depends on trust, negotiation, and context that AI cannot fully own; humans handle escalations and exceptions. Scaling affects quotas, customer experience, and operational risk; humans must make final decisions based on evidence and qualitative context.|Uses deterministic BI/SQL and classical statistical methods (A/B testing, regression, time-series) to quantify impact vs baseline, control for seasonality, and compute confidence intervals. Applies transparent rules to select participants (tenure, territory, quota attainment) and to calculate incentive payouts; reduces ambiguity and supports auditability. Flags missing/invalid CRM fields, sudden metric spikes/drops, and tracking breaks using thresholds or simple ML; prevents decisions based on corrupted data.

column8: Support end-to-end sales operations by accurately processing SMB sales orders, renewals, and amendments while following required controls and compliance steps for each transaction.
column15: This task mixes high-volume structured processing (well-suited to Traditional AI rule validation and anomaly detection) with cross-system workflow execution (best handled by Agentic AI orchestration) and unstructured intake/communication (where Generative AI adds leverage). However, sales order processing is control-heavy (SOX/approval matrices), and SMB deals still contain edge cases (non-standard terms, customer disputes, credit/tax/export flags). Humans remain essential for accountable approvals, resolving ambiguity, and ensuring audit evidence. Assumptions: core systems expose APIs or can be automated via RPA; policies are documented and accessible for retrieval; organization permits AI-assisted drafting but requires human send/approval for external comms and any pricing/term changes.
column16: Human oversight is essential at (1) approval gates: pricing exceptions, non-standard payment terms, backdating, cancellations/credits, and any legal/compliance escalations; (2) low-confidence extraction/validation: when GenAI confidence is low or documents conflict; (3) customer-intent ambiguity and negotiation: interpreting nuanced requests and confirming acceptance; (4) control sign-offs and audit evidence: ensuring required attachments/approvals are present and that segregation-of-duties is respected; (5) incident handling: suspected fraud, sanctions/export-control hits, or data privacy concerns. Humans provide accountability, policy interpretation, and risk acceptance that current AI cannot reliably assume.
column17: Risks: unintended actions across systems (wrong account, duplicate orders), over-automation bypassing approvals, brittle integrations, and opaque decision paths. Mitigations: least-privilege access, hard guardrails (cannot submit without required approvals), sandbox/testing, step-level confirmations for sensitive actions, idempotency keys, comprehensive audit trails, and human-in-the-loop for exceptions/high-risk branches.
column18: Risks: hallucinating terms/SKUs, misreading PDFs, or drafting non-compliant language; context drift across long threads; inadvertent leakage of customer data if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigations: retrieval with citations to source docs/SOPs, confidence scoring with mandatory human review for low confidence, redaction/DLP, restricted templates, and logging of prompts/outputs for audit.
column19: Risks: rule brittleness and maintenance burden as products/pricing/policies change; false positives/negatives in anomaly models; bias if historical data reflects poor practices. Mitigations: governance for rule updates, versioning and testing, monitoring model drift, explainability, and periodic calibration with compliance/pricing teams.
column20: Risks: inconsistent source-of-truth between CRM/CPQ/ERP, race conditions (updates out of order), conflicting recommendations (GenAI vs rule engine), and audit gaps if actions aren't logged end-to-end. Mitigations: canonical data model, event-driven orchestration with retries, reconciliation jobs, unified audit logging, clear precedence (rules/controls override GenAI), and defined escalation paths.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end order workflow across systems (CRM -> CPQ -> ERP/billing -> e-sign -> provisioning). Exception handling and case management for stalled/failed transactions|Extract and normalize order details from unstructured inputs (emails, PDFs, chat notes) into structured fields. Draft customer/partner communications and internal summaries. Policy/controls guidance at point of work (playbooks, checklists, rationale)|Final approval for high-risk or non-standard transactions (pricing exceptions, unusual terms, compliance escalations). Resolve ambiguous customer intent and negotiate corrections. Quality assurance and audit readiness (spot checks, evidence attachment, control sign-off)|Deterministic validation of structured order data and compliance rules. Anomaly/fraud/quality detection on orders and renewals
column23: Executes a multi-step plan: gathers required fields, triggers validations, routes for approvals, creates/updates records in each system via APIs/RPA, monitors status, retries on transient failures, and escalates exceptions. Best fit because it must coordinate sequential steps and adapt to branching paths (new order vs renewal vs amendment). Detects stuck states (e.g., missing tax ID, credit hold, pricing approval pending), opens/updates a case, requests missing info from the rep/customer, and proposes next actions while keeping an audit trail. Uses policies/guardrails to avoid unauthorized changes.|Uses document understanding to pull SKUs, quantities, term dates, billing contacts, ship-to, renewal uplift notes, and special terms; flags low-confidence fields for review. Useful when SMB orders arrive via varied formats. Generates compliant email drafts for missing information, renewal confirmations, amendment summaries, and handoff notes; summarizes key deal terms and changes for internal stakeholders, reducing rep time while keeping humans in approval loop. Answers rep questions like 'Does this amendment require legal review?' by retrieving and summarizing relevant SOPs/controls; produces step-by-step checklists tailored to transaction type, while citing sources to reduce hallucination risk.|Humans must make accountable decisions where policy interpretation, commercial judgment, or regulatory exposure exists; required for audit and governance (e.g., SOX controls, legal exceptions). When inputs conflict (e.g., customer asks for backdated start, partial cancellation, special invoicing), a human clarifies intent, negotiates acceptable terms, and documents rationale-areas where AI may misinterpret nuance. Ensures the transaction record contains correct supporting documents, approvals, and notes; validates AI outputs and signs off on control completion, especially for entry-level reps operating under supervision.|Applies rule engines and schema checks: required fields, SKU compatibility, discount thresholds, tax/VAT logic, export-control country screening triggers, SOX-required approvals, and segregation-of-duties constraints. Deterministic behavior is preferred for auditability. Uses classical ML/statistical models to flag outliers (unusual discounting, mismatched billing/shipping, duplicate orders, abnormal renewal churn patterns) and route to review. Produces explainable signals and thresholds.

column8: Manage daily operational workflows by tracking requests, prioritizing work queues, and coordinating handoffs to ensure timely completion of sales support activities.
column15: This workflow task mixes structured operations (routing, SLA tracking) with unstructured intake and communication. Traditional AI is best for deterministic validation/routing, SLA timers, and dedupe. Generative AI adds value by turning messy emails/chats into structured summaries and drafting handoff/status communications and briefings, but requires verification due to hallucination risk. Agentic AI can coordinate multi-step actions across CRM/ticketing and dynamically reprioritize/escalate within defined guardrails, reducing manual queue management. Humans remain essential for policy setting, exception handling, and relationship/compliance-sensitive decisions. Assumptions: the org uses a CRM + ticketing/queue system with APIs; clear SLAs and routing rules exist; agent actions are constrained (approval gates for high-impact changes).
column16: Human oversight is required to (1) approve or override priority changes that affect revenue/customer commitments, (2) validate ambiguous or incomplete requests where misinterpretation could cause wrong actions, (3) ensure compliance (privacy, pricing/contract language, regulated customer communications) and prevent unauthorized commitments, (4) handle escalations involving cross-team conflict, customer dissatisfaction, or reputational risk, and (5) periodically audit automation logs/metrics and update rules/guardrails when products, territories, or policies change. Edge cases: VIP/strategic accounts, legal/finance approvals, duplicate accounts with similar names, outages causing backlog spikes, and requests containing sensitive personal data.
column17: Autonomous orchestration can misroute, over-escalate, or create notification spam; emergent behavior may optimize for SLA at the expense of customer experience. Mitigations: strict action permissions, approval gates for reroutes/escalations, rate limits, simulation/testing, and continuous monitoring with rollback.
column18: May hallucinate details in summaries or draft messages that imply commitments (pricing, timelines). Risk of leaking sensitive data if prompts include PII or if outputs are sent without review. Mitigations: retrieval from source-of-truth records, constrained templates, redaction, confidence/uncertainty flags, and human approval for external comms or high-value accounts.
column19: Rules can become brittle/outdated (territory changes, new products), causing systematic misrouting; simple anomaly models may miss nuanced issues. Mitigations: versioned rule management, periodic audits, exception queues, and monitoring of precision/recall on routing/dedupe.
column20: Data inconsistency across CRM/ticketing/email can cause conflicting priorities and duplicate actions; identity matching errors can propagate; unclear ownership between agentic actions and human responsibilities can create gaps. Mitigations: unified IDs, event-driven architecture with idempotency, audit trails, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and clear RACI for handoffs.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end workflow across tools (CRM/ticketing/email/chat). Dynamic queue reprioritization and escalation management. Dependency tracking and handoff coordination|Summarize incoming requests and normalize unstructured inputs. Draft internal handoff notes and customer-facing status updates. Generate daily/weekly operational briefings|Set prioritization policy and handle exceptions/high-stakes decisions. Quality control, compliance checks, and relationship-sensitive communication. Process improvement and training data/feedback loop|Rule-based intake validation and routing. SLA timers, alerts, and anomaly detection on queue metrics. Deduplication and record matching
column23: Monitors multiple systems for new/updated requests, creates/updates tasks, assigns owners based on routing rules and capacity, and triggers handoff steps (e.g., notify SDR/AE/ops) while maintaining an auditable activity log; adapts to changing queue states (SLA breaches, owner OOO) within guardrails. Continuously recalculates priority using SLA timers, deal stage/value, customer tier, and dependency status; auto-escalates to managers or alternate queues when thresholds are met, requesting human approval for high-impact reroutes. Detects blockers (missing info, pending approvals), pings the right stakeholder, schedules follow-ups, and confirms acceptance of handoffs; can propose next-best actions when a downstream team rejects or delays a handoff.|Extracts intent, key fields (account, product, urgency, requested action), and missing information from emails/chats/notes; produces structured summaries for CRM/tickets and flags ambiguities for review. Generates concise, role-appropriate messages (to AEs, ops, finance, customer) using context from CRM/tickets; offers variants by tone and channel and includes required compliance disclaimers/templates. Creates digest reports of queue health (aging, SLA risk, top blockers), highlights anomalies, and suggests process improvements; links back to source records for verification.|Defines what 'priority' means (revenue impact, customer commitments), approves overrides (VIP accounts, legal/finance implications), and resolves conflicts between teams when automation cannot adjudicate. Reviews AI-generated summaries/messages for accuracy, tone, and policy adherence (pricing, commitments, data privacy); handles escalations requiring empathy or negotiation. Identifies recurring failure modes (misroutes, missing fields), updates playbooks/rules, and provides feedback to improve prompts, routing logic, and agent guardrails.|Applies deterministic checks (required fields present, correct queue, territory/product mapping) and routes requests using business rules; provides explainable outcomes and reduces variability. Computes aging, breach risk, and workload distribution; triggers alerts when metrics exceed thresholds; uses simple statistical/ML models to detect unusual spikes or drops. Uses fuzzy matching and deterministic keys to identify duplicate tickets/leads/requests and merge or link them, reducing rework and conflicting handoffs.

column8: Coordinate with internal partners and external vendors to resolve order, billing, and provisioning issues, ensuring clear ownership, timely follow-up, and documented outcomes.
column15: This Inside Sales Rep task is a multi-party, cross-system resolution workflow with both structured (IDs, statuses, SLAs) and unstructured (emails, call notes) inputs. Traditional AI is well-suited for deterministic checks, classification, and SLA timers (20%). Generative AI accelerates drafting communications and summarizing/documenting outcomes but must be constrained to provided facts to avoid hallucinations (20%). Agentic AI can orchestrate the end-to-end follow-up loop-creating tasks, polling systems, escalating, and keeping ownership clear-yet still needs guardrails and approvals for commitments and financial actions (25%). Humans remain essential for exception handling, approvals, and relationship/accountability management, especially when policies conflict or outcomes affect revenue/customer trust (35%). Assumptions: access to CRM/ticketing plus ERP/billing/provisioning APIs; clear SOPs and escalation matrices; vendor portals allow read access and templated messaging. Constraints: external vendors may not support automation; data quality varies; entry-level reps require coaching/oversight, increasing human share.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) any action that changes financial outcomes (credits, refunds, rebills), modifies contracts, or commits delivery/provisioning timelines; (2) privacy/security checks when sharing customer data with vendors (PII minimization, NDA/DPAs); (3) ambiguous root-cause scenarios where systems disagree or policy is unclear; (4) conflict resolution between internal teams/venders and escalation decisions that impact relationships; (5) final validation of closure notes for accuracy and customer-safe wording. Humans should also review AI-generated outbound messages for high-value accounts, regulated industries, or when tone/commitments could create liability. Edge cases: suspected fraud, chargebacks, provisioning impacting critical services, or repeated vendor non-performance-require immediate human escalation.
column17: Autonomous follow-ups could spam partners, escalate prematurely, or take unintended actions if permissions are broad; planning errors can create duplicate tickets or conflicting ownership. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, action allowlists, rate limits, explicit approval steps for escalations/credits, audit logs, and simulation/testing in sandbox environments.
column18: May hallucinate order/billing facts, invent commitments, or misquote policy; can leak sensitive data if prompts include PII; tone may be inappropriate for vendors/customers. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with citations, strict templates, redaction/PII filters, and human approval gates for external messages and financial commitments.
column19: Rules/ML can be brittle when products/processes change; misclassification can route cases incorrectly and miss SLAs; anomaly thresholds may generate noise. Mitigations: continuous monitoring, periodic rule reviews, feedback loops from resolution outcomes, and fallback to human triage when confidence is low.
column20: Data synchronization issues across CRM/ERP/vendor portals can cause inconsistent status and wrong actions; identity matching errors (account/order IDs) can mis-associate cases; unclear handoffs between AI components can create gaps in ownership. Mitigations: master data management, idempotent workflows, unified case IDs, confidence scoring with human-in-the-loop, end-to-end observability (dashboards), and clear RACI for ownership.
column22: Case intake, triage, and routing across systems. Workflow orchestration and follow-up automation. Cross-party status synchronization|Draft partner/vendor communications and clarifying questions. Summarize case history and produce resolution notes. Knowledge assistance for policies and playbooks|Handle exceptions, negotiations, and high-stakes decisions. Approve external-facing commitments and compliance-sensitive actions. Relationship management and accountability enforcement|Issue classification and prioritization scoring. Data validation and anomaly detection. SLA tracking, alerts, and escalation triggers
column23: Monitors inbound tickets/emails/CRM notes, extracts key fields (order ID, account, symptom), checks required data, and routes to the correct internal queue/vendor portal based on issue taxonomy and SLAs; creates/updates the case record and assigns an owner. Plans and executes multi-step follow-ups (ping partner, wait for response, escalate, schedule reminders), adapts based on SLA timers and responses, and maintains a next-best-action plan while keeping the Inside Sales Rep in the loop for approvals. Pulls status from ERP/billing/provisioning tools and vendor portals, reconciles discrepancies, and updates stakeholders with the latest state; flags blockers and proposes escalation paths.|Generates concise, role-appropriate emails/chat messages using case context, asks for missing artifacts (PO, invoice, provisioning logs), and tailors tone to internal vs external recipients; supports translation if needed. Summarizes long threads/call notes into a timeline, captures decisions and commitments, and drafts customer-safe internal documentation (RCA snippets, what was done, next steps) for CRM/ticket closure. Retrieves and paraphrases relevant SOPs (billing adjustments, provisioning retries, credit memo rules) and suggests compliant options; highlights uncertainty when policy is ambiguous.|Required for judgment calls (credits/refunds, contract interpretation, goodwill adjustments), negotiating with vendors/partners, and deciding when to escalate or deviate from SOP. Ensures accuracy and compliance (pricing, legal terms, privacy), approves messages that commit timelines/financial outcomes, and validates that documentation is customer-safe. Uses interpersonal skills to secure ownership across teams, resolve conflicts, and maintain partner relationships-areas where automation can support but not replace human influence.|Uses rules/ML on structured signals (product, region, error codes, customer tier, aging) to label issue type (order vs billing vs provisioning) and compute priority/SLA risk scores for queue ordering. Deterministically checks for mismatches (invoice vs contract price, duplicate orders, provisioning status vs shipment) and flags likely root causes using rules and statistical thresholds. Runs timers and rule-based escalation (e.g., no vendor response in 24h, provisioning stuck >48h), generating alerts and escalation tasks to managers/ops.

column8: Maintain complete documentation for sales processes, approvals, and exceptions, ensuring records are audit-ready and aligned with established governance requirements.
column15: This task is largely process-driven and benefits from automation, but it is compliance-sensitive. Traditional AI is best for deterministic governance checks (required artifacts, thresholds, routing) and explainable validation. Agentic AI adds value by coordinating multi-system workflows (CRM/CPQ/contracting), enforcing sequencing, and assembling audit packages with minimal rep effort. Generative AI reduces manual writing by drafting exception narratives and converting unstructured communications into structured, searchable notes, but requires constraints and review due to hallucination risk. Humans remain essential for accountable approvals, nuanced exception judgment, and attesting that records are truthful and complete. Assumptions: the sales stack has APIs/webhooks (e.g., Salesforce/HubSpot, CPQ, e-sign), policies are codified into checklists, and data access is governed (RBAC). Constraints: entry-level ISR work is high-volume; solutions must be low-friction and embedded in existing tools.
column16: Human oversight is essential at (1) approval decisions for high-discount/non-standard terms and any exception where policy interpretation or risk tolerance is required; (2) final attestation that summaries and dossiers accurately reflect customer commitments (preventing AI-introduced inaccuracies); (3) handling edge cases such as conflicting evidence (email vs. CRM), missing customer consent for recording/transcripts, regulated/strategic accounts, or legal disputes; (4) governance ownership-updating policies, thresholds, and templates, and deciding when to override automated blocks/escalations. Humans also must respond to audit inquiries and provide context beyond what systems capture.
column17: Autonomous workflow actions could block deals incorrectly, spam stakeholders, or route approvals to wrong owners due to context errors; emergent behavior in multi-step planning can create compliance gaps if it 'optimizes' for speed. Mitigations: bounded action space, approval gates for high-risk actions, audit logs of every action, simulation/testing in sandbox, and clear rollback/override mechanisms.
column18: May hallucinate approval rationales, misstate terms, or omit critical caveats when summarizing calls/emails; could leak sensitive customer data if prompts/logs are not controlled. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved policy sources, strict templates, citation/trace links to source artifacts, PII redaction, and mandatory human review for exceptions above thresholds.
column19: Rules can become outdated as policies change, causing false failures or missed requirements; ML classifiers may encode historical bias (e.g., stricter routing for certain segments) and can be brittle with new products/regions. Mitigations: policy-as-code versioning, periodic rule reviews, monitoring precision/recall, explainability, and change-management with compliance sign-off.
column20: Data mismatches across CRM/CPQ/e-sign (IDs, versions), inconsistent timestamps, and permissioning gaps can produce incomplete dossiers; conflicting outputs between GenAI summaries and rule checks can confuse reps. Mitigations: master data management, canonical opportunity IDs, end-to-end traceability, RBAC/least privilege, unified audit log, and UI that clearly distinguishes 'source of truth' artifacts from AI-generated summaries.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end documentation workflow across CRM/CPQ/e-signature and shared drives. Proactively chase missing approvals/exceptions and enforce sequencing. Maintain an audit-ready evidence package per opportunity|Draft and standardize exception justifications and approval summaries. Summarize and normalize unstructured communications into compliant notes. Answer governance Q&A and guide reps through documentation requirements|Make judgment calls on non-standard exceptions and provide rationale. Approve/attest to accuracy of final documentation and sensitive records. Maintain and update governance policies, templates, and escalation rules|Validate completeness via rules-based checklists and field validation. Classify deals into governance categories and route approvals. Detect anomalies and potential non-compliance patterns
column23: Monitors deal-stage events (e.g., discount request, non-standard terms, exception approvals) and triggers the right documentation steps, assigns tasks, routes for approvals, and follows up on missing artifacts using predefined playbooks and context from the opportunity. Detects when required approvals are absent (e.g., pricing exception without finance approval), pings owners, escalates based on SLA, and blocks downstream steps (quote send, order submission) until governance gates are satisfied. Automatically compiles a deal dossier (approvals, email threads, call notes links, contract versions, exception rationale) into a standardized folder/record, ensuring versioning and traceability; flags gaps for human resolution.|Generates concise, policy-aligned narratives from structured fields (discount %, competitor, customer segment) and unstructured notes, using approved templates and controlled vocabulary to reduce rep effort and improve consistency. Converts call transcripts, emails, and chat into structured CRM notes (who/what/when/why), highlighting approval-relevant statements and extracting key terms for later audit retrieval. Acts as an embedded assistant that explains what documents are needed for a given deal scenario, provides checklists, and suggests next steps; constrained to internal policy sources to reduce hallucinations.|Humans must assess business context, negotiate trade-offs, and articulate defensible rationale for exceptions where policy is ambiguous or risk is high. A rep/manager/compliance owner must confirm that generated summaries and compiled evidence accurately reflect reality, especially for audits, legal exposure, or regulated customers. Policy owners define what 'audit-ready' means, update thresholds and required artifacts, and handle edge cases; AI cannot authoritatively set governance without accountable human ownership.|Applies deterministic rules per deal type (e.g., discount threshold, payment terms, region) to verify required fields/documents exist; produces explainable pass/fail results and specific missing-item lists. Uses rules/ML on structured attributes to assign approval paths (standard vs. exception) and required approvers; integrates with CRM workflow engines for consistent routing. Runs anomaly detection on discounts, term deviations, approval timing, and missing artifacts to flag risky opportunities for review and coaching.

column8: Analyze sales and operational data to identify trends, exceptions, and root causes, then communicate findings to stakeholders to improve accuracy and reduce rework.
column15: The core of the task is structured data work (extract/clean/reconcile, KPI monitoring, anomaly detection, and driver analysis), which is mature and reliable with Traditional AI/analytics-hence the largest share. Generative AI adds high leverage in communicating insights and standardizing RCA narratives, but must be grounded in verified outputs to avoid fabrication. Agentic AI can automate the multi-step workflow and triage/routing, yet autonomy should be constrained (read-only data access, human approval for external comms or system changes) given operational risk. Humans remain essential to set business definitions, validate exceptions, interpret ambiguous causes, and secure stakeholder buy-in for process changes-especially for an entry-level Inside Sales context where domain nuance and accountability matter. Assumptions: access to CRM/ERP data, defined KPI dictionary, and a ticketing/communication channel; without these, human share increases and agentic share decreases.
column16: Human oversight is required to (1) define KPI targets/thresholds and what constitutes 'rework' across Sales Ops/Finance; (2) validate anomalies before escalation (promotions, one-off enterprise terms, data latency); (3) approve stakeholder communications that could affect customer commitments or rep performance perceptions; (4) decide corrective actions and prioritize fixes under resource constraints; (5) ensure compliance/privacy (PII in call notes, contractual data) and handle disputes. Edge cases: sparse data for new products/territories, seasonality shifts, system migrations, and conflicting sources of truth-humans must adjudicate.
column17: Autonomous actions could create noisy tickets, spam stakeholders, or trigger incorrect escalations; brittle tool integrations may cause partial execution; emergent behavior if goals are underspecified (optimize 'reduce rework' by suppressing reporting). Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates, rate limits, audit logs, sandbox testing, explicit policies/objectives, and fallback to human-in-the-loop on low-confidence cases.
column18: May hallucinate causes or overstate confidence; can misinterpret metrics if not grounded to a governed semantic layer; risk of leaking sensitive deal/customer info in prompts/outputs; tone or attribution errors in stakeholder comms. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved datasets, citation of source tables, constrained templates, redaction, and mandatory human review for external/high-stakes messages.
column19: Rules/models can become stale with process changes (new SKUs, pricing bands), leading to false positives/negatives; bias if historical data reflects uneven rep support; limited ability to capture unstructured drivers (call notes). Mitigations: monitoring/drift checks, periodic rule review, model retraining, explainability dashboards, and inclusion of domain experts in validation.
column20: Mismatch between metric definitions across systems can propagate errors into GenAI narratives; orchestration failures can mix time windows or duplicate records; feedback loops where agentic routing changes behavior and data distribution; security risks across connectors. Mitigations: governed semantic layer, end-to-end data lineage, versioned datasets, reconciliation tests, unified identity/access management, and staged rollout with KPI-based acceptance criteria.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end data refresh and analysis workflow. Automated exception triage and ticket routing|Narrative insight generation and stakeholder-ready summaries. Ad-hoc query assistance and hypothesis brainstorming. Create reusable templates and playbooks|Define business context, success criteria, and priority questions. Validate findings against ground truth and operational reality. Make decisions and drive change management. Handle edge cases and sensitive/customer-impacting exceptions|Data extraction, cleaning, and reconciliation rules. Trend detection and KPI monitoring. Anomaly detection and exception scoring. Root-cause analytics on structured drivers
column23: Plans and executes a multi-step pipeline (pull latest CRM/ERP/order data, run checks/models, generate outputs), monitors for failures, retries, and routes exceptions to the right owner with minimal human prompting. When anomalies are detected, autonomously gathers supporting evidence (related orders, call notes, pricing approvals), proposes likely root-cause categories, and creates/updates tickets in CRM/ITSM with recommended next actions.|Turns analytical outputs into clear explanations (what changed, why it matters, impact), drafts emails/Slack updates, and produces exec summaries tailored to Sales Ops, Finance, and Inside Sales leadership. Helps the rep translate business questions into metrics/filters, suggests plausible drivers (e.g., territory changes, discounting, data-entry errors), and proposes follow-up analyses while staying grounded to provided data. Drafts standard RCA write-ups, weekly trend report templates, and checklists for common rework drivers (duplicate accounts, wrong SKUs, missing PO), improving consistency for an entry-level rep.|Stakeholders/rep must decide which KPIs matter, acceptable thresholds, and what 'accuracy' and 'rework' mean in the local process; this is context- and policy-dependent. Human checks whether flagged issues are real (e.g., legitimate promo pricing) and confirms root-cause plausibility using tacit knowledge and conversations with Sales Ops/Finance. Humans approve corrective actions (process changes, training, system fixes), handle sensitive communications, and ensure alignment across teams. Complex deals, contractual terms, compliance constraints, and customer escalations require judgment and accountability beyond current AI autonomy.|Uses deterministic ETL, validation rules, and joins to reconcile CRM opportunities, orders, invoices, and activity logs; flags missing/invalid fields and duplicates with explainable rule outputs. Applies time-series baselines, control charts, and threshold alerts on KPIs (conversion rate, quote-to-order time, rework rate) to detect statistically meaningful shifts. Uses classical ML (isolation forest, z-scores) or rules to score unusual deals/orders (pricing outliers, abnormal cycle times) and rank them for review. Runs regression/decision trees and cohort slicing to quantify which structured factors (region, product, channel, rep, discount band) correlate with errors/rework.

column8: Improve processes through standardization and automation by updating templates, checklists, and system workflows, and by proposing enhancements that reduce manual effort.
column15: This task mixes (a) creative/linguistic standardization (templates, SOPs), (b) deterministic system configuration (validations, routing, checklists), and (c) multi-step execution/monitoring across sales tools. Generative AI is strong for drafting templates, documentation, and synthesizing qualitative feedback but must be grounded in approved facts and policies. Traditional AI/automation is best for stable, explainable rules (required fields, routing, dedupe) and structured analytics to quantify bottlenecks. Agentic AI can accelerate implementation by orchestrating changes and running iterative experiments, but autonomy must be constrained (sandbox, approvals, rollback) due to customer-impact risk. Humans remain essential to set priorities, approve customer-facing/compliance-sensitive changes, and validate edge cases typical in sales motions. Assumptions: the org uses a CRM (e.g., Salesforce/HubSpot), has API access/sandbox, and maintains brand/compliance guidelines; entry-level ISR contributes ideas and drafts but final approvals sit with team lead/ops.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) selecting which processes to standardize (trade-offs, stakeholder alignment), (2) approving any customer-facing language and workflow changes that affect consent, privacy, pricing, SLAs, or contractual commitments, (3) validating that automations don't harm lead/customer experience (misrouting, over-contacting, incorrect suppression), (4) handling exceptions (strategic accounts, partner leads, regional rules), and (5) go/no-go decisions, rollback, and change management/training. Humans should review AI-generated templates for factual accuracy and policy compliance, and approve agentic executions beyond low-risk, reversible changes.
column17: Autonomous actions can misconfigure CRM/workflows (routing errors, broken integrations), create unintended contact sequences, or over-optimize local metrics at expense of customer experience; limited transparency in decision paths. Mitigate with least-privilege access, sandbox-first deployment, approval gates, rate limits, audit logs, rollback plans, and constrained action libraries.
column18: May hallucinate policy details, product claims, or process steps; can introduce non-compliant language (privacy/consent) or inconsistent tone; may leak sensitive CRM data if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigate with grounding (retrieval from approved docs), redaction, template guardrails, and mandatory human approval for external content.
column19: Rules can become brittle/outdated as sales motions change; deterministic validations may increase friction if poorly designed; anomaly thresholds can miss novel failures. Mitigate with periodic rule reviews, A/B testing, feedback loops, and monitoring coverage for new segments.
column20: Conflicting recommendations (GenAI suggests changes that violate rule constraints), cascading failures across integrated tools, and unclear accountability when agentic actions use GenAI outputs. Mitigate with a single source of truth for policies, version control for workflows/templates, staged deployments, automated regression tests, and explicit RACI/approval workflows.
column22: Orchestrate workflow updates across tools (CRM, email sequences, ticketing) in a sandbox. Monitor process performance and trigger iterative improvements|Draft and refine standardized templates (emails, call scripts, follow-up messages) and checklist language. Synthesize pain points and improvement ideas from unstructured feedback. Create documentation and change communications|Define success criteria, constraints, and prioritize improvements. Compliance, brand, and customer-impact review/approval. Validate edge cases and provide domain context|Analyze structured CRM/process data to find bottlenecks and rule candidates. Implement deterministic standardization rules and validations. Regression checks and anomaly detection on workflow outcomes
column23: Plans and executes multi-step changes (e.g., create/modify CRM fields, update pipeline stages, adjust routing rules, update sequence steps) via approved APIs/RPA, runs in a test environment, and produces an audit trail for review. column15: The task mixes repeatable operational execution (provisioning, validations, monitoring) with ambiguous intake, cross-team coordination, and high-stakes security/routing decisions. Traditional AI and scripts handle deterministic checks and routine provisioning with strong auditability. Agentic AI can orchestrate multi-step runbooks and regression testing for standard, low-risk changes, reducing cycle time, but must be constrained by approvals and guardrails. Generative AI adds value in unstructured request triage, documentation, and test-case drafting, but cannot be the source of truth for permissions or routing logic. Humans remain essential for policy exceptions, strategic routing design, prioritization, and final production accountability. Assumptions: systems expose APIs/logs; change management exists (tickets, approvals); environment separation (sandbox/prod) is available; compliance requires audit trails.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) privileged access and SoD exceptions, including approvals and periodic access reviews; (2) defining/approving routing logic changes that affect revenue distribution, fairness, and sales strategy; (3) interpreting ambiguous or conflicting stakeholder requirements; (4) production deployment go/no-go, rollback decisions, and incident command during outages; (5) compliance/audit responses where intent and justification matter. Edge cases: mergers/reorgs causing mass permission changes; emergency access during incidents; routing conflicts between territory, named accounts, and partner leads; automation loops causing data corruption-require human judgment and escalation.
column17: Could execute incorrect changes at scale (mass permission grants, routing misconfig) due to misclassification or tool errors; emergent behavior in multi-step plans; mitigation: least-privilege tool access, approval gates, dry-run mode, rate limits, sandbox-first execution, immutable audit logs, and rollback automation.
column18: May hallucinate requirements, mis-extract critical fields (role, region), or generate incorrect SOPs; privacy risk if prompts include sensitive customer/employee data; mitigation: structured templates, retrieval from approved knowledge base, redaction, human review for externally visible comms and policy docs.
column19: Rules can become outdated after org/territory changes, causing false approvals/denials; anomaly thresholds may miss slow-burn issues; mitigation: version-controlled policy matrices, periodic rule reviews, monitoring calibration, and exception workflows.
column20: Conflicting sources of truth between GenAI summaries and rule engines; orchestration failures across APIs leading to partial deployments; audit gaps if actions aren't logged consistently; mitigation: single ticketing system as control plane, idempotent runbooks, end-to-end tracing, standardized schemas, and separation of duties between suggestion (GenAI) and execution (agentic/scripts).

Plans and runs multi-step test plans in sandbox (create test users/leads, simulate routing, validate automation outcomes), compares results to expected baselines, and coordinates deployment windows/rollback steps with systems teams.|Extracts intent, impacted teams, objects, and urgency from free-text requests; drafts clarifying questions and converts requests into structured templates (fields, acceptance criteria). column8: Develop and maintain sales operations documentation including playbooks, definitions, and standard operating procedures to enable scalable execution and consistent reporting.
column15: Documentation work is content-heavy and benefits strongly from Generative AI for drafting, rewriting, summarizing, and producing consistent playbooks/definitions. Agentic AI adds value by coordinating multi-step workflows (intake, routing, approvals, publishing) and by monitoring upstream system changes to keep docs current-tasks that are repetitive but require context and tool integration. Traditional AI is best for deterministic governance: template checks, link validation, PII keyword rules, tagging, and duplicate detection. Humans remain essential for authoritative decisions (process/metric truth), stakeholder alignment, and validating real-world edge cases. Assumptions: documentation lives in a central wiki with APIs; change logs exist for CRM/BI; an approved template/style guide is available; access controls prevent GenAI from seeing restricted data without redaction.
column16: Human oversight is required to (1) approve any process/metric definition that affects compensation, forecasting, or external reporting; (2) adjudicate conflicting stakeholder inputs and ensure alignment with GTM strategy; (3) validate exceptions (e.g., multi-year deals, channel attribution, renewals vs expansions) and confirm feasibility with frontline teams; (4) ensure compliance/privacy (PII, customer data, contractual terms) and enforce access controls; (5) sign off on major version changes and deprecations. Humans should also spot-check AI outputs for subtle inaccuracies, outdated assumptions, or tone that could reduce adoption.
column17: Over-automation could publish unapproved changes, spam stakeholders with tasks, or update the wrong pages due to mis-scoped triggers; opaque decisioning and cascading workflow errors across tools. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, explicit approval checkpoints, dry-run mode, audit logs, rate limits, and clear rollback/version control.
column18: Hallucinated steps/definitions, incorrect metric logic, or confident but wrong guidance; context drift when multiple versions exist; leakage of sensitive deal/customer data if prompts include PII; inconsistent terminology. Mitigations: retrieval from approved sources only (RAG), strict templates, citation requirements, redaction/DLP, human approval gates for high-impact docs, and automated regression checks against metric specs.
column19: Brittle rules may miss nuanced compliance issues or flag too many false positives; ML tagging can misclassify new processes/regions leading to poor discoverability. Mitigations: periodic rule review, feedback loops for classifier retraining, and human override for taxonomy changes.
column20: Mismatch between source-of-truth systems (CRM/BI) and documentation leading to inconsistent reporting; broken connectors/APIs causing silent drift; conflicting outputs between GenAI drafts and rule-based validators; governance gaps around ownership and versioning. Mitigations: define a single source of truth per artifact, automated reconciliation checks, monitoring/alerts for connector failures, unified taxonomy, and end-to-end change management with approvals and audit trails.

Metadata tagging, search indexing, and duplicate/near-duplicate detection

Watches CRM fields, BI metric definitions, compensation plans, and process changes (via change logs/APIs) and opens update tasks, proposes impacted sections, and schedules periodic reviews to prevent documentation drift.|Transforms fragmented inputs into structured SOPs (purpose, scope, steps, RACI, exceptions), generates role-specific playbooks, and rewrites for clarity/consistency while following an approved template and style guide. Generates plain-language definitions, examples, and "how calculated" narratives from approved metric specs; proposes synonyms and disambiguation notes to reduce confusion across Sales, Finance, and BI. Classical NLP/ML classifies docs by process area (lead-to-cash stage, region, segment), auto-tags owners/effective dates, and detects redundant pages to reduce sprawl.

column8: Partner cross-functionally with Finance, Marketing, and Customer Success to align metrics, definitions, and handoffs, and to resolve operational issues impacting revenue execution.
column15: This Sales Ops task mixes structured analytics (best for Traditional AI), heavy cross-functional communication and documentation (well-suited to Generative AI), and ongoing coordination/monitoring across tools (where Agentic AI can add leverage). However, final alignment on metric definitions and handoffs is inherently socio-technical: it affects incentives, forecasting, and sometimes revenue recognition, so humans must negotiate, decide, and own governance. Assumptions: the org has reasonably accessible data sources (CRM, billing, marketing automation, CS platform) and permissions for AI tooling; metric alignment decisions require stakeholder sign-off; AI outputs are reviewed before being treated as source of truth.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving canonical definitions that impact comp plans, forecasting, and financial reporting; (2) resolving conflicts between departments and setting governance/RACI; (3) validating AI-identified root causes before remediation (to avoid incorrect backfills or process changes); (4) compliance/privacy controls when sharing customer or financial data across tools; (5) exception handling (new products, acquisitions, atypical deal structures) where rules/models may fail. Humans should approve any changes to data pipelines, attribution logic, stage definitions, and customer-facing communications during incidents.
column17: Autonomous actions (creating tickets, changing workflows, pinging stakeholders) can cause noise, mis-escalations, or unintended process changes; opaque decisioning and feedback loops may reinforce wrong priorities; permission overreach across systems. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for high-impact actions, audit logs, rate limits, and clear escalation policies.
column18: May hallucinate or overconfidently merge conflicting definitions; can miss subtle accounting/RevRec nuances; risk of leaking sensitive customer/financial data in prompts; tone or wording may inflame cross-functional tensions. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations, redaction/DLP, constrained templates, and mandatory human review for canonical docs.
column19: Brittle rules and models can drift as processes/products change; false positives/negatives in anomaly detection; reconciliation logic may encode outdated mappings leading to incorrect KPIs. Mitigations: versioned metric logic, monitoring for drift, periodic rule reviews with owners, and test suites for data pipelines.
column20: Inconsistent sources of truth across CRM/billing/marketing/CS can cause AI agents to act on stale data; identity resolution issues (account/contact matching) propagate errors; governance gaps on who can change metric logic; compounded errors when GenAI narratives are based on Traditional AI alerts without validation. Mitigations: centralized semantic layer/metric store, master data management, end-to-end lineage, human approval checkpoints, and incident postmortems to update rules/prompts/workflows.

Monitor operational signals and trigger investigations/escalations|Synthesize and reconcile metric definitions across teams. Draft cross-functional documentation and communications. Root-cause analysis support from unstructured inputs|Negotiate and approve final metric definitions and governance. Manage stakeholder relationships and drive change adoption. Make high-stakes decisions during revenue-impacting incidents|Detect anomalies and data quality issues impacting revenue metrics. Automate metric computation and reconciliation across systems. Handoff compliance tracking and SLA reporting
column23: Uses a goal-driven workflow agent integrated with calendar/ticketing/docs to propose a project plan, assign action items, chase updates, and route artifacts (metric dictionary, RACI, handoff SOP) for review/approval; adapts plan based on blockers and SLA breaches. Runs rule-based ETL/ELT validations and reconciliation logic (joins, deduping, mapping tables) to ensure consistent KPI calculations across Finance, Marketing, and CS reporting layers; produces variance reports. Uses rules to measure handoff timeliness/completeness (e.g., MQL->SQL, Closed Won->Onboarding) and generates dashboards/scorecards by team/segment to pinpoint breakdowns.

column8: Provide ad hoc analysis and decision support by answering stakeholder questions, building targeted reports, and presenting insights that inform territory, quota, and capacity planning.
column15: The task mixes structured analytics (best served by Traditional AI for KPI computation, validation, and forecasting) with unstructured stakeholder interaction and narrative synthesis (GenAI). Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-system workflows and running repeatable scenario playbooks, reducing cycle time for ad hoc requests. However, territory/quota/capacity planning is high-stakes and assumption-driven; humans must set guardrails, resolve definition disputes, and own recommendations. Assumptions: access to a governed semantic layer/metric store, BI stack, and RAG over internal documentation; agentic actions are constrained by approvals and logging.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving metric definitions and planning assumptions (ramp, coverage ratios, segmentation) that materially change outcomes; (2) interpreting results in organizational context (strategy shifts, rep performance anomalies, one-off deals); (3) compliance and fairness review for quota/territory impacts, especially when HR/headcount data is used; (4) final sign-off and stakeholder alignment when outputs drive compensation or hiring decisions; and (5) edge cases like incomplete data, mid-quarter territory changes, M&A, or policy exceptions where automated playbooks may mislead.
column17: Autonomous workflows can propagate bad data (refresh wrong filters), overrun permissions, or trigger unintended actions (publishing reports broadly). Emergent behavior in multi-step planning could violate guardrails. Mitigate with least-privilege access, approval gates for publish/share, sandbox runs, deterministic playbooks, monitoring, and full audit logs.
column18: May hallucinate drivers or misstate numbers if not strictly grounded in retrieved tables; can drift on metric definitions (e.g., 'capacity' vs 'coverage'); risk of leaking sensitive quota/headcount info in prompts/outputs. Mitigate with RAG + citation requirements, restricted context windows, redaction, and human review for exec-facing insights.
column19: Models can be brittle under regime change (new comp plan, product launch), encode historical bias in quota setting, or fail silently when schemas change. Mitigate with model monitoring, backtesting, explainability, periodic recalibration, and robust data contracts/tests.
column20: Mismatch between semantic layer and GenAI interpretations; inconsistent versions of data across systems; feedback loops where agentic automation repeatedly refreshes/overwrites dashboards; unclear accountability when AI-generated narratives conflict with BI numbers. Mitigate with single source of truth, versioning, lineage, orchestration locks, and explicit 'numbers come from X' citations plus human sign-off for high-impact distributions.
column22: Intake, clarify, and route stakeholder requests across channels. Orchestrate data pulls and refreshes across systems. Automated scenario runs for territory/quota/capacity options|Translate stakeholder questions into analysis specs and metric definitions. Draft narrative insights, executive summaries, and slide/report text. Explain results and answer follow-up questions conversationally|Define business context, approve assumptions, and make final recommendations. Handle sensitive/ambiguous requests and stakeholder alignment. Governance: data access, compliance, and sign-off for high-stakes planning outputs|Data validation, anomaly detection, and KPI computation. Forecasting and capacity modeling using classical ML/statistics. Report templating and automated dashboard generation
column23: Monitors Slack/Email/CRM queues, asks follow-up questions (e.g., time period, segment, definition of 'capacity'), classifies urgency, and routes to the right template/workflow while maintaining an audit trail. Populates standardized report templates and BI dashboards with parameterized queries; ensures consistent formatting and repeatability for common stakeholder asks.

column8: Execute the Sales Operations strategic roadmap by translating priorities into scoped workstreams, milestones, and measurable outcomes aligned to sales leadership goals.
column15: This roadmap-execution task mixes strategic judgment with repeatable analysis and planning mechanics. Generative AI accelerates synthesis of messy inputs and produces high-quality drafts (workstreams, milestones, OKRs, comms) but cannot be trusted to set commitments without verification. Traditional AI is strong for structured sizing, prioritization scoring, feasibility checks, and data-quality governance. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the end-to-end workflow (collecting inputs, updating plans, routing reviews, monitoring change signals) and keeping the roadmap 'living,' but must operate under strict guardrails to avoid unauthorized changes. Humans remain essential for strategy, trade-offs, political alignment, and final approvals-hence the largest share of work remains human-led. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/BI/project data; defined governance; tool integrations (e.g., Salesforce, Snowflake/BI, Jira/Asana); and a stable operating cadence (QBR/MBR).
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) prioritization trade-offs when goals conflict (growth vs margin vs retention), (2) committing timelines/resources and negotiating cross-functional dependencies, (3) approving KPI/OKR definitions that drive incentives and performance management, (4) compliance/legal-sensitive domains (comp plans, territory changes, customer data handling), and (5) resolving ambiguous inputs or contradictory stakeholder requests. Humans must also validate AI-produced summaries against source-of-truth documents and ensure the roadmap aligns with leadership intent and organizational change capacity.
column17: Autonomous re-planning could create churn, update systems incorrectly, or route sensitive info to wrong audiences; emergent behavior and unclear accountability. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for scope/timeline changes, sandbox testing, audit logs, and policy constraints (no external sharing, no comp/territory changes without explicit approval).
column18: May hallucinate initiatives, misstate constraints, or invent metrics; can overfit to persuasive narratives and miss data realities; risk of leaking sensitive sales/comp data in prompts. Mitigations: retrieval from approved sources, citation requirements, redaction, human fact-check, and templates with controlled vocabularies for KPIs.
column19: Brittle rules and models can mis-prioritize if data shifts (new GTM motions) or if CRM hygiene is poor; optimization may ignore qualitative constraints. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, monitoring for drift, explainability, and incorporating human override and qualitative constraints.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs analytics), version-control issues across tools, and feedback loops where agent actions change data used for prioritization. Mitigations: single source of truth for roadmap, strong data governance, reconciliation steps (analytics-backed scorecards), staged rollouts, and clear ownership/RACI for updates.

Maintain living roadmap and trigger re-planning on change signals|Synthesize leadership priorities and translate into draft workstreams and problem statements. Approve scope, resourcing, and timelines; manage change management. Rule-based validation for KPI definitions, data quality checks, and compliance constraints (e.g., SOX-relevant reporting controls), ensuring consistency across workstreams.

column8: Manage end-to-end sales operations workflows, including intake, prioritization, handoffs, and status tracking to ensure timely and high-quality delivery.
column15: The workflow is partly automatable (ticket creation, routing, reminders, status sync) but still depends on human judgment for policy, exceptions, and stakeholder negotiation. Agentic AI is best suited to orchestrate multi-step, cross-tool execution (creating/updating tickets, chasing blockers, escalating per runbooks) and maintaining momentum end-to-end. Traditional AI provides stable, explainable automation for prioritization/routing baselines, data validation, and anomaly alerts-areas where deterministic behavior and auditability matter. Generative AI adds leverage on unstructured intake and communications (summaries, clarifying questions, narratives, documentation) but must be constrained due to hallucination risk. Assumptions: the org uses standard systems (CRM + ticketing/project tool), has defined SLAs/queues, and can integrate via APIs; sensitive actions (reprioritization, escalations to execs) require approval gates.
column16: Humans must (1) set and periodically recalibrate prioritization criteria/SLAs to reflect business strategy and capacity; (2) approve or override AI-proposed reprioritizations, especially when they impact strategic accounts, revenue recognition, or cross-functional commitments; (3) manage escalations involving conflicting stakeholder goals or incomplete information; (4) ensure compliance with data handling (PII, customer confidentiality, SOX/GDPR) and maintain an auditable trail; (5) perform QA sampling of AI-created tickets/status updates and investigate anomalies/edge cases (novel request types, system outages, quarter-end surges).
column17: Autonomous actions can create cascading errors (mass ticket updates, incorrect escalations, spamming stakeholders) if policies are misconfigured or context is wrong; transparency and auditability challenges; potential to over-optimize for SLA at the expense of fairness or quality. Mitigations: approval gates for high-impact actions, rate limits, sandbox/testing, clear runbooks, comprehensive logging, and fallback to manual mode during incidents.
column18: May hallucinate missing fields or mis-summarize requests, leading to wrong routing or missed requirements; can leak sensitive data if prompts/logs are not controlled; tone or wording in stakeholder updates may be inappropriate. Mitigations: retrieval from source-of-truth, structured output schemas, mandatory field checks, redaction, human review for external/exec-facing comms, and prompt/version governance.
column19: Rules/models can become brittle as products, territories, or processes change; biased prioritization if inputs encode historical inequities; false positives/negatives in anomaly detection can cause alert fatigue or missed issues. Mitigations: periodic rule/model reviews, monitoring drift, explainability checks, and stakeholder sign-off on scoring features.
column20: Inconsistent data across CRM/ticketing/project tools can cause status mismatches; unclear system-of-record leads to conflicting updates; permissioning/API failures can break orchestration; compounded errors when GenAI outputs feed routing/agentic actions. Mitigations: define a single source of truth per field, implement idempotent updates, schema validation between components, robust error handling, and end-to-end observability (dashboards, traces, audit logs).
column22: Workflow orchestration across systems (intake -> triage -> assignment -> handoff -> closure). SLA monitoring and proactive exception handling. Generate stakeholder-ready status narratives and QBR/weekly ops readouts|Define/approve prioritization policy, SLAs, and escalation thresholds. Handle ambiguous, high-stakes, or politically sensitive requests and escalations. Data validation and deduplication in CRM/ticketing
column23: Monitors shared intake channels (CRM cases, Slack/Teams, email, forms), creates/updates tickets, routes work to the right queue/owner based on policies and capacity, triggers handoff checklists, and follows up automatically when SLAs are at risk. Keeps an audit trail of actions taken. Pulls status from source-of-truth systems, reconciles discrepancies, updates dashboards/records, and sends targeted updates to requesters and leadership with the right cadence; adapts messaging based on stakeholder preferences and urgency.|Extracts key fields (request type, region, account, urgency, required data, due date) from unstructured text; drafts concise summaries and clarifying questions; suggests categorization tags for downstream routing. Generates and iterates documentation for intake criteria, prioritization rules, handoff templates, and escalation paths; tailors versions for different audiences (Sales, RevOps, Finance) and keeps change logs for review. Turns operational metrics and ticket notes into clear updates (what changed, risks, next steps), reducing manual reporting time while maintaining consistent tone and structure.|Requires business judgment and alignment across Sales/RevOps/Finance; humans set trade-offs (revenue impact vs fairness vs capacity) and approve changes to avoid unintended incentives. Humans audit samples for accuracy, ensure adherence to data privacy/controls (SOX/GDPR), review AI-driven actions, and run retrospectives to refine rules, prompts, and runbooks.|Applies deterministic scoring (e.g., deal stage, ARR, renewal risk, SLA tier, region) and routing rules to assign requests; provides explainable outputs and stable behavior for high-volume, repetitive cases. Uses statistical/ML models to flag unusual spikes in backlog, aging tickets, rework rates, or handoff failures; triggers alerts for investigation and continuous improvement. Runs validation rules (required fields, format checks), detects duplicates, and enforces controlled vocabularies to improve downstream reporting accuracy.
