Prioritization scoring using weighted models. Data quality checks and anomaly detection on inputs
column23: Runs a structured intake process (forms, meeting scheduling, reminders), collects inputs from Sales, Finance, RevOps, Marketing, Product, and CS, tracks completion, and maintains an auditable backlog; adapts follow-ups based on missing/contradictory inputs. Synchronizes artifacts across tools (CRM, project management, docs), detects changes in upstream objectives/constraints, proposes roadmap updates, and routes items for approval; can execute routine updates and status reporting with guardrails.|Summarizes leadership narratives, QBR notes, OKRs, and stakeholder feedback into coherent themes, assumptions, and draft priorities; highlights ambiguities and asks clarifying questions. Generates structured documents (PRDs-lite, initiative briefs, exec summaries) tailored to audiences; produces alternative framings and trade-off language. Proposes metric definitions, leading/lagging indicators, and measurement plans based on objective text and available data sources; drafts success criteria and acceptance tests. Generates what-if scenarios (e.g., headcount changes, quota shifts), identifies likely dependencies/risks from text inputs, and drafts mitigation options for review.|Requires judgment about market context, political realities, risk appetite, and opportunity cost; accountable for decisions impacting revenue and teams. Humans build trust, negotiate commitments, handle conflict, and secure buy-in across functions-areas where authority, empathy, and organizational context are essential. Ensures KPIs align with company policy, compensation implications, privacy/security constraints, and avoids perverse incentives; signs off on final deliverables. Confirms resourcing, system limitations, and timing (e.g., CRM changes, enablement capacity) and decides what to defer or descope.|Uses deterministic queries/BI models to compute current-state funnel metrics, SLA adherence, pipeline coverage, forecast accuracy, and operational throughput; provides explainable dashboards. Applies agreed scoring frameworks (RICE/WSJF/custom) with transparent weights to rank initiatives; supports sensitivity analysis when weights change. Flags missing fields, inconsistent definitions, outliers in KPI trends, and mismatched time windows across sources to prevent roadmap decisions based on bad data.

column8: Own sales forecasting and pipeline governance by defining standards, running cadence reviews, and improving forecast accuracy through data-driven insights.
column15: The task mixes policy-setting and accountability (human-led) with repeatable data processing and governance enforcement (AI-suited). Traditional AI is strong for structured forecasting models, KPI computation, and deterministic governance checks with explainability. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the operating cadence across CRM/BI/communication tools and driving remediation loops, but must be constrained by permissions and escalation rules. Generative AI is best for narrative synthesis (exec commentary, meeting notes) and drafting standards/playbooks, while its outputs must be grounded in approved definitions and verified metrics to avoid hallucinations. Assumptions: CRM/CPQ data is accessible via APIs; governance rules are codified; leadership requires human sign-off on forecast commits; organization tolerates semi-automated nudges/escalations. Constraints: data sparsity in new segments, changing GTM motions, and inconsistent rep data entry reduce automation reliability.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving/owning forecast commits and communicating to Finance/Execs; (2) setting/adjusting stage definitions, commit criteria, and exception policies to align with strategy and incentives; (3) adjudicating ambiguous or politically sensitive deals (channel conflict, multi-year/usage-based pricing, legal/regulatory constraints); (4) approving model changes and thresholds when business conditions shift (new product, pricing, macro shocks); (5) compliance/privacy review for any use of call notes/emails and for automated outreach/escalations. Humans should review: top variance drivers, any automated escalations to leadership, and any forecast changes beyond predefined tolerance bands.
column17: Over-automation could spam sellers, create incorrect CRM updates, or escalate prematurely; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals (optimize accuracy by pressuring stage changes); permission misuse across systems. Mitigations: least-privilege access, human-in-the-loop approvals for write actions, rate limits, audit logs, explicit policies/guardrails, and sandbox testing with rollback.
column18: Hallucinated metrics or incorrect causal claims in forecast narratives; leakage of sensitive deal/customer info if prompts/logs are mishandled; inconsistent tone or overconfident language that misleads executives. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from approved dashboards, citation of source metrics, redaction/DLP, and mandatory uncertainty statements for low-evidence hypotheses.
column19: Model drift and bias (segments with sparse history), brittle rules that incentivize gaming (stage inflation), and false positives in anomaly detection causing noise. Mitigations: drift monitoring, periodic recalibration, segment-level confidence intervals, governance of rule changes, and feedback loops from sales managers.
column20: Inconsistent definitions between BI, CRM, and GenAI narratives; cascading errors where bad data feeds models and then agentic workflows act on it; unclear ownership when AI recommendations conflict with manager judgment. Mitigations: single source of truth for metrics/definitions, data contracts, end-to-end lineage, escalation paths, and reconciliation reports comparing AI vs. human forecast with reasons.
column22: Orchestrate forecasting cadence and pipeline governance workflows. Autonomously monitor pipeline hygiene and drive remediation. Scenario planning and what-if execution across systems|Draft and refine forecasting standards, playbooks, and enablement content. Summarize cadence reviews and produce executive-ready narratives. Explain variance drivers and surface hypotheses from mixed signals|Set governance policy, definitions, and incentives alignment. Lead high-stakes forecast calls and make final commit decisions. Resolve edge cases, exceptions, and stakeholder conflicts|Build and maintain statistical/ML forecast models and accuracy tracking. Rule-based pipeline governance checks and anomaly detection. Data quality validation, deduplication, and metric computation
column23: Runs end-to-end weekly/monthly operating rhythm: pulls latest CRM/CPQ data, triggers reminders to reps/managers, assembles agenda packs, opens/updates tickets for missing fields, and routes exceptions for approval; adapts sequencing based on close dates, deal stage changes, and stakeholder availability. Continuously checks for stale opportunities, missing next steps, inconsistent stage/close dates, and policy violations; creates tasks in CRM, pings owners, escalates to managers when SLAs breach, and logs actions for auditability. Runs multi-step simulations (e.g., slip rates, win-rate shifts, capacity constraints) by calling analytics services, updating forecast scenarios, and publishing results to dashboards/Slack/Email with traceable assumptions.|Generates first drafts of definitions (stages, commit criteria, MEDDICC fields), SOPs, and training snippets tailored to Sales Ops context; rewrites for clarity and consistency; produces FAQs and change-logs. Turns raw pipeline movements, variance drivers, and rep/region notes into concise summaries, risk callouts, and action items; creates consistent commentary for forecast calls and QBR decks. Interprets structured metrics plus unstructured notes/emails to propose plausible drivers (pricing delays, legal cycle time, competitor) and suggests follow-up questions; flags uncertainty when evidence is weak.|Requires cross-functional judgment to balance rigor vs. seller burden, align with finance/rev rec, and ensure standards match GTM strategy and compensation behaviors. Humans must arbitrate ambiguous deals, incorporate qualitative intel, and own accountability to leadership/finance; final sign-off is a management responsibility. Handles disputes (stage criteria disagreements, territory/account ownership, one-off deal structures), approves overrides, and manages change adoption and communication.|Uses time-series/ML (e.g., gradient boosting, survival models) on historical opportunity data to predict close probability and expected value; computes forecast accuracy metrics (MAPE, bias) by segment and time horizon. Applies deterministic rules (stage aging thresholds, required fields, close-date change limits) and classical anomaly detection to flag outliers (sudden pipeline spikes, improbable discounts) with explainable triggers. Runs ETL validations (schema checks, referential integrity), dedupes accounts/opps, standardizes picklists, and computes core KPIs (coverage, conversion, slippage) feeding dashboards and models.

column8: Design, document, and optimize end-to-end sales processes including lead-to-opportunity, opportunity-to-close, and renewals to improve efficiency and scalability.
column15: End-to-end sales process design is socio-technical: AI can accelerate discovery, analysis, documentation, and enforcement, but humans must set policy and align stakeholders. Traditional AI is strong for deterministic enforcement (routing/validation) and explainable funnel analytics, so it carries a large share. Generative AI speeds documentation, requirements synthesis, and hypothesis generation but needs verification. Agentic AI is valuable for orchestrating multi-system data pulls, continuous monitoring, and executing approved rollouts, yet autonomy must be bounded by governance. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/CPQ/billing data, defined KPIs, and change-control practices; without these, human share increases and agentic automation decreases.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) setting stage definitions, qualification gates, and approval policies that reflect strategy and risk tolerance; (2) resolving cross-functional conflicts (e.g., Sales speed vs. Finance controls); (3) validating AI-derived insights against ground truth and edge cases (enterprise, channel, multi-year renewals); (4) approving any system changes that affect revenue recognition, pricing/discounting, or customer commitments; (5) ensuring privacy/security when using customer data in GenAI and when agentic systems take actions. Humans should also arbitrate when metrics improve but rep/customer experience degrades, and handle exceptions where rigid rules would block legitimate deals.
column17: Autonomous actions can create unintended workflow changes (e.g., misconfigured routing, mass field edits), trigger alert fatigue, or optimize local metrics at the expense of compliance. Mitigate with least-privilege access, sandbox-first deployments, approval gates, audit logs, rate limits, and clear rollback plans.
column18: May hallucinate process steps, misstate system behavior, or produce plausible-but-wrong SOPs; can leak sensitive customer/deal data if prompts/logs are mishandled; may encode biased assumptions about segments or territories. Mitigate with retrieval from approved sources, redaction, human review, and version-controlled documentation.
column19: Rules and models can become brittle as GTM strategy changes; deterministic routing may mishandle edge cases (partners, overlays, global accounts); anomaly thresholds can generate false positives/negatives. Mitigate with periodic rule reviews, monitoring drift, exception handling paths, and explainable dashboards.
column20: Data inconsistencies across CRM/CPQ/billing can cause conflicting recommendations; orchestration failures can create partial deployments; unclear ownership between AI outputs and human decisions can weaken accountability. Mitigate with a single source of truth for key objects, end-to-end testing, RACI, change-management governance, and unified observability for workflows and metrics.
column22: Orchestrate cross-system process discovery and data collection. Run continuous process monitoring and alerting loops. Governance, compliance, and final approval of process changes|Quantitative funnel and cycle-time analytics. Lead/opportunity routing and validation rules automation. Anomaly detection on process metrics

Autonomously watches leading indicators (cycle time, stage conversion, renewal risk signals, approval bottlenecks) and triggers workflows (create Jira tickets, notify owners, propose experiments) when thresholds are breached; adapts thresholds by segment while keeping audit trails. Implements approved changes (field updates, validation rules, routing logic, playbook tasks) via change-management runbooks, coordinates sandbox testing, UAT reminders, and phased deployment; rolls back on detected regressions.|Generates first-pass SOPs, RACI matrices, stage definitions, handoff checklists, and renewal playbooks from inputs (current policies, stakeholder notes, CRM metadata); tailors versions for reps, managers, and RevOps. Summarizes interviews, call transcripts, and feedback tickets into structured requirements, pain points, and acceptance criteria; highlights conflicting requirements and open questions. Proposes bottleneck fixes (e.g., qualification gates, automated routing, approval simplification), A/B test plans, and KPI definitions; generates example user stories for backlog grooming.|Requires business judgment to set qualification standards, approval authorities, discount/contract policies, and trade-offs between speed, risk, and customer experience; accountable for outcomes. Negotiates across Sales, Marketing, CS, Finance, Legal, and IT; resolves conflicts, secures buy-in, and manages adoption/training-areas where authority, empathy, and context are essential. Humans must review for regulatory/contractual compliance, data privacy, and unintended incentives; approve deployments and exceptions, especially for enterprise deals and renewals.|Uses deterministic BI queries and classical ML/statistics to compute conversion rates, stage aging, cohort comparisons, seasonality, and variance; produces explainable dashboards for lead->opp, opp->close, and renewals. Applies rule engines (territory, segment, round-robin, SLA timers) and deterministic checks (required fields, duplicate detection, compliance flags) to enforce the designed process consistently at scale. Flags outliers such as sudden stage-skips, unusually fast closes, renewal date shifts, or discount spikes using thresholds or lightweight ML; routes findings for review.

column8: Manage CRM and sales technology administration by defining requirements, coordinating enhancements, and ensuring data quality, user adoption, and system reliability.
column15: The task spans governance-heavy decisions (requirements prioritization, compliance, go/no-go) and operational execution (monitoring, data quality, enablement). Humans remain essential for accountability, stakeholder trade-offs, and high-risk approvals (35%). Agentic AI can reliably automate multi-step workflows and monitoring/incident response within guardrails (25%), reducing coordination overhead. Generative AI accelerates documentation, requirements drafting, and enablement content but must be reviewed due to hallucination/privacy risks (20%). Traditional AI is well-suited for deterministic data quality checks, dedupe, and adoption analytics with high precision and auditability (20%). Assumptions: CRM is a mainstream platform (e.g., Salesforce/Dynamics/HubSpot) with APIs, logging, and a ticketing system; organization permits automation with role-based access and change controls.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) prioritization and roadmap decisions where impact/effort is uncertain and political; (2) approvals for permission model changes, data retention, and any automation that could alter customer records at scale; (3) final UAT and go/no-go for releases impacting forecasting, compensation, or regulated data; (4) exception handling when AI flags anomalies that may reflect real business shifts (e.g., new pricing, territory changes) rather than errors; (5) ensuring auditability and adherence to internal controls (SOX-like change management) and vendor contract constraints.
column17: Autonomous actions could misconfigure CRM, trigger unintended workflows, or create alert fatigue; emergent behavior in complex workflows may bypass controls. Mitigations: least-privilege service accounts, approval gates for write actions, runbooks with rollback, sandbox-first execution, rate limits, and comprehensive logging.
column18: May hallucinate requirements or misstate system behavior; can leak sensitive customer/employee data if prompts include PII; may produce inconsistent definitions that harm governance. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved docs, redaction/DLP, mandatory human review, and template-based outputs with citations.
column19: Rules can become brittle as sales processes evolve; ML anomaly models can drift and over-flag during seasonality or GTM changes; dedupe can create false merges. Mitigations: periodic rule/model review, drift monitoring, human-in-the-loop for merges, and conservative thresholds.
column20: Conflicting signals between systems (ticketing, CRM, data warehouse) can cause incorrect automation; identity/permission mismatches can break workflows; poor observability makes root-cause unclear. Mitigations: canonical data model, robust API error handling, end-to-end monitoring, change management, and clear ownership/RACI across RevOps, IT, and Security.

Continuously checks integrations, API limits, job failures, sync latency, and key user journeys; opens incidents, gathers logs, suggests likely root causes, and can trigger predefined remediation actions (restart jobs, re-auth integrations) within guardrails, escalating when thresholds are exceeded.|Turns stakeholder notes into structured requirements (problem statement, scope, acceptance criteria), identifies ambiguities, proposes questions, and generates user stories for admins/devs; maintains consistent templates for Grade 9/10 governance. Summarizes survey responses, call transcripts, and support tickets to extract themes (friction points, missing fields, confusing stages) and proposes hypotheses and experiments to improve adoption.|Requires business judgment to balance revenue impact, change fatigue, and cross-functional constraints; humans negotiate trade-offs, approve roadmap, and ensure alignment with Sales leadership and RevOps policies. Humans validate that workflows match real selling motions and edge cases; they decide go/no-go, coordinate communications, and handle exceptions during rollout.|Applies deterministic rules (required fields, picklist constraints), fuzzy matching for duplicates, and statistical/ML anomaly detection on pipeline, activity, and lead/account data to flag outliers and likely errors for remediation. Computes metrics (login frequency, field completion, stage progression, feature usage) and segments by team/role; uses time-series baselines to detect adoption drops after releases.

column8: Develop and maintain dashboards and KPI reporting for bookings, pipeline health, activity, and conversion metrics, and present insights to leadership.
column15: Dashboards/KPI reporting combines (a) deterministic data engineering and metric computation (best fit: Traditional AI/automation), (b) workflow orchestration and monitoring across systems (best fit: Agentic AI), and (c) narrative synthesis and Q&A support (best fit: Generative AI). However, KPI definitions, governance, and leadership communication are high-stakes and context-dependent, so humans retain ownership of metric semantics, approvals, and final recommendations. Assumptions: access to CRM (e.g., Salesforce), BI (Tableau/Power BI/Looker), and a warehouse; mature data model exists or is being built; GenAI is constrained to approved data extracts and definitions to reduce hallucinations.
column16: Humans must (1) approve KPI definitions/changes and ensure alignment with Finance and GTM strategy; (2) validate any AI-generated narratives before leadership consumption, especially when metrics drive compensation or headcount decisions; (3) adjudicate anomalies where data issues vs real business shifts are ambiguous; (4) enforce privacy/role-based access and ethical use of rep-level activity data; (5) make final calls on actions (pipeline cleanup, enablement, territory changes) and communicate with appropriate nuance. Edge cases: mid-quarter process changes, CRM field changes, M&A/product line splits, and one-off large deals that distort trends require human contextualization.
column17: Autonomous workflows can propagate bad data (publishing incorrect dashboards), over-retry and overload systems, or take unintended actions (changing filters/permissions) if misconfigured. Mitigations: least-privilege credentials, approval gates for publish/permission changes, audit logs, sandbox testing, circuit breakers, and clear runbooks with human escalation.
column18: May hallucinate drivers or misstate numbers if not grounded in retrieved metric tables; can introduce biased framing about rep performance; risk of leaking sensitive pipeline data via prompts. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from governed datasets, numeric grounding/verification, redaction, strict access controls, and mandatory human review for exec outputs.
column19: Rule-based checks can be brittle when CRM schemas or sales processes change; ML scoring can drift with seasonality or comp-plan changes and may be misinterpreted as deterministic truth. Mitigations: schema/version monitoring, unit tests for KPI logic, periodic recalibration, explainability, and governance on model use in performance contexts.
column20: Metric definition mismatches between ETL, BI, and GenAI narratives can create conflicting 'truth'; identity/access mismatches can expose restricted data; orchestration failures can cause partial refreshes leading to inconsistent dashboards. Mitigations: centralized semantic layer/metric store, end-to-end lineage, consistent RBAC across tools, data contracts, and automated reconciliation checks before publishing.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end KPI reporting workflow (refresh, validation, publish, notify). Monitor KPI anomalies and open investigation tickets|Draft executive-ready narrative insights and commentary for weekly/monthly business reviews. Self-serve analytics assistant for ad-hoc questions|Define KPI taxonomy, business rules, and governance (single source of truth). Interpret insights, decide actions, and present recommendations to leadership. Handle sensitive exceptions and compliance (access control, privacy, rep performance)|Data preparation and metric computation (ETL, joins, aggregations). Data quality checks and anomaly detection on structured metrics. Forecast/pipeline health scoring components (optional)
column23: Runs scheduled multi-step workflows across CRM/BI/warehouse: triggers data refreshes, executes validation checks, updates dashboard datasets, publishes to BI, and notifies stakeholders; adapts to failures (retry, route to human) using runbooks and context. Continuously watches KPI deltas vs baselines, detects unusual movements, gathers supporting slices (region/segment/rep), and creates tickets with evidence and suggested next steps for Sales Ops review.|Summarizes KPI movements, highlights drivers, and produces leadership-friendly narratives, talking points, and slide notes grounded in provided metric tables and approved definitions. Translates leadership questions into metric definitions/filters, proposes analyses, and generates explanations/visual descriptions; can generate SQL/BI calculated-field drafts for analyst review.|Requires cross-functional judgment to align Sales/Finance definitions (bookings vs billings, pipeline inclusion, stage mapping), approve changes, and manage stakeholder trade-offs. Leadership-facing interpretation involves context (product launches, comp changes, territory shifts) and accountability; humans must validate narratives and decide interventions. Human oversight needed for ethical use of rep-level metrics, privacy constraints, and to prevent misuse or misinterpretation of performance data.|Uses deterministic pipelines and/or classical ML where appropriate to compute bookings, pipeline coverage, stage conversion, activity rates; enforces consistent KPI definitions and time windows. Applies rule-based thresholds (missing values, duplicates, out-of-range) and statistical methods (z-score, seasonal decomposition) to flag data issues and metric outliers with explainable signals. Uses interpretable models (logistic regression/GBM with constraints) to estimate stage conversion likelihood or risk flags, feeding dashboard indicators; retrained on historical outcomes with governance.

column8: Lead territory planning, quota setting support, and capacity modeling in partnership with Sales leadership and Finance to align coverage with growth targets.
column15: This Grade 9/10 Sales Ops task is high-stakes and cross-functional: AI can accelerate data work, modeling, and scenario iteration, but humans must own strategic choices and commitments. Traditional AI is well-suited for structured data prep, forecasting inputs, and optimization for territory/quota allocation (largest automatable share). Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-system workflows and running iterative scenario loops, reducing cycle time and ensuring repeatability. Generative AI is best for translating qualitative constraints into structured assumptions and producing executive narratives/Q&A, but must be grounded to avoid hallucinations. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/finance/headcount data; agreed definitions (ARR, pipeline, segments); governance for approvals and auditability.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving assumptions (market potential, segment focus, strategic accounts), (2) final quota/territory decisions that affect compensation and legal/HR exposure, (3) resolving conflicts between Sales and Finance targets, (4) validating fairness and unintended bias (e.g., systematically disadvantaging certain regions/rep cohorts), and (5) handling edge cases like mid-year reorgs, M&A, product launches, channel shifts, or macro shocks. Humans must also sign off on any automated changes pushed to CRM/comp systems and ensure compliance with internal controls (SOX-like change management) and privacy policies.
column17: Over-automation could push incorrect territory/quota updates into downstream systems; emergent behavior in multi-step planning loops may optimize the wrong objective (e.g., attainment optics vs growth). Mitigate with human-in-the-loop approvals, sandbox runs, policy constraints, rate limits, and full audit logs/rollback.
column18: Hallucinated explanations of quota methodology or drivers; leakage of sensitive comp/headcount data if prompts are not controlled; biased or overly persuasive narratives that mask uncertainty. Mitigate with retrieval-grounded generation, strict data access controls, red-teaming, and mandatory citation of source tables/versions.
column19: Models can encode historical bias (e.g., under-investing in emerging regions), degrade with concept drift, or be brittle to data quality issues; optimization may produce 'balanced' territories that are operationally unrealistic. Mitigate with monitoring, periodic recalibration, fairness checks, constraint reviews, and backtesting vs prior years.
column20: Inconsistent definitions/versions across CRM/finance/HR data can cause conflicting outputs; GenAI may narrate from stale scenarios; agentic orchestration can amplify upstream errors quickly. Mitigate with a single source of truth, data contracts, versioned scenario IDs, automated validation gates, and governance for model + narrative alignment.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end planning workflow across systems (CRM, HRIS, finance planning). Run iterative scenario planning loops and recommend candidate plans|Synthesize qualitative inputs and constraints into planning assumptions. Draft executive-ready narratives and artifacts. Explain model outputs and answer ad-hoc questions|Set strategic priorities and approve final territory/quota/capacity decisions. Resolve data ambiguities and edge cases with business context. Stakeholder alignment and change management|Data preparation, validation, and anomaly detection on core planning datasets. Predictive modeling for capacity and productivity inputs. Optimization/heuristics for territory design and quota allocation
column23: Coordinates data pulls, triggers model runs, manages versioning, routes outputs for review, and enforces deadlines/approvals; adapts the workflow when inputs change (e.g., headcount updates) while keeping an audit trail. Autonomously generates and evaluates multiple territory/quota/capacity scenarios under constraints (coverage ratios, ramp times, budget), flags trade-offs, and proposes a short list for human decision.|Turns leadership/Finance notes, product priorities, and market narratives into structured assumptions (e.g., focus segments, whitespace hypotheses) and drafts an assumptions log for sign-off. Creates territory plan summaries, quota methodology explanations, and slide/FAQ drafts tailored to Sales leadership and Finance; summarizes deltas vs prior plan and key risks. Provides natural-language explanations of drivers (pipeline, ARR potential, capacity), generates 'why' analyses, and produces consistent responses for stakeholder Q&A-grounded on approved data extracts.|Requires judgment on go-to-market strategy, risk appetite, and organizational politics; accountable for final commitments and trade-offs that models cannot own. Handles exceptions like strategic accounts, partner-led motions, rep performance anomalies, and one-off market events; validates whether model assumptions reflect reality. Negotiates with Sales leadership/Finance on targets, communicates changes to the field, and manages adoption-work that depends on trust, persuasion, and accountability.|Uses deterministic rules/SQL and classical ML to clean/merge CRM opportunity history, account firmographics, rep productivity, and headcount; flags outliers (e.g., abnormal win rates) and missing coverage. Applies time-series/regression models to forecast pipeline creation, conversion, ramp curves, and attainment distributions by segment/role to feed capacity models. Uses constrained optimization (e.g., linear/integer programming) and heuristics to balance territories by potential, travel/geo constraints, account counts, and to allocate quotas consistent with top-down targets.

column8: Drive sales enablement operations by coordinating playbooks, process training, and change management to ensure consistent execution across the sales team.
column15: This Sales Ops enablement-operations task mixes content creation, workflow coordination, measurement, and organizational change. Generative AI is strong for drafting/updating playbooks, training materials, FAQs, and synthesizing rep feedback, but needs human validation for accuracy and policy. Agentic AI can run the operational cadence-project plans, task routing, reminders, and escalations-yet should operate within guardrails and approval gates to avoid unintended rollouts. Traditional AI/analytics is mature for adoption measurement (dashboards, rule checks) and for lightweight predictive targeting of enablement interventions. Humans remain essential for strategy, prioritization, stakeholder alignment, and final approvals. Assumptions: the org uses standard systems (CRM like Salesforce, LMS, ticketing/project tools, BI) with API access; data quality is sufficient for adoption metrics; governance exists for content and change approvals.
column16: Human oversight is required to (1) set enablement priorities and decide which behaviors/processes to standardize, (2) approve any customer-facing or policy/regulatory-impacting guidance (pricing, claims, privacy, contracting), (3) arbitrate trade-offs when analytics suggest conflicting actions (e.g., adoption vs. selling time), (4) manage stakeholder resistance and communicate rationale credibly, and (5) review agentic actions before broad rollout (pilot selection, gating criteria, escalation thresholds). Humans must also intervene when data is incomplete, when changes affect compensation/territories, or when regional/legal constraints differ. Mandatory approvals and audit trails should be enforced for high-risk changes (pricing, legal terms, data handling).
column17: Over-automation may push changes without adequate readiness, spam reps with nudges, or mis-route approvals; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals (optimize completion at expense of selling time); limited transparency in why it escalated/changed plans. Mitigations: constrained action space, approval gates for broad communications, rate limits, simulation/pilot environments, clear rollback plans, logging and explainability, and human-in-the-loop for high-impact actions.
column18: Hallucinated or outdated process steps can create inconsistent execution; tone/positioning drift from approved messaging; leakage of sensitive deal/customer data if prompts include PII; IP/copyright risks if training content borrows from external sources. Mitigations: retrieval from approved sources (RAG), strict citation/links to source-of-truth, redaction/PII controls, human SME review, versioning and content governance.
column19: Brittle rules can mis-measure adoption (e.g., reps comply outside tracked fields); models can encode bias (flagging certain regions/tenures unfairly) and degrade with process changes; false positives create alert fatigue. Mitigations: periodic rule/model review, data quality checks, drift monitoring, threshold tuning, and pairing metrics with qualitative validation.
column20: Conflicting sources of truth (playbook vs. CRM vs. LMS) can cause inconsistent guidance; feedback loops where agentic nudges change behavior and invalidate model baselines; permissioning gaps across systems; audit/compliance issues if content and workflow actions aren't traceable. Mitigations: master data governance, unified taxonomy, role-based access control, end-to-end audit logs, staged rollouts, and clear ownership for each system and artifact.
column22: Orchestrate enablement project plan and cross-functional workflows. Automate change rollout communications and task routing|Draft and update sales playbooks and SOPs from source inputs. Create training content and reinforcement assets. Summarize feedback and field insights into actionable change requests|Define enablement strategy, priorities, and success criteria. Validate playbook/training accuracy and approve policy-sensitive content. Lead stakeholder management and change leadership. Handle exceptions and edge cases in the field|Measure adoption and compliance via dashboards and rule-based checks. Segment audiences and recommend training assignments using classical ML
column23: Creates and maintains a multi-step rollout plan (milestones, dependencies, owners) across Sales Ops, Enablement, RevOps, Product Marketing; triggers tasks in tools (Jira/Asana), monitors status, escalates blockers, and adapts timelines based on signals (missed deadlines, low adoption). Based on change type (pricing, CRM fields, qualification), routes approvals, schedules announcements, assigns training modules, and opens/updates tickets; can personalize routing by role/region and enforce completion gates before access to new processes.|Generates first-draft playbooks, talk tracks, objection handling, CRM step-by-step SOPs from product notes, win/loss insights, and policy docs; maintains consistent structure and version notes; flags missing inputs/questions for SMEs. Produces slide outlines, facilitator guides, microlearning scripts, quizzes, role-play scenarios, and call coaching rubrics tailored to personas (SDR/AE/CSM) and segments; can localize language and tone. Synthesizes qualitative inputs (Slack, surveys, call notes) into themes, pain points, and proposed fixes; drafts release notes and FAQs; highlights sentiment and recurring confusion areas.|Requires business judgment to align enablement with revenue strategy, capacity, and stakeholder trade-offs; sets what 'good' looks like and resolves conflicts between teams. column8: Coach and develop Sales Operations team members by setting goals, reviewing work quality, and establishing best practices for scalable operational execution.
column15: This task is fundamentally people-centric (coaching, motivation, judgment) so humans retain primary responsibility. Generative AI accelerates creation of goals, feedback narratives, documentation, and training materials, but must be grounded in accurate performance data and company context. Traditional AI is well-suited to consistent, explainable measurement of work quality and process compliance using structured operational metrics, providing objective inputs to coaching. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the coaching cadence and monitoring systems to trigger timely interventions, but autonomy should be bounded (no unilateral performance actions). Assumptions: access to CRM/BI/HRIS data with appropriate permissions; defined QA rubrics; willingness of team to use standardized coaching artifacts. Uncertainties: quality/availability of labeled performance data; variability of roles within Sales Ops; organizational policies on AI use in HR-adjacent processes.
column16: Humans must (1) make final decisions on goals, promotions, performance improvement plans, and any disciplinary actions; (2) interpret context behind metrics (e.g., territory changes, system outages, shifting priorities) to avoid unfair coaching; (3) ensure psychological safety and tailor delivery of feedback; (4) approve best-practice changes that affect cross-functional stakeholders and compliance (data privacy, SOX-like controls, customer data handling); (5) handle edge cases such as conflicting stakeholder feedback, suspected bias in metrics, or when AI recommendations contradict lived experience. Human oversight is also required to review GenAI outputs for accuracy/tone and to constrain agentic workflows (permissions, escalation paths).
column17: Could over-trigger interventions, create notification fatigue, or take actions beyond intent if permissions are broad; opaque decision logic may reduce trust; automation may be perceived as surveillance. Mitigations: least-privilege access, explicit action boundaries (draft/notify vs execute), audit logs, rate limits, and human-in-the-loop approvals for sensitive steps.
column18: May hallucinate performance facts, misattribute causes, or produce overly generic/biased feedback; risk of leaking sensitive employee data if prompts/logs are mishandled; tone mismatch can harm trust. Mitigations: retrieval from approved sources, redaction, prompt templates, human review, and policy controls for HR-adjacent content.
column19: Rules/thresholds can become outdated, incentivize gaming, or miss qualitative aspects of work; ML models may encode historical bias (e.g., stakeholder CSAT differences). Mitigations: periodic rubric review, monitoring for disparate impact, combining quantitative scores with qualitative review, and clear documentation of metrics.
column20: Data pipeline mismatches (identity resolution across HRIS/CRM), inconsistent definitions of 'quality', and feedback loops where AI-generated docs influence metrics; security/privacy risks when connecting systems; conflicting recommendations between models. Mitigations: single source of truth for metrics, governance for definitions, end-to-end access controls, model/output versioning, and staged rollout with A/B evaluation.
column22: Orchestrate recurring coaching workflows and reminders. Monitor operational execution signals and trigger coaching interventions|Draft role-specific goals/OKRs and development plans. Provide feedback summaries and coaching conversation guides. Codify and communicate best practices into scalable documentation. Approve and adapt best practices for real-world constraints|Score work quality using deterministic QA rubrics and metrics. Benchmark performance and identify skill gaps from historical data. Standardize best-practice compliance checks
column23: Plans and runs a cadence (1:1s, quarterly goal reviews, enablement sessions) by syncing calendars, prompting managers/ICs for inputs, tracking completion, and escalating overdue items; adapts schedules based on workload and business cycles. Continuously watches connected systems (CRM, ticketing, BI dashboards) for leading indicators (SLA misses, data hygiene drift, forecast process non-compliance) and opens coaching tasks/playbooks when thresholds are breached.|Generates SMART goals, competency matrices, and individualized development plans using role expectations (Sales Ops analyst/admin), current performance signals, and business priorities; proposes measurable outcomes and milestones. Summarizes work artifacts (dashboards, process docs, post-mortems) and produces structured feedback (what/so what/now what), 1:1 agendas, and difficult-conversation scripts aligned to company values and tone. Turns tribal knowledge and scattered notes into SOPs, checklists, QA rubrics, onboarding guides, and internal FAQs; tailors versions for different audiences (new hire vs senior analyst). Creates microlearning modules, quizzes, scenario-based exercises (e.g., handling pipeline hygiene exceptions), and role-play prompts for coaching practice.|Requires judgment to balance short-term delivery vs long-term capability building, interpret ambiguous context, and align goals with leadership expectations and organizational values. Effective coaching depends on empathy, trust, and nuanced interpersonal dynamics; humans must handle sensitive feedback, conflict, compensation/performance actions, and psychological safety. Humans validate that proposed SOPs fit cross-functional realities, legal/compliance constraints, and change-management readiness; they arbitrate trade-offs and exceptions.|Applies rule-based checks and classical ML on structured data (CRM field completeness, report refresh success, SLA adherence, error rates) to produce consistent quality scores and trend lines. Uses statistical analysis/clustering to compare individuals vs team baselines (cycle times, rework rates, stakeholder CSAT) and flags likely gap areas (documentation, stakeholder management, data accuracy). Implements validation rules, anomaly detection, and process conformance monitoring (e.g., required approval steps, naming conventions) to ensure scalable execution.

column8: Administer and configure the CRM platform for Sales Operations, including objects, fields, page layouts, validation rules, and automation to support defined sales processes and KPI tracking.
column15: CRM administration for Sales Ops mixes high-context process design and governance (human-led) with repeatable configuration, documentation, and testing where AI can accelerate work. GenAI is strongest for converting requirements into drafts (specs, formulas, docs) but must be validated due to hallucination and platform-specific nuances. Agentic AI can execute multi-step configuration/testing in sandbox and orchestrate deployments under strict guardrails, but production changes require approvals and careful oversight. Traditional AI adds value through deterministic governance checks and data/KPI anomaly monitoring. Assumes a modern CRM (e.g., Salesforce/Dynamics) with API access, sandbox environments, and existing change-control practices.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) defining/approving KPI semantics and sales process intent, (2) assessing impacts on integrations, security model, and regulatory/privacy requirements, (3) approving automation that can block deals or alter data, (4) final production deployment decisions and rollback planning, and (5) resolving edge cases like conflicting stakeholder requirements, ambiguous data definitions, or exceptions for strategic accounts.
column17: Autonomous actions can misconfigure permissions/automation, causing revenue-impacting outages or data loss; emergent behavior in multi-step plans may bypass intended governance. Mitigate with sandbox-only execution, least privilege, approval checkpoints, immutable audit logs, and automated rollback.
column18: May hallucinate platform capabilities or produce incorrect formulas/flow logic; can leak sensitive CRM data if prompts/logs are mishandled; may generate inconsistent field definitions leading to reporting errors. Static analyzers and rule engines can scan metadata for policy violations (e.g., unused fields, overly permissive profiles, conflicting validation rules) and generate compliance reports.

column8: Maintain CRM data quality by defining standards, monitoring duplicates and incomplete records, coordinating cleanup activities, and enforcing governance to keep reporting and forecasting reliable.
column15: CRM data quality is partly automatable (detection, standardization, monitoring) but governance decisions and ambiguous cases remain human-led. Traditional AI is best for deterministic checks, entity resolution, and anomaly detection on structured CRM data. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating end-to-end cleanup workflows across systems (CRM, enrichment tools, ITSM) with escalation and verification loops. Generative AI accelerates documentation, communication, and design ideation but should not be the system of record for factual decisions. Assumptions: CRM has API access, audit logging, role-based permissions, and a ticketing/workflow system; data-quality KPIs exist (duplicate rate, completeness score) and there is an approval process for merges/deletes.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving merges/deletions and schema/validation changes that can affect revenue attribution, forecasting, and legal entity structure; (2) interpreting edge cases (rebrands, subsidiaries, shared domains, channel partners) where automated matching can be wrong; (3) compliance decisions (PII minimization, retention, consent, regional rules) and audit readiness; (4) resolving conflicts between stakeholders (Sales vs Marketing definitions) and enforcing adoption through training and process changes; (5) incident response when automation flags widespread anomalies or when cleanup could disrupt integrations/downstream BI.
column17: Over-automation could merge/delete records incorrectly, trigger cascading integration failures, or spam users with tickets; autonomy can obscure why actions were taken. Mitigations: least-privilege service accounts, mandatory approval gates for destructive actions, simulation/dry-run mode, rate limits, comprehensive audit trails, and monitored rollback procedures.
column18: May hallucinate policy details or propose invalid validation rules; can leak sensitive CRM/PII if prompts/logs are not controlled; may produce persuasive but incorrect explanations that mislead stakeholders. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved docs, redaction, prompt logging, human review for externally shared content, and grounding outputs in metrics/screenshots.
column19: Brittle rules can create false positives/negatives; ML matching can encode bias (e.g., non-Western names/addresses) and degrade with data drift; explainability may be insufficient for disputed merges. Mitigations: periodic rule review, threshold tuning, bias testing across regions, human-in-the-loop sampling, and continuous evaluation against labeled duplicate sets.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI-suggested rules vs Traditional AI constraints), feedback loops (agent closes tickets without true fixes), and inconsistent identifiers across CRM/BI/enrichment tools can corrupt reporting. Mitigations: single source of truth for standards, versioned rule/config management, end-to-end testing in sandbox, data lineage tracking, and clear ownership/RACI for each automation step.

Coordinate cross-object cleanup campaigns with guardrails|Draft and maintain data standards, definitions, and governance documentation. Explain data-quality issues and recommend remediation to stakeholders. Assist with rule/validation design and edge-case analysis|Set governance policy, risk appetite, and approve high-impact changes (merges, deletions, schema/validation updates). Resolve ambiguous duplicate clusters and ownership disputes. Stakeholder alignment, training, and enforcement|Detect duplicates using deterministic + ML matching. Monitor completeness, conformity, and anomalies via rules and statistical checks. Automate enrichment/standardization of structured fields

Given a campaign goal (e.g., reduce duplicate Accounts by 30%), decomposes into actions (identify clusters, propose merges, request approvals, execute merges via API, log changes), while enforcing approval gates and rollback plans.|Generates first drafts of field definitions, naming conventions, picklist guidance, required-field rationale, and SOPs; summarizes stakeholder input and change history into clear, auditable documentation. Turns dashboards/anomaly outputs into plain-language narratives (what changed, likely causes, impacted reports), and drafts communications/training snippets for sales ops and reps. Proposes validation rules, dedupe matching criteria, and exception handling based on described business processes; helps enumerate edge cases (subsidiaries, rebrands, shared domains) for human review.|Requires business judgment and accountability for revenue-impacting records, compliance (privacy/retention), and trade-offs between data friction and quality; humans provide final approvals and sign-off. Humans negotiate standards across Sales/Marketing/RevOps, run enablement, and enforce adherence through incentives/process changes-areas where authority and change management are essential.|Uses fuzzy matching, phonetic algorithms, and supervised/unsupervised entity resolution on structured fields (name, domain, address, phone) to score duplicate candidates and form clusters with explainable features. Runs rule-based validations (required fields, format checks, picklist conformity) and statistical drift/anomaly detection on key fields (stage, close date, amount) to flag sudden changes affecting forecasting. Applies deterministic transformations (address normalization, country/state codes, casing, regex for phone/email) and controlled reference-data lookups to standardize values before reporting.

column8: Manage user access and security by provisioning accounts, assigning roles and permissions, maintaining profiles, and performing periodic access reviews aligned to internal controls.
column15: This CRM access-management task mixes repeatable operational steps (provisioning, deprovisioning, reconciliation) with governance and high-stakes approvals. Agentic AI is best suited to orchestrate multi-step workflows across ITSM/CRM/IAM and execute approved changes with guardrails, reducing cycle time and manual effort. Traditional AI (rules + anomaly detection) provides deterministic entitlement validation, SoD checks, and risk scoring that are auditable and stable. Generative AI adds value in drafting policy-aligned communications, translating requirements into role mappings, and producing readable audit summaries, but should not be the system of record due to hallucination risk. Humans remain essential for policy ownership, exception approvals, investigations, and audit attestation. Assumptions: CRM supports APIs/SCIM or admin automation; an IAM/ITSM system exists; internal controls require periodic reviews and documented approvals; least-privilege and SoD constraints are defined or can be codified.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) defining/maintaining RBAC and SoD rules, because these encode business risk decisions; (2) approving privileged roles, cross-functional access, and exceptions where compensating controls are needed; (3) validating identity proofing and employment status when signals conflict (contractors, shared mailboxes, mergers); (4) investigating anomalies and potential incidents, where context and intent matter; (5) final access review sign-off and audit evidence attestation, which requires accountable control owners. Humans should also review any AI-proposed remediation that could disrupt revenue operations (e.g., disabling a sales leader) and handle emergency access/break-glass procedures.
column17: Over-automation could grant/revoke access incorrectly due to bad inputs, ambiguous approvals, or workflow bugs; emergent behavior in multi-step planning; privilege escalation if the agent's credentials are mis-scoped; difficult root-cause analysis. Mitigations: least-privilege service accounts, step-level approvals for high-risk actions, policy-as-code constraints, sandbox testing, change windows, comprehensive audit logs, and kill-switch/rollback procedures.
column18: Hallucinated or overconfident recommendations about roles/permissions; may misinterpret internal policy text; risk of leaking sensitive identity/access data in prompts; inconsistent outputs across runs. Mitigations: restrict to retrieval-augmented generation over approved policy docs; redact/limit PII; require human review for entitlement recommendations; log prompts/outputs; use templates and constrained generation.
column19: Rule brittleness and drift (org changes make mappings outdated); false positives/negatives in anomaly detection leading to alert fatigue or missed risk; bias if models learn from historically over-privileged patterns. Mitigations: periodic rule/model reviews, versioning and approvals, monitoring precision/recall, clear explainability, and aligning baselines to current RBAC standards rather than historical entitlements.
column20: Inconsistent sources of truth between HR, IAM, CRM, and ITSM can cause incorrect provisioning; identity matching errors; logging gaps across systems hinder audits; conflicting recommendations between anomaly models and workflow automation; prompt-injection or data-poisoning via tickets/requests. Mitigations: master data management for identities, strong correlation keys, end-to-end audit trails, reconciliation jobs, secure connectors, input validation, and separation of duties between recommendation and execution components.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end access request workflow (ticket intake -> validation -> provisioning -> notifications). Execute automated joiner/mover/leaver (JML) actions with guardrails. Continuous monitoring and remediation suggestions|Draft and standardize access request/approval communications and knowledge articles. Explain role/permission implications and propose least-privilege mappings. Summarize periodic access review findings for auditors and stakeholders|Define and approve access policies, RBAC model, and segregation-of-duties (SoD) rules. Approve high-risk access, exceptions, and emergency/break-glass requests. Handle complex edge cases and investigate anomalies. Audit sign-off and evidence attestation|Policy/rule-based entitlement assignment and validation. Anomaly detection on access patterns and account hygiene. Automated periodic access review sampling and reconciliation
column23: Monitors access request channels (ITSM/CRM queue), gathers required attributes (user, role, justification, manager), routes for approvals, triggers downstream actions via CRM/IAM APIs, and posts status updates; adapts steps based on request type (new hire, role change, termination). After approvals, performs multi-step actions such as creating/deactivating accounts, assigning permission sets/roles, revoking access on termination, and confirming completion; uses policy constraints (least privilege, SoD) and requires human approval for exceptions. Periodically checks for drift (users with elevated roles, stale accounts), opens remediation tickets, and proposes corrective actions; can auto-remediate low-risk items (e.g., disable inactive accounts) under predefined thresholds.|Generates clear, policy-aligned templates for access request forms, approval prompts, user notifications, and internal runbooks; summarizes changes for audit trails while avoiding sensitive data leakage. Given a job function and required CRM capabilities, suggests appropriate standard roles/permission sets and highlights risks; supports admins by translating policy language into actionable configuration guidance. Turns structured review outputs (exceptions, remediation status, metrics) into narrative reports, executive summaries, and evidence packages; flags ambiguous items for human follow-up.|Requires organizational context, risk appetite, and accountability; policy decisions are governance responsibilities and must be defensible to auditors. Human judgment needed to weigh business urgency vs. security/compliance; ensures proper justification and compensating controls. Investigations may require cross-system context, interviews, and nuanced interpretation (e.g., legitimate unusual activity vs. compromise). Formal control ownership and attestation cannot be delegated to AI; humans ensure evidence completeness and accuracy.|Applies deterministic rules (RBAC matrices, department-to-role mappings, SoD constraints) to recommend/validate entitlements; produces explainable pass/fail results for approvals. Uses statistical/ML models to detect outliers (privileged access spikes, unusual login geos, dormant privileged accounts) and score risk to prioritize reviews. Generates review populations, compares current entitlements vs. approved baselines, identifies mismatches, and produces structured exception lists for sign-off.

column8: Build and maintain CRM reports and dashboards for sales leadership and operations, translating KPI and OKR requirements into accurate metrics and scheduled distribution.
column15: The task mixes ambiguous business translation (KPI/OKR interpretation) with technical implementation and ongoing operations. Generative AI is strong for drafting metric specs, documentation, and clarifying questions but cannot be trusted to define or compute metrics without verification. Traditional AI (deterministic rules/SQL plus lightweight ML) is best for repeatable metric computation and automated data-quality/anomaly checks. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating refresh/distribution workflows and monitoring/report-health remediation, reducing manual ops toil. Humans remain essential for governance, resolving definition disputes, approving high-stakes dashboards, and handling edge cases like reorganizations or incentive changes. column17: Autonomous workflows can propagate errors at scale (e.g., distributing incorrect dashboards), create excessive notifications, or take unintended actions when tool permissions are broad; debugging can be hard due to opaque decision traces. column8: Support sales teams by troubleshooting CRM issues, resolving tickets, documenting fixes, and coordinating with vendors or internal IT to meet service level expectations.
column15: The task mixes repeatable operational work (triage, monitoring, playbook diagnostics, documentation) with high-stakes judgment (production changes, prioritization, stakeholder management). Agentic AI can orchestrate multi-step workflows-enriching tickets, running safe diagnostics, coordinating with vendors/IT-reducing cycle time, but must be constrained by guardrails and approvals. Generative AI accelerates communication and documentation and helps interpret logs, but outputs require verification to avoid hallucinations. Humans remain essential for ambiguous incidents, governance/compliance, and decisions that trade off risk vs. business impact. Assumptions: a modern CRM stack (e.g., Salesforce/Dynamics) with APIs, centralized ticketing (ServiceNow/Jira), accessible logs/telemetry, and defined change-management policies; maturity allows limited autonomous actions in sandbox and low-risk production operations.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) prioritization when multiple sales-critical issues compete and business context is needed; (2) approving any production change impacting security/PII, data model, automation, or integrations; (3) validating root cause and confirming fixes before closure, especially where data integrity or revenue reporting is affected; (4) handling edge cases like conflicting requirements across regions, suspected security incidents, or vendor disputes; (5) ensuring communications are accurate and appropriate for executives/high-value accounts. Humans also set and tune AI guardrails, review audit logs, and own accountability for SLA commitments.
column17: Autonomous actions could apply incorrect fixes, change configurations, or spam stakeholders/vendors; emergent behavior in multi-step workflows may violate change controls.
