column8: Coordinate with sales, product, engineering, and customer success partners to align on requirements, resolve blockers, and deliver integrated solutions for customer needs.
column15: This ES Senior Manager task is coordination-heavy and high-stakes: aligning requirements and resolving blockers spans ambiguous inputs, cross-team incentives, and customer commitments. Generative AI is strong for synthesizing unstructured inputs into requirement artifacts and stakeholder communications, but must be grounded in authoritative sources to avoid errors. Agentic AI can reliably automate the operational backbone (scheduling, action-item tracking, escalations) when constrained by playbooks and permissions. Traditional AI adds value in deterministic routing, tagging, and KPI/anomaly dashboards over structured CRM/ticket data. Humans remain essential for prioritization, negotiation, and final commitments because these involve accountability, nuanced trade-offs, and relationship management. Assumptions: access to integrated toolchain (CRM/Jira/Slack/Email), defined escalation policies, and a knowledge base/architecture docs for grounding; without these, AI contributions drop and human load rises.
column16: Human oversight is required at (1) commitment points: what is promised to the customer (scope, dates, integrations), (2) prioritization and trade-offs across roadmap vs. deal urgency, (3) conflict resolution when stakeholders disagree or incentives misalign, (4) compliance/security/legal statements and handling of regulated customer data, and (5) edge cases where inputs are incomplete/contradictory or where AI-detected blockers require contextual judgment (e.g., strategic customer exceptions). Humans should approve externally shared requirement docs, escalation to execs, and any changes to playbooks/automation permissions.
column17: Over-automation (escalating too aggressively, spamming stakeholders), acting on wrong context (misassigned owners), permission misuse, and opaque decisioning in dynamic workflows. Mitigations: least-privilege access, constrained action space (playbooks), approval gates for high-impact actions (exec escalation, customer comms), simulation/testing in sandbox, and full audit logs/rollback.
column18: Hallucinated requirements or incorrect technical claims; context drift across long threads; leakage of sensitive customer data if prompts/logs are not controlled; tone/wording that implies contractual commitments. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from approved sources, redaction/DLP, templates with non-commitment language, mandatory human review for external artifacts, and citation of sources/links.
column19: Brittle rules leading to misrouting; model drift as org/product taxonomy changes; biased prioritization signals if trained on historical patterns that under-serve certain segments. column8: Maintain customer-facing and internal documentation, including solution guides, playbooks, and process runbooks, to ensure consistency, usability, and audit readiness.
column15: Maintaining audit-ready documentation is a continuous lifecycle task requiring both content creation and governance. Generative AI is best for drafting, rewriting, summarizing SME inputs, and producing consistent variants, but it cannot be trusted to assert facts without verification. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating end-to-end workflows (intake, routing, reminders, publishing) and monitoring upstream changes to trigger updates, yet it must operate within strict guardrails to avoid unintended edits or premature publication. Traditional AI is well-suited for deterministic compliance checks (templates, metadata, link checks), duplicate/staleness detection, and search/indexing improvements. Humans remain essential for authoritative technical accuracy, policy/compliance approvals, and prioritization decisions-especially in a Sales Support Engineering context where documentation can affect customer commitments, support outcomes, and audit findings. Assumptions: documentation lives in managed repositories with versioning; access to release notes/ticket data is available; approval workflows exist or can be implemented. Constraints/edge cases: rapidly changing products, conflicting SME guidance, and customer-specific exceptions require human arbitration.
column16: Human oversight is required to (1) approve externally facing solution guides and any content that could be construed as a product commitment, SLA, or security guarantee; (2) validate technical correctness of runbooks that drive operational actions (risk of outages if wrong); (3) adjudicate conflicts between sources (release notes vs observed behavior vs legacy process); (4) ensure compliance with internal controls (SOX/ISO/SOC2-style evidence, access controls, retention) and handle exceptions; (5) decide prioritization when multiple docs are stale but capacity is limited; and (6) review edge cases such as customer-specific configurations, regulated-industry guidance, or incident-driven temporary procedures before they become canonical.
column17: Autonomous agents could modify/publish content without proper approvals, spam stakeholders with tasks, or misinterpret change signals and create churn; tool permission misconfiguration can cause overreach. Mitigations: least-privilege access, stage-gated workflows, human-in-the-loop approvals, audit logs, sandbox testing, and explicit policies for when agents can auto-apply edits vs only propose changes.
column18: May hallucinate steps, prerequisites, or product capabilities; can introduce subtle inaccuracies or outdated guidance if retrieval is incomplete; may leak sensitive info if prompts include customer data; tone/claims may create implied commitments. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, citation requirements, redaction/DLP, constrained templates, and mandatory human approval for customer-facing or high-risk runbooks.
column19: Rules and models can become brittle as templates/processes evolve; similarity detection may miss nuanced conflicts or flag false positives; deterministic checks may encourage box-ticking over true usability. column8: Partner with Sales, Product, and User Experience stakeholders to scope customer needs, define solution requirements, and deliver high-quality technical support for active opportunities.
column15: This Sales Engineer task is inherently cross-functional and high-stakes: it mixes discovery, requirement definition, and live opportunity support where errors can create contractual, reputational, or revenue risk. Generative AI is well-suited for accelerating synthesis (summaries, drafts, collateral) and improving responsiveness, but must be grounded and reviewed to avoid hallucinations or overpromising. Agentic AI can reduce coordination overhead by orchestrating workflows and continuously refreshing opportunity context, yet should operate within guardrails (approval gates, limited action scope) due to autonomy risks. Traditional AI is effective for structured triage, checklist enforcement, and explainable risk scoring using CRM and support metadata. Humans remain essential for stakeholder alignment, final technical commitments, and nuanced customer engagement. Assumptions: access to CRM/call transcripts/knowledge base via approved connectors; strong governance for customer data; SEs retain final approval for external-facing outputs and commitments.
column16: Human oversight is essential at (1) discovery interpretation and prioritization when inputs conflict (e.g., Sales urgency vs. Product feasibility), (2) any externally shared requirements, architectures, security/roadmap/performance statements that could become contractual, (3) exception handling (custom integrations, non-standard terms, regulated-industry constraints), (4) escalation decisions and customer communications during incidents or sensitive negotiations, and (5) approval of agentic actions that change records, contact customers, or commit resources. Edge cases: incomplete/low-quality transcripts, novel use cases not covered by KB, customers requesting guarantees, and multi-product solutions with complex dependencies.
column17: Over-automation leading to premature stakeholder outreach, incorrect task assignments, or unintended CRM updates; emergent behavior when optimizing for speed over correctness; limited transparency in why actions were taken. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, approval workflows for external actions, action logging, sandbox testing, policy constraints (no commitments), and continuous monitoring with rollback mechanisms.
column18: Hallucination or subtle inaccuracies in requirements/technical claims; tone or positioning misaligned with approved messaging; leakage of confidential customer/internal data if prompts are not governed; bias in summarization (overweighting loud stakeholders). Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation, citation requirements, red-team prompts, PII redaction, mandatory human review for external outputs, and template-based constraints for security/roadmap language.
column19: Brittle rules/checklists that miss novel requirements; misrouting due to taxonomy drift; risk scores that become stale as product/market changes. Mitigations: periodic retraining and rule reviews, human-in-the-loop overrides, drift detection, and clear explanations of drivers/thresholds.
column20: Conflicting outputs (e.g., GenAI draft vs. checklist flags), propagation of incorrect context across systems, and governance gaps across CRM/support/doc repositories. Mitigations: single source of truth for opportunity brief, versioning, provenance/citations, consistent access controls, and orchestration that enforces sequencing (summarize -> checklist validate -> human approve -> publish).

column15: This Sales Engineer task mixes high-context persuasion and real-time interaction (best handled by humans) with substantial preparation and content tailoring (strong fit for Generative AI) and workflow orchestration across tools (fit for Agentic AI). column8: Design and validate solution architectures and UX integration approaches, including workflows, APIs, and data flows, to ensure feasibility and alignment with best practices.
column15: Designing and validating architectures for Sales Engineering is high-stakes and context-dependent: humans must own requirements, trade-offs, and approvals. Generative AI accelerates ideation, documentation, and mapping best practices to the proposed design, but cannot be solely trusted for correctness. Agentic AI is valuable for executing repeatable multi-step validation workflows (spinning up sandboxes, running tests, collecting evidence) and iterating on failures, reducing manual effort. Traditional AI provides reliable deterministic checks (spec/schema validation, rule-based governance, dependency/PII flow analysis) and lightweight sizing models. Assumptions: access to accurate internal API catalogs, reference architectures, and a safe sandbox; customer constraints are partially documented; tooling exists for CI-style validation. If these are missing, human share increases and agentic share decreases.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) interpreting ambiguous customer requirements and aligning architecture to business outcomes; (2) selecting among trade-offs (cost vs. latency, build vs. buy, synchronous vs. async) and accepting residual risk; (3) validating UX/workflow fit with real user roles, exceptions, and organizational constraints; (4) security/privacy/compliance decisions (PII handling, data residency, authN/Z, threat modeling) and formal sign-offs; (5) handling edge cases where tools disagree, tests are inconclusive, or constraints conflict with best practices; (6) customer communication and commitment management-AI cannot be the accountable party.
column17: Autonomous actions can cause unintended changes (e.g., provisioning costs, modifying configs, leaking data into logs) and may pursue incorrect goals if prompts/constraints are underspecified. Mitigations: least-privilege access, sandbox-only execution, approval gates for destructive actions, audit logs, bounded toolset, and explicit success criteria.
column18: May hallucinate APIs, constraints, or best practices; can produce plausible but incorrect data-flow/UX details; may miss subtle security requirements or invent compliance claims. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved internal docs, enforce citations, require human review, and validate outputs via deterministic tools/tests.
column19: Rule-based checks can be brittle/outdated and may not capture novel architectures; false positives/negatives in schema/PII detection; sizing models may be inaccurate under new workloads. Mitigations: continuous rule updates, calibration with real telemetry, allow overrides with justification, and combine with empirical testing.
column20: Mismatch between generated designs and what validators/test harnesses actually check; inconsistent sources of truth (API catalogs vs. code); orchestration failures leading to incomplete evidence; data privacy risks when sharing artifacts across tools. Mitigations: single source of truth, automated traceability from requirement->design->tests, standardized artifact formats (OpenAPI, AsyncAPI), secure data handling, and end-to-end review checkpoints.

column15: The task mixes content creation (well-suited to Generative AI), operational upkeep of demo/POC assets (best handled by Agentic AI orchestrating IaC/CI workflows), and repeatable governance/QA (Traditional AI via deterministic checks and classical ML for inventory/search). column17: Autonomous workflows can misconfigure environments, rotate credentials incorrectly, incur unexpected cloud costs, or propagate breaking changes across many demos; limited transparency in decision policies. Mitigations: least-privilege, approval gates for prod-like changes, cost budgets, canary demos, rollback plans, audit logs, and constrained action spaces (runbooks).

column8: Lead proof of concept engagements by defining success criteria, coordinating resources, managing timelines, and documenting outcomes to support deal progression.
column15: This PoC-lead task mixes relationship-driven decision-making with repeatable project operations. Humans remain primary because success criteria, scope control, and customer trust directly impact deal progression and carry legal/security implications. Agentic AI can materially reduce coordination overhead by maintaining plans, tracking blockers, and orchestrating routine workflows across CRM/ticketing/calendar-especially in multi-stakeholder PoCs. Generative AI accelerates drafting of success criteria, communications, and outcome reports, but requires grounding in customer-provided facts and human review to avoid misstatements. Traditional AI adds reliable, explainable automation for structured checks (artifact completeness, stage gates) and quantitative tracking (forecasting, telemetry dashboards). Assumptions: the org has integrated tooling (CRM, PM, ticketing, docs) and allows AI access under governance; customer data shared is sanitized or covered by agreements; PoC telemetry is available in structured form.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) final definition/approval of success criteria and any scope changes (commercial impact, stakeholder politics); (2) security/privacy decisions (data access, retention, environment changes) and compliance sign-off; (3) customer communications in sensitive moments (missed milestones, competitive displacement, pricing/contractual implications); (4) interpreting ambiguous results and deciding whether evidence is sufficient to progress the deal; (5) handling edge cases like conflicting stakeholder goals, unanticipated integration constraints, or when AI outputs conflict with product reality. Humans should approve any agentic actions that create external commitments, modify customer systems, or share customer-facing documents.
column17: Over-automation could trigger actions without proper approvals (e.g., provisioning, emailing stakeholders), causing security/compliance incidents or relationship damage; brittle tool integrations can create cascading workflow errors; opaque decision logic may make it hard to audit why timelines/escalations changed. Mitigations: permissioning/least privilege, approval gates for sensitive steps, audit logs, sandbox testing, rate limits, and clear rollback procedures.
column18: Hallucination or overconfident claims in success criteria/outcome reports; leakage of sensitive customer info if prompts/logs are not governed; tone or positioning misaligned with deal strategy; IP/copyright risk when generating diagrams or reusing text. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from approved sources, redaction, prompt/data policies, mandatory human review for customer-facing artifacts, and citation of evidence for claims.
column19: Models/rules may be outdated as products/processes change, leading to false alerts or missed risks; forecasting can be misleading with small sample sizes or atypical enterprise PoCs; anomaly detection may flag noise and distract teams. Mitigations: periodic rule/model review, human-tuned thresholds, monitoring precision/recall, and allowing manual overrides with rationale.
column20: Inconsistent data across CRM/PM/docs can cause AI to act on stale or conflicting information; identity/access misconfiguration can expose customer data; feedback loops where GenAI summaries feed agentic decisions can amplify errors. Mitigations: single source of truth, data validation layers, strong IAM, segregation of duties, provenance tracking (what data informed outputs), and requiring evidence links for key decisions.
column22: Orchestrate PoC project plan and task execution across tools. Coordinate environment provisioning and access workflows (semi-autonomous). Continuous status synthesis and risk/issue management|Draft PoC success criteria, test plans, and acceptance checklists. Create customer-facing communications and enablement artifacts. Document outcomes and produce executive-ready PoC summary|Own customer relationship, align stakeholders, and negotiate success criteria trade-offs. Make high-stakes decisions and approvals (security, scope, commitments). Technical leadership on solution fit and edge-case troubleshooting|Timeline forecasting and milestone tracking using structured signals. Template-driven compliance checks and completeness validation. column23: Given a human-approved PoC charter (scope, stakeholders, constraints), an agentic system can create and maintain a workback plan, open/assign tasks in Jira/Asana, schedule recurring check-ins, monitor blockers from updates, and propose re-plans when dependencies slip. Where integrations exist (IAM, sandbox provisioning, ticketing), agentic AI can submit standardized requests, track approvals, validate prerequisites, and sequence setup steps (e.g., data access -> connector config -> test run). It should pause for human approval on security-sensitive actions and customer-system changes. Agentic AI can ingest meeting notes, ticket states, and telemetry to maintain a live RAID log (risks, assumptions, issues, dependencies), detect schedule risk, and recommend mitigations (e.g., reduce scope, add SME time, adjust success tests).|Using discovery notes, product capabilities, and customer objectives, GenAI drafts measurable success criteria (KPIs, pass/fail tests), proposes experiment design, and produces an acceptance checklist aligned to the buyer's decision process. Rules/validators ensure required PoC artifacts are present (NDA, security questionnaire, data handling plan, success criteria, test evidence), enforce naming conventions, and check that fields are filled before moving stages in CRM. column8: Respond to technical due diligence requests including RFPs, security questionnaires, and architecture reviews, ensuring accurate and compliant information is provided on time.
column15: This task is high-stakes and externally facing: accuracy, contractual language, and security/compliance claims require accountable human approval. Generative AI provides the largest leverage by drafting and summarizing responses quickly, especially when paired with retrieval over approved artifacts. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the end-to-end workflow (intake, tasking, evidence collection, reminders) but should be constrained to approved systems and actions. Traditional AI is well-suited for deterministic checks (control mappings, required fields, forbidden phrases) and routing/analytics. Assumptions: the organization maintains an approved, versioned knowledge base (policies, SOC2/ISO artifacts, standard architecture diagrams) and has clear approval workflows; without these, human share increases and GenAI usefulness drops due to higher hallucination/verification burden.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) approving any statement that could be construed as a contractual commitment (SLAs, roadmap, security guarantees), (2) validating nuanced security/privacy interpretations (data residency, subprocessors, encryption boundaries, incident response specifics), (3) participating in live architecture reviews where requirements evolve in real time, (4) deciding how to respond when evidence is missing or controls are partially met (risk acceptance vs. remediation plan), and (5) handling edge cases such as regulated industries, cross-border data transfer constraints, or customer-requested nonstandard terms. Mandatory human sign-off should be enforced before submission, with named approvers from SE + Security/Compliance + Legal as applicable.
column17: Over-automation could send unapproved responses, pull the wrong artifact version, or spam SMEs; autonomous actions may be hard to audit. Mitigations: least-privilege tool access, approval gates before external submission, immutable audit logs, constrained action policies (allowlists), and sandbox testing with simulated questionnaires.
column18: May hallucinate controls/certifications, misstate architecture boundaries, or produce overconfident language; risk of leaking sensitive info if prompts include restricted artifacts; context drift across long questionnaires. Mitigations: retrieval-only from approved sources, citation requirement, response style guides with forbidden claims, redaction/DLP, and mandatory human review for all external answers.
column19: Rules/control mappings can become outdated as products and certifications change; brittle classification may misroute questions leading to delays or incorrect templates. Mitigations: versioned control matrices, periodic reviews, monitoring for low-confidence classifications, and feedback loops from human corrections.
column20: RAG + workflow orchestration can propagate a single wrong source across many answers; identity/access misconfiguration can expose confidential reports; inconsistent taxonomy between routing (Traditional AI) and drafting (GenAI) can cause mismatched responses. Mitigations: single source of truth with versioning, end-to-end provenance (source links per answer), unified taxonomy, automated regression tests on standard questionnaires, and staged rollout with metrics (accuracy, cycle time, rework rate).

Executes multi-step workflows to pull relevant artifacts (SOC2 report links, pen test summaries, architecture diagrams, product docs) from approved repositories, requests missing inputs from SMEs, and maintains an audit trail of sources.|Uses retrieval-augmented generation over the company's approved knowledge base to propose answers, cite sources, and tailor wording to the customer's terminology while preserving factual constraints. Condenses long policies/architecture docs into customer-facing explanations, creates executive summaries, and translates content while keeping key controls and caveats intact. When a requirement is partially unmet, drafts a compliant explanation, compensating controls, roadmap language, and asks clarifying questions-flagging items that require SME confirmation.|Humans must approve externally shared statements, ensure no over-commitment, validate nuanced security posture, and accept residual risk; required for contractual/compliance accountability. SEs interpret customer context, negotiate tradeoffs, and make design commitments; requires deep domain judgment and real-time interaction. For new products, unusual regulatory regimes, or ambiguous questions, humans decide the correct stance, gather authoritative evidence, and update the knowledge base/templates.|Uses rules/ML to categorize questions (security, privacy, infra, data handling), detect repeats across questionnaires, and route to the right template/owner to reduce manual sorting. Deterministically validates responses against a control matrix (e.g., SOC2/ISO controls, data residency options), required fields, and forbidden claims; flags inconsistencies and missing attachments. Applies deterministic scheduling logic and dashboards to track due dates, cycle time, bottlenecks, and escalation thresholds.

column8: Provide structured feedback to Product and User Experience teams based on customer interactions, helping prioritize enhancements and improve usability and adoption.
column15: The task blends qualitative judgment (what matters, why, and how it affects deals/adoption) with high-volume synthesis and workflow execution. Generative AI is best for summarizing and structuring unstructured customer interactions into PM/UX-friendly artifacts and drafting readouts. Agentic AI adds value by continuously collecting signals across tools, maintaining a living backlog, and routing items through product workflows with follow-ups-reducing manual coordination. Traditional AI supports reliable quantification (frequency/trends), deduplication, and compliance-oriented redaction/classification with higher determinism. Human Sales Engineers remain essential to validate intent and impact, avoid mis-escalation, and align feedback with commercial realities and relationship management. Assumptions: access to interaction data (transcripts/notes), a defined product taxonomy, and integration with product tooling; without these, agentic/traditional contributions drop and human effort rises.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) interpreting ambiguous feedback where root cause could be training/configuration vs product defect, (2) assessing business impact in context (deal stage, ARR, strategic logo, competitive threats), (3) making/approving prioritization recommendations that affect roadmap and customer commitments, (4) handling sensitive or regulated data and ensuring anonymization is sufficient, (5) resolving conflicts between customer requests and product strategy, and (6) edge cases like novel workflows, low-signal but high-risk issues (security, data loss), or when AI confidence is low. Humans should approve any feedback that triggers engineering work, roadmap communication, or escalations to leadership.
column17: Autonomous routing/escalation can create noise, spam stakeholders, or open incorrect tickets; may learn undesirable heuristics (optimizing for closure vs quality) and reduce human control/traceability. Mitigations: approval gates for ticket creation/escalations, rate limits, audit logs, policy constraints (allowed actions), sandbox testing, and monitoring KPIs (reopen rate, stakeholder satisfaction).
column18: May hallucinate details (e.g., incorrect reproduction steps), overgeneralize from a single loud customer, or misattribute sentiment/intent; can leak sensitive info if prompts include PII. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded summarization with citations to source snippets, structured templates with required fields, confidence/uncertainty flags, mandatory human review for high-severity items, and automated redaction before model input/output.
column19: Rule-based classification can become brittle as product taxonomy evolves; similarity/dedup thresholds can merge distinct issues or fail to merge duplicates; analytics can be misleading with biased sampling (only vocal customers). Mitigations: periodic rule/model recalibration, human-in-the-loop review for low-confidence classifications, drift detection, and clear labeling of sample sizes/coverage.
column20: Data silos and inconsistent identifiers can cause mis-linking feedback to accounts/opportunities; conflicting outputs (GenAI theme vs traditional metrics) can confuse prioritization; privacy/security risks increase when moving data across tools/models. Mitigations: master data management for customer IDs, unified taxonomy, provenance tracking (source links), role-based access controls, encryption, and a single 'system of record' for feedback with reconciliation logic.
column22: Continuously collect and normalize customer-interaction signals across systems. Route feedback items to the right owners and workflows with SLA tracking. Propose prioritization recommendations and run what-if scenarios|Summarize customer interactions into structured insights. Extract themes, pain points, and user stories from unstructured text. Draft feedback narratives and executive-ready readouts. Generate clarifying questions and follow-up prompts for customers|Validate interpretation, business impact, and prioritization trade-offs. Handle sensitive accounts, commitments, and cross-functional alignment. Provide nuanced UX context and reproduction fidelity. Approve external-facing or compliance-relevant summaries|Quantify frequency, trend, and correlation metrics. Rule-based classification and PII detection/redaction. Deduplication and similarity scoring for feedback items
column23: Orchestrates connectors to CRM (e.g., Salesforce), call transcripts, email threads, support tickets, demo notes, and product analytics; de-duplicates accounts/opportunities, tags interaction context (industry, stage, persona), and maintains an always-updated feedback backlog with minimal prompting. Automatically creates/updates items in Jira/Aha!/Productboard, assigns to PM/UX based on component taxonomy, monitors status, pings stakeholders for missing details, and escalates when SLAs are breached; adapts routing rules based on outcomes. Uses multi-step reasoning over impact (ARR at risk, adoption friction), effort proxies, and strategic themes to suggest priority ordering; can simulate alternative prioritization policies and summarize trade-offs for human approval.|Transforms transcripts/notes into concise summaries with fields like problem statement, reproduction steps, user goal, severity, frequency, and quotes; produces consistent templates for PM/UX consumption. Performs semantic clustering and converts recurring issues into user stories/jobs-to-be-done, acceptance criteria, and UX heuristics violations; highlights sentiment and friction moments. Generates weekly/monthly feedback reports, release-impact notes, and 'voice of customer' briefs tailored to PM vs UX audiences, including anonymized examples and recommended next questions. Suggests targeted questions to validate root cause, quantify impact, and capture UX context (environment, workflow, constraints), improving data quality for prioritization.|Sales Engineer applies domain knowledge of customer intent, deal context, and competitive landscape; confirms whether an issue is a true product gap vs misconfiguration/training; decides what to escalate. Humans manage relationship risk, set expectations, and ensure feedback does not create unintended commitments; negotiate priorities with PM/UX and Sales leadership. Humans capture edge-case workflows, environment constraints, and step-by-step reproduction that AI may miss; verify screenshots/logs and ensure accuracy before engineering action. Human review required when feedback includes regulated data, contractual terms, or could influence roadmap communications; ensures adherence to privacy, security, and messaging policies.|Uses deterministic analytics/ML on tagged feedback to compute counts, trends over time, cohort breakdowns, and correlations with churn risk, NPS/CSAT, feature usage, and funnel drop-offs; outputs explainable dashboards. Applies rules/models to map feedback to product areas, severity tiers, and customer segments; detects and redacts PII/PHI/secrets before sharing with broader teams to reduce compliance risk. Uses embeddings/similarity thresholds or classical clustering to merge duplicates and maintain a clean backlog; provides confidence scores and audit trails.

column8: Contribute to team development by documenting processes, mentoring peers, and improving internal playbooks that increase efficiency and innovation within Sales Engineering.
column15: This task mixes high-context leadership (prioritization, mentoring, accountability) with heavy content and workflow overhead. Generative AI is well-suited to drafting SOPs, summarizing debriefs, and producing coaching artifacts, but needs grounding and review. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the documentation lifecycle and continuously proposing improvements based on operational signals, yet should not publish or mandate changes without approval due to organizational and compliance risk. Traditional AI reliably handles structured governance: tagging, duplicate detection, KPI dashboards, and rule-based checks. Humans remain essential for strategic alignment, interpersonal mentoring, and final validation of technical and commercial accuracy. Assumptions: access to sanitized internal data sources, an approved knowledge base, and clear review/approval gates; uncertainty: quality of available telemetry (call notes, debrief consistency) and change-management maturity in the SE org.
column16: Human oversight is required to (1) set priorities and success criteria for process changes, balancing efficiency with customer experience and sales strategy; (2) approve any playbook guidance that could affect customer commitments, security posture, pricing/packaging statements, or legal/compliance language; (3) perform technical validation where product behavior is nuanced or rapidly changing; (4) deliver sensitive mentoring (performance feedback, conflict resolution, career guidance) and calibrate coaching to individual context; (5) handle edge cases such as regulated-industry guidance, exceptions to standard process, and disagreements between stakeholders (Sales, Product, Legal). Humans should also audit AI-generated content for confidentiality and ensure no proprietary/customer data is leaked.
column17: Autonomous workflows can create unintended changes (publishing outdated guidance, spamming notifications, misrouting approvals) and may optimize for measurable KPIs at the expense of qualitative outcomes; transparency and auditability challenges. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, mandatory approval steps, sandbox testing, rate limits, full audit logs, and clear rollback/version control.
column18: May hallucinate product capabilities, invent steps, or produce overly confident guidance; can drift from current roadmap or policy; risk of leaking sensitive customer/proprietary info if prompts include raw data; tone may be misaligned for internal culture. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from approved sources, redaction, citation requirements, human review gates, and restricted data access.
column19: Rules and models can become brittle as processes/products evolve; tagging/duplicate detection may misclassify, harming discoverability; KPI correlations may be misinterpreted as causation. Mitigations: periodic rule/model review, human-in-the-loop corrections, monitoring precision/recall, and clear metric definitions.
column20: Conflicting outputs between systems (e.g., GenAI draft vs. rule-based compliance checks), data quality issues across sources, and identity/permission mismatches can cause leakage or incorrect automation. Mitigations: unified taxonomy, source-of-truth governance, consistent access controls, staged pipelines (draft->check->review->publish), and automated tests for workflows and templates.
column22: Continuously monitor SE workflow signals and propose playbook updates. Orchestrate documentation lifecycle (intake -> draft -> review -> publish -> notify). Metrics and anomaly detection on enablement/process KPIs. Template enforcement and compliance checks
column23: Connects to approved systems (CRM, ticketing, call notes, enablement platforms) to detect recurring friction (e.g., repeated technical objections, long handoff times), then autonomously drafts a change proposal, routes it for review, and tracks adoption metrics over time. Runs a multi-step workflow: collects inputs from SMEs, assigns reviewers, enforces SLAs, manages versioning, and publishes to the knowledge base; adapts plans when reviewers are unavailable or priorities shift. Uses role/territory/product context to recommend relevant playbook sections, micro-quizzes, and practice scenarios; schedules reminders and gathers feedback, escalating gaps to human leads.|Turns rough notes, meeting transcripts, and SME bullet points into structured SOPs, checklists, decision trees, and templates aligned to SE motions (discovery, demo, POC, security review). Condenses win/loss reviews, POC postmortems, and deal debriefs into key patterns, anti-patterns, and recommended actions; tags content for searchability. Generates scenario-based coaching materials and role-play prompts tailored to product, persona, and industry; proposes variations for different customer maturity levels.|Requires contextual judgment about strategy, product direction, and customer impact; humans decide which process changes are worth the organizational cost and align with leadership goals. Humans must verify that playbooks do not overpromise capabilities, violate legal/compliance constraints, or conflict with current product roadmap; final approval is needed for high-risk content.|Uses deterministic rules and classical NLP/IR (keyword + embeddings similarity thresholds) to auto-tag documents, detect near-duplicates, and suggest canonical pages to reduce sprawl. Computes dashboards for cycle time, POC duration, content usage, and win-rate correlations; flags anomalies (e.g., sudden drop in playbook usage) using statistical thresholds. Applies rules to ensure required sections exist (security, data handling, disclaimers), links are valid, and naming/versioning conventions are followed before publication.

column8: Partner with Sales, Product, and Customer Success stakeholders to scope opportunities, align on objectives, and deliver high-quality technical support for the sales cycle.
column15: This Senior Sales Engineer task is inherently cross-functional and high-stakes: aligning objectives and delivering technical support involves negotiation, accountability, and customer-facing judgment, so it cannot be fully automated. Generative AI is best for accelerating knowledge work (summaries, drafts, tailored messaging) but needs grounding and review. Agentic AI adds value by coordinating multi-step workflows (deal-room upkeep, follow-ups, scheduling) and reducing operational drag, yet should operate within guardrails and approval gates. Traditional AI is most reliable for structured scoring, compliance checklists, and KPI analytics. Assumptions: access to CRM/call transcripts/product docs via secure retrieval; clear templates/playbooks exist; organization permits AI-assisted drafting with human approval for external outputs.
column16: Humans must (1) run/steer discovery and align stakeholders when incentives conflict; (2) approve any external-facing technical claims, pricing/packaging implications, security statements, and roadmap/feature commitments; (3) decide trade-offs under uncertainty (customization vs. standard product, risk acceptance, escalation to Product/Engineering); (4) manage edge cases like regulated customers, ambiguous requirements, or adversarial procurement/RFP language; (5) ensure compliance with confidentiality, data handling, and customer communication policies. Human oversight is essential at stage gates: scope sign-off, architecture approval, security questionnaire finalization, and escalation decisions.
column17: Over-automation causing inappropriate outreach/scheduling, spamming stakeholders, or updating CRM incorrectly; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals; privilege misuse if connected to enterprise systems. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval workflows for external actions, rate limits, audit logs, sandbox testing, and clear stop conditions/escalation rules.
column18: Hallucinated product capabilities or incorrect security/architecture claims; context drift across long deal cycles; leakage of sensitive customer data if prompts/logs are mishandled; biased or inconsistent messaging. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with approved sources, citation/grounding requirements, red-teaming for security questionnaires, strict data governance, and mandatory human review for external artifacts.
column19: Brittle rules leading to mis-scoring opportunities or missing nuanced fit; model drift if ML scoring isn't retrained; false sense of objectivity in prioritization. Mitigations: periodic calibration with win/loss reviews, explainable scoring factors, override mechanisms, and monitoring for bias across segments.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI draft vs. rule-based compliance), stale knowledge in the deal-room, and unclear accountability when AI updates records. Toolchain risks include data synchronization errors between CRM, doc stores, and ticketing. Mitigations: single source of truth, versioning, provenance metadata, human-in-the-loop gates, automated consistency checks, and incident response for erroneous actions.
column22: Orchestrate cross-functional workflow and follow-ups. Maintain deal-room knowledge base and action tracker|Summarize discovery inputs and draft opportunity scope. Draft technical collateral for the sales cycle. Generate stakeholder-aligned messaging|Lead discovery, negotiate trade-offs, and align objectives. Validate technical accuracy and make commitments. Handle sensitive customer interactions and escalations|Qualification and prioritization scoring. Compliance checks and template enforcement. Analytics on technical support performance
column23: Monitors CRM/email/calendar for deal-stage changes, triggers playbooks, schedules stakeholder syncs, assigns action items, and nudges owners; adapts cadence based on risk signals (e.g., slipping close date, unanswered technical questions). Continuously ingests approved artifacts (notes, requirements, RFPs), updates a structured deal brief, tracks open questions/owners/SLAs, and escalates blockers to the Sales Engineer.|Turns call transcripts, notes, and emails into a concise scope: problem statement, success criteria, constraints, stakeholders, and next steps; proposes clarifying questions tailored to the customer's domain. Produces first drafts of solution overviews, architecture diagrams (textual descriptions), security questionnaires, RFP responses, and demo scripts using internal templates and retrieved product docs; flags low-confidence areas. Creates role-specific narratives (Sales value, Product feasibility, CS adoption/ops) and objection-handling talk tracks consistent with positioning and customer context.|Requires nuanced judgment, relationship management, and real-time negotiation across Sales/Product/CS; humans resolve conflicting incentives and set final success criteria. Humans must approve architectures, timelines, integrations, and any statements that create contractual/roadmap commitments; accountable for customer trust and risk. High-stakes conversations (security incidents, competitive displacement, executive stakeholders) require empathy, credibility, and situational awareness beyond current AI reliability.|Applies deterministic rules/ML on structured CRM fields (industry, ARR, stage, product fit, support load) to score opportunity priority and recommend standard engagement level. Runs rule-based validation for required fields (e.g., data residency, security attestations), detects missing sections in RFP templates, and enforces naming/versioning conventions. Computes metrics like response time, win-rate by use case, common blockers, and escalation frequency to inform process improvements and stakeholder alignment.

column8: Lead discovery sessions to understand customer user experience goals, technical constraints, and success criteria, and translate findings into solution recommendations.
column15: Discovery leadership is high-context and relationship-driven, so humans retain primary responsibility for facilitation, judgment, and final recommendations. Generative AI is strong for drafting question sets, summarizing transcripts, and producing first-pass recommendation narratives, accelerating documentation and consistency. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the workflow around discovery (planning, scheduling, follow-ups) and providing in-meeting prompts/action tracking, but must be constrained due to autonomy risks. Traditional AI is best for deterministic tagging, CRM field population, and capability-matrix checks where structured rules and auditability matter. Assumptions: meeting transcription is available; customer permits recording/processing; product capability matrices and CRM schemas are maintained; integrations (calendar/CRM) are approved by IT/security.
column16: Humans must (1) obtain consent for recording/AI use and ensure privacy/compliance, (2) steer conversations when stakeholders disagree or when sensitive topics arise (security incidents, competitive displacement), (3) validate AI-generated summaries/recommendations against ground truth and product realities, (4) decide trade-offs and commitments (scope, timelines, success metrics), and (5) handle edge cases like contradictory requirements, political blockers, or novel architectures not covered by matrices. Mandatory human approval should gate any customer-facing deliverable and any CRM updates that affect forecasting or contractual terms.
column17: Autonomous actions (sending emails, updating CRM, scheduling) can create customer friction or compliance issues; agents may optimize for completion over correctness and be hard to audit. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, approval workflows for external communications, action logs, sandbox testing, and guardrails limiting tool use to predefined playbooks.
column18: May hallucinate requirements, misattribute statements in summaries, or overstate product capabilities; can introduce biased framing or inappropriate tone. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved product docs, citation of transcript snippets, structured templates, and mandatory human review for customer-facing outputs.
column19: Rules/classifiers can be brittle when terminology varies; outdated capability matrices can yield false flags or missed incompatibilities. Mitigations: governance for rule/matrix updates, confidence thresholds with human escalation, and periodic evaluation against labeled discovery datasets.
column20: Data leakage across tools (transcripts/CRM), inconsistent source-of-truth between AI outputs and CRM, and compounding errors when agentic workflows act on generative summaries. Mitigations: centralized data governance, versioning and provenance (what came from transcript vs inference), human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and monitoring for drift/quality KPIs (summary accuracy, action-item completion, customer satisfaction).
column22: Pre-discovery planning and agenda orchestration. Real-time meeting assistance and action tracking|Question bank generation and tailoring to persona/use case. Summarization of discovery outputs into structured artifacts. Drafting solution recommendations and narrative|Build rapport, manage stakeholder dynamics, and negotiate scope. Validate nuanced requirements and interpret intent. Make final solution recommendation and commit to outcomes|Structured data extraction, tagging, and routing. Constraint and compatibility checks against known matrices
column23: Given CRM context, prior emails, and product scope, an agent can propose a tailored discovery plan (stakeholders to invite, time-boxed agenda, question tree) and adapt it as new info arrives; it can also coordinate scheduling, send pre-reads, and manage follow-ups with minimal prompting. During calls, an agent can monitor transcripts, detect unanswered critical questions (e.g., SSO, data residency), prompt the SE with suggested probes, and autonomously create action items, owners, and due dates in CRM/project tools.|Generates role-specific discovery questions (end users, IT, security, procurement) and variants for the customer's industry, maturity, and stated goals; helps avoid gaps and keeps language customer-friendly. Converts notes/transcripts into structured summaries: goals, current-state journey, pain points, constraints, success metrics, risks, and open questions; drafts a discovery readout and internal handoff. Produces first-pass solution options, trade-offs, and value narrative mapped to stated success criteria; drafts slides, emails, and proposal sections for human review.|Requires empathy, trust-building, and real-time judgment to handle politics, objections, and ambiguity; critical for eliciting candid constraints and aligning on priorities. Humans resolve ambiguity, detect inconsistencies, and interpret non-verbal cues; they confirm what the customer truly means by 'success' and reconcile conflicting stakeholder goals. Accountable decision-making (commercial, technical, ethical) and risk acceptance must remain with the Senior Sales Engineer; includes tailoring to customer context and company strategy.|Uses deterministic rules/ML classifiers to tag transcript segments to categories (security, integrations, UX, KPIs), populate CRM fields, and route items to SMEs; provides consistent, auditable processing. Applies rules/lookup tables to validate requirements against product capability matrices (supported browsers, auth methods, API limits, deployment regions) and flags mismatches for review.

column8: Design and deliver tailored product demonstrations and user experience walkthroughs that map capabilities to customer workflows and measurable business outcomes.
column15: The task mixes high-stakes customer interaction with substantial preparation. Generative AI is strong for drafting tailored narratives, scripts, and collateral quickly, but must be grounded in approved product facts and customer context. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-system prep (CRM->sandbox->assets->calendar) and acting as a live-session copilot to capture actions and adapt the demo path, yet autonomy must be constrained to avoid missteps. Traditional AI is best for deterministic module selection, explainable KPI/ROI calculations from structured inputs, and telemetry analytics. Humans remain essential for discovery interpretation, credibility in delivery, and accountable approval of claims-driving the largest share of work. Assumptions: access to clean CRM data, a modular demo environment, and governance over what content/models can use customer data; without these, AI contributions drop and human effort rises.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) validating customer needs and political context that are not fully captured in systems; (2) approving any quantified business outcomes/ROI claims and ensuring they match contractual, legal, and product realities; (3) handling security/compliance discussions (e.g., data residency, certifications) where incorrect statements create material risk; (4) real-time judgment during the live demo-prioritizing stakeholder concerns, managing objections, and deciding when to defer questions; (5) edge cases such as regulated industries, competitive displacement, or when the product has gaps requiring careful positioning. Humans should gate: final demo agenda, any externally shared collateral, and any commitments on roadmap/integrations.
column17: Autonomous actions across CRM/sandbox/email can create unintended changes (wrong environment, data leakage, premature follow-ups) and opaque decision paths; may over-optimize for completion vs customer experience. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval checkpoints for external actions, audit logs, sandboxed execution, policy constraints (no external send without approval), and fallback to manual control during live sessions.
column18: May hallucinate features, integrations, or certifications; can produce persuasive but inaccurate ROI narratives; tone may misalign with executive vs technical audiences; privacy risk if customer data is pasted into prompts. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved knowledge base, strict citation/grounding, red-team prompts, PII controls, and mandatory human review for customer-facing outputs and quantified claims.
column19: Rules/models can be brittle when product catalog changes or when customer context is incomplete; ROI models may encode biased historical assumptions and fail in novel industries; telemetry analytics can misattribute causality. Mitigations: regular rule/model maintenance, versioning tied to product releases, transparent assumptions, confidence intervals, and human validation of recommendations.
column20: Context mismatch between systems (CRM notes vs demo script), stale data leading to wrong personalization, and cascading errors when agentic workflows consume generative outputs; security/compliance risks when connecting meeting tools, CRM, and LLMs. Mitigations: unified customer context layer, data freshness checks, schema validation, content provenance/citations, end-to-end access governance, and monitoring with rollback procedures.
column22: Orchestrate demo preparation workflow across systems (CRM, product sandbox, analytics, calendar). Run interactive demo assistant during live sessions (guided navigation, Q&A routing, artifact capture)|Draft tailored demo storyline, talk track, and UX walkthrough script. Create customer-specific demo artifacts (slides, one-pagers, ROI hypotheses, use-case diagrams). Generate objection-handling prompts and alternative demo paths|Conduct nuanced discovery and validate workflow-to-outcome mapping. Deliver the live demo with relationship management and real-time judgment. Approve claims, compliance/security statements, and final narrative|Select relevant features/use-cases using rules and retrieval over structured metadata. Compute KPI/ROI estimates from structured inputs and benchmarks. Telemetry-driven personalization and performance analytics
column23: Plans and executes a multi-step checklist: pulls opportunity context from CRM, provisions/refreshes a demo environment, loads relevant sample data, schedules dry-runs, and tracks readiness; adapts steps based on customer industry, stage, and discovered constraints. Monitors the meeting context (agenda, timeboxes, attendee roles) and suggests next screens, retrieves supporting assets on demand, captures questions/objections, and creates follow-up tasks; can dynamically adjust the demo path when the customer pivots topics.|Generates a narrative that maps product features to the customer's stated workflows, personas, and KPIs; produces speaker notes, transitions, and value framing aligned to the sales stage. Produces editable collateral and lightweight workflow diagrams; summarizes discovery notes into outcome statements and measurable success criteria; proposes ROI drivers and assumptions for validation. Creates contingency branches (e.g., security deep-dive, integration focus) and suggested responses tailored to the customer's industry and constraints; helps the Sales Engineer rehearse.|Requires domain judgment to interpret ambiguous needs, political dynamics, and success criteria; validates assumptions behind ROI and ensures alignment with stakeholder priorities. Human presence is needed to read the room, handle sensitive questions, negotiate scope, and maintain credibility; decides when to deviate from script and what to commit to. column8: Develop proof of concept plans, configure environments, and validate integrations to demonstrate feasibility, performance, and usability for target customer scenarios.
column15: This PoC task mixes high-stakes customer alignment and governance (human-led) with repeatable planning, setup, and validation work that AI can accelerate. Generative AI is strongest for drafting PoC narratives, producing integration scaffolding, and synthesizing results, but must be grounded in authoritative docs and validated to avoid hallucinated configs. Agentic AI can coordinate multi-step execution (provisioning requests, running checks, iterating experiments) and reduce manual toil, yet needs approval gates for privileged actions and customer-impacting changes. Traditional AI excels at deterministic validation: benchmark computation, rules-based pre-checks, and structured test tracking-areas where reliability and repeatability matter. Assumptions: access to product documentation/SDKs, telemetry/logs, and CI/IaC pipelines; customer provides a sandbox or non-production environment; organization permits AI use with data controls. Constraints: customer-specific security policies, limited time windows, and integration variability drive the need for human judgment and controlled automation.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) defining PoC scope, success criteria, and demo storyline that match the customer's business process and buying committee concerns; (2) approving any action involving credentials, network connectivity to customer systems, data ingestion/export, or policy exceptions; (3) validating that generated configs/code reflect actual product behavior and supported versions; (4) interpreting results in context (e.g., whether a performance shortfall is acceptable given sizing assumptions) and deciding go/no-go, workaround vs. escalation; (5) handling edge cases like undocumented customer constraints, ambiguous errors, or situations requiring coordination with engineering/support. Mandatory human approvals should gate environment creation with elevated privileges, data seeding with customer data, and final customer-facing claims about feasibility/performance.
column17: Autonomous actions could misconfigure environments, over-consume cloud resources, rotate/overwrite credentials, or trigger unintended calls into customer systems; debugging agent decisions can be opaque. Mitigations: least-privilege service accounts, approval checkpoints, sandbox-only execution, rate limits/budgets, full audit logs, deterministic runbooks, and kill switches.
column18: May hallucinate unsupported features, incorrect configuration parameters, or insecure integration patterns; can leak sensitive info if prompts include customer data; may produce plausible but wrong performance interpretations. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation over approved docs, redaction/DLP, template-based outputs, mandatory human review, and automated linting/schema validation of generated configs.
column19: Rules/compatibility matrices can become outdated, causing false passes/fails; anomaly detection may miss novel failure modes or over-flag noise; brittle scripts can break across versions. Mitigations: versioned rule sets, continuous updates tied to release process, monitoring for drift, and fallback to manual verification.
column20: Toolchain coupling (LLM outputs feeding agents/scripts) can propagate errors quickly; inconsistent sources of truth (docs vs. deployed versions) can cause misalignment; data governance gaps across systems increase leakage risk. Mitigations: artifact versioning, environment tagging, contract tests, staged pipelines (generate->validate->apply), centralized secrets management, and clear RACI for approvals.
column22: Orchestrate PoC execution plan and task sequencing. Automate environment provisioning workflows (with guardrails). Continuous integration validation loop|Draft PoC plan narrative and success criteria aligned to customer scenario. Generate configuration snippets and integration scaffolding. Summarize test results and produce executive-ready readouts|Customer scenario discovery, scope negotiation, and risk acceptance. Security/compliance approvals and handling of sensitive data. Hands-on troubleshooting for novel edge cases and product gaps|Performance benchmarking and anomaly detection on PoC telemetry. Rules-based integration pre-checks. Test case management and coverage tracking
column23: Creates and maintains a multi-step PoC runbook (milestones, dependencies, owners), adapts the plan as blockers arise, and coordinates tool-driven actions (ticket creation, environment provisioning requests, test runs) with human approvals at gates. Uses workflow agents to trigger IaC pipelines, request sandbox accounts, rotate credentials, and configure baseline services; pauses for approvals when privileged actions or customer systems are involved. Runs iterative integration checks (API connectivity, auth flows, data mappings), monitors logs/metrics, and proposes fixes or next experiments based on observed failures.|Generates a customer-facing PoC document: objectives, scope, assumptions, acceptance criteria, demo script, and FAQ; tailors language to industry and stakeholder personas while referencing provided product capabilities. Produces example configs (YAML/JSON), API request templates, sample payloads, and glue-code stubs based on product docs/SDKs supplied; accelerates setup while requiring validation before use. Turns logs, benchmark outputs, and notes into concise findings: what worked, gaps, risks, and recommended next steps; highlights evidence and links to artifacts.|Requires domain judgment to choose the right scenario, define what 'good' means, manage expectations, and decide trade-offs (time vs. fidelity) with customer stakeholders. Senior Sales Engineer expertise is needed to interpret ambiguous failures, coordinate with product/engineering, and decide whether to implement workarounds or escalate roadmap issues.|Applies deterministic scripts/ML to compute latency/throughput/error rates, detect regressions, and flag outliers across runs; produces repeatable metrics dashboards. column8: Respond to technical questions and complete security, architecture, and compliance questionnaires by coordinating inputs from engineering and governance teams.
column15: The task mixes structured form-filling with nuanced, high-liability security representations. Generative AI is well-suited to drafting and harmonizing narrative answers when grounded in approved sources, but requires verification to avoid hallucinations or scope creep. Agentic AI adds value by coordinating multi-party inputs, tracking progress, and assembling evidence packages, reducing cycle time and missed follow-ups; however, autonomy must be constrained by approval gates. Traditional AI is effective for deterministic classification, template matching, and validation against control matrices, improving consistency and completeness. Humans remain essential for final accountability, interpreting ambiguous questions, handling exceptions, and making risk-based disclosure decisions. Assumptions: the organization has an approved answer library, governed document repositories, and access controls enabling retrieval; without these, AI contributions drop and human effort rises.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) any statement that could be construed as a contractual security/compliance commitment (e.g., certifications, breach notification timelines, data residency), (2) scoping to the specific product edition, deployment model, and customer environment, (3) approving disclosures of sensitive artifacts (SOC reports, pen test details) and redactions, (4) resolving conflicts between SME inputs or between marketing claims and actual controls, and (5) handling edge cases such as novel regulatory regimes, customer-specific addenda, or incidents/roadmap questions. Humans must set policy constraints for agentic routing/escalation and provide final sign-off before submission.
column17: Autonomous actions (sending requests, sharing documents) could violate least-privilege or disclose restricted evidence; may spam stakeholders or miss nuanced prioritization; emergent behavior if goals are underspecified. Mitigations: role-based permissions, allowlists for repositories/recipients, approval checkpoints for external sharing, rate limits, and transparent activity logs.
column18: May hallucinate controls/certifications, overgeneralize from similar products, or produce overly confident language; could leak sensitive data if prompts include restricted artifacts. Mitigations: retrieval-only from approved sources, citation requirements, red-team prompts, restricted context windows, DLP, and mandatory human review for all externally shared answers.
column19: Rule sets/control mappings can become outdated as product/security posture changes; brittle classification may misroute questions or apply wrong standard answers. Mitigations: versioned control matrix ownership, periodic audits, feedback loops from completed questionnaires, and monitoring of misclassification rates.
column20: RAG pipelines may retrieve stale/incorrect documents; inconsistent source-of-truth across systems can create contradictions; handoffs between agentic orchestration and GenAI drafting can lose context or citations. Mitigations: single governed knowledge base, document versioning, end-to-end provenance (answer-to-evidence links), automated consistency checks, and clear human approval workflow.

Summarize SME inputs and reconcile into consistent narrative. Resolve ambiguous or novel customer requirements with SMEs|Auto-classify questions and map to standard answer library. Completeness and consistency checks

Condenses long engineering/governance replies, highlights conflicts, and proposes harmonized wording while preserving technical accuracy and compliance constraints. Rewrites answers to match customer templates, removes ambiguity, expands acronyms, and ensures consistent terminology (e.g., encryption at rest/in transit, key management) without changing meaning.|Senior Sales Engineer and governance must ensure answers are truthful, scoped correctly to the product/tenant, and legally/commercially acceptable; humans are accountable for representations to customers. When questionnaires ask non-standard items (e.g., specific regional regs, bespoke encryption/KMS, on-prem constraints), humans negotiate interpretations, obtain authoritative decisions, and document exceptions.|Uses rules/ML classifiers to detect question type (data retention, access control, incident response) and suggest canonical responses and required evidence fields. Deterministically validates required fields, detects missing attachments, checks numeric/date formats, and flags contradictions against a structured control matrix (e.g., ISO/SOC2 control mappings).

column8: Create and maintain scalable sales enablement assets such as demo scripts, reference architectures, and competitive positioning to improve team efficiency.
column15: The task mixes content creation (well-suited to Generative AI), ongoing maintenance and coordination (best handled by Agentic AI), and structured governance/measurement (Traditional AI). However, enablement assets directly affect revenue outcomes and can create legal/brand/technical risk, so humans must set strategy, validate technical truth, and approve competitive claims. Assumptions: the org has an approved knowledge base (product docs, release notes, win/loss notes) accessible via secure connectors; publishing occurs in a controlled enablement system (e.g., LMS/wiki/CRM). Under these constraints, GenAI can produce high-volume drafts and variants (35%), Agentic AI can keep assets current via monitoring and workflow automation (20%), Traditional AI can enforce templates and measure adoption (10%), while humans provide strategic direction, SME validation, and final approvals (35%).
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) factual verification of product capabilities, roadmap statements, and competitor comparisons; (2) legal/compliance review of claims, trademarks, and regulated-industry guidance; (3) strategic positioning decisions (what to emphasize/de-emphasize by segment) and handling ambiguous trade-offs; (4) customer-safety checks for reference architectures (security, data residency, failure modes) and ensuring guidance matches supported configurations; (5) edge cases such as rapidly changing competitor moves, sensitive deal-specific intel, or conflicting internal sources. Humans should approve any externally shareable asset, any competitive claim without strong citations, and any architecture intended for regulated customers.
column17: Over-automation could publish unreviewed updates, create noisy tickets, or pull from untrusted sources; autonomous actions may be hard to audit; misconfigured permissions could expose sensitive content. Mitigations: least-privilege connectors, human-in-the-loop approvals for publish steps, allowlists of sources, rate limits, and full audit trails with rollback/versioning.
column18: Hallucinated features/benchmarks, outdated or uncited competitive claims, tone/brand inconsistency, leakage of confidential deal intel if prompts include sensitive data, and copyright/trademark misuse when paraphrasing competitor materials. Mitigations: retrieval with citations, restricted data scopes, red-teaming for claims, mandatory human approval gates, and style/terminology guides.
column19: Brittle rules can reject valid content or miss nuanced issues; classifiers may mis-tag assets leading to poor discoverability; analytics can be misleading due to attribution bias (views = impact). Mitigations: periodic rule tuning, human spot checks, feedback loops from users, and combining telemetry with qualitative win/loss reviews.
column20: RAG pipelines can surface stale docs; inconsistent taxonomy across systems can break orchestration; conflicting outputs (GenAI draft vs rules engine) can stall workflows; security/compliance gaps across connectors. Mitigations: unified taxonomy, source-of-truth governance, automated freshness checks, staged environments, and end-to-end access control with DLP and logging.

Creates structured architecture write-ups (components, data flows, security, sizing assumptions) and diagram specifications (e.g., Mermaid/PlantUML) from solution requirements and constraints. Aggregates enablement platform telemetry (views, attach rates, influence on opportunities) and runs statistical models to identify high-performing assets and gaps. Applies similarity search and deterministic rules to detect near-duplicate assets, outdated versions, and broken links; triggers remediation tickets.

column8: Provide feedback to product and engineering on user experience gaps, feature requests, and recurring customer objections to influence roadmap priorities.
column15: The task mixes qualitative synthesis and stakeholder influence (high human judgment) with scalable collection, structuring, and quantification (AI-suitable). Generative AI is best for summarizing and drafting structured narratives/PRD fragments from unstructured field inputs, but must be grounded with citations to avoid hallucinations. Traditional AI is reliable for taxonomy tagging, deduplication, and impact/trend analytics on structured data. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating end-to-end intake and workflow routing across CRM/support/Jira, reducing manual overhead and ensuring follow-through. Humans remain essential for interpreting nuanced objections, validating business impact, and making/negotiating roadmap trade-offs. Assumptions: access to call transcripts/CRM notes is permitted; product tooling supports APIs; a shared taxonomy exists or can be created; governance requires human approval before items influence roadmap decisions.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) prioritization decisions that trade off revenue, strategy, and engineering constraints; (2) validating that summarized objections/requests accurately reflect customer intent and environment specifics (enterprise security, deployment topology, integrations); (3) approving any statements that could be construed as roadmap commitments; (4) handling sensitive accounts, regulated industries, and confidentiality constraints (PII, customer names, contract terms); (5) resolving conflicts when feedback is contradictory across segments; and (6) escalation and relationship management with PM/engineering leadership. Edge cases: small-sample but high-stakes objections from marquee accounts; feedback driven by misconfiguration/training gaps rather than product gaps; competitor FUD requiring careful framing.
column17: Risks: autonomous creation of excessive/low-quality tickets (noise); incorrect routing/priority leading to stakeholder fatigue; unintended actions across systems (permissions, data writes); feedback loops that reinforce wrong themes; limited transparency in why it escalated items. Mitigations: guardrails (rate limits, approval gates for ticket creation), role-based access, audit logs, sandbox testing, explicit escalation criteria, and periodic human calibration of routing rules.
column18: Risks: hallucinating root causes or inventing customer quotes; over-generalizing from limited examples; leaking sensitive data when summarizing; tone mismatch that alienates engineering; bias toward more verbose customers. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded summarization with source links; require verbatim quote extraction; redaction/PII filters; human review for high-impact items; prompt templates enforcing uncertainty labels and confidence.
column19: Risks: brittle taxonomies and misclassification when product changes; trend metrics misleading due to data quality issues (missing ARR, inconsistent tagging); similarity/dedup errors merging distinct issues; fairness concerns if certain segments are underrepresented in data. Mitigations: continuous evaluation with labeled samples, drift monitoring, taxonomy governance, data validation checks, and manual override workflows.
column20: Risks: inconsistent identifiers across CRM/support/Jira causing wrong linkage; compounding errors (misclassification feeding GenAI narratives); security/privacy exposure via connectors; conflicting priorities between analytics and agentic routing; unclear ownership of the feedback backlog. Mitigations: master data management for account/opportunity IDs, end-to-end provenance, least-privilege connectors, staged rollout, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and clear RACI for backlog ownership.

column8: Support deal execution by estimating effort, defining implementation approaches, and contributing to proposals and statements of work with clear assumptions and risks.
column15: This task is high-stakes and contract-adjacent: AI can accelerate drafting, structuring, and baseline estimation, but humans must own final commitments. Mandatory human approvals should gate: estimate sign-off, risk/assumption register, and final SOW/proposal submission.
column17: Autonomous orchestration could spam stakeholders, pull wrong CRM fields, or update documents incorrectly; may take unintended actions (e.g., sending drafts externally) if permissions are misconfigured. column19: Estimation models can be biased by outdated historical data, underrepresent new product features, or fail on atypical deals; rule checks can be brittle and miss nuanced legal issues. column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs. Traditional AI estimate), data drift between CRM and documents, and unclear source-of-truth can cause inconsistencies in SOWs. column8: Execute the User Experience solutions architecture roadmap by translating strategic priorities into scoped initiatives, milestones, and measurable outcomes aligned to Sales needs.
column15: This roadmap-execution task mixes strategic judgment, cross-functional alignment, and accountable decision-making (human-led) with heavy synthesis, documentation, and structured analysis (AI-assisted). Generative AI is best for turning messy strategic inputs into coherent initiative drafts, charters, and stakeholder narratives, accelerating scoping and communication. Traditional AI is well-suited for explainable prioritization scoring and KPI/dashboard computation where inputs are structured and auditability matters. Agentic AI can automate the operational glue-keeping Jira/Confluence artifacts in sync, monitoring signals, and proposing adjustments-but should remain bounded by policies and require approvals for scope/prioritization changes. Assumptions: access to up-to-date Sales strategy, pipeline/VOC data, and delivery metrics via governed connectors; clear taxonomy for initiatives/metrics; and an established UX architecture governance process. Constraints: sensitive customer and revenue data, frequent context shifts, and the need for executive buy-in limit full automation.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final prioritization and trade-offs when initiatives compete for scarce engineering/design capacity; (2) commitments that affect customer expectations, contractual obligations, or revenue forecasts; (3) architecture guardrails (accessibility, privacy, security, brand, platform constraints) and acceptance of technical/UX debt; (4) resolving ambiguous or conflicting stakeholder inputs and managing organizational politics; (5) approving KPI definitions and interpreting outcomes where causality is unclear (e.g., pipeline changes driven by seasonality). Mandatory approvals should gate any agentic action that changes scope, dates, resourcing, or externally communicated milestones. Edge cases: sudden Sales motion changes (new pricing/packaging), regulatory constraints, major incident/postmortem learnings, or strategic pivots require human-led replanning.
column17: Risks: unintended changes to roadmaps/tickets, cascading updates across tools, or spamming stakeholders; optimizing for activity over value; brittle behavior when tools/APIs change; limited transparency in why it re-prioritized. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval workflows, sandbox/dry-run mode, action logs, rate limits, policy constraints (cannot change dates without approval), and continuous evaluation on representative scenarios.
column18: Risks: hallucinated facts about Sales priorities or metrics; overconfident scope statements; leakage of sensitive pipeline/customer data in prompts; biased framing that over-optimizes for certain segments; inconsistent terminology across artifacts. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with governed sources, citation requirements, redaction/DLP, prompt templates with required fields (assumptions/non-goals), and human review for executive-facing outputs.
column19: Risks: scoring models encode outdated heuristics; garbage-in/garbage-out from inconsistent effort estimates; dashboards incentivize vanity metrics; anomaly detection false positives/negatives. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, clear data definitions, model cards for scoring logic, sensitivity analysis, and governance reviews of KPI selection.
column20: Risks: inconsistent source-of-truth between GenAI drafts, agentic updates, and BI dashboards; feedback loops where AI-generated metrics drive AI-driven reprioritization; security gaps across connectors; versioning conflicts. Mitigations: single authoritative roadmap repository, schema/ontology for initiatives and metrics, human approval gates, end-to-end audit trails, automated reconciliation checks, and staged rollout with monitoring.
