column8: Manage day-to-day UX solution workflows by tracking intake, prioritizing requests, coordinating handoffs, and ensuring timely delivery across multiple concurrent engagements.
column15: The task mixes structured operations (intake logging, SLA tracking, routing) with high-context prioritization and stakeholder management typical for a Grade 7/8 Solutions Architect supporting Sales UX engagements. Traditional AI is best for deterministic SLA calculations, routing, and dashboards. Generative AI accelerates unstructured-text heavy work (summarizing requests, drafting tickets, comms, and retrieving playbooks) but must be constrained due to hallucination/privacy risks. Agentic AI can automate multi-step workflow actions (creating tickets, updating statuses, scheduling handoffs, sending reminders) and propose reprioritizations, yet should operate under approval gates for commitment changes. Humans remain essential for final prioritization, negotiation, and quality/compliance sign-off. Assumptions: access to integrated tooling (Jira/ADO/ServiceNow, Teams/Slack, CRM tags), labeled metadata for requests, and an internal knowledge base for RAG; without these, AI contributions drop and human share increases.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) committing dates/scope to clients and sales leadership, (2) resolving conflicting priorities across concurrent engagements, (3) interpreting ambiguous or politically sensitive requests, (4) approving external communications and any changes that affect contractual SLAs, pricing, or legal/brand/accessibility compliance, and (5) handling edge cases like escalations, security/privacy constraints on client data, and novel UX problems where playbooks don't apply. Humans should also review agentic actions that reassign owners, change priorities, or close tickets, and validate GenAI-generated summaries before they become system-of-record.
column17: Over-automation may re-prioritize or reassign work inappropriately, spam stakeholders, or create cascading workflow errors via tool integrations; emergent behavior under ambiguous goals; limited auditability of decisions. Mitigations: explicit guardrails (no client commitments), approval gates for priority/date changes, rate limits, sandbox testing, comprehensive logging, and rollback mechanisms.
column18: Hallucinated requirements or incorrect summaries can propagate into tickets and mis-scope work; leakage of client confidential data if prompts/logs are not governed; tone/claims in drafted comms may create contractual risk. Mitigations: RAG with citations, redaction/DLP, restricted templates, human review for external outputs, and grounding to system-of-record fields.
column19: Brittle rules can misroute requests when taxonomy changes; forecasting models may be biased by past throughput and fail during demand shocks; false alerts can cause fatigue. Mitigations: periodic rule/model review, monitoring drift, configurable thresholds, and human override.
column20: Inconsistent data across CRM/ticketing/knowledge bases can cause conflicting priorities; identity/permission mismatches may expose data; feedback loops where GenAI text becomes training/knowledge without validation. Mitigations: master data governance, least-privilege access, unified taxonomy, provenance tracking, and validation workflows before updates become authoritative.

Continuously recomputes suggested priorities using signals (SLA, revenue impact tags, effort, blockers), runs what-if scenarios, and proposes updated sprint/engagement plans for human approval.|Extracts intent, scope, constraints, and acceptance criteria from emails/forms/meeting notes; drafts concise tickets and clarifying questions; flags missing info and potential UX risks. Drafts handoff notes, daily/weekly status updates, RAID logs, and meeting agendas/minutes tailored to sales/UX/engineering audiences; maintains consistent tone and reduces manual writing. Uses RAG over internal playbooks to suggest relevant UX patterns, prior proposals, and checklists to speed triage and handoffs; cites sources when available.|Requires contextual judgment on revenue impact, relationship risk, strategic alignment, and negotiation; accountable for commitments and escalation handling. Reviews UX outputs for correctness, accessibility, brand/legal constraints, and client-specific sensitivities; approves external-facing communications and commitments.|Rule-based automation to compute due dates, aging, SLA breaches; triggers alerts; routes requests by region/product/engagement type using deterministic logic for predictability and auditability. column8: Analyze product, customer, and sales process data to identify experience gaps, quantify impact, and recommend architecture decisions that improve usability and adoption.
column15: The task mixes structured analytics (best handled by Traditional AI) with unstructured synthesis and communication (GenAI) and multi-system workflow execution (Agentic AI). However, selecting the right success metrics, interpreting causality in a sales context, and making accountable architecture decisions require human expertise and governance. Assumptions: data access is available via approved connectors; telemetry/CRM schemas are sufficiently stable; organization permits AI-assisted analysis under privacy controls. Constraints: high stakes (customer impact, revenue) and compliance needs prevent full automation.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) KPI/metric definition and segmentation choices that reflect sales motions and product strategy; (2) validating that detected 'gaps' are real and actionable (triangulating with customer interviews, deal context, roadmap); (3) approving architecture decisions affecting security, privacy, cost, and contractual obligations; (4) handling edge cases like sparse data for strategic accounts, conflicting signals across regions, or when model outputs suggest actions that could disadvantage protected groups or violate policy.
column17: Autonomous actions could pull/export data beyond least-privilege, run costly queries, or spam stakeholders with low-quality backlog items; emergent behavior in iterative loops may optimize proxy metrics. Mitigate with scoped permissions, rate limits, approval gates for external actions, and audit logs.
column18: Hallucinated causal claims or incorrect summaries of customer feedback; leakage of sensitive customer/deal data in prompts; overconfident narratives that mask uncertainty. Mitigate with retrieval from approved sources, citation requirements, redaction, and human review of all externally shared artifacts.
column19: Biased or brittle models due to skewed sales data, missing telemetry, or changing processes; false attribution (correlation vs causation); model drift as product and sales stages evolve. Mitigate with monitoring, periodic retraining, holdout validation, and causal sensitivity analyses.
column20: Inconsistent definitions across systems (account IDs, stages) can cascade into wrong recommendations; GenAI may misinterpret model outputs; agentic orchestration may chain errors. Mitigate with a shared metrics layer/semantic model, automated data quality checks, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and end-to-end testing of workflows.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end data pull and refresh across systems (CRM, product telemetry, support tickets, enablement tools). Iterative hypothesis testing loop for experience gaps. Generate and maintain a prioritized backlog of architecture recommendations with owners and next actions|Synthesize qualitative signals into structured themes (VOC, call notes, win/loss, support cases). Draft narrative insights and executive-ready artifacts. Translate analytical results into solution options and trade-offs|Define business context, success metrics, and acceptable evidence thresholds. Validate findings with stakeholders and customer reality. Make final architecture decisions and secure approvals (risk, security, compliance)|Data cleaning, entity resolution, and feature engineering on structured datasets. Quantify impact via statistical analysis and predictive models. Detect anomalies and friction points in funnels and journeys
column23: Plans and executes multi-step workflows: authenticates to approved sources, schedules extracts, monitors failures, and adapts queries/joins based on schema changes; escalates when access/quality issues occur. Autonomously proposes candidate friction points (e.g., drop-offs in trial-to-paid, time-to-first-value), runs successive analyses, and refines hypotheses based on results while keeping an audit trail for review. Transforms findings into trackable items (e.g., identity/SSO changes, onboarding flows, reference architectures), routes to stakeholders, and updates status based on new data and feedback.|Summarizes and clusters unstructured text, extracts pain points, personas, and journey stages; produces consistent taxonomy proposals for human validation. Creates first drafts of experience-gap reports, architecture decision records (ADRs), and customer-facing recommendations; tailors messaging to sales, product, and engineering audiences. Given metrics and constraints, generates alternative architectures, pros/cons, assumptions, and adoption impact narratives; flags where evidence is weak or missing.|Requires domain judgment on what 'adoption' means per product/segment, which KPIs matter for sales motions, and what level of statistical confidence is decision-worthy. Humans conduct/interpret customer conversations, confirm causality vs correlation, and reconcile conflicting signals across sales, product, and support. Applies regression/causal inference approximations, propensity scoring, churn/expansion prediction, and uplift modeling to estimate revenue/adoption impact of identified gaps. Uses anomaly detection, segmentation, and clustering on event streams and CRM stage transitions to surface drop-offs, long cycle times, and outlier behaviors.

column8: Partner with internal teams and external vendors to align requirements, validate feasibility, and ensure proposed solutions integrate cleanly with existing platforms and processes.
column15: This Solutions Architect task is inherently socio-technical: aligning requirements and vendor/internal expectations depends on negotiation, accountability, and risk acceptance (human-led). Generative AI is strong for synthesizing unstructured inputs (meetings, emails) into clear artifacts and drafting communications and trade-off narratives, accelerating alignment. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the multi-party workflow (routing, reminders, evidence gathering, playbook execution) but must be constrained to approved actions and human approvals. Traditional AI is best for deterministic governance (checklists, policy conformance) and structured catalog matching/risk scoring. Assumptions: access to transcripts/docs, integrated tooling (Jira/Confluence/ServiceNow/CMDB), and a controlled action framework for agentic automation; without these, AI contributions shift toward GenAI-only drafting and manual workflow management.
column16: Humans must: (1) resolve conflicting requirements and priorities, especially when sales commitments, delivery capacity, and vendor constraints collide; (2) approve feasibility conclusions, architecture choices, and any deviations from standards; (3) validate security/privacy/compliance interpretations and sign off on data flows, identity, and regulatory impacts; (4) manage vendor negotiations and contractual commitments (SLAs, liability, support); (5) handle edge cases like ambiguous requirements, incomplete vendor documentation, or politically sensitive stakeholder disagreements. Human oversight is essential at decision gates (requirements baseline, feasibility sign-off, integration pattern selection, go/no-go).
column17: Autonomous actions can create unintended changes (e.g., opening tickets, requesting access, contacting vendors) or spam stakeholders; planning errors can propagate across systems; limited transparency in why it chose steps. Mitigations: least-privilege credentials, allowlisted actions, human-in-the-loop approvals for external outreach and system changes, audit logs, rate limits, and sandbox-first execution.
column18: May hallucinate requirements, misattribute decisions, or overstate feasibility; can leak sensitive data if prompts include confidential vendor/customer info; may produce persuasive but incorrect trade-offs. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations, redaction/DLP, structured templates, and mandatory human review for decision logs and external communications.
column19: Rules can become outdated as platforms/processes evolve; rigid checks may block valid exceptions; ML risk scores can be misleading if trained on biased/limited historical integrations. Mitigations: governance ownership for rule updates, exception workflows, periodic model recalibration, and explainability with thresholds tuned by architects.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs. rule-based checks), toolchain brittleness (API changes), identity/access misconfiguration, and provenance gaps (no traceability from recommendation to source). Mitigations: single source of truth (requirements repository), shared ontology, end-to-end audit trail with citations, automated consistency checks, and clear decision gates where humans reconcile discrepancies.
column22: Orchestrate cross-team requirement intake workflow (routing, reminders, status tracking). Automate evidence collection for feasibility (pull logs, configs, API specs, usage metrics). Compatibility and dependency matching against known catalogs. Risk scoring and anomaly detection on integration metrics

Uses classical models to flag unusual latency/error-rate patterns in sandbox tests or historical integrations; supports feasibility assessment with quantitative signals.

column8: Produce and maintain architecture artifacts including solution designs, integration diagrams, decision logs, and runbooks to support consistent delivery and knowledge transfer.
column15: The task mixes high-volume documentation work with high-stakes architectural judgment. Generative AI is well-suited to drafting and standardizing narrative artifacts, ADRs, and diagram-as-code text from structured inputs, reducing time spent on writing and formatting. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the end-to-end artifact lifecycle (triggered updates, routing reviews, publishing, and reminders) and by proactively detecting when artifacts should be refreshed based on change signals. Traditional AI is best for deterministic governance: template compliance, link/section checks, metadata extraction, indexing, and drift detection via rules and diffs. Humans remain essential for requirements elicitation, final decisions, and approvals where accountability, customer context, and compliance interpretation matter. Assumptions: artifacts are maintained in accessible systems with APIs; organization adopts templates/ADRs; sensitive data access is controlled; and diagramming can be expressed as text (Mermaid/PlantUML) for automation. Constraints: customer-specific nuances, incomplete inputs, and rapidly changing solution scope limit full automation.
column16: Human oversight is essential at (1) decision points with material impact-security posture, data residency, cost/performance trade-offs, vendor selection, and risk acceptance; (2) stakeholder alignment-confirming requirements, resolving conflicting inputs, and ensuring the documented architecture matches what was sold and what will be delivered; (3) compliance and contractual obligations-interpreting policies, approving exceptions, and ensuring no sensitive/customer data is inappropriately included in artifacts; (4) final QA for customer-facing deliverables-verifying diagrams and runbooks reflect reality and are actionable. Edge cases requiring humans: novel architectures with limited precedent, ambiguous integration boundaries, regulated workloads, and incidents where runbook steps could cause outages if wrong.
column17: Over-automation could publish unapproved artifacts, spam stakeholders, or create excessive tickets; autonomous actions may mis-handle permissions or operate on wrong repositories; opaque decisioning in prioritizing updates. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates, audit logs, sandbox/dry-run modes, rate limits, and clear escalation policies.
column18: Hallucinated components/interfaces, incorrect technical claims, or overconfident ADR rationales; context drift when inputs are partial; leakage of sensitive customer data if prompts include confidential details; inconsistent terminology across artifacts. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, strict templates, citation/link requirements, redaction, and mandatory human review for externally shared/high-risk docs.
column19: Brittle rules may flag false positives/negatives as standards evolve; metadata extraction errors can mis-tag owners/systems; drift detection may miss semantic changes not captured by heuristics. Mitigations: versioned rule sets, periodic calibration, human override workflows, and monitoring precision/recall of checks.
column20: Toolchain fragmentation (Confluence/Git/CMDB/diagram tools) can cause inconsistent sources of truth; conflicting outputs between GenAI drafts and rule-based validators; identity/permission mismatches across systems; provenance issues (which artifact is authoritative). Mitigations: define a single source of truth per artifact type, enforce workflow orchestration with state, use signed approvals, maintain provenance metadata, and implement automated validation gates before publish.

Pulls relevant alerts, dashboards, known errors, and prior incident timelines; composes stepwise procedures and validation checks; links to scripts and owners; schedules periodic runbook validation drills.|Transforms meeting notes, requirements, and constraints into structured design docs (context, options, trade-offs, NFRs, risks) and ADR templates; summarizes discussions and produces consistent language for knowledge transfer. Creates concise executive summaries, onboarding guides, and FAQs from multiple artifacts; highlights key dependencies, assumptions, and operational considerations for new team members.|Requires accountable judgment on trade-offs, cost, security, compliance, and customer context; ensures alignment with enterprise strategy and validates that artifacts reflect the intended solution. Involves negotiation, domain expertise, and contextual understanding of customer constraints; clarifies non-functional requirements and acceptance criteria that AI cannot reliably infer. Human oversight needed for regulatory interpretation, data classification, and risk acceptance; approves deviations from standards and ensures contractual obligations are met.|Applies deterministic rules/linters to ensure required sections exist (security, data retention, SLAs), naming conventions, link integrity, and template compliance; flags missing approvals and outdated references. Uses NLP-lite/classical ML for entity extraction (systems, APIs, owners), tagging, and classification; updates CMDB/architecture catalog entries and enables retrieval via faceted search. Compares versions of diagrams/specs to deployed configs or interface registries; uses heuristics to flag unexpected changes (new endpoints, permission changes) and raises alerts.

column8: Maintain documentation and audit readiness by ensuring approvals, traceability, and evidence are captured for key decisions, changes, and releases in governed repositories.
column15: This task mixes (a) high-accountability approvals and risk decisions (human-led) with (b) repeatable evidence capture and compliance checks (automation-friendly). Traditional AI is best for deterministic policy validation and traceability graph checks with explainable outputs. Generative AI accelerates drafting ADRs/change/release narratives and standardizing language, but requires grounding and review due to hallucination risk. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step workflows across governed repositories and continuously driving remediation, yet must operate under tight guardrails to avoid unauthorized changes. Assumptions: governed repositories and systems of record exist (e.g., Confluence/SharePoint + Git + ticketing), APIs/webhooks are available, and controls are codified into checklists/rules. Constraints: approvals and risk acceptance remain with accountable humans; some evidence (e.g., verbal approvals) must be captured via human attestation.
column16: Humans must (1) provide legally/organizationally binding approvals for key decisions, changes, and releases; (2) adjudicate exceptions (emergency fixes, retroactive approvals, SoD conflicts) and document risk acceptance; (3) verify factual accuracy of GenAI-produced summaries against source artifacts, especially for customer-impacting or regulated workloads; (4) define control requirements, approver matrices, and retention policies, and respond to auditor inquiries. Human oversight is essential at control boundaries: before finalizing an ADR/change record, before marking an item audit-ready, and before any automated agent writes to governed repositories or closes remediation tickets.
column17: Autonomous actions could create/modify records incorrectly, spam stakeholders, or bypass governance if misconfigured; emergent behavior in complex workflows; privilege misuse if given broad access. Mitigations: least-privilege service accounts, allowlisted actions, approval gates for write operations, sandbox testing, rate limits, and full audit logs/rollback.
column18: May hallucinate rationale, misattribute approvals, or omit critical evidence; could leak sensitive data if prompts include regulated/customer info; may produce plausible but non-compliant wording. Mitigations: retrieval with citations to source artifacts, constrained templates, redaction/DLP, human review gates for high-risk items, and logging of prompts/outputs.
column19: Rules can become outdated as policies/tools change; brittle handling of edge cases (emergency changes, merged repos, atypical release trains) leading to false positives/negatives. Mitigations: version-controlled rules, periodic control owner review, exception workflows, and monitoring precision/recall of findings.
column20: Inconsistent identifiers across tools can break traceability; race conditions between CI/CD events and documentation updates; conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs rule-engine findings); data residency and retention mismatches across repositories. Mitigations: canonical ID strategy, event-driven architecture with idempotency, single source of truth for control status, reconciliation jobs, and unified audit logging across systems.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end evidence capture workflow across tools (e.g., Jira/Azure DevOps, Git, CI/CD, Confluence/SharePoint, ServiceNow). Continuously check audit readiness and drive remediation|Draft and update decision/change/release records (ADRs, change summaries, release notes) from source artifacts. Normalize and enrich documentation metadata and narratives|Make and approve key decisions and exceptions (architecture, risk acceptance, change approvals). Define/maintain governance controls, templates, and audit narratives|Validate completeness and policy compliance using deterministic rules. Traceability graph construction and anomaly detection on structured links
column23: Monitors events (PR opened/merged, pipeline run, change request created/approved, release tagged) and triggers the right actions: request missing approvals, open documentation tasks, collect artifacts, and file them into governed repositories with correct metadata and retention labels. Runs scheduled audits against a control checklist (e.g., required approvers, segregation of duties, linkage between requirement->design->code->test->release) and automatically creates/assigns remediation tickets, escalates overdue items, and compiles status dashboards.|Summarizes PR discussions, meeting notes, architecture diagrams, and ticket history into standardized templates; proposes traceability links and highlights missing fields/evidence for human confirmation. Suggests consistent terminology, control references, and rationale statements; rewrites content to match governance standards (e.g., ISO/SOC2 wording) while preserving factual grounding via citations to source artifacts.|Accountable for correctness, business risk, and compliance; must validate that generated summaries reflect intent and that approvals meet governance/SoD requirements, especially for high-risk releases. Interprets auditor expectations, updates control mappings, and resolves ambiguous cases (e.g., emergency changes, third-party constraints) that require contextual judgment and organizational authority.|Applies rule engines/checklists to ensure required fields, approver roles, timestamps, and repository locations are present; enforces naming conventions, mandatory links, and retention tags; produces explainable pass/fail results. Builds requirement->ticket->commit->build->test->release link graphs from IDs and metadata; flags broken links, duplicate records, unusual approval patterns, or missing segregation-of-duties using classical analytics/heuristics.

column8: Proactively identify delivery risks, dependencies, and constraints, then propose mitigation plans and process improvements to protect quality, scope, and timelines.
column15: The task mixes structured signal detection (well-suited to Traditional AI), continuous cross-tool coordination (best handled by Agentic AI), and synthesis/ideation for mitigations and communications (strong fit for Generative AI). However, in a Sales Solutions Architecture context at Professional grade, the highest-value work is judgment-heavy: assessing customer impact, contractual/compliance implications, and negotiating trade-offs-requiring human ownership. Assumptions: access to delivery tooling APIs and clean metrics; defined escalation playbooks; data governance for customer information. Constraints: model outputs must be explainable and auditable for customer-facing decisions; agent autonomy should be bounded to avoid unauthorized commitments.
column16: Humans must (1) confirm factual accuracy and materiality of risks before customer escalation, (2) decide trade-offs among scope/timeline/quality and approve mitigation spend or scope changes, (3) handle contractual commitments, pricing/SoW changes, and regulatory/security approvals, and (4) manage sensitive stakeholder dynamics and accountability. Edge cases needing human judgment include ambiguous root causes (e.g., performance vs. requirements), conflicting metrics across tools, partner/vendor disputes, and situations where mitigation implies architectural rework or security exceptions.
column17: Autonomous actions could spam stakeholders, create incorrect tickets, escalate prematurely, or change statuses without context; emergent behavior from tool integrations may cause unintended workflow loops. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for escalations/customer comms, rate limits, sandbox testing, clear playbooks, and comprehensive logging/rollback.
column18: May hallucinate root causes, overstate confidence, or propose mitigations that are infeasible given the customer's stack/contract; risk of leaking sensitive customer data in prompts/outputs; may produce persuasive but incorrect narratives. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations, redaction/DLP, constrained templates, and mandatory human review for external communications and commitments.
column19: Brittle rules and models can miss novel risks (new tech, new delivery model) or generate false positives due to data quality issues; predictive models may not generalize across projects. Mitigations: periodic recalibration, monitoring precision/recall, human-in-the-loop threshold tuning, and data validation checks.
column20: Conflicting outputs (e.g., GenAI narrative vs. metric-based score), feedback loops where agent actions alter metrics, and provenance gaps that reduce auditability. Mitigations: single source of truth for risk register, explicit confidence/provenance metadata, orchestration layer with conflict resolution, and governance for prompt/tool changes.
column22: Continuously monitor delivery signals across tools and trigger risk workflows. Orchestrate dependency follow-ups and evidence collection|Synthesize risk narratives and stakeholder-ready summaries. Generate mitigation options and process improvement proposals. Scenario analysis and what-if exploration support|Validate risk materiality, prioritize trade-offs, and make final recommendations. Negotiate and align mitigation plans with stakeholders (customer, delivery, product, partners). Approve changes impacting contract, compliance, security, or architecture|Quantitative risk scoring and trend/anomaly detection on delivery metrics. Dependency mapping from structured project data
column23: Connects to Jira/Azure DevOps, CI/CD, incident logs, cloud cost/usage, and comms channels; watches leading indicators (slippage, defect trends, environment instability, vendor delays) and autonomously opens/updates risk items, pings owners, and schedules reviews based on thresholds and context. Creates and assigns action items to dependency owners, requests missing artifacts (HLD/LLD, runbooks, test evidence), tracks responses, and escalates per playbooks; maintains an auditable trail of decisions and status.|Drafts concise risk statements (cause-impact-likelihood), executive summaries, and customer-facing updates from heterogeneous inputs; adapts tone for sales/solutions architecture context and highlights assumptions/unknowns. Uses patterns from prior engagements and best practices to propose mitigation plans (technical, resourcing, sequencing) and process changes (gates, checklists, RACI, definition of done), including pros/cons and prerequisites. Produces structured what-if scenarios (e.g., vendor slip, scope creep, performance risk) and suggests contingency triggers; helps prepare for steering committees and customer Q&A.|Requires domain judgment on customer context, contractual obligations, and acceptable risk; balances scope/time/cost/quality and decides escalation paths. Needs relationship management, persuasion, and accountability setting; handles conflicts, commercial sensitivities, and political constraints. Human sign-off is mandatory for scope changes, security exceptions, regulatory commitments, and architectural decisions with long-term consequences.|Applies deterministic rules and classical ML to compute schedule variance, burn-down health, defect leakage, lead time, change failure rate; flags anomalies and predicts slippage using historical baselines. Builds dependency graphs from Jira epics, milestones, integration points, and release calendars; identifies critical path and single points of failure using graph algorithms.

column8: Conduct solution reviews and quality checks to confirm deliverables meet UX standards, accessibility expectations, and agreed acceptance criteria before release.
column15: The task mixes deterministic checks (best handled by Traditional AI), document-heavy synthesis and communication (best handled by Generative AI), and multi-tool coordination/re-run automation (best handled by Agentic AI). However, UX quality and accessibility readiness include subjective judgments, client-specific expectations, and accountability for contractual acceptance criteria, requiring human oversight and final approval. Assumptions: the org has a design system, documented acceptance criteria in an issue tracker, CI/CD hooks available, and permissioned access to artifacts. Constraints: regulated clients may require human sign-off and evidence retention; some UX/accessibility issues are not machine-detectable. The work split reflects that automation can cover much of the mechanical checking and reporting, while humans retain responsibility for standards-setting, nuanced evaluation, and release decisions.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) setting and updating UX/accessibility standards and severity thresholds per product/client, (2) interpreting ambiguous findings (e.g., contrast exceptions for brand colors, complex widgets, cognitive accessibility), (3) validating real-world assistive technology behavior and usability beyond rule checks, (4) approving waivers/known issues with documented risk acceptance, and (5) final go/no-go decisions tied to contractual acceptance criteria, compliance, and reputational risk. Humans must also handle edge cases: novel UI patterns, incomplete requirements, conflicting stakeholder priorities, and when AI outputs disagree or confidence is low.
column17: Autonomous orchestration can create unintended actions (e.g., closing tickets, blocking releases) or spam stakeholders; may mis-handle permissions or operate on the wrong branch/build. Harder to audit decisions if policies are unclear. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for destructive actions, dry-run mode, comprehensive logging, policy-as-code, and rollback procedures.
column18: May hallucinate acceptance criteria, misread context from partial tickets, or propose incorrect WCAG guidance; can drift from the actual design system and produce plausible but wrong remediation steps. Privacy/IP risk if proprietary designs or client data are sent to external models. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, strict citation requirements, redaction, on-prem/private models, and mandatory human review for any normative guidance.
column19: Rule-based scanners have false positives/negatives and limited coverage (e.g., dynamic content, custom components, cognitive accessibility). Brittle tests can fail due to UI changes, causing noise and alert fatigue. Mitigations: maintain test suites, calibrate severity, add component-level accessibility unit tests, and periodically validate tools against real assistive-tech testing.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI summary vs scanner results), broken traceability links, and workflow deadlocks if agentic orchestration depends on flaky CI signals. Data lineage and evidence retention may be inconsistent across tools, impacting audits. Mitigations: single source of truth for requirements, standardized schemas for findings, confidence scoring, human-in-the-loop escalation rules, and end-to-end monitoring with SLAs for the review pipeline.
column22: Orchestrate end-to-end review workflow across tools (design repo, issue tracker, CI/CD). Continuously monitor changes and enforce release gates|Summarize acceptance criteria and map them to testable checks. Review UX copy, micro-interactions, and consistency against design system guidance. Draft review reports and stakeholder-ready release notes for quality findings|Define/interpret UX standards, accessibility expectations, and risk-based thresholds for release. Perform nuanced UX evaluation and edge-case accessibility review. Make final go/no-go decision and approve exceptions|Run deterministic accessibility and linting checks (WCAG rules, ARIA, contrast, keyboard traps). Execute automated UI regression and acceptance tests. Validate structured acceptance criteria coverage and traceability
column23: Coordinates multi-step review runs: pulls latest build/design artifacts, triggers automated checks, opens/updates tickets, routes items to owners, and re-runs checks on new commits based on defined policies and release gates. Watches PRs/builds for UX/accessibility/acceptance-criteria signals; blocks promotion when thresholds fail; escalates to humans when confidence is low or exceptions are requested.|Ingestes user stories, PRDs, and tickets; extracts acceptance criteria; proposes a checklist and traceability matrix linking criteria to UI screens, components, and test cases. Analyzes UI text and interaction descriptions/screenshots; flags inconsistencies (terminology, tone, error messages) and suggests improved copy aligned to brand and product guidelines. Generates concise QA/UX/accessibility review summaries, prioritizes issues, and drafts remediation guidance and communication for product, engineering, and sales stakeholders.|Requires contextual judgment about user impact, brand, and business risk; sets what 'good enough' means per client/industry and resolves trade-offs (time vs quality). Humans catch issues scanners miss (cognitive load, information architecture, real assistive-tech behavior, complex flows) and validate with lived experience and domain knowledge. Accountability for release readiness, contractual acceptance, and compliance cannot be delegated; humans adjudicate conflicting signals and document rationale.|Uses established rule-based scanners (e.g., axe-core, Lighthouse, pa11y) and static analysis to detect violations with reproducible results and severity scoring. Runs scripted tests (Cypress/Playwright/Selenium), snapshot diffs, and API contract checks; produces pass/fail outputs and trend metrics for release readiness. Applies rules/ML classifiers to ensure each acceptance criterion has linked tests, evidence, and sign-off fields; flags missing mappings or stale artifacts.

column8: Build trusted relationships with sales, product, engineering, and delivery stakeholders to align pre-sales activities with customer goals and internal priorities.
column15: This task is fundamentally relationship- and trust-centric, with accountability for commitments and conflict management resting on the Pre-Sales Consultant. AI can materially reduce coordination overhead and improve clarity: Generative AI accelerates preparation (briefs, comms) and converts unstructured interactions into actionable artifacts; Agentic AI can run the multi-step coordination loop (scheduling, follow-ups, ticket creation) within guardrails; Traditional AI provides reliable, explainable monitoring (dashboards, SLA/risk scoring) and deterministic compliance checks. Assumptions: the organization permits AI access to collaboration tools under strict RBAC; meeting transcripts/notes are available; there are defined templates/policies for pre-sales communications. Constraints: stakeholder politics, ambiguous priorities, and high-stakes promises limit automation; AI outputs require review when they influence commitments or customer-facing narratives.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) any commitment that affects scope, timeline, pricing, or delivery feasibility; (2) interpreting stakeholder intent, emotions, and organizational politics; (3) resolving conflicts and negotiating trade-offs; (4) approving escalations and sensitive messages (exec stakeholders, strained accounts, legal/compliance implications); (5) validating AI-generated summaries/priority inferences to avoid misalignment. Edge cases needing humans: contradictory stakeholder statements, rapidly changing delivery capacity, regulated customers where wording/claims are constrained, and situations where trust is fragile (missed deadlines, prior project failures).
column17: Autonomous scheduling/escalation can annoy stakeholders, create meeting overload, or escalate prematurely; could create/modify CRM/Jira records incorrectly, causing downstream confusion; emergent behavior if goals are mis-specified (optimizing responsiveness over relationship quality). Mitigations: tight permissions, approval gates for external comms and escalations, rate limits, audit logs, simulation/testing in sandbox, clear stop conditions and human-in-the-loop for high-impact actions.
column18: May hallucinate stakeholder positions, misstate technical feasibility, or produce overly confident language that damages trust; tone mismatch can inflame conflict; risk of leaking confidential deal/customer info if prompts or logs are mishandled. Mitigations: retrieval from approved sources, citation/quote linking to notes, red-team prompts, mandatory human review for commitments, DLP and tenant isolation.
column19: Brittle rules may mis-route requests or mis-score alignment health when deal context changes; models trained on historical data may encode bias (e.g., deprioritizing certain regions/teams) or fail under novel products. Mitigations: periodic rule/model review, explainability, feedback loops, monitoring for drift, and override mechanisms.
column20: Inconsistent sources of truth (CRM vs notes vs Jira) can lead to conflicting actions; identity/RBAC mismatches may expose data; automation loops can amplify errors (bad summary -> wrong ticket -> wrong commitment). Mitigations: master-data governance, event-driven architecture with idempotency, human approval for cross-system writes, end-to-end audit trails, and clear ownership of records.

Draft alignment communications and negotiation language. Scenario ideation for internal alignment|Establish rapport, credibility, and psychological safety with stakeholders. Make judgment calls on trade-offs and commitments. Handle sensitive conflict resolution and executive alignment. Validate customer context and ensure alignment reflects reality|Stakeholder and deal signal analytics from structured data. Compliance and policy checks on outbound/internal artifacts. Routing and prioritization rules for requests to SMEs
column23: Monitors CRM/calendar/email/Slack signals (with permissions), proposes agendas, schedules recurring syncs, triggers reminders, and opens follow-up tasks when dependencies (e.g., product answers, engineering estimates) are pending; adapts cadence based on deal stage and stakeholder responsiveness. Continuously updates who the decision-makers/influencers are, their priorities, open questions, and commitments by extracting signals from meeting notes and CRM; keeps an 'alignment backlog' of actions needed to keep pre-sales on track. Creates/updates Jira tickets for technical asks, routes questions to the right SMEs, tracks SLA breaches, and escalates to the consultant when risk thresholds are met (e.g., no response in 48h before customer workshop).|Drafts concise briefs per stakeholder (sales/product/engineering/delivery) summarizing customer goals, value hypothesis, risks, and asks; tailors tone and depth (commercial vs technical) using approved internal knowledge sources. Turns transcripts/notes into structured summaries (decisions, owners, due dates, open questions) and highlights misalignments (e.g., delivery capacity vs promised scope) for human review. Generates emails/Slack updates, pre-read docs, and escalation messages that frame trade-offs and propose options; suggests phrasing to preserve trust and reduce conflict. Proposes alternative solution approaches, phased delivery options, and compromise packages to reconcile stakeholder constraints; useful for workshop preparation, not as final decision-making.|Trust-building relies on authentic interpersonal behavior, empathy, and reputation; humans read nuance, politics, and emotions and adjust in real time. Deciding what to promise, when to escalate, and how to balance customer goals vs internal constraints requires accountability, domain expertise, and risk ownership. High-stakes disagreements (scope, timelines, resourcing) require negotiation, authority, and ethical judgment; AI can support but not lead. Humans verify that summaries and inferred priorities are correct, especially when data is incomplete, ambiguous, or politically sensitive.|Uses rules/ML on CRM fields (deal stage, ARR, industry), engagement metrics (response times), and ticket aging to score alignment health and predict risk of internal slippage; outputs explainable dashboards. Deterministic checks for required disclaimers, forbidden claims, PII leakage patterns, and template adherence in drafts before human approval. Applies rules based on product area, region, workload, and severity to assign technical questions to the right engineering/product/delivery contacts and enforce SLAs.

column8: Lead discovery sessions to understand customer business needs, user experience requirements, and success criteria, and translate findings into solution direction.
column15: Discovery is high-ambiguity and relationship-driven, so humans must lead conversations, interpret nuance, and secure alignment. Generative AI is strong for drafting agendas/questions, summarizing sessions, and producing first-pass solution narratives, but needs validation due to hallucination/context drift. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the multi-session workflow (scheduling, follow-ups, backlog/decision-log maintenance) and ensuring nothing falls through, yet must operate within guardrails and approvals. Traditional AI is best for deterministic extraction, normalization, and checklist-based scoring/prioritization where explainability and consistency matter. Assumptions: customer permits recording/transcription or at least AI-assisted note processing; tools can integrate with CRM (e.g., Salesforce), meeting platforms, and document repositories; sensitive data handling follows company policy. Constraints: regulated industries may restrict data sharing with external LLMs, reducing GenAI/Agentic contributions.
column16: Humans must (1) set discovery strategy and adjust in real time to stakeholder emotions, politics, and ambiguity; (2) validate AI summaries and ensure requirements reflect true intent, especially for edge cases like conflicting stakeholder goals or implicit constraints; (3) approve any externally shared artifacts (agendas, summaries, solution direction) to prevent misrepresentation; (4) make accountable decisions on scope, trade-offs, and success criteria; (5) ensure compliance with privacy/security (recording consent, data residency, NDA terms) and handle sensitive topics (pricing, competitive displacement, risk acceptance). Human oversight is essential when AI outputs influence commitments, timelines, or contractual language.
column17: Autonomous actions (sending emails, updating CRM, scheduling) can create customer friction if incorrect; may propagate wrong interpretations into backlogs; emergent behavior from tool access could violate process (e.g., contacting wrong stakeholders). Mitigations: least-privilege tool access, approval gates for outbound comms, audit logs, sandbox testing, and explicit policies for when it can act vs suggest.
column18: May hallucinate requirements, misattribute statements, or over-generalize from similar customers; can miss subtle UX nuances or non-verbal cues; risk of leaking confidential data if prompts/transcripts are sent to non-compliant services; tone may be inappropriate for executive stakeholders. Mitigations: use approved enterprise LLM with data controls, require human review, cite source snippets, and keep a clear 'assumptions/open questions' section.
column19: Rule/ML extraction can be brittle with domain jargon and new product terms; misclassification of requirements can bias prioritization; checklist logic may become outdated as offerings/regulations change. Mitigations: periodic model/rule retraining, human spot checks, versioned checklists, and exception handling paths.
column20: Inconsistent schemas between notes, CRM, and requirement backlogs can cause data loss or duplication; conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs Traditional AI fields) can confuse teams; identity/permission mismatches may expose data. Mitigations: define a canonical data model, enforce provenance links (meeting->quote->field), reconciliation workflows, and centralized access control with monitoring.
column22: Orchestrate discovery workflow (planning, scheduling, follow-ups, artifact routing). Maintain a live requirements backlog and decision log across sessions|Draft tailored discovery agenda, question bank, and hypotheses. Real-time note-taking support and post-session summarization. Translate findings into solution direction narratives and artifacts|Lead the live discovery conversation and build trust/rapport. Validate interpretations, resolve trade-offs, and secure stakeholder alignment. Make final solution direction decisions and ensure commercial/compliance fit|Extract and normalize structured entities from discovery data. Scoring and prioritization support using rules/ML
column23: Uses goals (e.g., 'complete discovery by date') to plan multi-step actions: propose agendas, schedule sessions, send pre-reads, track open questions/owners, and route notes/actions into CRM/PM tools; adapts based on attendee responses and progress signals. Autonomously updates a structured backlog (epics, user stories, NFRs, assumptions, risks) from approved notes; flags conflicts, missing stakeholders, and unresolved decisions; prompts for clarifications and schedules targeted follow-ups.|Generates role-specific questions (business, IT, security, UX), maps to common frameworks (JTBD, value drivers), and proposes hypotheses to validate; adapts language to customer domain and maturity. Transcribes (if permitted), summarizes key needs, pain points, workflows, UX expectations, constraints, and success metrics; produces structured outputs (problem statements, personas, journey steps, acceptance criteria) for review. Drafts solution outline, value proposition, high-level architecture options, demo storyline, and success criteria/KPIs; generates slides/one-pagers and identifies gaps/assumptions needing confirmation.|Requires empathy, negotiation, reading room dynamics, handling politics, and adapting in-the-moment to ambiguity; humans manage sensitive topics and establish credibility. Humans must confirm what is 'true' and 'important', arbitrate conflicting needs, and obtain explicit agreement on scope, success criteria, and constraints; accountability cannot be delegated to AI. Final recommendations must consider risk appetite, contractual terms, pricing/packaging, regulatory obligations, and ethical considerations; requires accountable sign-off.|Deterministic NLP/ML models tag entities (stakeholders, systems, integrations, dates, KPIs), classify requirements (functional vs NFR), and populate CRM fields with higher consistency and auditability than free-form generation. Applies predefined scoring (e.g., MoSCoW, RICE-like heuristics) and compliance checklists to rank requirements and highlight mandatory items (security, data residency), producing explainable prioritization suggestions.

column8: Develop and deliver tailored user experience focused product demonstrations and presentations that clearly communicate value, differentiation, and expected outcomes.
column15: The task mixes high-stakes interpersonal delivery and accountability (human-led) with substantial preparatory content and configuration work (AI-assisted). GenAI is best for drafting narratives, slides, FAQs, and follow-ups quickly, but requires human fact-checking to avoid misrepresentation. Agentic AI adds value where multi-step orchestration is needed-pulling context from CRM, configuring demo tenants, running validations, and acting as a supervised co-pilot-yet autonomy must be constrained due to risk of unintended actions or promises. Traditional AI contributes reliably to structured recommendations (module selection, segmentation) and compliance/brand checks with auditable rules. Assumptions: the organization has curated templates, approved knowledge bases, and secure integrations to CRM/demo environments; without these, agentic contribution drops and human effort rises.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final approval of messaging, differentiation claims, ROI/outcome statements, and any roadmap discussion; (2) live delivery decisions-pacing, handling objections, reading stakeholder sentiment, and ethical persuasion; (3) compliance/security commitments (data handling, certifications, contractual terms) and escalation to SMEs; (4) edge cases where discovery data is incomplete/contradictory or the buyer requests unsupported customizations. Humans must also supervise any agentic actions that modify environments or send external communications, and approve any generated content used customer-facing.
column17: Autonomous actions could misconfigure demo tenants, expose data, or trigger unintended emails/calendar invites; may pursue wrong objectives if prompts are ambiguous; limited transparency in decision paths; tool/connector permissions can be abused. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for external actions, sandboxed environments, detailed logging, deterministic runbooks, and fallback to human when confidence is low.
column18: May hallucinate features, overstate differentiation, or invent ROI benchmarks; tone may mismatch executive audiences; may leak sensitive customer data if prompts include confidential info; IP/copyright risk when generating visuals or reusing competitor language. Mitigations: retrieval from approved sources, strict citation/grounding, red-team prompts, human review, and data-loss-prevention controls.
column19: Rules/models can become stale as products and messaging evolve; segmentation may misclassify atypical opportunities; analytics may encode historical bias (e.g., favoring certain industries) and miss novel use cases. Mitigations: periodic retraining/rule reviews, monitoring drift, explainability checks, and override mechanisms.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs compliance rules), context mismatch between CRM and demo environment, and brittle toolchains can cause errors under time pressure. Data governance risks arise when combining customer data with LLMs and agents. Mitigations: unified source-of-truth content library, schema/metadata standards, end-to-end testing, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, and clear ownership for updates to templates/guardrails.
column22: Orchestrate demo preparation workflow across systems (CRM -> discovery notes -> demo environment). Configure and validate a tailored demo environment (data seeding, feature flags, user roles). Create presentation assets (slides, scripts, FAQs, objection handling). Summarize and translate technical capabilities into business value and expected outcomes. Validate accuracy of claims, ROI assumptions, and differentiation statements. column23: With approved connectors, an agent can pull opportunity context (industry, persona, stage), retrieve discovery call notes, open the correct demo tenant, and assemble a run-of-show checklist; it adapts steps based on missing inputs (e.g., requests SME input) and logs progress for auditability. Agentic AI can execute multi-step setup scripts, provision accounts, seed sample data aligned to the prospect's use case, and run smoke tests; it can iterate when validations fail (e.g., broken integration) and escalate with diagnostics. In a supervised mode, an agent can monitor the agenda, surface next best screens, retrieve answers from approved knowledge bases, and suggest pivots when the buyer asks for adjacent scenarios-without autonomously making commitments.|GenAI converts discovery inputs into a coherent story: problem framing, workflow walkthrough, value moments, and outcome statements; it can generate variants for different stakeholders (IT, business, end-user) and maintain UX-focused language. GenAI produces slide outlines, speaker notes, demo scripts, and Q&A/objection responses; it can tailor tone to executive vs practitioner audiences and align messaging to differentiation points provided by product marketing. GenAI maps features to benefits, KPIs, and ROI hypotheses using provided benchmarks; it can generate concise value statements and outcome metrics placeholders to be validated by the consultant. GenAI drafts recap emails, meeting notes, action items, and a tailored next-steps plan, referencing what was shown and questions asked; it can produce a lightweight success plan template.|Humans must interpret nuanced buyer politics, latent needs, and competitive context; they decide what to emphasize/de-emphasize and what commitments are safe, especially in ambiguous or high-stakes deals. A pre-sales consultant reads the room, handles interruptions, builds trust, and adjusts pacing; these interpersonal and ethical responsibilities are not reliably automatable. Humans verify product truth, roadmap boundaries, legal/commercial constraints, and ensure no misleading statements; they approve final messaging. Complex customer-specific constraints require expert judgment, negotiation, and coordination with product/engineering/legal; AI can assist but not own accountability.|Rules/ML models classify opportunity by industry, persona, product line, and stage to recommend the most relevant demo modules, case studies, and slide templates; deterministic rules help ensure consistency. Classical analytics on historical demo engagement (click paths, time on module, conversion outcomes) can rank which demo sequences correlate with progression, informing module selection. Rule-based checks scan slides/scripts for prohibited claims, missing disclaimers, pricing/contract language, and brand style violations; outputs are explainable and auditable.

column8: Create solution proposals, statements of work inputs, and high level implementation approaches that reflect scope, assumptions, and user experience deliverables.
column15: This pre-sales task mixes high-stakes commitments (scope, assumptions, UX deliverables) with substantial drafting and assembly work. Generative AI is well-suited to produce first drafts of proposal/SOW narratives and implementation approaches from structured inputs and approved messaging, accelerating writing and tailoring. Traditional AI reliably handles deterministic elements-template/clause selection, completeness checks, and benchmark-based estimation-where rules and historical data apply. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the end-to-end workflow (collecting inputs, tracking gaps, routing approvals) but should be constrained due to governance and unpredictability risks. Humans remain essential for final scope commitments, feasibility validation, negotiation, and accountability; hence collaboration. Assumptions: access to clean discovery notes, approved clause library, and historical delivery data; without these, AI contributions drop and human effort rises.
column16: Human oversight is essential at (1) scope/assumption sign-off to avoid overcommitment and ensure delivery feasibility; (2) validation of customer-specific facts (current architecture, constraints, success criteria) and relationship-sensitive positioning; (3) approval of legal/security/privacy commitments and any deviations from standard terms; (4) resolving ambiguous or conflicting requirements and negotiating trade-offs; (5) edge cases such as regulated industries, novel integrations, non-standard UX deliverables, aggressive timelines, or when AI outputs suggest assumptions that materially change risk/cost. Humans should also review any AI-generated estimates and implementation plans before sharing externally.
column17: Autonomous actions (sending drafts, updating CRM/CPQ, requesting approvals) can propagate errors quickly; may choose wrong workflow path or over-escalate; transparency/auditability challenges. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, human-in-the-loop gates for external sharing and system-of-record updates, deterministic policies for routing, comprehensive logging, and sandbox testing per deal type.
column18: May hallucinate capabilities, timelines, or deliverables; can introduce unapproved claims or inconsistent scope language; may leak sensitive customer data if prompts/logs are mishandled; tone may misalign with brand or stakeholder expectations. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved content, strict grounding to structured inputs, redline mode with citations, PII controls, and mandatory human review for external-facing artifacts.
column19: Rules/models can be brittle when deal parameters are novel; historical benchmarks may encode past inefficiencies or bias; template rules may lag policy changes. Mitigations: regular rule/threshold updates, model monitoring and recalibration, exception handling paths, and governance ownership by legal/delivery ops.
column20: Inconsistent data between CRM, CPQ, and document drafts can cause mismatched scope/pricing; prompt injection via imported customer text; versioning conflicts across tools; unclear accountability when AI types disagree (e.g., GenAI draft vs rules-based compliance). Mitigations: single source of truth for structured scope fields, automated cross-checks, secure content ingestion, robust version control, and explicit RACI with approval checkpoints.
column22: Orchestrate proposal/SOW assembly workflow across systems. Automated requirements follow-up and gap closure|Draft solution proposal narrative and executive summary. Draft SOW sections (scope, assumptions, deliverables, exclusions) from structured inputs. Create high-level implementation approach and UX deliverables description. Summarize and tailor content for different stakeholders|Validate solution fit, commercial strategy, and final scope commitments. Confirm assumptions, risks, and dependencies with SMEs and delivery leadership. Ensure customer context accuracy and relationship-sensitive messaging. Approve compliance, legal, and ethical considerations|Template selection, clause retrieval, and compliance checks. Effort/price estimation support using historical data. Consistency and completeness validation
column23: Coordinates multi-step workflow: pulls latest templates, triggers SME questionnaires, gathers inputs from CRM/CPQ/knowledge base, tracks gaps, and routes drafts for review/approval with audit trails; adapts plan based on deal type and deadlines. Monitors missing scope/assumption fields, generates targeted clarification questions, schedules follow-ups with account team/customer, and updates a structured requirements log; escalates when risk thresholds are hit.|Generates customer-specific narrative (problem statement, proposed solution, value, differentiators) using provided discovery notes, industry context, and approved messaging; produces multiple tone/length variants. Transforms structured requirements, constraints, and standard clauses into coherent SOW-ready text; highlights ambiguities and proposes default assumptions aligned to internal playbooks. Produces phased implementation plan (workstreams, milestones, roles, dependencies) and articulates UX deliverables (journeys, wireframes, prototypes, acceptance criteria) based on target platform and user personas. Condenses technical approach into customer-friendly language; creates slide-ready bullets and Q&A prep aligned to stakeholder concerns (IT, security, business owners).|Requires accountable judgment on what the company will commit to, trade-offs, and positioning vs competitors; ensures alignment with sales strategy and delivery capability. Humans must negotiate and secure buy-in on resourcing, timelines, and constraints; resolves conflicts between customer asks and internal feasibility. Human reviews for factual accuracy, political nuance, and stakeholder sensitivities; prevents overpromising and ensures proposal aligns with discovery reality. Final accountability for regulatory, privacy, security, and contractual commitments; handles edge cases and exceptions to standard terms.|Uses rules/metadata to select correct proposal/SOW templates by region, product, and deal type; retrieves approved clauses; runs deterministic checks for mandatory sections, legal language, and version control. Applies regression/lookup models and rules to estimate level-of-effort ranges, staffing mix, and timelines from deal parameters; flags outliers vs historical benchmarks. Validates that scope aligns with deliverables, assumptions, and exclusions using structured field matching; checks for missing signatures, dates, and required artifacts.

column8: Provide subject matter expertise on user experience methods, research, and design best practices to guide solution design and address customer questions.
column15: The task is advisory and customer-facing, requiring contextual judgment, credibility, and accountability-so it cannot be fully automated. Generative AI is strong for drafting tailored explanations, synthesizing playbooks, and producing UX artifacts quickly, but must be verified to avoid hallucinations and misalignment with product/service realities. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step retrieval, drafting, routing, and updating workflows across CRM/knowledge bases, reducing coordination overhead; however, autonomy must be constrained with approvals. Traditional AI is reliable for retrieval/ranking of approved assets, segmentation, and rule-based compliance checks. Humans remain essential for final recommendations, live customer interaction, and risk-bearing decisions; hence the largest share. Assumptions: access to curated UX knowledge base, approved templates, and permissioned customer data; without these, AI contributions drop and human share increases.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final sign-off on customer-facing guidance and any commitments, (2) resolving ambiguity and making trade-offs under constraints, (3) ensuring alignment with actual product capabilities, delivery scope, and organizational policy, (4) handling regulated/accessibility/privacy topics where incorrect advice creates legal exposure, and (5) managing relationship dynamics in workshops and executive discussions. Edge cases needing human control: conflicting stakeholder goals, incomplete/biased research inputs, requests for guarantees (e.g., conversion uplift), and novel domains where internal precedent is weak.
column17: Autonomous actions could send unapproved guidance, update CRM incorrectly, or over-escalate/under-escalate sensitive issues; emergent behavior from tool chaining can be hard to audit. Mitigations: least-privilege tool access, approval gates for outbound comms, sandboxed execution, deterministic policies for escalation, comprehensive logging, and kill-switches.
column18: May hallucinate UX "best practices," mis-cite standards (e.g., WCAG levels), or present generic advice as tailored; can leak sensitive customer info if prompts/logs are mishandled; may produce culturally inappropriate tone. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations, red-teaming for regulated topics, prompt/data-loss-prevention controls, mandatory human review for external outputs, and use of approved style/claims libraries.
column19: Rules/models can become outdated as UX standards and product offerings evolve; brittle keyword rules may miss nuanced queries; segmentation models may encode bias (e.g., assuming maturity by industry). Mitigations: governance for rule updates, periodic model recalibration, human-in-the-loop exception handling, and bias testing with documented thresholds.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI draft vs. rule-based compliance), provenance gaps, and feedback loops where agentic systems act on unverified generative content. Mitigations: enforce source-of-truth hierarchy (approved assets > generated), schema for citations/provenance, staged pipelines (retrieve->generate->validate->approve), and monitoring for drift and incident response.
column22: Orchestrate UX discovery support workflow across tools (CRM, knowledge base, research repository). Continuously monitor customer communications for UX-related questions and route to the right SME/materials|Draft customer-facing explanations of UX methods and research approaches tailored to the customer's domain. Create solution-design guidance artifacts (workshop agendas, journey maps, personas, research plans) from inputs. Summarize and synthesize research findings and best-practice guidance from internal documents. Generate Q&A responses and objection handling for common UX/design questions|Make final judgment on UX recommendations and trade-offs in the customer's specific context. Lead high-stakes customer conversations and handle ambiguous or contentious questions. Validate factual accuracy, ensure alignment with product capabilities and delivery feasibility. Approve sensitive areas: accessibility, privacy, regulated-industry constraints, and contractual statements|Retrieve and rank relevant UX assets using keyword/semantic search and metadata filters. Compliance and quality checks on drafted guidance (terminology, required disclaimers, accessibility standards references). Customer segmentation and context enrichment for tailoring recommendations
column23: Plans and executes a multi-step workflow: pulls account context from CRM, retrieves relevant UX case studies/playbooks, drafts an agenda and question list for discovery, and updates artifacts/tasks; adapts based on new customer inputs while keeping an audit trail for review. Watches email/chat/ticket streams (with permissions), detects UX-method/design-best-practice queries, proposes responses and links to approved assets, and escalates high-risk items (accessibility/legal claims) to humans.|Generates clear, audience-appropriate narratives (e.g., when to use usability testing vs. interviews), adapting to industry and maturity level; produces variants for exec vs. practitioner audiences. Transforms discovery notes into structured UX deliverables and checklists; proposes assumptions and open questions; highlights gaps needing validation. Condenses long playbooks, prior proposals, and research reports into key recommendations, constraints, and citations to source documents for verification. Produces draft answers, analogies, and trade-off explanations (e.g., speed vs. rigor), including suggested next steps and risks; flags uncertainty when sources are missing.|Requires nuanced expertise to balance constraints (budget, timeline, org politics, risk tolerance) and to avoid over-prescriptive guidance; accountable for outcomes. Humans manage rapport, negotiation, and real-time sensemaking; can probe for hidden constraints and respond safely when requirements are unclear. Prevents overpromising; confirms that suggested UX approaches fit the actual solution architecture, implementation plan, and services scope. Human oversight is mandatory to manage legal/compliance exposure and ensure commitments match organizational policy.|Uses deterministic rules and classical ML ranking over tagged repositories (industry, product, method, region) to surface approved templates, case studies, and standards quickly. Applies rule-based validation: checks for banned claims, required legal language, inclusion of WCAG references where applicable, and completeness of standard sections in proposals. Uses structured CRM fields and scoring models (industry, size, digital maturity) to suggest which UX methods are likely feasible and to parameterize templates.

column8: Partner with account teams to support opportunity strategy, qualification, and competitive positioning, including responding to RFP and RFI requests.
column15: The task mixes high-stakes commercial judgment (strategy, concessions, customer dynamics) with repeatable content/process work (RFP drafting, summarization, workflow management). Generative AI is well-suited to drafting and synthesizing large volumes of text but must be constrained to approved sources to reduce hallucinations. Agentic AI adds value by coordinating multi-step RFP/RFI workflows and keeping opportunity intelligence current across CRM/document systems, but autonomy should be bounded with approvals. Traditional AI is best for deterministic checks (compliance, guardrails) and structured qualification scoring. Assumptions: the organization has an approved content library, CRM hygiene is moderate, and integrations (CRM, SharePoint/Drive, email, ticketing) are available; without these, agentic/traditional contributions drop and human effort rises.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) final approval of all customer-facing RFP/RFI submissions, especially security/legal/financial commitments; (2) deciding win strategy, pricing posture, and concessions when signals conflict or data is incomplete; (3) validating any competitive claims, roadmap statements, and quantified benefits with evidence; (4) handling exceptions (non-standard terms, unusual compliance regimes, public-sector procurement rules) and escalations; (5) ensuring ethical conduct and alignment with account leadership. Humans should also review AI-generated content for tone, confidentiality, and customer-specific nuances, and approve any agentic actions that send external communications or modify CRM stages.
column17: Over-automation could trigger premature communications, wrong assignments, or missed deadlines due to brittle integrations; emergent behavior may optimize for speed over compliance. Mitigations: constrained action space, approval gates for external sends and pricing/terms, robust logging, rollback/version control, and monitoring with kill-switches.
column18: Hallucinated or outdated claims (security certifications, SLAs, roadmap), tone mismatch, leakage of confidential info if prompts include sensitive data, and inadvertent plagiarism from prior proposals. Mitigations: retrieval from approved knowledge base, citation requirements, red-team prompts, PII/secret scanning, and mandatory human review for high-risk sections.
column19: Rule brittleness (policy changes not reflected), false positives/negatives in qualification scoring, and bias if historical win/loss data reflects skewed selling patterns. Mitigations: governance for rule updates, periodic model recalibration, explainability dashboards, and human override paths.
column20: Inconsistent source-of-truth between CRM, document repositories, and content libraries can cause conflicting outputs; permissioning errors can expose sensitive data; orchestration failures can mix drafts with approved content. Mitigations: unified identity/access controls, data lineage and provenance tags, environment separation (draft vs approved), automated tests for workflows, and clear RACI for approvals.
column22: Orchestrate RFP/RFI response workflow across systems (intake -> assignments -> reminders -> versioning). Continuously gather and refresh opportunity intelligence from internal sources|Draft and tailor RFP/RFI narrative responses and executive summaries. Summarize discovery inputs and synthesize win themes/positioning options. Create competitive battlecards and comparison tables (draft)|Set opportunity strategy and make trade-off decisions (scope, pricing posture, concessions). Validate factual accuracy and evidence for differentiators and compliance-sensitive claims. Conduct/lead customer-facing interactions and stakeholder alignment|Opportunity qualification scoring and routing (rules/ML). Compliance checks and content validation against approved libraries. Competitive/price guardrail alerts

Pulls latest CRM notes, call transcripts, product updates, pricing guardrails, and prior proposals; flags changes that affect positioning and prompts humans for missing critical inputs.|Generates first-draft answers aligned to customer requirements, industry, and opportunity context; reuses approved content snippets and adapts tone/structure to the RFP template. Condenses meeting notes, emails, and research into key pains, decision criteria, and differentiators; proposes messaging pillars and objection-handling language for review. Produces structured comparisons, strengths/weaknesses, and talk tracks based on provided competitor intel and approved claims; highlights where evidence is needed.|Requires judgment under uncertainty, accountability for commercial risk, and alignment with account leadership; involves negotiation strategy and ethical considerations. Humans must ensure claims are true, current, and defensible (security, legal, regulatory, roadmap); approve final wording and exceptions. Relationship building, reading political dynamics, and real-time negotiation/discovery are not reliably automated; humans manage trust and escalation.|Applies defined qualification frameworks (e.g., MEDDICC/BANT fields completeness, deal stage signals) and simple predictive models to score fit, forecast risk, and route to the right pre-sales resources. Deterministically checks responses for required clauses, forbidden terms, missing attachments, SLA/uptime numeric consistency, and presence of mandatory legal/security language using rules and structured templates. Triggers alerts when proposed discounts, terms, or claims exceed policy thresholds; uses rule-based thresholds and anomaly detection on pricing/terms fields.

column8: Coordinate proof of concept and pilot activities, including planning, success metrics, and stakeholder communication, to validate solution fit and reduce risk.
column15: Coordinating a PoC/pilot blends structured execution (scheduling, tracking, dashboards) with high-context judgment (scope trade-offs, executive alignment, risk acceptance). Generative AI accelerates drafting of plans, metrics proposals, and summaries but must be grounded in customer-provided facts to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI can reliably orchestrate workflows and reminders across systems when constrained by approvals and runbooks, reducing coordination overhead. Traditional AI is well-suited for deterministic KPI computation, compliance checks, and explainable risk indicators from structured data. Humans remain essential for negotiating success criteria, approving security/legal decisions, and managing stakeholder dynamics. Assumptions: access to CRM/project tools, telemetry sources, and approved templates; organization permits AI-assisted communications with review gates for external messages.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) defining/approving PoC scope, exit criteria, and any commercial commitments; (2) approving privileged actions (data access, environment changes), security/privacy/legal compliance, and exception handling; (3) validating that success metrics reflect true customer value and are not gamed by proxy KPIs; (4) reviewing all customer-facing communications for accuracy, tone, and confidentiality; (5) go/no-go decisions and escalation handling when pilots fail, stakeholders disagree, or requirements shift. Edge cases: regulated data, ambiguous ownership, conflicting stakeholder objectives, and pilots involving safety-critical or production-impacting changes.
column17: Over-automation could spam stakeholders, change schedules incorrectly, or execute actions beyond intent; opaque decision logic when reprioritizing; integration permissions could be abused if compromised. Mitigations: strict guardrails, least-privilege access, approval checkpoints for privileged steps, audit logs, rate limits, and safe-fail behavior (pause/escalate on uncertainty).

column8: Capture and share reusable assets, playbooks, and lessons learned to help the team scale pre-sales capabilities and improve consistency across deals.
column15: This task mixes content creation/standardization (best handled by Generative AI), workflow coordination and continuous upkeep (Agentic AI), and reliable organization/retrieval (Traditional AI). Assumptions: the org has a central knowledge base (e.g., Confluence/SharePoint/Guru), access-controlled repositories, and permission to process internal transcripts; customer data handling policies are enforced.
