column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approval of playbooks that affect messaging, pricing, security, or contractual commitments; (2) redaction of confidential/customer-identifiable information and validation of customer permissions for reuse; (3) resolving ambiguous or conflicting lessons learned (e.g., whether a loss was due to product gap vs. execution); (4) deciding when to retire guidance or escalate systemic issues to product/leadership. Edge cases: regulated customers, competitive/legal sensitivity, and situations where GenAI summaries could overgeneralize from a single deal.
column17: Autonomous workflows could publish unreviewed content, mis-route sensitive artifacts, or spam stakeholders with requests; emergent behavior from poorly scoped goals. column8: Design, configure, and deliver tailored product demonstrations that map user experience capabilities to prospect needs, value drivers, and success criteria across the sales cycle.
column15: The task spans both creative/communicative work (narrative, talk track, tailoring) and operational execution (environment setup, validation) plus high-stakes interpersonal delivery. Generative AI is strong for synthesizing discovery inputs into a coherent storyline and producing demo scripts and collateral quickly, but must be grounded in approved product facts to avoid hallucinated capabilities. Agentic AI adds leverage by orchestrating multi-step preparation (pulling CRM context, provisioning sandboxes, running tests) and adapting when requirements change, yet needs guardrails and approvals for risky actions. Traditional AI is best for structured recommendations (segmenting opportunities, selecting modules, compliance checks) where deterministic behavior and explainability matter. Humans remain essential for interpreting ambiguous needs, making strategic value-framing choices, and delivering the live demo with real-time improvisation and trust-building. Assumptions: access to CRM/discovery artifacts, a repeatable demo environment with automation hooks, and governance for approved claims/content; without these, human share increases and agentic automation decreases.
column16: Human oversight is essential at (1) discovery-to-demo translation when inputs are incomplete/contradictory, (2) any decision that changes commercial positioning (what to promise, what to defer), (3) approval of security/privacy statements, data handling, and roadmap/competitive claims, and (4) live delivery where audience dynamics, objections, and relationship cues require judgment. Humans must also intervene when automated setup fails, when agentic actions request elevated permissions, when GenAI outputs cite unverified features, and for edge cases like regulated industries, data residency constraints, or prospects requesting custom workflows that resemble professional services commitments.
column17: Unintended configuration changes, excessive automation leading to brittle demos, or actions taken with insufficient context (e.g., enabling non-approved features); opaque decision paths and cascading failures across integrated tools. Mitigations: least-privilege credentials, policy-as-code guardrails, approval gates for high-impact actions, sandbox-only execution, audit logs, and rollback plans/fallback demo flows.
column18: Hallucination of product capabilities, incorrect technical details, or overconfident ROI claims; leakage of sensitive prospect data if prompts/logs are mishandled; tone/terminology mismatch across stakeholders. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved docs, claim-checking against a feature registry, redaction of PII, and mandatory human review for externally shared assets and talk tracks.
column19: Model/rule brittleness when CRM data is incomplete or outdated; biased recommendations if historical win/loss data reflects past targeting biases; false positives/negatives in compliance checks. Mitigations: data quality checks, periodic retraining/rule reviews, explainability dashboards, and human override with feedback loops.
column20: Context drift between systems (CRM vs notes vs demo env), conflicting recommendations (GenAI narrative vs Traditional AI module selection), and security risks from connecting LLMs/agents to internal tools. column8: Develop and maintain demo environments, sample data, and scripted scenarios to ensure reliable performance, realistic workflows, and consistent messaging for target industries.
column15: The task mixes creative/communication work (scripts, messaging), engineering operations (environment provisioning, refresh, validation), and reliability monitoring. Generative AI is strong for drafting scenario narratives, documentation, and producing synthetic artifacts quickly, but needs human review to prevent inaccurate claims and ensure industry credibility. Agentic AI can automate repeatable environment lifecycle operations and multi-step validation/self-healing via integrations with CI/CD and cloud tooling, yet must be constrained with guardrails and approvals for destructive actions. Traditional AI is best for deterministic health checks, anomaly detection, and regression scoring where explainability and stability matter. Humans remain essential for industry positioning, compliance/security governance, and deciding realism vs. simplicity. Assumptions: demo environments are cloud-based with CI/CD access; product has test hooks/APIs; organization permits synthetic data generation; regulated-industry demos require stricter approvals.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) approving industry messaging and claims to avoid misrepresentation; (2) selecting/validating workflows that reflect real customer processes; (3) security and compliance decisions-data provenance, anonymization, secrets management, and access controls; (4) approving high-impact agentic actions (environment teardown, credential rotation, rollback) and handling ambiguous failures (third-party outages, product bugs) where business impact and prioritization are needed; (5) edge cases like demos for regulated sectors (health/finance/public sector), where legal review and auditability may be required.
column17: Autonomous actions can cause outages/cost overruns (e.g., infinite provisioning loops), mis-handle secrets, or apply incorrect configs; emergent behavior from toolchains may reduce predictability; limited transparency in decision paths. Mitigations: least-privilege credentials, action allowlists, sandboxing, rate limits/budgets, mandatory approvals for destructive steps, detailed audit logs, and staged rollout with canary environments.
column18: May hallucinate product capabilities or industry regulations, creating misleading scripts; can generate biased or unrealistic data; risk of leaking sensitive info if prompted with proprietary/customer data; IP/copyright concerns for generated artifacts. Mitigations: use approved knowledge bases/RAG, claim-checking checklists, red-team prompts, data-loss prevention, and human sign-off for external-facing scripts.
column19: Brittle rules/thresholds can miss novel failures or create alert fatigue; models can drift as product changes; may not capture qualitative demo quality (narrative coherence). Mitigations: periodic recalibration, baseline updates per release, combine with synthetic test cases, and keep deterministic checks for critical paths.
column20: Conflicting signals between monitoring (Traditional AI) and agentic remediation can cause oscillations; GenAI-generated scripts/data may not match environment constraints leading to flaky demos; toolchain security risks when connecting LLMs/agents to CI/CD and cloud APIs. Mitigations: clear ownership boundaries, contract tests for data/schema, versioned demo assets, human-in-the-loop gates, centralized secrets management, and end-to-end auditability.

column8: Capture and synthesize feedback from demos and stakeholders, then communicate insights to Product and Engineering to influence roadmap, usability improvements, and messaging.
column15: The task mixes high-volume information processing (well-suited to GenAI summarization/synthesis and Traditional AI tagging/metrics) with cross-tool coordination (Agentic AI workflow orchestration) and high-stakes judgment/influence (human). Agentic AI can reduce operational overhead by ensuring every demo generates a feedback artifact, routing it, and chasing clarifications, but should not autonomously commit to priorities or roadmap promises. Traditional AI provides stable taxonomies, dedupe, and trend dashboards that are auditable and low-risk. Assumptions: access to call recordings/transcripts, CRM metadata, and a defined product taxonomy; organization permits AI processing under privacy policies; feedback destinations include Jira/Linear and Slack/Confluence.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) factual validation of customer statements, technical constraints, and reproduction steps; (2) sensitivity/compliance checks (PII, confidential customer info, competitive intel) before sharing broadly; (3) prioritization and escalation decisions that affect roadmap commitments and customer expectations; (4) interpreting ambiguous feedback, sarcasm, or political dynamics; (5) final messaging changes to ensure brand alignment and avoid overpromising. Edge cases needing humans: regulated customers, security incidents, contractual commitments, conflicting stakeholder feedback, and when AI outputs cite evidence incorrectly or omit critical context.
column17: Autonomous actions could spam stakeholders, create incorrect tickets, misroute sensitive feedback, or change statuses without context; emergent behavior from poorly constrained goals. column8: Develop and maintain customer success frameworks and engagement models that align Sales goals with User Experience outcomes, including segmentation, health scoring, and lifecycle milestones.
column15: This task mixes strategy, cross-functional alignment, and relationship-sensitive decisions (human-led) with repeatable analytics and operationalization (AI-assisted). Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating data refreshes and running ongoing monitoring/playbooks, but must be constrained due to risk of unintended actions. Generative AI accelerates synthesis of qualitative feedback and creation of documentation/presentations, but cannot be the source of truth for metrics. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/product/support data; defined outcome labels (churn/renewal/expansion); and organizational willingness to operationalize playbooks in tooling (CRM/CS platform). Human sign-off should be mandatory before deploying new scoring versions or enabling autonomous outreach/escalations.
column17: Autonomous playbooks may trigger inappropriate escalations or outreach, overwhelm teams with tasks, or act on stale/incorrect data; opaque decision paths complicate accountability. column18: Hallucination or overconfident synthesis could misstate VoC themes or invent causal links between UX and revenue; risk of leaking sensitive customer info when prompting; tone/policy mismatch in generated enablement content. column19: Brittle rules or models can drift as product instrumentation changes; biased features may disadvantage certain customer types; false positives/negatives in health scoring can misallocate TAM effort. column20: Data inconsistencies across CRM/product/support systems can propagate errors into scores and agent actions; conflicting definitions of KPIs between Sales and UX can cause misalignment; feedback loops where interventions change the data distribution can degrade models. column8: Recruit, onboard, and enable new partners by delivering training on products, sales plays, and processes, and ensuring partners can position and sell effectively.
column15: The task blends relationship-heavy partner recruitment and coaching (human-led) with repeatable enablement operations and content work (AI-augmented). Agentic AI can reliably orchestrate multi-step onboarding workflows and monitor readiness signals across CRM/PRM/LMS, reducing manual coordination, but should operate with bounded autonomy and escalation paths. Traditional AI is effective for structured segmentation, readiness scoring, compliance rule checks, and analytics. Assumptions: the organization has a PRM/LMS/CRM with APIs, an approved messaging repository, and governance for partner communications; partner data quality is moderate; regulated-industry partners require stricter review. Edge cases include new product launches (limited documentation), partners in highly regulated markets, and low-data new partners where scoring is unreliable.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) partner selection/qualification and negotiation of mutual commitments; (2) approving partner-facing positioning, competitive claims, and any non-standard enablement promises; (3) handling escalations (security reviews, legal/compliance, pricing, channel conflict) and executive stakeholder management; (4) validating that AI-generated content matches current product reality and brand/compliance requirements; (5) deciding when to pause/terminate onboarding due to risk or misalignment. Humans must review outputs when confidence is low, sources are missing, or the partner operates in regulated industries; humans should approve any outbound communications triggered by agentic workflows beyond routine reminders.

Creates PIR drafts (timeline, impact, root cause hypotheses, corrective actions) from ticket logs and meeting notes; proposes KB articles and playbook updates to prevent recurrence.|Resolves conflicts (e.g., credit issuance vs. policy, expedited ops work vs. capacity), sets final priority when data is ambiguous, and negotiates commitments/ETAs with accountable owners. Manages relationship risk, de-escalates emotionally charged situations, and communicates decisions that affect revenue, contracts, or reputation; ensures empathy and strategic alignment. Reviews/approves credits, refunds, contract deviations, data access, and regulatory/privacy-sensitive steps; ensures adherence to governance and audit requirements.|Applies deterministic rules and classical ML on structured data (tier, ARR, contractual SLA, severity codes, aging) to compute priority, due dates, and breach risk scores; produces explainable flags for escalation. Uses statistical/ML anomaly detection on time-series metrics (delivery times, refund rates, payment failures) to flag outliers and trend breaks that warrant investigation; complements agentic monitoring with more stable, auditable models.

column8: Identify and implement process improvements for partner lifecycle management, including documentation, playbooks, and automation that increase efficiency and consistency.
column15: The task mixes strategic judgment (what to improve, governance, partner impact) with repeatable analysis, documentation, and automation. Generative AI accelerates creation and standardization of playbooks, SOPs, and enablement content, but needs grounding and review to avoid inaccuracies. Traditional AI is best for deterministic controls and quantitative KPI/bottleneck analysis on structured CRM/PRM data, providing explainable signals for where improvements matter. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step workflows and executing cross-system automations with monitoring and exception handling, but should operate within guardrails. Humans remain essential for prioritization, stakeholder alignment, compliance approvals, and handling high-stakes partner exceptions. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/PRM data, documented lifecycle stages, and API/RPA connectivity; maturity typical of Grade 7/8 PAM with cross-functional influence but not full engineering ownership.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) setting objectives/OKRs and deciding trade-offs between efficiency and partner experience; (2) approving changes that affect contractual obligations, data privacy, or revenue recognition; (3) validating that playbooks reflect real-world nuance (strategic partners, regional/legal differences, bespoke deal terms); (4) reviewing GenAI-generated artifacts for factual accuracy, policy alignment, and tone; (5) authorizing agentic automations that create/modify records or trigger partner communications, with staged rollout, audit trails, and kill-switches; (6) adjudicating exceptions and escalations where automation could damage relationships or violate policy.
column17: Over-automation causing unintended partner communications or incorrect CRM updates; cascading failures across integrated systems; difficulty explaining why an action was taken; security risks from broad tool permissions; mitigation: least-privilege access, sandbox/pilot environments, human-in-the-loop approvals for high-impact actions, policy constraints, monitoring, audit logs, rate limits, and emergency stop mechanisms.
column18: Hallucinated or outdated process steps; inconsistent terminology across docs; leakage of sensitive partner data if prompts/logs are not governed; IP/copyright risk when reusing external content; mitigation: retrieval-augmented generation from approved sources, redaction, prompt/data governance, mandatory SME review, version control and approval workflows.
column19: Brittle rules leading to false blocks or missed exceptions; model drift or biased segmentation/routing if trained on historical inequities; limited adaptability to new partner programs; mitigation: periodic rule/model reviews, fairness checks, feedback loops, and clear override paths.
column20: Data quality mismatches between CRM/PRM/wiki/ticketing; conflicting sources of truth causing incorrect automations; prompt injection or malicious inputs via tickets/emails; orchestration complexity leading to unclear accountability; mitigation: master data management, schema validation, signed/approved knowledge bases, input sanitization, end-to-end testing, RACI for ownership, and observability across the workflow.

Humans should also approve any agentic actions that change coverage, incentives, or partner status.
column17: Autonomous workflow actions could trigger inappropriate escalations, spam stakeholders, or recommend changes that violate governance (e.g., reallocating accounts without approvals). column8: Recruit, onboard, and enable channel partners by defining partner profiles, negotiating participation, and delivering training on products, pricing, and sales processes.
column15: The task mixes relationship-heavy, high-stakes decisions (partner selection, negotiation, exception handling) with repeatable operational work (recruitment/onboarding workflows) and content-heavy enablement. Generative AI is best for drafting partner profiles, messaging, and training assets, accelerating enablement creation but requiring review for accuracy and policy. Agentic AI adds value by coordinating multi-system workflows (CRM/PRM, scheduling, provisioning, reminders) and adapting next steps based on partner responses, reducing cycle time. Traditional AI is well-suited to structured scoring/segmentation and compliance-style reporting where deterministic, explainable outputs matter. Assumptions: organization has CRM/PRM/LMS data access, approved templates/guardrails, and integration permissions; partner contracts/pricing require human approval; regulated markets may increase human share.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) approving partner ICP criteria when it affects channel conflict, territory strategy, or brand/reputation; (2) any binding commitments-pricing exceptions, MDF, exclusivity, data processing terms, and legal clauses; (3) validating GenAI-produced product/pricing/process content to prevent misstatements; (4) handling edge cases like suspected fraud, sanctions/KYC flags, partner underperformance disputes, or escalations from key accounts; (5) ensuring compliance with competition law (e.g., resale price maintenance), privacy, and internal approval matrices. Humans should set guardrails, approve outbound sequences for new regions, and review agentic actions that create accounts, send offers, or change partner tiering.

column8: Manage partner pipeline and forecast accuracy by reviewing deal stages, validating partner commitments, and updating CRM data to support predictable revenue outcomes.
column15: This task mixes structured data operations (CRM hygiene, rule checks, risk scoring) with relationship-driven validation and accountability for forecast commitments. Traditional AI is well-suited for deterministic pipeline rules and explainable risk/anomaly detection on structured CRM/PRM data. Generative AI adds value by converting unstructured partner communications into structured updates and producing leadership-ready narratives, but must be grounded to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI can coordinate the end-to-end workflow-pulling data, prompting partners, assembling evidence, and proposing/performing updates-yet requires guardrails and approvals for material changes. Humans remain essential for final forecast ownership, interpreting partner intent, and making trade-offs under uncertainty (e.g., quarter-end pressure). Assumptions: CRM/PRM APIs and communication data access exist; governance allows AI-suggested updates; historical data is sufficient for risk models. Constraints: partner data may be incomplete; different partners use different processes; some updates require internal approvals.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final forecast submission and accountability (auditability, SOX-like controls, internal governance), (2) approving material changes to stage/amount/close date/probability, especially for top deals and quarter-end, (3) interpreting ambiguous or conflicting signals from partners (political dynamics, competitive threats, procurement realities) that are not reliably captured in data, (4) handling edge cases such as channel conflict, deal registration disputes, pricing exceptions, and compliance/privacy constraints on data ingestion, and (5) deciding escalation paths (exec alignment, enablement investment) where business judgment and relationship management dominate. Humans should also review AI outputs when confidence is low, when data sources disagree, or when actions could impact partner trust.
column17: Autonomous follow-ups or CRM writes could create partner friction (spammy outreach), propagate incorrect updates at scale, or violate governance if it bypasses approvals. Mitigations: strict permissioning, rate limits, approval workflows for material fields, sandbox/testing, audit logs, and policy-based action constraints.

Privacy risk if ingesting emails/call transcripts without proper consent and retention controls. column19: Rule brittleness can generate false positives (e.g., nonstandard sales cycles) and ML risk models can drift when product/partner mix changes, leading to miscalibrated slippage predictions. column20: Data synchronization issues between CRM/PRM/email systems can cause inconsistent truth sources; conflicting recommendations (GenAI summary vs ML risk score) may confuse users. Security risks from broad API tokens and prompt injection via partner-supplied text. Mitigations: master-data governance, source-of-truth hierarchy, reconciliation checks, unified audit trail, secure secret management, content sanitization, and human-in-the-loop exception handling.

Generative AI accelerates drafting and synthesis (briefs, comms, summaries, scenario options) but requires human validation due to hallucination and policy constraints. Humans should also review any AI-generated partner-facing content and any agentic actions that could commit spend, change pricing, or trigger external communications.

Proposes incentive structures (rebates, SPIFFs, MDF usage ideas), messaging angles, and A/B test hypotheses; produces pros/cons and assumptions but does not finalize commercial terms.|Defines program objectives, prioritizes partners/segments, negotiates with marketing/product/finance/ops on scope and resourcing, and obtains mandatory approvals (pricing, legal, finance) where accountability cannot be delegated. Handles high-stakes partner conversations, exceptions, and conflict resolution; ensures messaging aligns with relationship context and avoids reputational damage. Makes judgment calls when data is incomplete (e.g., competitive response, channel conflict), owns compliance interpretations, and decides on go/no-go and exception handling.|Uses regression/time-series models and rules on historical sell-through, seasonality, partner performance, and promo history to estimate uplift ranges, cannibalization risk, and budget burn; outputs explainable drivers. Applies deterministic rules/constraints (partner tier, geography, deal registration, margin floors, accrual limits) to validate proposed incentives and flag violations before finance/legal review. Monitors structured operational KPIs (inventory, lead times, claim rates, portal uptime) and flags anomalies that could derail launch; triggers alerts when thresholds are breached.

column8: Monitor partner performance and compliance with program terms, pricing guidance, and policy adherence issues, escalating risks and implementing remediation plans as needed.
column15: The task mixes structured monitoring (well-suited to Traditional AI), cross-system workflow execution (best handled by Agentic AI), and narrative/communication work (GenAI). However, enforcement decisions, contractual interpretation in edge cases, and relationship-sensitive escalations require human judgment and accountability. Assumptions: partner data is accessible via governed integrations; policies/terms are codified in a controlled knowledge base; agentic actions are constrained by approval gates for high-impact steps.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) setting and periodically revising thresholds/playbooks to reflect changing program terms and market conditions; (2) adjudicating ambiguous signals (e.g., justified discounting, regional exceptions, force majeure) and determining intent; (3) approving actions with legal/financial consequences (incentive holds/clawbacks, suspension, contract amendments, public-facing statements); (4) handling escalations involving strategic partners, discrimination/retaliation allegations, or regulatory exposure; and (5) validating AI outputs when data quality is poor or sources conflict.
column17: Autonomous actions could over-enforce (wrongly pausing incentives) or under-enforce due to misconfigured goals; may create cascading workflow errors across systems; limited transparency in decision paths. Mitigations: strict guardrails, role-based permissions, approval checkpoints, simulation/sandbox testing, comprehensive logging, and kill-switches.
column18: May hallucinate policy clauses or misstate partner performance if not grounded; can leak sensitive pricing/PII in prompts/outputs; tone or wording may create legal exposure. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation over approved documents, citations, redaction/DLP, template constraints, and mandatory human review for external/high-stakes communications.
column19: Rules/models can become brittle as program terms change; false positives from simplistic thresholds; bias if risk scoring correlates with region/partner size. Mitigations: versioned rule management, periodic recalibration, explainability/feature review, and monitoring for disparate impact.
column20: Data mismatches across CRM/PRM/ERP can trigger incorrect flags; inconsistent definitions of KPIs; feedback loops where agent actions change data and retrigger alerts; security risks from broad integrations. Mitigations: canonical data model, reconciliation checks, idempotent workflows, event de-duplication, least-privilege access, and end-to-end audit trails.
column22: Orchestrate continuous monitoring workflow across systems (CRM, PRM, ERP, deal desk, support tickets). Case management and escalation routing. Execute remediation playbooks (low/medium risk)|Summarize partner performance/compliance status and generate executive-ready narratives. Draft partner communications and remediation documentation. Policy/program term interpretation support (Q&A)|Define/approve compliance policies, thresholds, and enforcement posture. Investigate high-severity or ambiguous cases and make final decisions. Conduct sensitive partner conversations and negotiate remediation commitments. Approve external communications and actions with financial/legal impact|KPI computation, scorecards, and threshold-based compliance checks. Anomaly detection and risk scoring on structured signals. Entity matching and data quality controls
column23: Sets up and runs scheduled/trigger-based checks, pulls partner KPIs and compliance signals from multiple sources, maintains state (open issues, SLAs), and routes items through a defined playbook with minimal human prompting. Creates/updates cases, assigns owners, schedules follow-ups, and escalates based on severity/thresholds; can coordinate with legal/finance/channel ops via workflow tools while keeping an audit trail. Initiates partner outreach sequences, requests documentation, pauses incentives, or triggers training enrollment per policy; adapts steps based on partner responses while respecting guardrails and approval gates.|Produces concise weekly/monthly summaries from dashboards, emails, call notes, and case histories; highlights trends, exceptions, and likely root causes with citations/links to source artifacts. Generates tailored emails, corrective action plans, meeting agendas, and training guidance aligned to program terms; supports multilingual communication and tone control. Acts as a copilot to answer questions about program terms/pricing guidance, retrieve relevant clauses, and propose next steps; must be constrained to approved knowledge bases to reduce hallucinations.|Requires business judgment, legal/compliance alignment, and consideration of partner relationships and market context; sets what constitutes a breach and acceptable remediation. Humans assess intent, context, and contractual nuance; decide on sanctions (incentive clawbacks, suspension/termination) and manage reputational/legal risk. Relationship management, negotiation, and conflict handling are high-stakes and context-heavy; humans ensure empathy, alignment, and accountability. Mandatory approvals for actions like pricing exceptions, incentive holds, or contract changes; ensures auditability and prevents unauthorized commitments.|Deterministically calculates attainment, pipeline hygiene, MDF usage, discount levels vs guidance, and contract term adherence; applies rules/SQL and produces explainable pass/fail flags. Uses classical ML/statistics to detect unusual discounting, sudden revenue spikes/drops, deal registration abuse, or policy breach patterns; outputs calibrated risk scores and feature attributions. Performs deduplication and matching of partner accounts/deals across systems; validates required fields and detects missing/invalid entries that can mask compliance issues.

column8: Analyze market, competitor, and channel insights to identify growth opportunities, optimize territory and partner segmentation, and refine program investments.
column15: This task mixes structured analytics (best handled by Traditional AI), unstructured synthesis and drafting (best handled by Generative AI), and multi-step data/analysis orchestration plus scenario iteration (suited to Agentic AI). However, the Channel Sales Manager (Mgmt Grade 9/10) must set strategy, interpret insights in context, and own decisions that impact partner relationships, territory fairness, and budget allocation-keeping the overall task in Human-AI Collaboration. Assumptions: access to governed CRM/PRM/POS data, defined territory/partner hierarchies, and a controlled environment where agent actions are limited to analysis and recommendations (not auto-enacting changes). Constraints: data quality varies by partner; competitor intel is noisy; program ROI attribution is imperfect, increasing need for human judgment.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) setting objectives/guardrails (e.g., protected accounts, strategic partners, compliance with channel policies), (2) validating causality vs. correlation in insights (e.g., whether a dip is seasonality, execution, or competitor action), (3) approving segmentation/territory changes to ensure fairness, legal/HR alignment, and stakeholder buy-in, and (4) handling exceptions and partner communications. Edge cases: sparse data for new partners/regions, conflicting signals across sources, sudden market shocks (regulatory changes, competitor acquisition), and situations where recommendations could disadvantage protected segments or violate partner agreements.
column17: Autonomous workflows can propagate bad data, over-query systems, or take unintended actions if permissions are broad; opaque reasoning may reduce trust. Mitigations: least-privilege access, sandboxed execution, approval gates before any write-back, audit logs, and bounded objectives/constraints.
column18: May hallucinate competitor facts or overstate confidence; can misinterpret metrics without grounding; risk of leaking confidential partner/pricing data if prompts are not governed. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation with approved sources, citation requirements, redaction/DLP, and human review for external-facing outputs.
column19: Models can encode historical bias (favoring large incumbents), degrade with market shifts, and give false precision when attribution is weak. Mitigations: periodic retraining, drift monitoring, explainability, fairness checks, and using confidence intervals rather than point estimates.
column20: Mismatched definitions (TAM, partner tiers), inconsistent IDs, and feedback loops where GenAI narratives amplify weak signals from models. Orchestration failures can create stale dashboards. Mitigations: shared metric dictionary, master data management, end-to-end lineage, automated validation tests, and requiring citations + uncertainty reporting in generated recommendations.
column22: Orchestrate recurring insight pipeline across data sources (CRM, PRM, POS, market research, web, partner portals). Run what-if simulations and recommend actions with traceable rationale|Synthesize qualitative market/competitor/channel signals into executive-ready narratives. Hypothesis generation for growth plays and partner motions. Draft segmentation and program investment recommendations (first draft)|Define strategic objectives, constraints, and success metrics. Validate insights with field reality and partner context. Make final decisions on territory/partner changes and program investments. Manage sensitive communications and change management|Data cleaning, normalization, and entity resolution (accounts/partners/territories). Quantitative segmentation and scoring (partner potential, territory opportunity). Trend detection and anomaly alerts in channel performance
column23: Plans and executes multi-step workflows: pulls data via connectors/APIs, schedules refreshes, monitors failures, and adapts queries when schemas change; escalates anomalies to humans. Iteratively tests scenarios (e.g., shifting MDF, changing tier thresholds, reallocating territories) using defined constraints, then proposes ranked actions and triggers follow-up analyses when confidence is low.|Summarizes research reports, win/loss notes, partner feedback, and news into themes, implications, and talking points tailored to leadership and partner audiences. Generates candidate growth hypotheses (new vertical plays, attach motions, co-sell offers) from patterns in structured metrics plus unstructured notes; proposes experiments and KPIs. Produces draft memos, QBR slides, and investment rationales using provided metrics/constraints; flags assumptions and requests missing inputs.|Requires business judgment on priorities (growth vs. margin), channel strategy, product focus, and constraints (coverage, partner commitments, compliance). Humans confirm whether signals reflect real market dynamics (e.g., competitor discounting, partner capacity) and resolve ambiguous/contradictory data. High-stakes decisions affect revenue, relationships, and fairness; requires accountability, stakeholder alignment, and approval governance. Territory and tier changes can trigger conflict; humans handle negotiation, exceptions, and messaging to partners/sales leadership.|Uses deterministic rules + classical ML for deduplication, mapping partner IDs, standardizing industry/geo, and building a reliable analytical dataset. Applies clustering, propensity models, and scoring (e.g., TAM/SAM, whitespace, pipeline velocity, partner influence) with explainable features for repeatable segmentation. Time-series models and control charts detect shifts in bookings, attach, churn, deal registration conversion; generates alerts for investigation.

column8: Prepare executive-ready reporting on channel results, including revenue, margin, pipeline health, and program ROI, and present recommendations to leadership.
column15: The task mixes (a) structured KPI computation and validation (best handled by Traditional AI/BI), (b) multi-system workflow execution and variance-investigation playbooks (Agentic AI), and (c) executive narrative, recommendations framing, and slide drafting (Generative AI). However, because channel reporting influences leadership decisions and must align with finance definitions and partner strategy, humans must set/approve KPI definitions, sanity-check drivers, and own final recommendations and delivery. Assumptions: data access to CRM/ERP/partner systems exists via governed connectors; KPI definitions are documented but may need periodic updates; leadership expects a recurring cadence (weekly/monthly) with ad-hoc deep dives. Edge cases (M&A partners, new programs, one-off deals) increase human share due to interpretation needs.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) KPI definition governance (sourced/influenced attribution, margin allocation, ROI methodology) and any changes to these definitions; (2) approval of numbers that must reconcile to finance close, especially near quarter-end; (3) interpreting anomalies and contextual factors (deal desk exceptions, rebates, returns, supply constraints, partner disputes) that data alone may not capture; (4) deciding and approving recommendations that affect partner relationships, budgets, and compliance; (5) ensuring confidentiality and appropriate audience-level disclosure in executive materials. Humans should also intervene when data quality checks fail, when agentic workflows request elevated permissions, or when GenAI outputs cite unsupported causal claims.
column17: Autonomous workflows can propagate errors at scale (pull wrong date ranges, overwrite dashboards, notify wrong audiences) and may behave unpredictably under ambiguous instructions; permission misuse is a risk. Mitigations: least-privilege access, sandbox runs, approval gates for publishing, audit logs, deterministic playbooks, and monitoring/rollback.
column18: May hallucinate drivers, overstate causality, or misquote figures if not grounded to locked KPI tables; may produce overly confident recommendations or inappropriate tone for executives. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation from approved datasets, numeric locking/citation, constrained templates, and mandatory human review for final narrative/recommendations.
column19: Brittle rules and models can drift when business definitions change (new partner tiers, pricing, rebate structures) leading to silent KPI miscalculation; anomaly models may flag expected seasonality or miss novel issues. Mitigations: versioned metric definitions, reconciliation to finance, periodic model recalibration, and unit tests on KPI pipelines.
column20: Mismatch between KPI computation (Traditional AI/BI) and GenAI narrative can create inconsistencies; agentic orchestration may trigger GenAI before data is finalized; data lineage and governance gaps can cause compliance issues. Mitigations: single source of truth layer, data contracts, workflow sequencing with 'finance-close' status, provenance metadata passed to GenAI, and end-to-end QA checks before executive distribution.
column22: Orchestrate data pulls and refresh cadence across systems (CRM, ERP/finance, partner portal, BI). Automated variance investigation workflow (driver analysis). Assemble and route an executive reporting pack for review/approval|Draft executive narrative and insights from KPI tables/charts. Generate recommendations and scenario options (e.g., program adjustments, partner focus). Create presentation artifacts (slide headlines, speaker notes, one-page summary)|Define/approve metric definitions, attribution logic, and executive success criteria. Validate insights and recommendations against ground truth and context. Deliver the leadership presentation and handle objections/decisions|Compute and validate core KPIs (revenue, gross margin, pipeline coverage, conversion, ROI). Anomaly detection and threshold-based alerts on channel metrics. Partner/program segmentation and cohort analysis
column23: Plans and executes multi-step workflows to extract the latest channel revenue, margin, pipeline, and program spend/benefits from multiple sources; monitors job success, retries failures, and alerts on missing feeds while respecting access controls. When KPIs move materially, autonomously runs a playbook: segments by partner/region/product, checks recent program changes, compares to historical baselines, and compiles likely drivers with supporting evidence for human review. Creates a task plan to compile charts/tables, attach narrative draft, route to stakeholders (finance ops, sales ops) for sign-off, track approvals, and publish to the agreed channel (deck/wiki/email) with audit trail.|Summarizes performance vs plan/YoY/QoQ, highlights key wins/risks, and converts quantitative outputs into concise exec-ready language tailored to leadership expectations. Uses provided constraints (budget, strategic priorities, partner tiers) to propose actions, expected impact, and trade-offs; produces alternative options and talking points for Q&A. Transforms structured findings into slide-ready text, consistent terminology, and clear story flow; can adapt tone for different leadership audiences.|Requires business judgment and alignment with finance and leadership; prevents misleading KPIs due to ambiguous definitions (e.g., sourced vs influenced revenue, margin allocation). Humans confirm whether drivers are real (e.g., one-time deals, channel conflict, supply constraints) and ensure recommendations fit partner relationships and strategy. Executive communication, negotiation, and accountability for decisions (budget shifts, partner actions) require human ownership and real-time judgment.|Deterministic calculations and classical analytics on structured data; applies consistent definitions, currency conversions, attribution rules, and reconciliation checks against finance totals. Uses statistical rules/ML (e.g., z-scores, seasonal baselines) to flag unusual swings in margin, pipeline aging, win rates, or program spend efficiency for further investigation. Clusters or rule-segments partners by tier, industry, region, attach rate, and program participation to enable like-for-like comparisons and ROI benchmarking.

column8: Partner with Sales, Marketing, Product, and Operations stakeholders to define alliance priorities, success metrics, and joint execution plans that support revenue and strategic objectives.
column15: This task is fundamentally cross-functional strategy and alignment work. Humans must own final prioritization, negotiation, and accountability. Generative AI accelerates synthesis and drafting of priorities, metrics definitions, and planning artifacts, reducing time spent on documentation and ideation. Traditional AI is best for structured KPI baselining, scoring, and governance to ground discussions in reliable data. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the planning workflow end-to-end (scheduling, routing, action tracking) and by monitoring KPI drift to trigger replanning, but must operate under strict guardrails because it can inadvertently create commitments or mis-route sensitive information. Assumptions: stakeholders use digital systems (CRM/BI/project tools) with accessible APIs; metric definitions can be standardized; organization permits AI-assisted note-taking/summarization with appropriate privacy controls.
column16: Human oversight is essential for (1) final decisions on alliance priorities and resource allocation; (2) resolving conflicts between Sales/Marketing/Product/Ops incentives; (3) approving KPI definitions that affect compensation, reporting, or external claims; (4) any partner-facing commitments, pricing, legal terms, or data-sharing arrangements; (5) validating AI-generated summaries against source context to avoid misrepresentation; and (6) handling edge cases such as strategic-partner escalations, ambiguous attribution, or sudden market/competitive shifts where judgment and relationship context dominate.
column17: Autonomous orchestration can overstep (e.g., scheduling the wrong attendees, sending sensitive pre-reads, creating tasks that imply commitments) and may behave unpredictably when tools fail or data is incomplete. Mitigations: least-privilege access, human-in-the-loop approvals for outbound communications, sandbox testing, explicit policies, and audit logs/rollback.
column18: May hallucinate priorities/metrics, mis-summarize stakeholder intent, or introduce biased framing; can leak confidential partner or pipeline data if prompts/logs are mishandled; may draft partner-facing language that creates unintended commitments. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations, redaction/DLP, approval workflows for external artifacts, and style/claims guardrails.
column19: Models/rules can encode outdated assumptions (e.g., partner tiers, TAM) and produce misleading scores if CRM data quality is poor; may be brittle to new products/markets. Mitigations: continuous data quality monitoring, periodic recalibration, explainability checks, and governance over feature/label definitions.
column20: Conflicting outputs (GenAI narrative vs BI numbers), metric definition drift across systems, and automation chains that propagate errors (bad data -> wrong score -> wrong plan). Security/privacy risks increase when connecting CRM, BI, calendars, and partner portals. Mitigations: single source of truth for metrics, versioned definitions, reconciliation steps, role-based access, and staged deployments with monitoring.
column22: Orchestrate cross-functional planning workflow (agenda, pre-reads, action tracking). Continuously monitor alliance performance signals and trigger replanning cycles|Synthesize stakeholder inputs into draft alliance priorities and narratives. Draft success metrics (OKRs/KPIs) and measurement definitions. Create joint execution plan artifacts. Scenario planning and risk/assumption documentation|Set strategic direction and make final prioritization trade-offs. Negotiate and align commitments across functions and with partners. Approve success metrics and execution plans for accountability and compliance. Handle edge cases and high-stakes communications|Quantitative analysis of historical alliance performance and pipeline. Stakeholder/partner segmentation and prioritization scoring. Data quality checks and metric governance enforcement
column23: Coordinates a multi-step workflow across stakeholders: proposes meeting cadence, drafts agendas based on open decisions, routes pre-reads, captures decisions, assigns owners, and follows up on overdue actions via integrated tools (calendar/CRM/project management). Uses policies/guardrails and requires human approval for external-facing commitments. Watches dashboards/CRM/partner portals for KPI drift (pipeline, activation, churn, co-marketing performance), detects threshold breaches, and initiates a replanning playbook (request updates, schedule reviews, propose corrective actions) while keeping an audit trail.|Summarizes notes, emails, and strategy docs into a coherent set of candidate priorities (e.g., segments, solutions, regions), articulating trade-offs and rationale aligned to revenue and strategic themes. Generates metric options and precise definitions (numerator/denominator, attribution windows, data sources), plus pros/cons and leading vs lagging indicators tailored to alliance motions. Produces first drafts of joint business plans, RACI matrices, launch plans, QBR decks, and partner-facing summaries; adapts tone for internal vs partner audiences and localizes content as needed. Generates scenarios (best/base/worst), identifies assumptions (conversion rates, enablement capacity), and drafts risk registers and mitigation options for stakeholder review.|Requires executive judgment to balance competing objectives (revenue vs strategic bets), manage politics, and decide what to stop/start given constraints and risk appetite. Alignment involves persuasion, relationship management, and real-time negotiation; humans must secure buy-in, resolve conflicts, and handle sensitive escalations. Humans must validate feasibility, legal/commercial implications (pricing, data sharing, co-marketing claims), and ensure plans reflect real capacity and contractual constraints. When data is ambiguous or stakes are high (strategic partners, reputational risk), humans must craft/approve messaging and decisions.|Uses BI/ML models and deterministic analytics to compute baseline KPIs, cohort trends, funnel conversion, partner-sourced vs influenced revenue, seasonality, and forecast deltas; outputs explainable charts/tables. Applies rule-based scoring or classical ML (e.g., logistic regression/GBM) using structured features (TAM, win rate, attach rate, partner tier, product fit) to rank alliance opportunities and identify whitespace. Runs validation rules for CRM hygiene, deduplication, attribution logic, and threshold alerts to ensure metrics used in planning are consistent and auditable.

column8: Identify, evaluate, and onboard new channel and alliance partners using defined selection criteria, due diligence steps, and internal approval workflows to ensure strategic fit.
column15: This alliance-manager task mixes structured screening and workflow execution with high-stakes judgment and relationship work. Traditional AI is well-suited for deterministic eligibility checks, structured lead scoring, and explainable risk flags. Generative AI accelerates unstructured research synthesis and produces drafts (outreach, briefs, business cases) but must be grounded to sources to avoid hallucinations. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the end-to-end onboarding workflow across CRM/PRM, ticketing, and document systems, reducing cycle time and ensuring process compliance; however, it should operate within guardrails and approval gates. Humans remain essential for strategy, negotiation, exception handling, and accountable approvals. Assumptions: organization has CRM/PRM and standardized workflows; access to compliance screening vendors; AI tools can be integrated with role-based access controls. Uncertainty: maturity of agentic orchestration depends on system APIs and governance readiness.
column16: Human oversight is essential at (1) partner strategy and criteria setting (trade-offs, market timing, channel conflict), (2) relationship assessment and negotiation (trust, cultural fit, commercial terms), (3) risk acceptance decisions when due diligence flags appear (legal/finance accountability), and (4) final internal approvals and exception management. Humans must also review any AI-generated summaries used in approvals, validate source evidence, and ensure compliance with privacy/competition law. Edge cases needing humans: incomplete/contradictory partner data, politically sensitive alliances, potential channel conflict with existing partners, and jurisdictions with strict regulatory requirements.
column17: Over-automation could trigger outreach or onboarding steps prematurely; unintended actions across systems (wrong partner record, incorrect routing); opaque decision logic; security risks if granted broad permissions. Mitigations: least-privilege access, approval gates for external communications and contract steps, sandbox testing, audit logs, deterministic policies for actions, and kill-switch/rollback.
column18: Hallucinated or overstated partner capabilities; summarization that omits critical red flags; leakage of confidential data if prompts include sensitive info; biased language affecting partner perception. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations, red-flag checklists, prompt/data minimization, human review for approval artifacts, and content filters.
column19: Brittle rules leading to false rejects/accepts; model drift as markets change; fairness issues in scoring (e.g., disadvantaging smaller/new partners); limited handling of nuanced qualitative factors. Mitigations: periodic rule/model review, monitoring for drift and disparate impact, explainability reports, and human override paths.
column20: Data quality and identity resolution issues across CRM/PRM/legal systems; inconsistent definitions of criteria; feedback loops where GenAI narratives amplify flawed scores; orchestration failures causing compliance gaps. Mitigations: master data management, schema/criteria governance, source-of-truth linking with citations, end-to-end testing, and clear RACI with audit trails.
column22: Orchestrate partner onboarding workflow across systems. Autonomously monitor pipeline and recommend next-best actions|Draft and tailor partner outreach and enablement materials. Summarize partner research and due diligence findings. Generate evaluation scorecard narratives and business case drafts|Define/adjust partner strategy and selection criteria. Conduct relationship-building, negotiation, and final partner selection. Approve due diligence outcomes and manage exceptions. Secure internal stakeholder alignment and final sign-offs|Partner discovery and lead scoring from structured signals. Criteria-based screening and compliance checks. Risk scoring and anomaly detection in submitted documents/data
column23: Coordinates multi-step process (intake -> screening -> due diligence -> approvals -> onboarding) by triggering tasks in CRM/PRM, creating tickets, requesting documents, routing for approvals, tracking SLAs, and escalating blockers based on policy and context. Continuously watches partner funnel signals (response latency, missing artifacts, risk flags) and proposes/executes actions like scheduling meetings, sending reminders, or initiating deeper checks within predefined guardrails.|Generates personalized outreach emails, pitch decks outlines, partner program summaries, and onboarding guides aligned to the partner's industry and value proposition; adapts tone to Professional (Grade 7/8) alliance context. Synthesizes unstructured inputs (websites, news, analyst notes, call transcripts, questionnaires) into structured briefs: strategic fit, capabilities, overlaps, risks, and open questions; produces exec-ready summaries for approvals. Turns structured scoring outputs and notes into coherent justification text for internal stakeholders (sales, legal, finance), including assumptions and scenario comparisons.|Requires market judgment, relationship strategy, and alignment with sales priorities; criteria trade-offs and exceptions are business decisions with accountability. Trust-building, negotiation of commercial terms, and assessing cultural fit rely on human interpersonal skills and contextual judgment; high-stakes decisions need accountable owners. Humans must review red flags, approve risk acceptance, and ensure compliance with legal/finance policies; handles edge cases where data is incomplete or conflicting. Cross-functional alignment (sales leadership, legal, finance, product) involves persuasion, prioritization, and accountability that cannot be delegated to AI.|Uses rules/ML on structured data (firmographics, territory coverage, historical win rates, product adjacency, engagement metrics) to rank potential partners and detect duplicates in CRM/PRM. Applies deterministic rules and classical models to validate minimum requirements (certifications, revenue thresholds, geographic coverage), run sanctions/PEP checks via vendors, and flag policy violations. Detects inconsistencies (mismatched legal names, unusual payment terms, missing tax IDs) and assigns risk scores using explainable models; supports audit trails.

column8: Manage ongoing partner relationships through regular business reviews, performance tracking, and issue resolution to improve partner satisfaction and drive mutual growth.
column15: The task mixes structured operations (tracking KPIs, alerts) with high-context relationship management and judgment. Traditional AI is best for auditable KPI computation, segmentation, and anomaly alerts. Generative AI accelerates narrative work (QBR materials, summaries, partner comms) but requires grounding to avoid inaccuracies. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating recurring workflows (scheduling, data pulls, action-item follow-up) and proactive triage, yet must be constrained by playbooks and approval gates. Humans remain essential for trust-building, negotiation, and accountable decisions on escalations and concessions. Assumptions: access to clean CRM/BI/support data; approved templates/playbooks; tool integrations (calendar, CRM, ticketing) and role-based access controls. Constraints: partner communications and commitments require review; some partners may restrict recording/transcription.
column16: Human oversight is required for (1) any partner-facing message that includes commercial terms, performance claims, roadmap statements, or legal language; (2) decisions to escalate to executives, offer concessions/credits, change partner tier/status, or adjust targets; (3) resolving conflicts where intent, tone, or politics matter; (4) validating root-cause analyses when data is ambiguous or cross-functional ownership is disputed; (5) compliance/privacy checks (PII, contractual confidentiality, recording consent) and ensuring AI actions stay within delegated authority. Edge cases: data gaps causing misleading health scores; partners in regulated industries; multi-party alliances where incentives conflict; crisis incidents requiring real-time judgment.
column17: Unintended actions (sending emails, booking meetings, escalating tickets) without proper context; automation loops spamming stakeholders; policy violations if it commits to concessions or roadmap items; opaque decision paths. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, approval workflows for external comms and escalations, rate limits, audit logs, deterministic playbooks, simulation/testing in sandbox, clear stop conditions.
column18: Hallucinated KPIs or incorrect attributions in QBR narratives; tone/cultural mismatch harming relationships; leakage of confidential partner data via prompts; overconfident troubleshooting guidance. Mitigations: retrieval-grounded generation with citations, locked KPI tables from BI, redaction/PII controls, approved templates, mandatory human review for sensitive outputs.
column19: Brittle rules/thresholds causing false alarms or missed risks; biased health scoring due to historical data; metric drift when definitions change; integration/ETL failures producing wrong dashboards. Mitigations: metric governance, periodic recalibration, monitoring for drift, explainability checks, data quality tests, human review of score impacts.
column20: Inconsistent data across CRM/BI/ticketing leading to conflicting recommendations; identity/permission mismatches causing data exposure; cascading errors where GenAI narratives use outdated Traditional AI metrics or Agentic AI triggers actions from false alerts. Mitigations: single source of truth for KPIs, versioned metric definitions, end-to-end validation, unified audit trail, confidence thresholds and human-in-the-loop gates, rollback procedures.
column22: Orchestrate recurring partner business review (QBR/MBR) workflow. Proactive issue triage and escalation routing. Next-best-action recommendations for mutual growth|Prepare partner-facing narratives and communications. Summarize meetings and convert discussions into action items. Knowledge assistance for issue resolution and enablement|Relationship stewardship and strategic negotiation. Final decision-making on escalations, concessions, and commitments. Governance, ethics, and context validation|Performance tracking dashboards and KPI computation. Anomaly detection and threshold-based alerts. Partner segmentation and health scoring
column23: Plans and executes the end-to-end cadence: pulls latest partner data from CRM/BI, drafts agenda, proposes dates across time zones, books meetings, assigns owners for action items, and follows up until closure; adapts the workflow based on partner tier, renewal dates, and open risks. Monitors signals (support tickets, SLA breaches, churn risk flags, negative sentiment) and autonomously opens/updates cases, routes to the right internal teams, pings stakeholders, and tracks SLA timers; suggests escalation paths and mitigations based on playbooks. Combines partner performance trends, pipeline, product adoption, and contractual milestones to propose actions (enablement session, joint campaign, MDF usage, exec sponsor outreach) and sequences them into a plan; requests human approval for high-impact moves.|Drafts QBR decks, executive summaries, renewal health updates, and partner emails using structured inputs (KPIs, wins, gaps, roadmap); tailors tone to partner persona and region; produces variants for different stakeholders. Transcribes/summarizes calls, extracts decisions, risks, and commitments; generates follow-up notes and action-item lists with owners/dates for entry into CRM/project tools. Retrieves and synthesizes internal docs (policies, integration guides, pricing rules, partner program terms) to propose troubleshooting steps, FAQs, and enablement materials; flags uncertainty and cites sources when available.|Builds trust, manages politics, and conducts sensitive conversations (pricing, contract terms, performance disputes); applies judgment on trade-offs and long-term partnership strategy that AI cannot reliably infer. Approves credits, exceptions, roadmap commitments, and executive escalations; ensures alignment with legal/compliance and company strategy; accountable for outcomes. Validates KPI interpretations, ensures communications are accurate and appropriate, manages confidentiality, and corrects AI outputs when partner context is nuanced or data is incomplete.|Uses deterministic ETL, BI metrics, and rule-based calculations to track revenue, pipeline, activation, attach rates, SLA compliance, NPS/CSAT, and program compliance; provides explainable, auditable numbers for reviews. Applies statistical/ML models and rules to detect drops in usage, delayed deal registration, unusual discounting, ticket spikes, or missed milestones; triggers alerts with confidence scores and contributing factors. Builds interpretable scoring models (logistic regression/GBM with constraints) and rule sets to classify partners by tier, growth potential, and risk; supports prioritization and cadence decisions.

column8: Develop and execute joint go-to-market plans with partners, including co-selling motions, lead sharing processes, and campaign coordination to increase pipeline and bookings.
column15: This Alliance Manager task mixes strategy/relationship work (human-led) with repeatable planning, content, and operational execution (AI-augmentable). Generative AI accelerates creation of GTM artifacts, messaging, and summaries but needs grounding and review. Agentic AI is valuable for orchestrating multi-step execution across CRM/PRM/marketing tools and monitoring SLAs, yet must operate within strict guardrails and approvals. Traditional AI is best for structured analytics: segmentation, scoring, attribution, and data-quality enforcement. Assumptions: the org has integrated CRM/PRM and marketing automation, defined co-sell/lead-sharing policies, and approved playbooks; without these, agentic automation share drops and human share rises.
column16: Humans must (1) define/approve partner strategy, targets, incentives, and brand messaging; (2) approve any external-facing commitments/content for strategic partners; (3) adjudicate channel conflict, pricing/terms exceptions, and data-sharing consent/territory disputes; (4) oversee compliance (privacy, antitrust, MDF rules) and ensure auditability; (5) handle low-data or novel situations (new partner, new product, crisis comms) where AI confidence is low. Human sign-off gates are essential before: enabling automated lead sharing for a new partner, launching campaigns, changing routing rules, or executing actions that affect customer communications or contractual obligations.
column17: Over-automation could trigger unauthorized outreach, incorrect lead routing, or premature campaign launches; emergent behavior from poorly specified goals; limited explainability for why actions were taken. column19: Brittle rules and model drift can mis-segment partners/accounts, bias routing, or misattribute pipeline; poor handling of sparse data for new partners. Mitigations: periodic rule/model reviews, monitoring for drift, fairness checks, and fallback to human review when confidence is low.

column8: Negotiate and maintain partner agreements, program terms, and commercial frameworks in coordination with Legal and Finance to support scalable and compliant partnerships.
column15: Negotiating and maintaining partner agreements is high-stakes and accountability-heavy (legal liability, revenue impact, regulatory exposure), so humans must own strategy, approvals, and final commitments. Generative AI is well-suited for drafting/redlining, summarization, and producing program documentation, accelerating cycles but requiring verification to avoid hallucinated or non-compliant language. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating the multi-stakeholder workflow (intake, routing, reminders, audit trail) and by monitoring obligations/renewals post-signature; however, autonomy must be constrained to avoid unauthorized commitments. Traditional AI provides reliable, explainable checks and calculations (policy rules, risk triage, margin math) that reduce errors and standardize governance. Assumptions: organization has a clause library/playbooks, CLM/CRM systems, and defined approval matrices; without these, AI leverage drops and human share increases.
column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) negotiation posture and concession decisions (pricing, exclusivity, MFN, termination rights) where business context and relationship dynamics dominate; (2) any acceptance of legal/compliance risk (liability caps, indemnities, data processing, sanctions/export controls, anti-bribery) requiring accountable sign-off; (3) resolving conflicting guidance between Sales, Legal, and Finance and setting precedent for future deals; (4) validating factual inputs (partner identity, scope, data flows, revenue forecasts) that AI may misinterpret; (5) final review of generated contract language and partner-facing commitments before sending/signing. Edge cases needing humans: novel partnership models, cross-border data transfers, regulated industries, public-sector clauses, and urgent escalations where policy exceptions may be warranted.
column17: Could overstep authority (sending commitments, triggering signatures), mis-route approvals, or create cascading workflow actions from incorrect state; opaque decisioning can hinder auditability. Mitigations: hard permissioning, human-in-the-loop gates for external communications and approvals, deterministic guardrails, comprehensive logging, and rollback/kill switches.
column18: May hallucinate clauses, misstate agreed terms, or introduce inconsistent definitions; can leak confidential info if prompts/logs are mishandled; may produce biased or overly aggressive negotiation language. column19: Rules can become outdated with policy/regulatory changes; brittle handling of unusual deal structures; risk models may encode historical bias (e.g., over-penalizing certain regions). Mitigations: governance for rule updates, periodic model validation, exception pathways, and transparent feature/threshold documentation.
column20: Inconsistent sources of truth between CRM/CLM/finance systems can cause AI to draft from stale terms; handoff errors between GenAI drafts and rule-checkers can miss deviations; security and access control gaps across tools increase leakage risk. Mitigations: master data management, version control for templates, automated reconciliation checks, unified audit trails, and least-privilege access with environment segregation.

Automates calculations for revenue share, rebates, MDF, payment terms, and FX assumptions using structured inputs; flags outliers vs benchmarks.

column16: Human oversight is essential for: (1) KPI and incentive governance (prevent metric gaming; align with finance/legal), (2) approving forecast-impacting statements and partner-facing communications, (3) adjudicating ambiguous root causes where data is incomplete or confounded (e.g., territory changes, channel conflict), (4) deciding trade-offs among efficiency vs. partner experience, and (5) handling edge cases like suspected fraud, compliance breaches, or strategic partner escalations. column17: Autonomous actions (creating tasks, escalating, changing cadences) can cause noise, stakeholder fatigue, or unintended commitments; opaque decision logic and tool-permission misuse are risks. column15: This task mixes content creation (best suited to Generative AI), workflow coordination and data gathering (Agentic AI), and measurement/classification (Traditional AI), but it ultimately depends on human judgment for prioritization, partner/context nuance, and accountable change management. Agentic AI can reduce operational overhead by running an improvement backlog and collecting signals, but should operate within guardrails and approval steps. column8: Support end-to-end partner operations within Sales by accurately processing partner-related requests, ensuring strong controls, and maintaining compliance across all activities and required approvals.
column15: Partner operations is a mixed structured/unstructured workflow with compliance gates. Traditional AI is best for deterministic controls (field/approval validation, matching, SLA scoring) and anomaly flags. Generative AI accelerates understanding and documentation of unstructured requests and produces compliant drafts and audit narratives, but cannot be the source of truth due to hallucination risk. Agentic AI adds value by orchestrating multi-step execution across tools (case creation, routing, reminders, low-risk updates) under strict guardrails and stop conditions. Humans remain essential for accountable approvals, exception handling, and sensitive partner interactions. Assumptions: systems expose APIs/workflows; policies are codified into approval matrices; GenAI is deployed with retrieval over current SOPs; entry-level PDR handles routine ops with escalation paths. Constraints: regulated data handling, least-privilege access, and auditability reduce feasible autonomy.
column16: Human oversight is required at (1) approval gates mandated by policy (legal/finance/privacy, bank/tax changes, contract amendments), (2) exceptions where rules conflict or data is incomplete, (3) high-impact changes (tier upgrades, payout terms, access to restricted programs), (4) fraud/anomaly alerts requiring investigation, and (5) partner-facing communications that could create contractual commitments or reputational risk. Humans must also periodically review automation logs, sample completed cases for quality, and update SOPs/rules when programs or regulations change. Edge cases: similarly named partners causing mis-linking; cross-border compliance differences; urgent escalations where standard SLAs are overridden; conflicting instructions from multiple partner contacts.
column17: Over-automation could execute incorrect updates, route to wrong approvers, or spam stakeholders; emergent loops in follow-up/escalation; insufficient transparency for audits. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, explicit guardrails/allowlists, step-level approvals for high-risk actions, rate limits, simulation/testing in sandbox, and immutable audit logs with replay.
column18: Hallucinated policy guidance or fabricated evidence links; leakage of sensitive partner data in prompts; tone/commitment risk in partner emails (creating implied obligations); context drift when SOPs change. Mitigations: RAG with versioned SOPs and citations, restricted templates, PII redaction, human review for external comms, and automated confidence/uncertainty flags.
column19: Brittle rules leading to false rejects/accepts when programs change; biased anomaly models causing disproportionate flags; poor handling of novel request types. Mitigations: governance for rule updates, monitoring drift, periodic recalibration, explainability reports, and clear fallback to human review.
column20: Inconsistent source-of-truth between CRM/PRM/ticketing; handoff failures between GenAI summaries and rule engines; identity/access misconfiguration across tools; audit gaps if actions occur outside logged workflows. Humans own governance: updating rules, training data, and guardrails; validating that automation remains compliant as programs change.|Rule engines enforce completeness (mandatory fields), program eligibility, and approval routing based on partner tier/region/deal size; produces explainable pass/fail outputs. Uses fuzzy matching/classical ML to link requests to the correct partner account and detect duplicates; reduces misattribution and rework. Statistical/ML models flag unusual bank detail changes, sudden volume spikes, or mismatched tax/identity attributes for human review. Applies scoring rules to prioritize cases by age, severity, and revenue impact; triggers alerts when thresholds are breached.

column8: Manage day-to-day operational workflows for Channel and Partnerships, including intake, routing, and tracking of partner inquiries and tasks to meet service levels and internal standards.
column15: The workflow is largely process-driven (intake, validation, routing, tracking) where Traditional AI and automation excel, while Agentic AI adds value by coordinating multi-step actions across tools and proactively managing SLAs. Generative AI is best for unstructured text handling (summaries, drafting communications, narrative reporting) but must be constrained due to hallucination risk. Human involvement remains essential for policy setting, relationship-sensitive escalations, and QA/compliance. Assumptions: the org uses a CRM/helpdesk with APIs, has defined SLAs and partner tiers, and can implement guardrails (approval thresholds, audit logs). Constraints: entry-level role implies standardized playbooks and high volume; AI should reduce manual triage while keeping humans in control for exceptions.
column16: Humans must (1) approve/adjust routing rules and SLA policies, especially when they affect partner experience or internal resourcing; (2) review and handle escalations involving contractual commitments, pricing, legal/privacy, or reputational risk; (3) perform periodic QA audits of ticket summaries, classifications, and outbound messages; (4) adjudicate low-confidence or novel inquiries, conflicting CRM data, and cases where automation could contact the wrong partner or expose sensitive information. Mandatory human checkpoints: first-time partner onboarding issues, VIP/strategic partners, any message containing commitments/timelines, and any action that changes account ownership or closes a disputed ticket.
column17: Over-automation causing misrouting, premature closure, or unintended escalations; opaque decision paths; cascading errors across integrated systems. Mitigations: least-privilege permissions, action allowlists, simulation/testing, approval gates for high-impact actions, and comprehensive audit trails with rollback procedures.
column18: Hallucinated facts in summaries or partner communications; tone/policy drift; leakage of sensitive partner data if prompts/logs are mishandled. Mitigations: retrieval-augmented generation from approved knowledge bases, strict templates, redaction, confidence scoring, and human review for external messages above risk thresholds.
column19: Brittle rules leading to misclassification when partner programs change; bias/fairness issues if prioritization encodes partner tier inappropriately; false anomaly alerts. Mitigations: regular rule/model reviews, monitoring drift, explainability, and feedback loops from human corrections.
column20: Data inconsistency between CRM/helpdesk/email causing duplicate or conflicting tickets; race conditions when multiple automations act on the same case; unclear ownership between AI components; security/compliance gaps across vendors. Mitigations: single source of truth, idempotent workflows, event-driven architecture with locking, unified logging, and governance for data access and retention.

Generate operational insights narratives from queue data|Define/maintain routing policies, SLAs, and partner tier exceptions. Handle sensitive/high-stakes partner issues and escalations. Quality assurance, compliance review, and edge-case adjudication|Deterministic intake validation and enrichment. Rules/ML-based routing and prioritization. Dashboarding, KPI computation, and anomaly detection
column23: Uses workflow automation with planning to create cases from inbound channels, enrich with context, assign owners, set SLAs, follow up, and close-loop updates across CRM/helpdesk/Slack/Email; adapts routing based on workload and exceptions within guardrails. Continuously monitors queues and deadlines, triggers reminders/escalations, re-routes stalled items, and schedules follow-ups; can propose mitigation actions (e.g., reassign, request info) and execute low-risk steps automatically. Detects exceptions, runs multi-step playbooks to request missing fields, merge duplicates, or reclassify; pauses and asks for human approval when confidence is low or policy-sensitive.|Extracts intent, key entities (partner name, deal ID, region, urgency), and produces a concise summary for the ticket; drafts clarifying questions when details are missing. Generates templated yet contextual updates, acknowledgements, and resolution notes aligned to tone and policy; supports multilingual responses if needed. Turns metrics and logs into readable weekly summaries (themes, top blockers, suggested process improvements) for managers and cross-functional stakeholders.|Requires business judgment, alignment with leadership, and periodic updates as partnerships strategy changes; humans arbitrate trade-offs and approve policy changes. Humans audit AI outputs for accuracy, privacy, and contractual compliance; resolve ambiguous intents, conflicting data, or novel request types not covered by playbooks.|Applies rules/regex and lookup tables to validate required fields, map partner domains to accounts, tag region/segment, and auto-fill structured fields from CRM/master data.
